description: Knowledge acquisition necessary to work as an emergency medical responder (EMR) to help sustain life, reduce pain and minimize the consequences of injury or sudden illness until more advanced medical help takes over. Appropriate decisions about the care to provide in a medical emergency. Understanding the role of an EMR as a crucial link in the emergency medical services (EMS) system.
subject: ATEP

title: Emergency Medical Responder Lab
description: Clinical applications necessary to work as an emergency medical responder (EMR) to help sustain life, reduce pain and minimize the consequences of injury or sudden illness until more advanced medical help takes over. Appropriate decisions about the care to provide in a medical emergency. Skills an EMR needs to act as a crucial link in the emergency medical services (EMS) system.
subject: ATEP

title: Introduction to Therapeutic Interventions in Athletic Training
description: This course is designed to address the basic knowledge and techniques needed to plan, operate, document, and evaluate therapeutic interventions used in treatment of athletic injuries/illnesses. Includes basic knowledge and techniques of therapeutic interventions; modalities and rehabilitation used by athletic trainers.
subject: ATEP

title: Principles of Evidenced-Based Medicine
description: Development of essential skills for integrating evidence into healthcare practice. Students will learn how to explore critical questions by accessing, interpreting, evaluating, and integrating relevant research literature in healthcare.
subject: ATEP

title: Principles of Athletic Training Lab
description: Foundational knowledge and skills for athletic trainers in injury prevention, care and recognition, emergency management and their role as a healthcare provider within the larger context of a changing healthcare system.
subject: ATEP

title: Clinical Experiences in Athletic Training I
description: A clinical education experience to develop athletic training skills in a student mentor-guided model. Course content will focus on improvement in a selected area of clinical practice, specifically emergency action planning and providing immediate care.
subject: ATEP

title: Clinical Experiences in Athletic Training II
description: A clinical education experience to develop athletic training skills in a student mentor-guided model. Course content will focus on improvement in a selected area of clinical practice, specifically assessment, treatment and rehabilitation of lower extremity orthopedic conditions.
subject: ATEP
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ATEP 711.

title: Clinical Experiences in Athletic Training III
description: A clinical education experience to develop athletic training skills in a student mentor-guided model. Course content will focus on improvement in selected areas of clinical practice, specifically prevention and management of head, neck, spine, and abdominal injuries and the response to medical emergencies.
subject: ATEP
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ATEP 712.

title: Clinical Experiences in Athletic Training IV
description: A clinical education experience to develop athletic training skills in a student mentor-guided model. Course content will focus on improvement in a selected area of clinical practice, specifically assessment, treatment and rehabilitation of upper extremity orthopedic conditions.
subject: ATEP
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ATEP 713.

title: Clinical Experiences in Athletic Training V
description: A clinical education immersive experience in a specific health care setting to develop athletic training skills in a mentor-guided model. Course content will focus on improvement and integration of culminating skills and knowledge into the practice of athletic training.
subject: ATEP
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ATEP 714.

title: Behavioral Health and Wellness
description: Integration of physiological, psychological, and social constructs in relationship to physical performance and clinical decision making to enhance patient care. Specific focus will be on understanding individual differences in behavior in the areas of physical fitness, nutrition, and mental health.
subject: ATEP

title: Emergency Management Practices in Athletic Training
description: Examination of common injuries and illnesses that can cause medical emergencies in sport and physical activity. The majority of the class will be analyzing research related to these conditions to determine prevention and treatment strategies. Education, gender issues, politics, and media will also be a platform for class discussions.
subject: ATEP

title: Evidence Based Practice in Medical Emergencies
description: Examination of common injuries and illnesses that lead to medical emergencies (e.g., sudden death) in sport and physical activity. Critical analysis of research to determine prevention and treatment strategies.
subject: ATEP

title: Evidence-Based Approach to Evaluation, Treatment, and Rehabilitation
description: Advanced study of principles of evidence-based medicine and the interpretation of clinical research that assesses evaluation, treatment, and rehabilitation of injuries.
subject: ATEP

title: Contemporary Issues in Athletic Training
description: Examination of issues shaping the athletic training profession with an emphasis on practical application and professional development.
subject: ATEP

title: Advanced Treatment and Rehabilitation of Athletic Injuries
description: Advanced study of the treatment of athletic injuries focusing on the concepts and principles of a comprehensive rehabilitation program, including therapeutic exercise and therapeutic modalities.
subject: ATEP

title: Current Research in Athletic Training Education
description: Examination of current literature in athletic training education as it pertains to the clinical and didactic experiences of athletic training students, clinical instructors, and practicing professionals.
subject: ATEP

title: Advanced Athletic Training Practicum I
description: Provides advanced practical experience and the integration of evidence-based practice in the sports medicine settings. Course content will focus on graduate research project and topics related to athletic training education.
subject: ATEP

title: Advanced Clinical Practicum in Athletic Training II
description: Provides advanced practical experience and the integration of evidenced-based practice on the sports medicine settings. Course content will focus on graduate research project and topics related to athletic training administration and management.
subject: ATEP

title: Evidence Based Practice in Weight Management Assessment
description: Critical analysis of the current literature on weight control and health, metabolism, energy balance, and roll of diet and exercise in prevention and/or treatment in weight management in the physically active population.
subject: ATEP

title: Advanced Clinical Skills in Athletic Training
description: This course is designed to assess the advanced clinical skills of graduate athletic training students in their ability to evaluate, manage and prevent athletic injuries. The content of this course will focus on advanced athletic training clinical skills and evidence-based practice.
subject: ATEP

title: Traumatic Catastrophic Injury Management
description: An examination of traumatic injuries that can cause medical emergencies. Specific focus on preventing, recognizing and treating traumatic injuries that may present during work, physical activity, and sport.
subject: ATEP

title: Management of Exertional Injuries and Medical Conditions
description: An examination of exertional or medical conditions that can cause emergencies. Specific focus on preventing, recognizing and treating such conditions that may present during work, physical activity, and sport.
subject: ATEP

title: Evaluation and Therapeutic Intervention of Lower Extremity Injuries
description: Study of the lower extremities as they relate to the prevention; recognition, evaluation and assessment; immediate care; and treatment, rehabilitation and reconditioning of injuries and illnesses to athletes and others engaged in physical activity.
subject: ATEP

title: Evaluation Therapeutic Intervention of Lower Extremity Injuries Lab
description: Integration of knowledge and skills for orthopedic/physical assessment of common injuries to the lower body.
subject: ATEP

title: Evaluation and Therapeutic Intervention of Head, Neck and Spine Injuries
description: Study of the Head, Neck and Spine as they relate to the prevention; recognition, evaluation and assessment; immediate care; and treatment, rehabilitation and reconditioning of injuries and illnesses to athletes and others engaged in physical activity.
subject: ATEP

title: Evaluation Therapeutic Intervention of Head, Neck, Spine Injuries Lab
description: Integration of knowledge and skills for orthopedic/physical assessment of common injuries to the head, neck and spine.
subject: ATEP

title: Evaluation and Therapeutic Intervention of Upper Extremity Injuries
description: Study of the upper extremities as they relate to the prevention; recognition, evaluation and assessment; immediate care; and treatment, rehabilitation and reconditioning of injuries and illnesses to athletes and others engaged in physical activity.
subject: ATEP

title: Evaluation Therapeutic Intervention of Upper Extremity Injuries Lab
description: Integration of knowledge and skills for orthopedic/physical assessment of common injuries to the upper body.
subject: ATEP

title: Concepts of Behavioral Mental Health in Athletic Training
description: This course includes theoretical and applied information regarding mental and behavioral health theories, models, and concepts in Athletic Training and in the context of the larger social services system. A variety of didactic training in clinical techniques will be explored (e.g., screening, ethics reporting, confidentiality, recognition referral process, etc.).
subject: ATEP

title: Mental Health Challenges in Sport and Physical Activity
description: The course will provide an overview of mental wellness, mental health challenges, and mental illnesses that athletic trainers providing care to people in work, life, and sport may experience.
subject: ATEP

title: Assessing and Treating Feeding and Eating Disorders, Exercise and Food Addictions
description: This course will introduce students to epidemiology, clinical presentation, clinical work-up, diagnosis and treatment approaches and medical complications of patients with various feeding and eating disorders. This course will integrate both psychiatric and medical knowledge and compliment clinical and didactic experiences across athletic training.
subject: ATEP

title: Supporting Mental Wellness in Athletic Training
description: Explore therapies, treatments, and interventions for mental health challenges experienced by patients in athletic training settings. The course will discuss supportive interventions within the scope of athletic training, as well as explore therapies and treatments that referral is required for in athletic training.
subject: ATEP

title: Primary Care in Athletic Training
description: This course presents the most common pathological conditions, clinical presentations (signs and symptoms) and diagnostic methods involving the many body systems, with an emphasis on how pathological conditions affect the body system and subsequent sport participation. The goals of the course are for students to learn the clinical presentations of diseases, how to clinically reason through the examination, and creating a care plan.
subject: ATEP

title: Pharmacological Interventions in Sports Medicine
description: This course presents students with knowledge and skills to meet patient needs as it relates to pharmacology in sports medicine. The goals of the course are for students to learn legal considerations for medication management; develop skills that are patient-centered and allow them to work in interprofessional teams; identify at-risk populations for medication abuse and misuse; and develop management strategies using common pharmacological drugs in physically active populations.
subject: ATEP

title: Research Methods Prospectus Writing in Athletic Training
description: The study of applicable methods and tools of research in athletic training. Introduction of methods of research in athletic training, encompassing aspects of study planning, research design, participant sampling, measurement, data analysis, ethics, and reporting in sports medicine.
subject: ATEP

title: Clinical and Practice-Based Research
description: A research design and methods course focused on athletic training clinical practice including patient-oriented outcomes, quality improvement, clinical-rated assessments, clinical CASE reports, and current clinical concepts.
subject: ATEP

title: Athletic Training Administration
description: Examination of fundamental principles of administration and assessment of the delivery of athletic training services in the context of the larger health care system. Specific focus business management principles associated with athletic training clinical practice and as well as leadership and professional development to achieve the best patient outcomes.
subject: ATEP

title: Clinical Pathology and Pharmacology in Athletic Training
description: Examination of injury, illness and/or disease of various body systems; specific understanding of medical diagnostics, interventions (including pharmacology) and participation considerations for the athletic population are addressed.
subject: ATEP

title: Clinical Pathology Pharmacology in Athletic Training Lab
description: Integration of knowledge and skills for athletic trainers in the physical assessment of common injury, illness and/or disease of various body systems.
subject: ATEP

title: Project in Athletic Education
description: Independently executed project designed to expand the student's knowledge of athletic training.
subject: ATEP

title: Business Careers in the Global Economy
description: Course outlines job search essentials for business careers in a global economy. Business networking, interviewing, and career planning with an international focus will be covered. Completion of lower division business courses.
subject: BADM

title: Mastering Artificial Intelligence for Business
description: Core concepts and algorithmic foundations of artificial intelligence (AI) and generative Al (genAI); leveraging those tools to solve real-world business challenges including ethically defending, augmenting, and transforming organizations.
subject: BADM

title: Practical AI in Business
description: Introduction to contemporary applications of AI and related technology across the core functions of business; introduction to the different functional areas of work organizations.
subject: BADM

title: Independent Study
description: Not to include Business Internship. (See BADM 499 ) Contract approved by instructor, advisor and undergraduate division head is required.
subject: BADM

title: Planning and Managing a Career in Business
description: Course will focus on defining and enhancing life/career goals using leadership assessment, life planning processes, and emotional intelligence discussions, all linked to transition to work.
subject: BADM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BADM 301.

title: Business Internship
description: Supervised work experience of at least nine hours per week (for 15 weeks in a semester) or minimum 135 hours total; approved by area program director, to include one class meeting a month and individual consultation. Contract approved by instructor, advisor, and undergraduate division head is required.
subject: BADM
prereq: Prerequisites: Cumulative GPA of 2.8.

title: Master of Science Project
description: Preparation of an applied research project in a functional area of business administration under the supervision of a graduate faculty member.
subject: BADM

title: Special Topics in Business
description: Analysis of current topics, issues, and practices in business. May be repeated as content varies by title for a maximum of 9 credit hours per program of study.
subject: BADM

title: Biological Principles I
description: Introductory survey of macromolecules, cell structure and function, genetics, and molecular biology.
subject: BIOL

title: Biological Principles I
description: Introductory survey of macromolecules, cell structure and function, genetics, and molecular biology. Three lecture hours per week. Restricted to students who have credit for BIOL 101L but lack the lecture.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 101L.

title: Biological Principles I Laboratory
description: (Recommended concurrent with BIOL 101 ). Experimental examination of basic principles of cell biology, genetics and metabolism. Three hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Biological Principles II
description: Introductory survey of plant and animal development, physiology, ecology, and evolution.
subject: BIOL

title: Biological Principles II
description: Introductory survey of plant and animal development, physiology, ecology, and evolution. Three lecture hours per week. Restricted to students who have credit for BIOL 102L but lack the lecture.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 102L.

title: Biological Principles II Laboratory
description: Experimental examination of structure and function of plant and animal systems, biodiversity, ecology. BIOL 101 , BIOL 102 , BIOL 101L and BIOL 102L must be completed prior to enrolling in 300 -level or above Biology courses.
subject: BIOL

title: General Biology
description: Basic biological concepts and issues for non-biology majors. Credit may not be given for both this course and BIOL 120 . Three lecture, two laboratory hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: General Biology (Audio-Tutorial)
description: Addendum to BIOL 110 .
subject: BIOL

title: Human Biology
description: Fundamental principles of human biology. Credit may not be given for both BIOL 110 and BIOL 120. Three lecture hours per week. Not for major credit.
subject: BIOL

title: Laboratory in Human Biology
description: Exercises dealing with basic concepts of human biology. Not for major credit.
subject: BIOL

title: Plant Science
description: An introduction to plant science for the non-major. This course does not carry major credit, and is not designed as a prerequisite for other biology courses. Plant development, physiology, genetics, evolution, and ecology will be considered. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Plant Science Laboratory
description: Laboratory exercises, demonstrations, and audio-visual supplements to BIOL 200 . Not for major credit. Two hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Infectious Diseases, Human Health, and Ethics
description: An introduction to pathogens including bacteria, viruses, and protozoans. Basic elements of the immune system coupled with discussion of how pathogens cause disease. This information is placed in the context of how scientific research leads to the discovery, treatment, and prevention of the disease. The ethical implications of each pathogen and its treatment strategies are discussed.
subject: BIOL

title: Exploration of Infectious Diseases
description: This class goes beyond the classroom to visit sites affected by infectious diseases, like cholera and bubonic plague, in order to put these diseases into context, gaining a deeper understanding of the implications of infectious disease on human health and ethics. This course will experience infectious diseases through the scope of art, literature, and architecture.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in BIOL 202.

title: Genetics and Society
description: (Designed for non-major students.) Genetic principles, emphasizing human heredity. Relevance of recent advances in genetics. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Our Hungry World from Malthus to McDonalds
description: Scientific and social issues concerning the interrelationship of culture and agricultural biotic diversity and technology, climate change, resources management, food security, and human health.
subject: BIOL

title: The Biology of Dogs
description: The study of domestic dogs (Canis lupus familiaris) and their relatives. Genetics, evolution, behavior, and physiology in dogs.
subject: BIOL

title: Elementary Life Science
description: This course will ensure that elementary education majors will understand the fundamental concepts of Biology. Cannot be used for biology major credit.
subject: BIOL

title: Anatomy
description: Functional anatomy of the human body and its relation to disease processes. Not for biology major credit.
subject: BIOL

title: Anatomy Laboratory
description: The principles of anatomy as demonstrated by microscopic studies and animal dissection. Three hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Applied Human Physiology
description: Fundamentals of functional human biology and knowledge of contemporary medical problems. Not for major credit.
subject: BIOL

title: Human Anatomy and Physiology I
description: Functional anatomy and physiology of the human body, including the integumentary, skeletal, muscular, and nervous systems. Not available for biology major credit. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Human Anatomy and Physiology Laboratory
description: The principles of anatomy and physiology as demonstrated by microscopic studies, animal dissection, and physiological experiments. One three-hour laboratory per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Human Anatomy and Physiology II
description: Functional anatomy and physiology of the human body, including the cardiovascular, endocrine, excretory, reproductive, digestive, and respiratory systems. Not available for biology major credit. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 243.

title: Human Anatomy and Physiology Laboratory
description: A continuation of BIOL 243L . One three-hour laboratory per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Microbiology
description: An introduction to bacteria and viruses, emphasizing structure, metabolism, and pathogenesis. Discussion of infectious diseases, antigen-antibody relationships, and anti-microbial agents in chemotherapy. Not available for biology major credit. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: College-level Biology and Chemistry.

title: Microbiology Laboratory
description: Not available for biology major credit. Three hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Physiology
description: Physiology of human systems especially susceptible to disturbance: immunobiology, circulation, excretion, metabolism, endocrinology, and muscle physiology. Not for biology major credit. Intended for pharmacy students.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 102.

title: Introduction to Environmental Biology
description: Basic ecological principles and the impacts of human population growth and technology. Not for major credit.
subject: BIOL

title: Introduction to Environmental Biology Laboratory
description: Demonstrations, data analyses, discussions, and films relating to human ecology, resource use, and environmental impact. Not for major credit. Two hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Ecology and Evolution Laboratory
description: Experiments, exercises, and demonstrations. Three hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Cell and Molecular Biology
description: Principles of eukaryotic cell structure, molecular organization, and physiology. Genome organization and expression. Cell growth, division, and cell-cell interactions.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 101; C or better in BIOL 102 or MSCI 311; C or better in CHEM 112 or CHEM 142.

title: Cell and Molecular Biology Laboratory
description: Experiments, exercises, and demonstrations. Three hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Fundamental Genetics
description: Basic principles of transmission and molecular genetics; quantitative inheritance; recombination; biochemical aspects of gene function and regulation; developmental genetics and population genetics. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 101 and BIOL 102 or a C or better in MSCI 311.

title: Fundamental Genetics Laboratory
description: Observational and experimental examination of principles of genetics and inheritance.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 101, BIOL 101L, BIOL 102, and BIOL 102L or a C or better in MSCI 311.

title: Introduction to Animal Science
description: Exploration of current careers in the animal industry including a brief overview of the sciences involved in animal production such as genetics and selection, behavior, physiology, reproduction, and nutrition of cattle (beef and dairy), horses, swine, sheep, poultry, and others.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 102.

title: Introduction to Animal Science Laboratory
description: Explorations into the use of animals in various industries in today’s society.
subject: BIOL

title: Laboratory Teaching Experience
description: Participation in preparation and teaching of undergraduate biological sciences laboratories.
subject: BIOL

title: Independent Study
description: Contract approved by instructor, advisor, and department head is required for undergraduate students.
subject: BIOL

title: Cellular and Molecular Neurobiology
description: Cellular and molecular mechanisms underlying the development and functions of the nervous system, such as nervous system patterning, neuronal differentiation/migration, formation of neuronal projections, development of synapses, apoptosis, refinement of neuronal circuits, and how cells and neurons respond to signals from the environment.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 302.

title: Comparative Vertebrate Anatomy
description: Phylogenetic and comparative aspects of anatomy, reproduction, and embryology of the vertebrates. Three lecture hours and one three-hour laboratory period per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 102 or MSCI 311.

title: Survey of the Plant Kingdom Laboratory
description: Three hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Medicinal Botany
description: A survey of plants affecting human health and how they are used historically and in modern times, with emphasis on the biologically active constituents.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 301 and BIOL 302.

title: Plant Form and Function Laboratory
description: Illustration of principles of introductory botany and plant physiology using experiments, exercises, and demonstrations. Three laboratory hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Plant Ecology
description: Structure and dynamics of plant populations and communities, including life histories, adaptations, and plant interactions.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in BIOL 301 and BIOL 303.

title: Plant Ecology Laboratory
description: Laboratory and field exercises in plant ecology.
subject: BIOL

title: Principles of Biological Oceanography
description: Principles and methods of measuring production in the sea. Emphasis on the ocean’s role in the global carbon budget. Three lecture hours per week. Scheduled field trips are required.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: MSCI 311, BIOL 301.

title: Advanced Human Physiology
description: Functional physiology of human organ systems.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 302 or MSCI 311.

title: Advanced Human Physiology Laboratory
description: Experiments on organ system functions using different animal models.
subject: BIOL

title: Advanced Human Anatomy
description: Structure, function, and development of human anatomy.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in any two of the following: BIOL 301, BIOL 302, or BIOL 303.

title: Advanced Human Anatomy Laboratory
description: Practical exercises in structure, function, and development of anatomy using digital and animal models.
subject: BIOL

title: Advanced Microbiology
description: The taxonomy, morphology, metabolism, genetics, and ecology of microorganisms.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 302.

title: Advanced Microbiology Laboratory
description: Practical exercises with the taxonomy, morphology, metabolism, genetics, and ecology of microorganisms.
subject: BIOL

title: Domestic Animal Nutrition
description: Elements of nutrition and animal feeding in veterinary practice. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 302.

title: Undergraduate Seminar in Biological Sciences
description: Student seminars and a survey of research in the fields of Biological Sciences.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 301, BIOL 302, and BIOL 303, or Instructor's Permission

title: Introduction to Biological Research
description: Methodologies of biological research with emphasis on hypothesis formation, research design, and data collection, and current issues in biology.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in any BIOL 300-level or higher laboratory course; C or higher in any two of BIOL 301, BIOL 302, and BIOL 303.

title: Practicum in Biological Sciences
description: Supervised training in Biological Sciences conducting experiments, analyzing data, and solving scientific problems. Contract required.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in BIOL 399.

title: Environmental Microbiology
description: An overview of the microbial world including a survey of the distribution, functioning, and diversity of microorganisms in natural systems. Discusses the crucial roles that microorganisms play in ecosystem function, biogeochemical cycles, and environmental quality.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in MSCI 102 or BIOL 102; C or better in CHEM 112.

title: Developmental Biology
description: An introduction to how cell-cell communication, gene expression, cell division, cytoskeletal dynamics, and interactions with the extracellular matrix result in the differentiation, pattern formation, morphogenesis, and growth necessary to generate a new individual.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 302.

title: Developmental Biology Laboratory I
description: Descriptive and experimental exercises related to embryology. One three-hour laboratory per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Developmental Biology II
description: Molecular aspects of development from gamete formation through tissue and organ differentiation in plants and animals. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 505.

title: Developmental Biology Laboratory II
description: A series of experimentally oriented laboratory exercises will be performed. One three-hour laboratory per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Human Embryology
description: An introduction to the processes involved in normal human embryology and how genetic and environmental disruptions result in congenital disorders.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in BIOL 302 and BIOL 303.

title: Invertebrate Zoology
description: Phylogenetic and comparative aspects of anatomy, physiology, reproduction, and embryology of the invertebrates. Three lecture and one three-hour laboratory period per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 301 or MSCI 311.

title: Plant Development
description: Descriptive and molecular examination of the processes and mechanisms used by plants in organogenesis, differentiation, and morphogenesis. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 302 and BIOL 303.

title: Plant Developmental Laboratory
description: Experiments utilizing a genetic approach to the study of plant development. Three laboratory hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Mycology
description: Taxonomy and morphology of fungi; cultivation, life histories, and economic importance; all classes and major orders considered. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 301.

title: Marine Plants
description: Diversity, distribution, physiology, ecology, evolution, and economic importance of marine algal, seagrass, and mangrove communities. Three lecture and three laboratory hours per week. Scheduled field trips are required.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 301 or MSCI 311.

title: The Fall Flora
description: Two lecture and four laboratory hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 301.

title: The Spring Flora
description: Two lecture and four laboratory hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 301.

title: The Summer Flora
description: Two lecture and four laboratory hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 301.

title: Histology
description: An introduction to the tissues that make up the human body. The microscopic anatomy of tissues is examined and discussed in terms of function and physiology. Three lecture hours and four laboratory hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Parasitology
description: Parasites of biological, economic, and public health importance. Three lecture and three laboratory hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: 300 level Biology course or equivalent.

title: Herpetology
description: The evolutionary ecology of reptiles and amphibians, as well as conservation biology of the major groups, global diversity of herpetofauna, with emphasis on reptiles and amphibians of the southeastern US.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 301.

title: Animal Behavior
description: A comparative survey of behavior patterns of animals from protists to humans and the physiological mechanisms underlying behavior.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 301 or MSCI 311.

title: Animal Behavior Laboratory
description: Observational and experimental methods used in classifying animal behavior patterns and in determining underlying control mechanisms. One three-hour laboratory per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Fishery Management
description: Management and conservation of aquatic and marine resources, with emphasis on fisheries. Data procurement and analysis; commercial and recreational fisheries; sociological, political, legal, and environmental factors that affect fishery management; and fish biodiversity.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 301.

title: Ichthyology
description: Phylogeny, morphology, behavior, and ecology of fishes. Three lecture and 3 laboratory hours plus three field trips to be arranged.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 301 or MSCI 311.

title: Aquaculture
description: Introduction to the practical and scientific aspects of the commercial culture of freshwater and marine organisms. Three lecture hours per week. One all-day field trip required.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 301 or MSCI 311.

title: Behavior of Marine Organisms
description: The identification of behavioral adaptations of estuarine and marine organisms: their ecology, physiology, development, and evolutionary history; field observations.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 101 and BIOL 102 or MSCI 311.

title: Biochemistry
description: Description of biological macromolecules and major metabolic pathways.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 334.

title: Biochemistry Laboratory
description: Experiments and demonstrations illustrating the principles of biochemistry. Three laboratory hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Comparative Physiology
description: An integrative and comparative study of the structure, function, and evolution of the physiological systems of animals. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 302 or MSCI 311.

title: Comparative Physiology Laboratory
description: Laboratory exercises to illustrate principles from BIOL 543 . Three hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Protein Biochemistry and Metabolism
description: Comprehensive introduction to the structure and function of major biological macromolecules—amino acids, proteins, carbohydrates, and lipids. Topics include enzyme catalysis, kinetics, inhibition, and core metabolic pathways such as glycolysis, gluconeogenesis, the citric acid cycle, oxidative phosphorylation, lipid metabolism, and amino acid metabolism. Concludes with an integrated study of mammalian fuel metabolism and the regulation and coordination of metabolic pathways.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 334, or departmental permission.

title: Molecular Biochemistry of the Cell
description: Examination of key topics in molecular biology, including DNA and RNA metabolism, regulation of gene expression at transcriptional and post-transcriptional levels, protein synthesis, and mechanisms of cell cycle control and cancer development. Includes principles and applications of recombinant DNA technology.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in BIOL 302, or departmental approval.

title: Plant Physiology
description: A general survey of the major physiological processes in plants. Two lecture and four laboratory hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 302 and BIOL 425.

title: Bacteriology
description: Introduction to bacteria and viruses emphasizing ultrastructure, physiology, genetics, and growth. Discussion of public health, industrial, and environmental microbiology. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 302 or MSCI 311.

title: Bacteriology Laboratory
description: Three laboratory hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Population Genetics
description: An introduction to the principles of population genetics, with emphasis on the origin, maintenance, and significance of genetic variation in natural populations.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 301 or MSCI 311.

title: Genomics
description: Current concepts and applications of genomics, addressing questions from throughout biological inquiry.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 301, BIOL 303.

title: Epigenetics
description: An examination of the differences between genetic and epigenetic influences on gene expression, epigenetic mechanisms that regulate gene expression, how epigenetic modifications are propagated, and the phenotypic consequences of abnormal epigenetic regulation in disease, development, and evolution.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 302 and BIOL 303 or permission of the department.

title: Stem Cells and The Physiological Environment
description: Discussion of how physiological factors, like nutritional status, influence systemic signals to alter stem cell activity, and the physiological stimuli that impact stem cell activity in a variety of organisms (from worms to humans).
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C of higher in BIOL 302.

title: Principles of Ecology
description: Interactions of organisms and the environment; ecosystem structure and functions. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 301 or MSCI 311.

title: Principles of Ecology Laboratory
description: Three hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Conservation Biology
description: Principles of conservation biology. Importance of biodiversity, causes of decline and extinction, and restoration and conversation policy in terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 301.

title: Freshwater Ecology
description: Quantitative study of the population, community and evolutionary ecology of freshwater habitats (lakes, ponds, rivers, streams, wetlands). Includes mandatory fieldtrips.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 301.

title: Insect Ecology
description: Processes governing insect ecology (distribution, abundance, population regulation, community assembly, and diversity patterns across the globe), and investigations of the life-history, behavior, and development of South Carolina insects.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in BIOL 301 or MSCI 311, or permission of the instructor.

title: Marine Conservation Biology
description: Exploration of how human activities affect marine natural populations, species, communities and ecosystems, including threats to biodiversity; approaches to marine conservation; and ecological and evolutionary responses to anthropogenic disturbance.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 301.

title: Marine Ecology
description: Structure, dynamics, and interactions between populations and communities in marine ecosystems. Attendance at designated departmental seminars is required. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: CHEM 111 and BIOL 301 or MSCI 311.

title: Marine Ecology Laboratory
description: Laboratory and field exercises in coastal environments. Three hours per week plus field trips.
subject: BIOL

title: Marine Fisheries Ecology
description: Interdisciplinary examination of the distribution, reproduction, survival, and historical variation of the principal commercial marine fisheries.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 301.

title: Ecology of Coral Reefs
description: Structure, productivity, and biodiversity of coral reefs, emphasizing their sensitivity, stability, and sustainability. Taught as an extended field experience with daily lectures and guided research activities.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 301 or MSCI 311.

title: Genomic Data Science
description: This course focuses on quantitative knowledge for interdisciplinary applications in genetics as well as hands-on experience in analyzing genetic data. In this course, students will have programming exercises in using analysis tools to conduct genome-wide analysis, annotation, and interpretation of genetic data using R/Bioconductor packages.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in STAT 201 or higher.

title: Topics in Biology
description: Current developments in biological sciences. Readings and research on selected topics. Course content varies and will be announced in the schedule of classes by title.
subject: BIOL

title: Hallmarks of Cancer
description: Survey of current concepts regarding the molecular and genetic factors that regulate the origin and progression of cancer. Readings based on current primary literature.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 302 and BIOL 303.

title: Virology - Classical and Emerging Concepts
description: Advanced study of viruses with regard to biochemical, molecular, pathological, epidemiological, and biotechnological aspects. Focus on animal viruses with particular emphasis on human pathogens.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 302.

title: Stem Cell Biology
description: Focuses on the understanding of how stem cells can be used to make fundamental biological discoveries with a special focus in neuroscience.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 302.

title: Immunobiology
description: Basic immunological concepts including antibody structure, function, and genetics; cellular immunology; transplantation; hypersensitivity; autoimmunity; and immunity to infectious diseases.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 302.

title: Medical Mycology
description: Advanced study of infectious diseases caused by fungi. Etiology, symptoms, and treatment of fungi related illnesses.
subject: BIOL

title: Marine Phytoplankton
description: Examines the physiology and ecology of phytoplankton, including environmental controls on community composition, primary productivity, and detection and characterization of water quality (eutrophication) and harmful algal blooms.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: MSCI 102 or MSCI 450 or BIOL 450.

title: Biology of Birds
description: Biology of birds at molecular, organismal, and population levels, emphasizing unique adaptations of the class of Aves.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 301, BIOL 302, and BIOL 303.

title: Biology of Human Neurodevelopmental Disorders
description: An overview of neurodevelopmental disorders, focusing on the known genetic and environmental mechanisms leading to altered nervous system function. Discussion for each disorder will include how the nervous system is altered, the underlying biological mechanisms, and current/future treatments. Includes discussion of scientific journal articles.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in BIOL 302.

title: Biology of Neurological Diseases
description: Advances in molecular and cellular neurobiology that bring new understanding for human neurological disease.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 302 and SCHC 330 or BIOL 405.

title: Neurophysiology
description: Descriptive and computational exploration of the molecules, cells, and circuits in the nervous systems of animals that make observable behaviors possible.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in BIOL 302.

title: Neurophysiology Laboratory
description: Experimental investigation of the neurons and neural circuits that underlie observable behaviors in animals.
subject: BIOL

title: Microbial Ecology
description: Interactions of microorganisms with each other, with more complex organisms, and with their environments. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 550 and either BIOL 301 or MSCI 311.

title: Biophysical Ecology
description: This course examines how the mechanisms by which animals and plants interact with their physical environments influence organismal physiology.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 301, MATH 141 or MATH 122.

title: Biochemical Evolution
description: Advanced study of related aspects of biological evolution. Rose of life from physical and chemical precursors, biochemical basis of adaptation to ecological pressures, and biochemical aspects of the origins and maintenance of biodiversity.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 301, BIOL 302, BIOL 303.

title: Limnology
description: A study of the aquatic environment and its biota. Three lecture and four laboratory hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 301.

title: Evolutionary Biology
description: An advanced course in evolutionary biology, including natural selection, neutral evolution, molecular evolution population genetics, quantitative genetics, sexual selection, speciation, human evolution, and the evolution of disease.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 301 and BIOL 303.

title: Bioinformatics
description: Studies of the principles of genetics and molecular biology as applied to adaptive evolution of genes and genomes.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 302, BIOL 303.

title: Speciation and the Origin of Biodiversity
description: Speciation as the source of biological diversity. Analysis of genetic and ecological models of speciation using primary literature.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 301 and BIOL 303.

title: Biotechnology
description: Studies in molecular biology and genetics with emphasis on the use of newly developed techniques in biotechnology. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 302 and BIOL 303.

title: Experimental Biotechnology
description: Techniques used in biotechnology will be employed in the context of an experimental project. Twelve laboratory hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 302, BIOL 302L.

title: Biology of Mammals
description: Evolution, systematics, genetics, ecology, and adaptation of mammals. Emphasis on native South Carolina species. Two lectures and one two-hour laboratory per week, plus five field trips to be arranged.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 301 or MSCI 311.

title: Signal Transduction and Pathogenesis
description: Signaling pathways involved in human diseases, such as cancer, AIDS, autoimmune diseases and diabetes, and cellular processes involving apoptosis, cell cycle, cell-cell adhesion, growth factors, hormones, G protein-couples receptors, cytokines and immune response.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 302 and BIOL 303.

title: Human Molecular Genetics
description: Molecular mechanisms underlying gene action and differentiation in man; the genetic bases for human variability and inborn metabolic errors leading to inherited diseases.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 302 and BIOL 303.

title: Molecular and Genetic Mechanisms of Disease Pathogenesis
subject: BIOL

title: Metabolic Biochemistry of Human Disease
description: Core concepts of biochemistry as applied to human health and disease.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 555/BIOL 545 or CHEM 550/BIOL 541.

title: Plant Responses to the Environment
description: Physiological, molecular, and genetic examination of induced plant responses to various biotic and abiotic environmental stresses.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 302.

title: Ultramicroscopy
description: Theoretical and practical aspects of scanning and transmission electron microscopy, digital image acquisition and energy dispersive x-ray spectroscopy. Two lecture and one laboratory hour per week, plus a research project to be arranged.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 302 or MSCI 311.

title: Selected Topics in Biochemistr
description: Selected biochemical topics emphasizing research literature. One lecture hour per credit per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: two semesters of biochemistry.

title: Selected Topics in Plant Biology
description: Selected botanical topics emphasizing research literature. One lecture hour per credit per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: two semesters of botany.

title: Selected Topics in Ecology
description: Selected ecology topics emphasizing research literature. One lecture hour per credit per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: two semesters of ecology.

title: Selected Topics in Genetics and Developmental Biology
description: Selected genetic and developmental biology topics emphasizing research literature. One lecture hour per credit per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Selected Topics in Zoology
description: Selected zoological topics emphasizing research literature. One lecture hour per credit per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Scientific Teaching and Pedagogy
description: This course is designed to increase teaching proficiency on the science behind student learning, scientific teaching, assessments and rubrics, active learning, project-based learning, teaching technology, inclusive teaching and universal design, classroom and course management, and course design. Participants will begin to develop their own set of course content (specific to their area of expertise) and will deliver that course content as the designated instructor of record after completion of this course. Students will also develop a proper syllabus for their designated course and prepare their own teaching philosophy at the end of this course.
subject: BIOL

title: Structure and Function of Nucleic Acids
description: A detailed study of nucleic acids including their structure/chemistry, biosynthesis, processing, and biological functions.
subject: BIOL

title: DNA Transactions and Gene Expression
description: Advanced topics in Mendelian genetics, DNA repair/recombination, and mechanisms of gene expression. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 303 or equivalent and BIOL 711.

title: Advanced Cell Biology
description: Problems of cellular organization, interactions, and control. Cell growth and death, cell-cell recognition and communication, intracellular transport, the structure and assembly of cellular organelles, somatic cell genetics, and evolution of cells. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 541 or equivalent.

title: Biological Chemistry
description: A comprehensive treatment of the chemistry, metabolism, regulation, and function of biological systems.
subject: BIOL

title: Biological Chemistry II
description: A continuation of BIOL 717 . Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 717.

title: Aquatic Bacteriology
description: The ecology and physiology of freshwater and marine bacteria. The functions of bacteria in aquatic habitats and the public health aspects of pollution as they relate to microbiology. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 330 or equivalent.

title: Aquatic Bacteriology Laboratory
description: Three laboratory hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Embryology of Angiosperms
description: Two lectures and two laboratory periods per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Soil-Plant Relationships
description: Two lecture and three laboratory hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 102.

title: Marine Phytoplankton
description: Three lecture hours and one three-hour laboratory per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 627.

title: Advanced Phycology
description: Three lecture hours and one three-hour laboratory per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 627.

title: The Biology of Fish
description: Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: The Biology of Fish
description: One seminar and six laboratory hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Advanced Invertebrate Zoology I
description: Principles of systematics and an in-depth study of invertebrate phylogeny and ecology. Two lecture and three laboratory hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: invertebrate zoology.

title: The Vertebrates
description: Three lectures or conferences per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Advanced Developmental Biology
description: The biochemical and molecular mechanisms by which a variety of organisms develop. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 340 or BIOL 505, or equivalent.

title: Fungal Physiology
description: Three lecture and three laboratory hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Molecular Endocrinology
description: A brief introduction to general endocrinology followed by an in-depth examination of the molecular mechanisms of hormone action, including receptors, second messengers, and hormonal control of transcription/translation. The evolution of hormone-receptor systems will also be examined.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: CHEM 332.

title: Methods in Molecular and Cell Biology
description: Team-taught course on the theory and practice of laboratory techniques for investigating the structure and function of cellular components, especially organelles, proteins, and nucleic acids. Three lecture hours per week. Lectures will be supplemented with laboratory demonstrations.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: one semester of biochemistry.

title: Advanced Biological Oceanography
description: Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 450/MSCI 450.

title: Marine Biogeochemistry
description: Biological, geological, and physical processes that influence the cyling of major bioactive elements (C, O, N, P, S) in marine waters and sediments.
subject: BIOL

title: Developmental Genetics
description: The action of genes in development and differentiation at the molecular, cellular, and organ (tissue) levels, with examples taken from microorganisms, plants, animals, and man. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 350 and two semesters of biochemistry or equivalents.

title: Oceanographic Techniques
description: Shipboard experience with basic techniques used by geological, physical, chemical, and biological oceanographers.
subject: BIOL

title: Quantitative Ecology
description: An intensive field course centered around field problems in a variety of habitats (freshwater, terrestrial, estuarine). Students will use a variety of quantitative sampling methods to test ecological hypotheses on several two-day field trips.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 570.

title: Special Topics in Biology
description: An intensive consideration of topics of current interest in biology. One lecture hour per credit per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Research
description: Appropriate designation will be made for the particular program in any given semester.
subject: BIOL

title: Physiological Ecology
description: Two lecture and three laboratory hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Electron Microscopy
description: Theory and design of modern electron microscopes; advancement in the theory and practice of specimen preparation of biological materials; interpretation of ultrastructure of cells and tissues. Three lecture hours a week.
subject: BIOL

title: Electron Microscopy Laboratory
description: Four laboratory hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Wetlands Ecology
description: A survey of the structure and function of wetland ecosystems emphasizing the current literature.
subject: BIOL

title: Biology of Populations
description: Three lecture and two laboratory hours per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Advanced Plant Physiology
description: Study of modern advances in plant physiology. Plant biotechnology topics, such as tissue culture, nitrogen fixation, photosynthesis, weed and pest control, molecular cloning, and genetic manipulation. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 549.

title: Theoretical Ecology
description: Theoretical bases of ecology are explored from current literature with topics from organismal, populational, community, and ecosystem approaches. Principles for the construction and testing of hypotheses and models.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 570.

title: Evolutionary Biology
description: Theoretical and empirical studies of the evolutionary process. Historical perspective of major developments in evolution as well as modern quantitative and ecological genetic studies.
subject: BIOL

title: Ecological Modeling and Environmental Planning
description: Concepts in systems ecology and ecological modeling. Emphasis on the use of models and computer simulations in examining environmental interactions, predicting environmental impact, and facilitating the process of environmental planning. Lab practice in model development and computer simulation analysis.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: MATH 111 or equivalent, ecology, ENHS 660.

title: Reproductive Ecology
description: Theoretical aspects and examples of the variety of reproductive and life history patterns found in animals and plants as adaptations to various environmental constraints. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: BIOL
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 570.

title: Current Topics in Molecular Biology
description: Recent developments in cellular and molecular biology including genetic mechanisms, ultrastructure, and function of organelles and membranes. Lectures supplemented with readings from current literature. Primarily for the MAT program. Not available for MS or PhD credit in biology.
subject: BIOL

title: Current Topics in Developmental Biology
description: Concepts of growth, differentiation, and morphogenesis of organisms in light of recent advances in biological knowledge. Lectures supplemented with readings from current literature. Primarily for the M.A.T. program. Not available for M.S. or Ph.D. credit in biology.
subject: BIOL

title: Current Topics in Ecology
description: Ecological concepts with reference to recent advances in environmental sciences. Special attention to the ecology of the coast, swamps, and other habitats of importance in the Southeast. Primarily for the M.A.T. program. Not available for M.S. or Ph.D. credit in biology.
subject: BIOL

title: Plants of South Carolina
description: Introduction to the major forms of plant life in the state. Includes fungi, algae, bryophytes, and vascular plants. Lecture-laboratory-field course primarily for the M.A.T. program. Not available for M.S. or Ph.D. credit in biology.
subject: BIOL

title: Animals of South Carolina
description: Introduction to the major forms of animal life in the state. Animals will be studied and/or collected in their native habitat. Includes identification, behavior, and ecology of animals with emphasis on vertebrates. Lecture-laboratory-field course primarily for the M.A.T. program. Not available for M.S. or Ph.D. credit in biology.
subject: BIOL

title: Research in Biology
description: Directed laboratory research and readings in the biological sciences for M.S. and Ph.D. students prior to preparation of theses and dissertations.
subject: BIOL

title: Directed Readings in Molecular, Cellular, and Developmental Biology
description: Assigned readings in special topics in molecular, cellular, and developmental biology followed by classroom discussions. Designed to teach critical analysis of the scientific literature.
subject: BIOL

title: Seminar in Plant Biology
description: A review of current literature in plant biology involving student presentations of seminars. One discussion hour per credit per week. The course may be repeated for credit.
subject: BIOL

title: Seminar in Ecology
description: A review of current literature in ecology involving student presentations of seminars. One discussion hour per credit per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Seminar in Molecular, Cellular, and Developmental Biology
description: Student presentations of papers from the current literature in molecular, cellular, and developmental biology. Designed to give experience in oral presentations. May be repeated.
subject: BIOL

title: Seminar in Zoology
description: A review of current literature in zoology involving student presentations of seminars. One discussion hour per credit per week.
subject: BIOL

title: Perspectives in Biological Research
description: Recent trends in biological research from the perspective of individual faculty members in the department. May be repeated.
subject: BIOL

title: Communication in Biological Research
description: Research in biology requires diverse skills, many of which are not formally taught. Students will develop essential skills in science communication, presentation, writing and peer review. This course is designed for Masters and PhD students.
subject: BIOL

title: Introduction to Biostatistical Modeling
description: Statistical modeling, primarily using applications in public health. Measures of agreement, principles of statistical inference, correlation, simple and multiple linear regression, categorical independent variables, interaction, repeated measures, and logistic regression.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: STAT 205 or equivalent.

title: Independent Study
description: Enrollment and topic to be approved in advance by advisor and instructor. May be repeated.
subject: BIOS

title: Introduction to Biostatistics
description: Health-related statistical applications. Descriptive statistics, probability, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, regression, correlation, ANOVA. May not be used as part of a degree program in epidemiology or biostatistics. Three lecture hours and one laboratory hour per week.
subject: BIOS

title: Concepts and Methods of Biostatistics
description: Descriptive and inferential statistical applications to public health. Probability, interval estimation, hypothesis testing, measures of association. Three lecture hours and one laboratory hour per week. Intended for those who will be involved in research applications of biostatistics.
subject: BIOS

title: Basic Software for Public Health
description: Working with public health data using statistical software. Effective ways to store, clean, merge, and format public health data for analysis.
subject: BIOS

title: Effective Data Management for Public Health
description: Statistical data management techniques. Microcomputer applications, communication between microcomputers and mainframe, tape and disk storage, access of large health-related databases.
subject: BIOS

title: Introduction to R Programming
description: Students will learn the software program R for performing data management. The course covers basic to advanced commands for properly formatting output, merging data, working with functions, graphing, using programming loops for preparing data for analysis for public health data.
subject: BIOS

title: Introduction to Stata Software
description: Students will learn the software program Stata for performing data management. The course covers basic to advanced commands for properly formatting output, merging data, working with functions, graphing, using programming loops for preparing data for analysis for public health data.
subject: BIOS

title: Introduction to MS Access for Public Health
description: This course focuses the uses of Microsoft Access for data management in public health. The course takes the student through building tables, forms, queries, reports and finishes with automated scripts for each of use with Access.
subject: BIOS

title: Advanced SAS Methods for Public Health
description: This course focuses on advanced programming for managing and analyzing data using SAS. Building upon skills learned in BIOS 709 (Introduction to SAS), students will learn data management using PROC SQL . Students will also become familiar with the SAS Macro Language which prepares data for conducting efficient statistical analysis.
subject: BIOS

title: Advanced Data Management in Public Health
description: This course teaches advanced techniques for creating and using large data sets. Students will learn advanced techniques for working with data, cleaning data, and preparing it for analysis using three software packages (SAS, STATA, MS Access) for data entry, data management, and presentation. For statistical analysis, students will learn advanced SAS, the primary statistical package used for data analysis in other biostatistics and epidemiology courses.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOS 710.

title: Machine Learning for Public Health Applications
description: This course introduces the fundamental concepts and applications of machine learning (ML) in the context of public health, biostatistics, and related fields (e.g., epidemiology, psychology, neuroscience, genetics). Students will learn how to prepare data, select and implement appropriate machine learning methods, and evaluate model performance—all using R. Emphasis will be placed on conceptual understanding, practical implementation, and interpretation of results rather than on mathematical derivations. By the end of the course, students will be prepared to incorporate ML techniques into their own research projects, with a special focus on the unique challenges and ethical considerations inherent in analyzing public health data.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in BIOS 757and BIOS 755 or equivalent.

title: Seminar in Biostatistics
description: Analysis of current and prospective issues in biostatistics, including historical foundations. Includes student exploration of unsolved problems and examination of central issues in biostatistics.
subject: BIOS

title: Introduction to Complex Survey Data Analysis
description: Students will learn the basics of data collection methods, sampling design for linear, logistic, and survival analysis complex models using survey data. Students will also learn about weight adjustments, imputation methods with an emphasis on both applied models and the theory behind them.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in STAT 512 and STAT 513 or equivalent.

title: Community Health Studies
description: Process, skills, and management of undertaking health studies in the human community.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOS 700, EPID 700.

title: Discrete Data Analysis
description: Analysis of discrete data in public health studies. Relative risk, odds ratio, rates and proportions, contingency tables, logistic regression, introduction to other advanced topics. Not for biostatistics majors.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 701, BIOS 710, BIOS 757.

title: Introduction to Longitudinal Data Analysis
description: Introduction to principles and methods for longitudinal multi-level modeling. Focus on data analysis and interpretation.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOS 757.

title: Intermediate Biostatistics
description: Public health applications of correlation, regression, multiple regression, single and multi-factor analysis of variance and analysis of covariance.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: a course in introductory statistics.

title: Advanced Linear Models in Biostatistics
description: Public health applications of correlation, regression, multiple regression, single and multi-factor analysis of variance and analysis of covariance. Additional topics in analysis of health data including regression diagnostics, multi-collinearity of observational data, ridge/nonlinear regression, principal components, random/mixed effects, unbalanced designs, repeated measures and sampling and design effects.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOS 701.

title: Theory and Methods of Discrete Data Analysis
description: The concepts, principles, and biostatistical techniques necessary to analyze categorical epidemiological data including dose response curves, life tables, and discrete measures of association. Estimation of parameters for logistic and other commonly used epidemiological models.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 701, BIOS 757.

title: Biostatistical Methods in Clinical Trials
description: The basic and advanced statistical techniques necessary for the design, conduct, analysis, and interpretation of results of clinical trials.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 741, BIOS 757.

title: Survival Analysis
description: Methods for the analysis of survival data in the biomedical setting. Underlying concepts; standard parametric and nonparametric methods for one or several samples; concomitant variables and the proportional hazards model.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOS 757 or BIOS 758.

title: Biostatistical Modeling of Genomic Data
description: This course is an introduction to important topics and key concepts in statistical genetics, with emphasis on statistics methods and their applications to human complex diseases. The course will cover major concepts and classical statistical methods for the analysis of family and population based human genetic data.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOS 757 or equivalent.

title: Research Design in the Biomedical Sciences
description: Fundamentals of constructing, analyzing, and interpreting biomedical studies; internal and external validity, sample size determination, completely random designs, blocking crossover designs, factorial designs, confounding, nested designs, repeated measure designs.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 741, BIOS 757.

title: Spatial Statistics for Public Health
description: Spatial statistics methods commonly used in public health including mapping, tests for spatial correlation, hypothesis tests for spatial clustering, and regression models with spatial random effects.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in BIOS 700 or BIOS 701.

title: Applied Longitudinal Data Analysis
description: Modern methods for the analysis of repeated measures, correlated outcomes, and longitudinal data, including repeated measures ANOVA, generalized linear models, random effects, and generalized estimating equations.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOS 757 or BIOS 758 or STAT 701 or STAT 705.

title: Artificial intelligence for Biomedical Data
description: Introduces deep learning architectures for analyzing large-scale biomedical data including genomics, electronic health records, and medical imaging, emphasizing model interpretability, ethical considerations, multi-modal data integration with real-world example applications, and implementation using Python.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in BIOS 757.

title: Biostatistical Aspects of Bioinformatics
description: Bioinformatics analyses related to public health and biomedical research. Gene-gene and gene-environment interaction, phylogeny analysis in disease classification, and clustering for expression data. Data analyses, simulation studies, algorithms, and interpretation of health data.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOS 757.

title: Introduction to Quantile Regression
description: Principles and methods of quantile regression, a robust and distribution-free statistical approach that extends the classical mean regression to the analysis of complex treatment effects.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOS 757.

title: Independent Study
description: Directed research on a topic to be developed by M.P.H. or M.S.P.H. student and instructor. May be repeated.
subject: BIOS

title: Selected Topics in Biostatistics
description: Content varies by title. Course may be repeated for a total of 6 credit hours.
subject: BIOS

title: Survival Analysis II
description: Parametric survival analysis, accelerated failure time model, frailty model, competing risk mode and multi-state model. Techniques motivated by applications in epidemiology and clinical medicine research, applications demonstrated using public health data sets.
subject: BIOS

title: Generalized Linear Models
description: Statistical theory and applications extending regression and analysis of variance to non-normal data. An integrated treatment encompassing logistic and other binary regressions, log-linear models, and gamma regression models.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: STAT 713 or STAT 513 and STAT 705 or BIOS 757.

title: Advanced R Programming in Public Health
description: R is a free and open source software environment for statistical computing and graphics. This course provides the principles and techniques to efficiently design, implement, and execute simulation and data analysis routines in quantitative fields like biostatistics, statistics, engineering, finance, and data science.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOS 711.

title: Advanced Computational Statistics for Signal and Network Analysis
description: An overview of advanced computational statistics for signal and network analysis with a wide variety of social, genomic and neuroscientific applications. All course modules include a hands-on component.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in BIOS 701, BIOS 757, BIOS 711, STAT 512 and STAT 513.

title: Bayesian Biostatistics and Computation
description: Bayesian statistical methods including hierarchical modeling and the use of the Markov Chane Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: STAT 705 or BIOS 757.

title: Statistical Methods in Spatial Epidemiology
description: A comprehensive introduction to the statistical methods used in the analysis of geo-referenced spatial health data. Topics range from disease mapping to prospective surveillance.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOS 757 and BIOS 759.

title: Multivariate Biostatistics
description: Analysis of multivariate data as found in biomedical studies: multivariate linear models, principal component analysis, factor analysis, discriminant and cluster analysis. Other special multivariate topics such as principal component regression.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: STAT 516 or BIOS 757.

title: Biostatistical Machine Learning for Public Health
description: The focus of this course will be on using biostatistical models to predict and provide information on complex public health datasets. We will focus more on prediction of outcome(s) than estimation of the impact of a risk factor. However, some interferential methods for risk factors will be reviewed and (for all methods) techniques to measure variable importance will be discussed. Further, unsupervised learning methods (e.g., clustering) will be discussed. Prediction and predictive inference will be main themes in the course along with learning how to implement the methods in R software. See the syllabus course schedule for a full list of topics.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in BIOS 770 or equivalent.

title: Doctoral Seminar
description: May be repeated for credit.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: complete at least one semester of course work and consent of instructor.

title: Binary Dose Response Theory and Methods
description: Threshold, mass action and target theory; empirical dose response functions; methods in current use among health science researchers.
subject: BIOS
prereq: Prerequisites: STAT 512.

title: Independent Study
description: Directed research on a topic to be developed by doctoral student and instructor. May be repeated.
subject: BIOS

title: Selected Topics in Biostatistics
description: Discussion on current and emerging issues in biostatistics.
subject: BIOS

title: Doctor of Public Health Practicum
description: Students are required to conduct applied public health methods and strategies as a part of their practicum experience. In particular, the student should successfully implement and interpret the results of biostatistical methods in the organization.
subject: BIOS

title: Dissertation Preparation
description: Prerequisite: one full year (18 hours) of graduate study beyond the master’s level.
subject: BIOS

title: Introduction to Biomedical Engineering
description: Introduction to topics comprising the field of Biomedical Engineering, including their ethical impacts. Familiarization with resources and basic skills necessary to succeed in this major and field.
subject: BMEN

title: Fundamentals of Biomedical Systems
description: Fundamentals of static equilibrium, free body diagrams, force and momentum balances; introduction to linear circuit analysis.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in MATH 115 or higher or placement through the Math Assessment of Prerequisites (MAP).

title: Cellular and Molecular Biology with Engineering Applications
description: Introduction to molecular, cellular, and physical biology principles and concepts and application of engineering principles to further the understanding of biological systems. Protein and nucleic acid structure and function; DNA replication, mutations, and repair; transcription, translation, and post-translational processing; cellular organization; molecular transport and trafficking; and cellular models.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 101.

title: Introduction to Biomechanics
description: Mathematical and theoretical analysis of the mechanical properties and functions of materials, including those of biological origin and clinical relevance. Stress, strain, mechanical properties of materials, axial loading, torsion, bending, and stress/strain transformations. Application of the categories and methodology of solid and fluid mechanics to study biological tissues and events.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BMEN 212 and PHYS 211.

title: Materials in Medicine
description: Molecular properties of biomaterials including natural, polymeric, ceramic, metallic, and composite biomaterials; mechanical properties, surface modification, degradation, protein adsorption, blood, cell and tissue interaction of biomaterials; host response to implanted biomaterials; rational design of biomaterials.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 101.

title: Introduction to Biomaterials
description: Properties of metals, ceramics, polymers, natural materials and composites; methods to modify surface and bulk properties of biomaterials; mechanisms of degradation in physiological environments; cell- and tissue-biomaterial interactions; host response to implanted biomaterials; blood-biomaterial interactions; rational design of biomaterials for specific biomedical applications.
subject: BMEN
