prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in CHEM 333; C or better in BMEN 290 or ECHE 311.

title: Thermodynamics of Biomolecular Systems
description: First, second, and third law of thermodynamics; free energy and chemical equilibrium in biological processes; phase equilibrium for biomedical systems; energy and metabolism; membrane potentials and depolarization.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BMEN 240 and PHYS 211.

title: Professional Development and Ethics in Biomedical Engineering
description: Analysis and discussion of biomedical industries, standards, regulations, products, and patents. Ethical issues associated with research, introduction of new products, animal subjects, and human subjects.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in BMEN 101 or ENCP 101.

title: Professional Development and Ethics in Biomedical Engineering
description: Analysis and discussion of industries, products, patents, industrial inventiveness, and biomedical research. Ethical issues associated with research, introduction of new products, animal subjects, and human subjects.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: BMEN 101.

title: Biomonitoring and Electrophysiology
description: Basic electric circuits and equivalent cell model circuits used in biomonitoring and electrophysiology. Ohm’s and Kirchoff’s Laws. Applications of electrical components, such as operations amplifiers, filter, and Wheastone bridge, in biomonitoring and electrophysiology. Origins of bioelectricity. Biopotential and electrochemistry including Nernst and Goldman-Hudgkin-Katz equations for describing membrane potential of nerve and muscle cells. Ion transport involved in maintaining cell pH, action potential, muscle contraction, sensory perception.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BMEN 212, C or better in BMEN 240, C or better in MATH 242.

title: Biochemistry with Engineering Applications
description: Introduction to principles of organic chemistry and biochemistry as applied to biomedical engineering. Molecular, cellular, and physical biology principles and concepts with application of engineering principles. Introduction to biomanufacturing, bioinformatics, and advanced mathematical cellular models.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BMEN 240.

title: Infectious Disease Immunology for Biomedical Engineers
description: Qualitative and quantitative aspects of infectious diseases; principles of diagnosis and control. Elements of human immunological response and immune disorders; influence on biomedical engineering of explants and implants.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 101.

title: Human Anatomy and Physiology for Biomedical Engineers
description: Foundations for biomedical engineering with a focus on human anatomy and physiology. Introduction to the inter-relationships between tissue/organ structure and function; demonstration of how an engineering approach can promote understanding of these relationships. Recent biomedical engineering advances and their relations to underlying anatomy and physiology.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BMEN 240.

title: Medical Microbiology for Biomedical Engineers
description: Qualitative and quantitative aspects of human system based medical microbiology; principles of diagnosis and control of representative human diseases. Elements of human immunological response and immune disorders.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 101.

title: Biotransport
description: Basics of convective and diffusive transport applied to biological and biomedical systems. The effect of fluid flow and mass transport upon biochemical interactions. Scaling and design of biotransport systems.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in BMEN 263 and C or better in MATH 242.

title: Biomedical Analysis
description: Introduction to analytical methods as used in biomedicine. Applications of bioinstrumentation in disease diagnosis.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in BMEN 345 and BMEN 240.

title: Biomedical Instrumentation
description: Sensing and measurement of biophysical and biochemical properties and signals in the human body for quantitative molecular, cell, and tissue analysis. Overview on the theory, design and application of common biomedical instrumentation used for diagnosis, treatment, and scientific study of physiological parameters in clinical medicine and biomedical research.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in BMEN 321 or BMEN 360.

title: Biomedical Engineering Laboratory I
description: Introduction to laboratory techniques and tools used for physiological measurements in biomedical engineering, with focus on biological, physical, and biomaterial methods. Data processing and analysis, as well as effective communication of results in written and oral form.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in BMEN 263, D or better in STAT 509.

title: Biomedical Engineering Laboratory II
description: Introduction to laboratory techniques and tools used for physiological measurements in biomedical engineering, with focus on measurement of biosignals and common analytical methods employed in biomedical research and clinical settings. Data processing and analysis, as well as effective communication of results in written and oral form.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in BMEN 321 and BMEN 381.

title: Special Topics in Biomedical Engineering for Undergraduates
description: Course content varies and will be announced in the schedule of classes by title. May be repeated as topic varies.
subject: BMEN

title: Kinetics in Biomolecular Systems
description: Kinetic theory applied to biomedical systems, including enzymatic reactions, cell growth, and kinetic models of biological systems.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in BMEN 340, CHEM 550, or BIOL 541; C or better in BMEN 290, ECHE 310, EMCH 290, or ENCP 290; C or better in MATH 242.

title: Fundamentals of Biochemical Engineering
description: Biological systems are used in chemical industries for a wide variety of applications, including the formation of important products (e.g. pharmaceuticals), sensor technology, degradation, and waste water treatment. This class will provide an overview of materials needed to investigate and model biosystems.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: CHEM 333.

title: Modeling and Simulation of Biomedical Systems
description: Introduction to modern computational modeling tools used in biomedical engineering. Analysis, visualization and image processing using engineering software as applied to problems of interest in biomedical engineering.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in BMEN 271 and BMEN 354.

title: Senior Biomedical Engineering Design I
description: Integrated team work/project management, voice of the patient , design specifications, design functions, design concepts, economic factors, concept selection and product architecture. The initial feasibility study, selection of the final design approach, and preliminary specifications are required by the end of the semester.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in BMEN 381 or BMEN 382.

title: Senior Biomedical Engineering Design II
description: Design for manufacturability, ergonomic and aesthetic considerations, prototype construction and testing, fabrication and biological testing of tissue engineered constructs, statistical methods/design of experiments, ethics/product liability and social/environmental impact. The final engineering design (specifications, drawings, bill of materials, including assessment of economics) will be completed by the end of the semester. Both written and oral reports are to be provided.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in BMEN 427.

title: Independent Research
description: Individual investigation or studies of special topics. A maximum of 3 credits may be applied to Biomedical Engineering program requirements. Contract approval by instructor, department, and college is required.
subject: BMEN

title: Micro/nanofluidics and Lab-on-a-Chip
description: Basic fluid mechanics, capillary, drop and micro/nanoparticle, electrokinetics; micropump, mixer, preconcentrator, electrophoresis, microactuator and particle manipulator; sensors for pressure, velocity, concentration, temperature in environmental monitoring/biodefence, clinical diagnostics, drug discovery/delivery.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in CHEM 112 and CHEM112L or CHEM 142.

title: Delivery of Bioactive Agents
description: Routes of administration; mechanisms of drug absorption and biological barriers; pharmacokinetic modeling of drug distribution; drug excretion and biotransformation; design and evaluation of controlled release systems, targeted release systems, and responsive release systems.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in BMEN 345.

title: Immunoengineering
description: Engineering approaches to study and control immune reactions and their applications in therapy and diagnostics for infectious disease, cancer, allergy, autoimmunity, and transplantation.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BMEN 240.

title: Cardiovascular System: From Development to Disease
subject: BMEN

title: Advanced Biomechanics
description: Mathematical and theoretical analysis of the mechanical properties and functions of soft biological tissues to include arterial vessels.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in BMEN 263.

title: Tissue Engineering
description: Molecular basis of bioregenerative engineering; biomaterial design; biocompatibility assessment; cell isolation and characterization; rapid prototyping, scaffold fabrication, and biofabrication; protein and gene delivery; bioreactor design; transport in biological tissues; applications of tissue engineering in regenerative medicine.
subject: BMEN

title: Engineering of Soft Materials
description: Introductory overview of fundamental concepts in science and engineering of soft materials; the relation between microstructure and macroscopic behavior in a variety of soft matter systems; key applications in chemical and biomedical engineering.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in MATH 122 or MATH 141.

title: Special Topics in Biomedical Engineering
description: Course content varies and will be announced in the schedule of classes by title. May be repeated as topic varies.
subject: BMEN

title: Modeling and Simulation of Biomedical System
description: Analytical and quantitative techniques applied to engineering problems in biomedical transport, tissue mechanics, celluar and organ physiology, and control of medical devices.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: MATH 242.

title: Human Cell and Molecular Biology for Biomedical Engineers
description: Advanced examination of the organization and function of the cell with emphasis on the biophysical and quantitative aspects of cellular function. Emphasis will be on the biomedical engineering applications of regulation of cell division, protein transcription and translation within the cell, cellular energetics, and intracellular networks for cell signaling and cell function.
subject: BMEN

title: Transport Phenomena in Biomedical Systems
description: Conservation of momentum, energy, mass, physico-chemical properties of biofluids, blood rheology, circulation models and cardiovascular regulation, solute and oxygen transport in tissues, gas transport in lungs and respiratory gas exchange models, kinetics and compartmental modeling, modeling of artificial organs.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in BMEN 354.

title: Anatomy and Physiology for Biomedical Engineers
description: An examination of human biological structure and function from an engineering perspective. Engineering principles will be used to analyze anatomical structures and physiological functions at the tissue, organ, and systems levels.
subject: BMEN
prereq: Prerequisites: EXSC 224 or BIOL 244.

title: Biomedical Engineering Literature
description: Critical reading and literacy in the biomedical engineering discipline as it relates to students’ research. Graduate Standing in the Biomedical Engineering Program.
subject: BMEN

title: Biomedical Engineering Doctoral Research
description: Individual research to be arranged with the instructor. Graduate Standing in the Biomedical Engineering Program.
subject: BMEN

title: Graduate Seminar in Biomedical Engineering
description: Graduate seminar on current topics in biomedical engineering. Instruction on critical analysis and communication in the discipline.
subject: BMEN

title: Biomedical Engineering Masters Thesis Preparation
description: Thesis preparation for Masters Program. Graduate Standing in the Biomedical Engineering Program. Grading: Pass/Fail.
subject: BMEN

title: Doctoral Seminar in Biomedical Engineering
description: Seminar for doctoral students on current biomedical engineering topics and instruction in professional preparation in the discipline. Graduate Standing in the Biomedical Engineering Program.
subject: BMEN

title: Biomedical Engineering Doctoral Dissertation Preparation
description: Dissertation preparation for the Ph.D. Program. Graduate standing in the Biomedical Engineering program.
subject: BMEN

title: Introduction to Biomedical Research
description: Training and skill development necessary for biomedical research.
subject: BMSC

title: Biomedical Science Interdisciplinary Laboratory II
description: Intensive tutorial for advanced biomedical science graduate students in laboratory techniques and/or methodology outside of department of specialization.
subject: BMSC
prereq: Prerequisites: BMSC 700.

title: Medical Cell Biology I
description: The structure and assembly of eucaryotic cells, mechanisms of gene expression, and the cell biology of the immune system. Methods in cell biology are also discussed.
subject: BMSC
prereq: Prerequisites: basic biochemistry.

title: Communication Skills for Pre-Health Professions: Basics and Practice
description: This course is designed to improve the written and oral communication skills of students interested in pursuing careers in health related professions.
subject: BMSC

title: Experimental Design, Data Management, and Analysis for Biomedical Research
description: This course provides graduate students in biomedical sciences with applied training in experimental design, data management, and analysis within mentored research environments. Emphasizing rigor, reproducibility, and interpretability, it teaches students to design structured experiments, manage data transparently, and analyze results through realistic, literature-based, and practice-aligned research scenarios.
subject: BMSC

title: Medical Cell Biology II
description: A continuation of BMSC 702 , this course will cover cell-cell communication, cell motility, extracellular matrices, stem cells, cellular embryology, and the interactions of viruses with cells. Relevant methods will also be presented.
subject: BMSC
prereq: Prerequisites: BMSC 702.

title: Responsible Conduct of Biomedical Research
description: Discussion of issues surrounding the responsible conduct of biomedical research. Two hours of lecture and small-group discussion per week.
subject: BMSC

title: Biochemistry for the Biomedical Sciences
description: This course will cover the structure and function of proteins, lipids and carbohydrates. Special emphasis will be on the mechanisms of enzyme catalysis and the influence of the cellular environment on catalysis. The role of specific enzymes such as kinases in signal transduction and metabolic control will be emphasized.
subject: BMSC

title: Human Cell and Molecular Biology for Biomedical Sciences
description: Examination of the organization and function of the cell with emphasis on the biophysical and quantitative aspects of cellular function. Emphasis will be on the regulation of cell division, protein transcription and translation within the cell, cellular energetics, and intracellular networks.
subject: BMSC

title: Medical Molecular Biology
description: Theory and practice of molecular biology as applied to medical research. Nucleic acid structure and function, methodology, genome organization and DNA dynamics.
subject: BMSC
prereq: Prerequisites: BMSC 754, BMSC 755.

title: Signal Transduction
description: Transmembrane signaling processes and regulatory mechanisms involved in neurotransmission and drug action. Emphasis on membrane receptors linked to ion channels, guanine nucleotide binding proteins, tyrosine kinase activity and intracellular receptors.
subject: BMSC
prereq: Prerequisites: BMSC 755.

title: Cardiovascular Science
description: Anatomy, pathology, pharmacology and physiology of the cardiovascular system taught from a research-oriented perspective.
subject: BMSC

title: Human Anatomy for Health Sciences
description: Instruction in normal human anatomy and embryology, as well as pathological variations during disease states, to include advanced anatomical imaging techniques such as radiography and ultrasonography.
subject: BMSC

title: Pathophysiology for Health Sciences
description: Pathophysiologic mechanisms that underlie common diseases related to different organ systems.
subject: BMSC

title: Summative Experience for Physician Assistants
description: Assessing the Physician Assistant student's readiness for clinical practice. The course will consist of a series of summative evaluations and board preparation.
subject: BMSC

title: Clinical Immersion for PAs
description: This course is designed to prepare students for the transition from the didactic to clinical phase of their education by refining the student's clinical thought process and critical thinking skills.
subject: BMSC

title: Interprofessional Seminar for Health Professionals
description: Formal and informal codes of interprofessional conduct of the health science disciplines. Group discussion of implications for interprofessional research, clinical practice, and administration.
subject: BMSC

title: Medical Interviewing
description: Instruction in medical interviewing, medical documentation, patient assessment, and patient management.
subject: BMSC

title: Physical Diagnosis
description: Instruction in developing the techniques necessary to begin the evaluation of patients by ascertaining symptoms and evaluating physical signs of disease.
subject: BMSC

title: Diagnostic Testing
description: Analysis, utilization and interpretation of diagnostic testing modalities in medicine.
subject: BMSC

title: Surgery and Emergency Medicine
description: Specialized medical topics in emergency and surgical medicine.
subject: BMSC

title: Clinical Medicine-Across the Lifespan
description: Instruction in clinical skills and knowledge required in primary care practices, including patients of all ages.
subject: BMSC

title: Population Health and Health Policy
description: Instruction in patient-centered health care delivery systems to include patient safety, patient education, and preventive medicine with a focus on population health.
subject: BMSC

title: Behavioral Health
description: Instruction in clinical skills and knowledge to understand the presentation and mechanisms of mental health problems, emphasizing a biopsychosocial model.
subject: BMSC

title: Medical Law and Ethics
description: Ethical issues and legal implications in patient-centered health care delivery systems.
subject: BMSC

title: Physician Assistant Professional Practice
description: Instruction related to PA professional issues.
subject: BMSC

title: Biomedical Biochemistry I
description: First of a two-semester sequence covering the major areas of biochemistry in a biomedical context. Chemistry of amino acids and proteins, enzymology, metabolism of carbohydrates and lipids. Emphasis is on biomedical research applications. Four lecture hours per week.
subject: BMSC

title: Medical Genetics and Laboratory Diagnostics
description: Fundamental genetics, the role of human genetic factors in clinical medicine, and interpretation of laboratory data.
subject: BMSC

title: Advancing Medical Practice through Research
description: Offered annually in the fall semester of the didactic year, this course is designed to cover concepts and skills related to the advancing medical practice through research of scientific research and medical literature that facilitate current professional practice and practice improvement. Topics include the principles of evidence-based practice (EBM), study design, methodology, and statistical analysis with application to practice-based decisions and quality improvement in patient care.
subject: BMSC
prereq: Prerequisites: Successful completion of first 2 PA didactic semesters.

title: Internal Medicine
description: Supervised clinical practicum for physician assistant students in Internal Medicine.
subject: BMSC

title: Women's Health
description: Supervised clinical practicum for physician assistant students in OB/GYN.
subject: BMSC

title: Pediatrics
description: Supervised clinical practicum for physician assistant students in Pediatrics.
subject: BMSC

title: Behavioral Medicine
description: Supervised clinical practicum for physician assistant students in Behavioral Medicine.
subject: BMSC

title: General Surgery
description: Supervised clinical practicum for physician assistant students in General Surgery.
subject: BMSC

title: Emergency Medicine
description: Supervised clinical practicum for physician assistant students in Emergency Medicine.
subject: BMSC

title: Orthopedics
description: Supervised clinical practicum for physician assistant students in Orthopedics.
subject: BMSC

title: Practicum Elective
description: Supervised clinical practicum for physician assistant students in area of student's choice with approval of the Director of Clinical Education. Elective can be in a specialty or a core area.
subject: BMSC

title: PA Clinical Medicine and Therapeutics I
description: Examines diseases related to different organ systems including review of pathophysiological basis of disease, resulting clinical signs/symptoms and overview of treatment strategies.
subject: BMSC
prereq: Prerequisites: BMSC 740 and PHPH 701.

title: PA Clinical Medicine and Therapeutics II
description: Examines diseases related to different organ systems including review of pathophysiological basis of disease, resulting clinical signs/symptoms and an overview of treatment strategies.
subject: BMSC
prereq: Prerequisites: BMSC 766.

title: PA Clinical Medicine and Therapeutics III
description: Examines diseases related to different organ systems including a review of pathophysiological basis of disease, resulting clinical signs/symptoms and an overview of treatment strategies.
subject: BMSC
prereq: Prerequisites: BMSC 767.

title: PA Clinical Medicine and Therapeutics IV
description: Examines disease related to different organ systems including review of pathophysiological basis of disease, resulting clinical signs/symptoms and overview of treatment strategies.
subject: BMSC
prereq: Prerequisites: BMSC 766, BMSC 767, BMSC 768.

title: Clinical Skills Lab
description: Performing clinical procedures and using technology such as ultrasonography in clinical practice.
subject: BMSC

title: Biomedical Research
description: Research project conducted with mentor focused on advancing medical practice.
subject: BMSC

title: Family Medicine PA Program Practicum
description: First of two supervised clinical practicums for physician assistant students in Family and Preventive Medcine.
subject: BMSC
prereq: Prerequisites: Successful completion of the didactic PA program coursework during the first year.

title: Family Medicine PA Program Practicum 2
description: 2nd of two four-week supervised clinical rotations for physician assistant students in Family and Preventive Medicine.
subject: BMSC
prereq: Prerequisites: BMSC 781.

title: Physician Assistant Capstone Portfolio
description: Portfolio project conducted with faculty mentor focused on community service, continuing medical education, evidence-based medicine, research, and advancing medical practice.
subject: BMSC

title: Seminar in Biomedical Science
description: Professional development and scientific update by attending Biomedical Science Seminar Series and meeting with speakers in section one for one credit. Section two for two credits also includes student presentations of literature review topics and current research. May be repeated for credit.
subject: BMSC

title: Molecular Foundations of Medicine
description: An eight-hour, spring semester course designed for first-year medical students incorporating biochemistry, molecular biology and genetics. With a focus on connecting fundamental biological processes to human pathologies, the course explores properties and functions of nucleic acids, mechanisms of inheritance, structure and function of proteins and enzymes, and cellular metabolism and bioenergetics. Examples of pathological changes at the molecular level will be used to illustrate how normal processes can be altered to induce a disease state. While the primary mode of instruction is lecture-based, incorporation of clinical correlation presentations and pathology perspectives will solidify the connections between basic processes and human diseases. Student mastery of course content will be assessed using department developed multiple choice exams and the National Board of Medical Examiners (NBME) biochemistry exam.
subject: BMSC

title: Fundamental Chemistry I
description: A science elective surveying inorganic and solution chemistry. First of a terminal two-semester sequence. Three lecture, one recitation, and two laboratory hours per week.
subject: CHEM

title: Fundamental Chemistry II
description: An introductory survey of organic and biochemistry. Three lecture, one recitation, and two laboratory hours per week.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: 1 year high-school chemistry, CHEM 101, CHEM 111, or equivalent.

title: Chemistry and Modern Society I
description: A conceptual and qualitative approach to chemistry, its evolution, achievements, and goals and its impact on technology, the environment, and modern life and thought. (Specifically designed for non-science majors.) Three lecture and three laboratory hours per week.
subject: CHEM

title: Chemistry and Modern Society II
description: Introduction to general, organic, and analytical chemistry principles; chemical characteristics of organic compounds related to synthetic and natural drugs; application of analytical techniques for drug identification; includes readings, lectures, laboratory experiments, and demonstrations.
subject: CHEM

title: Introduction to Forensic Science
description: Surveys chemical aspects of criminal investigation and adjudication including drug, arson, DNA, paint, and fiber identification.
subject: CHEM

title: Introduction to Forensic Science Laboratory
description: An analytical approach to surveys of criminal investigation and adjudication of crime scene analysis and collection and preservation of evidence. Analysis will include but not be limited to drug, arson, DNA, fingerprints, blood splatter, and fiber identification. Note: Three laboratory hours per week.
subject: CHEM

title: General Chemistry I
description: Survey of the principles that underlie all chemistry with applications illustrating these principles. Three lecture and one recitation hours per week.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in MATH 111 or higher (or by placement score into MATH 115 or higher).

title: General Chemistry I Lab
description: Introduction to the principles and techniques of experimental chemistry with emphasis on formula investigations, equations, elementary statistics, and chemical reactivity.
subject: CHEM

title: General Chemistry II
description: Continuation of CHEM 111 . Special emphasis on chemical equilibrium. Three lecture and one recitation hours per week.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in MATH 111 or higher MATH (or by placement score into MATH 115 or higher).

title: General Chemistry II Lab
description: Continuation of CHEM 111L with emphasis on solution properties, kinetics, equilibrium, acids and bases, and qualitative analysis.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 111L or CHEM 141L.

title: Computational Chemistry I
description: Introduction to the use of computers in solving chemical problems. One discussion and two laboratory hours per week.
subject: CHEM

title: Principles of Chemistry I
description: Advanced general chemistry I. Atoms and chemical bonds. Three lecture hours, one recitation hour per week. Credit cannot be received for both CHEM 111 and CHEM 141.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in MATH 111 or placement into MATH 115 or higher MATH.

title: Principles of Chemistry I Laboratory
description: This laboratory-based course supports and extends key topics in principles of general chemistry, with a focus on the experimental investigation of atomic structure, chemical bonding, stoichiometry, chemical reactions, thermodynamics, gas laws, and intermolecular forces.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in MATH 111 or placement into MATH 115 or higher MATH.

title: Principles of Chemistry II
description: Advanced general chemistry II. Chemical kinetics, equilibria, and thermodynamics. Three lecture hours, one recitation hour per week. Note: Credit cannot be received for both CHEM 112 and CHEM 142.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in MATH 115 (or by placement score into MATH 141 or higher); C or higher in CHEM 141 or CHEM 112.

title: Principles of Chemistry II Laboratory
description: Advanced general chemistry II laboratory. Chemical kinetics, equilibria, and thermodynamics. Three laboratory hours per week. Note: Credit cannot be received for both CHEM 112L and CHEM 142L.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in MATH 115 (or by placement score into MATH 141 or higher); C or higher in CHEM 141 or CHEM 112.

title: Undergraduate Research Initiative: Foundations of Scientific Research
description: Fundamental laboratory and professional research training emphasizing lab safety, solution preparation, data collection, and operation of UV-Vis, fluorescence, and FT-IR spectroscopic instrumentation. Includes development of scientific communication skills, understanding of research ethics, literature analysis, and career preparation.
subject: CHEM

title: Undergraduate Research Initiative: From Data to Discovery
description: Advanced laboratory methods in cyclic voltammetry, nanoparticle synthesis, and computational chemistry. Emphasis on research communication, graduate school preparation, and professional engagement through faculty presentations and the Discover USC event.
subject: CHEM

title: Computational Chemistry II
description: A continuation of CHEM 118 , with applications to more advanced chemical problems. One discussion and two laboratory hours per week.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CSCE 145 or CSCE 106 and in CHEM 118.

title: Quantitative Analysis
description: Gravimetric, volumetric, and introductory instrumental analysis. Three lecture and one recitation hours per week.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 112 or CHEM 142; C or higher in MATH 111 (or by placement score in MATH 115 or higher).

title: Quantitative Analysis Laboratory
description: Laboratory methods supporting Gravimetric, volumetric, and introductory instrumental analysis. Credit cannot be received for both CHEM 321L and CHEM 322L .
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 112L or CHEM 142L.

title: Analytical Chemistry
description: Qualitative analysis, quantitative analysis, fundamental or method analysis, and molecular characterization.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 112 or CHEM 142; C or higher in MATH 111 or higher (or placement in MATH 115 or higher).

title: Analytical Chemistry Laboratory
description: Laboratory skill building in analytical techniques. Applications of stoichiometry, spectroscopy, phase transfer, electrochemistry and kinetics. Credit cannot be received for both CHEM 321L and CHEM 322L.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 112L or CHEM 142L.

title: Essentials of Organic Chemistry Laboratory I
description: Laboratory safety, syntheses, separation, and purification of carbon compounds. For non-majors.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CHEM 112L or CHEM 142L.

title: Essentials of Organic Chemistry Laboratory II
description: Continuation of CHEM 331L . Spectroscopic identification of carbon compounds. For non-majors. Three lab hours per week.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 331L.

title: Organic Chemistry I
description: Contemporary theories, nomenclature, reactions, mechanisms, and syntheses of carbon compounds. Three lecture and one recitation hours per week.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 112 or in CHEM 142.

title: Comprehensive Organic Chemistry Laboratory I
description: Laboratory safety, synthesis, separation, and purification of carbon compounds. Required for chemistry majors. Six laboratory hours per week.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CHEM 112L or CHEM 142L.

title: Organic Chemistry II
description: Continuation of CHEM 333 . Three lecture and one recitation hours per week.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 333.

title: Comprehensive Organic Chemistry Laboratory II
description: Continuation of CHEM 333L . Spectroscopic identification of carbon compounds. Required for chemistry majors. Six laboratory hours per week.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 333L.

title: Elementary Biophysical Chemistry
description: A non-calculus approach to the study of the principles of physical chemistry emphasizing their application to significant biochemical and biological systems. Chemical thermodynamics, kinetics, equilibrium, solution chemistry, the structure of macromolecules, and acid-base properties of biomolecules. Credit for a degree will not be given for both CHEM 340 and CHEM 541 .
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 112 and CHEM 112L or in CHEM 142.

title: Undergraduate Seminar
description: Student seminars and a survey of biochemical and molecular biology research at the University of South Carolina. Required of all biochemistry majors.
subject: CHEM

title: Independent Study
description: Contract Required.
subject: CHEM

title: Industrial Chemistry Capstone Experience
description: Prepares students for future roles in chemical industry or graduate school and provides career-enhancing interpersonal skills, including team-building, public speaking, resume preparation, and interviewing.
subject: CHEM

title: Undergraduate Research
description: Introduction to the methods of chemical research. A written report on work accomplished is required at the end of each semester. Nine hours of library and laboratory per week. Contract Required.
subject: CHEM

title: Undergraduate Research
description: Introduction to the methods of chemical research. A written report on work accomplished is required at the end of each semester. Nine hours of library and laboratory per week. Contract Required.

description: Introduction to the methods of chemical research. A written report on work accomplished is required at the end of each semester. Nine hours of library and laboratory per week. Contract Required.
subject: CHEM

title: Undergraduate Research
description: Introduction to the methods of chemical research. A written report on work accomplished is required at the end of each semester. Nine hours of library and laboratory per week. Contract Required.
subject: CHEM

title: Inorganic Chemistry
description: Consideration of atomic structure, valence, complex compounds, and systematic study of the periodic table.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 334, PHYS 212, and MATH 241.

title: Comprehensive Organic Chemistry III
description: Selected organic reactions from synthetic and mechanistic viewpoints. For Undergraduate Credit Only.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 334.

title: Physical Chemistry I: Thermodynamics and Kinetics
description: Chemical thermodynamics and kinetics.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 112 or CHEM 142; C or higher in MATH 241 or department permission.

title: Physical Chemistry Laboratory I: Thermodynamics and Kinetics
description: Applications of physical chemical techniques in Thermodynamics and Kinetics. Five laboratory hours and one recitation hour per week.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 321L or CHEM 322L, or departmental permission.

title: Physical Chemistry II: Quantum Mechanics and Spectroscopy
description: Spectroscopy, statistical mechanics, and chemical applications of quantum mechanics.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 112 or CHEM 142; C or higher in MATH 241 and PHYS 212, or departmental approval.

title: Physical Chemistry Laboratory II: Quantum Mechanics and Spectroscopy
description: Applications of physical chemical techniques in quantum mechanics and spectroscopy. Five laboratory hours and one recitation hour per week.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 321L or CHEM 322L or by departmental permission.

title: Physical Biochemistry
description: A survey of physical methods essential for studies of biomacromolecules. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 541 and in CHEM 550 or CHEM 555.

title: Biochemistry
description: Description of biological macromolecules and major metabolic pathways.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 334.

title: Biochemistry Laboratory
description: Experiments and demonstrations illustrating the principles of biochemistry. Three laboratory hours per week.
subject: CHEM

title: Protein Biochemistry and Metabolism
description: Comprehensive introduction to the structure and function of major biological macromolecules—amino acids, proteins, carbohydrates, and lipids. Topics include enzyme catalysis, kinetics, inhibition, and core metabolic pathways such as glycolysis, gluconeogenesis, the citric acid cycle, oxidative phosphorylation, lipid metabolism, and amino acid metabolism. Concludes with an integrated study of mammalian fuel metabolism and the regulation and coordination of metabolic pathways.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 334, or departmental permission.

title: Molecular Biochemistry of the Cell
description: Examination of key topics in molecular biology, including DNA and RNA metabolism, regulation of gene expression at transcriptional and post-transcriptional levels, protein synthesis, and mechanisms of cell cycle control and cancer development. Includes principles and applications of recombinant DNA technology.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in BIOL 302, or departmental approval.

title: Special Topics in Inorganic Chemistry
description: Current developments in inorganic chemistry. Readings and research on selected topics. Course content varies by title and will be announced in the schedule of classes. May be repeated for credit.
subject: CHEM

title: Instrumental Analysis
description: Chemical instrumentation including electronics, signal processing, statistical analysis, molecular/atomic spectroscopy, electrochemical methods, chromatography, and mass spectrometry. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 321 or CHEM 322.

title: Instrumental Analysis Lab
description: Methods, principles and strategies for chemical instrumentation in analysis. Chemical instrumentation laboratory with environmental, forensic, and biotechnology applications. Three laboratory hours per week.
subject: CHEM

title: Forensic Analytical Chemistry
description: Analytical chemical methods in forensic science, including gathering of evidence, toxicology, drug identification, analysis of trace evidence, arson analysis, and DNA/serology. Three lecture hours and three laboratory hours per week.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 321 and CHEM 321L or CHEM 322 and CHEM 322L; C or higher in CHEM 334; C or higher in CHEM 332L or CHEM 334L.

title: Introductory Environmental Chemistry
description: Study of the chemical reactions and processes that affect the fate and transport of organic chemicals in the environment. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 321, in CHEM 333, and in MATH 142.

title: Aquatic Chemistry
description: Study of the chemical reactions and processes affecting the distribution of chemical species in natural systems. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: CHEM

title: Special Topics in Analytical Chemistry
description: Current developments in inorganic chemistry. Readings and research on selected topics. Course content varies by title and will be announced in the schedule of classes. May be repeated for credit.
subject: CHEM

title: Introduction to Polymer Synthesis
description: Special emphasis on the modern synthesis of polymeric materials. Definitions, characterization, and applications of polymers will be briefly presented.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 334.

title: Special Topics in Organic Chemistry
description: Current developments in organic chemistry. Readings and research on selected topics. May be repeated as content varies by title.
subject: CHEM

title: Computational Chemistry
description: This course is designed to familiarize students with theory and use of modern electronic structure codes, as well as to develop critical thinking and problem-solving skills and to improve computer literacy.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 541 or CHEM 542.

title: Materials Chemistry
description: Introduction to materials science; structural and electronic description of inorganic-based solids; experimental techniques in materials chemistry; interfacial energetics and optoelectronic processes at metal and semiconductor surfaces.
subject: CHEM

title: Special Topics in Physical Chemistry
description: Current developments in physical chemistry. Readings and research on selected topics. Course content varies by title and will be announced in the schedule of classes. May be repeated for credit.
subject: CHEM

title: Metabolic Biochemistry of Human Disease
description: Core concepts of biochemistry as applied to human health and disease.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 555/BIOL 545 or CHEM 550/BIOL 541.

title: Special Topics in Biochemistry
description: Selected topics in the field of biochemistry. May be repeated as content varies by title.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in CHEM 555/BIOL 545 or CHEM 550/BIOL 541.

title: Methods of Solving Problems in Chemistry
description: Various approaches to solving problems in gas laws, solution chemistry, and equilibrium. Comparison of the pedagogical merits of the different approaches. For teachers of chemistry, M.A.Students. Three lectures per week.
subject: CHEM

title: Seminar
description: Required of all graduate students. Fall or Spring limit of 2 credits.
subject: CHEM

title: Seminar
description: A survey of chemical research at the University of South Carolina. Required of all first-year degree candidates in chemistry.
subject: CHEM

title: Structure and Bonding in Covalent Molecules
description: Covalent bonding in compounds of the first short period elements, with emphasis on those of boron, carbon, and nitrogen. Structure of molecules, some important functional groups, resonance in unsaturated compounds, stereochemistry, and organometallic compounds. For teachers of chemistry, M.A.T., or M.Ed. students. Three lectures and one discussion period per week.
subject: CHEM

title: Molecular Reactions
description: General types of organic reactions, including those of biochemistry. Industrial preparations of both organic and inorganic compounds of major importance. For teachers of chemistry, M.A.T., or M.Ed. students. Three lectures and one discussion period per week.
subject: CHEM

title: Energy, Equilibrium, and Chemical Change
description: The basic laws of chemical thermodynamics, chemical kinetics, and equilibrium, with emphasis on the practical and theoretical importance of the interconversion of chemical energy with other forms of energy. For teachers of chemistry, M.A.T., or M.Ed. students. Three lectures and one discussion period per week.
subject: CHEM

title: Modern Instrumental Methods in Chemistry
description: A survey of the applications of modern instrumental techniques to the solution of chemical problems, with emphasis on development of a basic understanding of the experiment and on interpretation of data. For teachers of chemistry, M.A.T., or M.Ed. students. Three lectures and one discussion period per week.
subject: CHEM

title: Chemistry in Living Systems
description: The structures and functions of proteins, nucleic acids, lipids, enzymes, and other biologically important molecules; the role of these molecules in the major metabolic pathways. For teachers of chemistry, M.A.T., or M.Ed. students. Three lectures and one discussion period per week.
subject: CHEM

title: Special Topics in Chemical Education
description: Selected chemical topics with emphasis on modern chemical concepts. For teachers of chemistry, M.A.T., I.M.A. and M.Ed. students. Lectures, discussion, laboratories, depending on credit offered.
subject: CHEM

title: Physical-Inorganic Chemistry
description: The use and interpretation of modern physical measurements of particular application to inorganic chemistry, including X-ray, ESR, magnetic measurements, Mössbauer spectra, ligand field theory, and reaction mechanisms.
subject: CHEM

title: The Chemistry of Transition Elements
description: Systematic study of the reactions and bonding of the d and f transition elements.
subject: CHEM

title: The Chemistry of the Representative Elements
description: Systematic study of the structure and bonding of the inorganic compounds of main group elements.
subject: CHEM

title: Special Topics in Inorganic Chemistry
description: May be repeated as content varies by title.
subject: CHEM

title: Electroanalytical Chemistry
description: Theory and application of classical and modern electrochemical techniques.
subject: CHEM

title: Spectrochemical Methods of Analysis
description: A comprehensive study of the theory, instrumentation, methodology, and analytical applications of modern atomic and quantitative molecular spectrometry.
subject: CHEM

title: Separation and Detection Methods in Analytical Chemistry
description: Theory and application of modern techniques for analytical separation and detection. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: CHEM

title: Advanced Analytical Chemistry and Instrumentation
description: Survey of modern analytical chemistry with an emphasis on fundamental principles that govern sensitivity, selectivity, and speed of analysis. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: CHEM

title: Special Topics in Analytical Chemistry
description: May be repeated as content varies by title.
subject: CHEM

title: Introduction to Polymer Chemistry
description: A study of the field of polymer chemistry including synthesis, characterization, and chemical and physical properties. Three lecture hours per week.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CHEM 334 or equivalent second semester organic chemistry.

title: Nanoscale Behavior and Characterization of Polymers
description: This course covers important concepts for understanding the behavior and characterization of polymers.
subject: CHEM

title: Physical Chemistry of Polymers
description: This course covers important concepts for understanding the structures and physical properties of polymers.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in CHEM 731; C or better in CHEM 541.

title: Structural and Mechanistic Organic Chemistry
description: Basic concepts of structure, bonding, stereochemistry, and reaction mechanisms as applied to organic compounds and synthetic transformations.
subject: CHEM

title: Mechanistic and Synthetic Organic Chemistry
description: A continuation of CHEM 735 with special emphasis on organic synthesis.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: CHEM 735.

title: Special Topics in Organic Chemistry
description: May be repeated as content varies by title.
subject: CHEM

title: Chemical Thermodynamics
description: A development of classical thermodynamics and its application to chemical changes.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: CHEM 542.

title: Surface Science
description: The principles of surface processes – structure and electronic properties, adsorption and reactions, surface characterization using spectroscopy and microscopy.
subject: CHEM

title: Quantum Chemistry
description: An introduction to the application of quantum mechanics to problems in chemistry.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: CHEM 542; differential equations.

title: Statistical Mechanics
description: Calculations of the thermodynamic properties of chemical systems from molecular properties. Theory and applications.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: CHEM 542; differential equations.

title: Introductory Crystallography
description: Point and space groups. Matrix representation and the derivation of the space groups. Significance of general and special positions. Powder and single crystal methods. Limitation imposed upon molecules by space group considerations. Introduction to structure analysis. Patterson and electron density functions. Refinement techniques.
subject: CHEM

title: Spectroscopy and Molecular Structure
description: Study of the rotational, vibrational, and electronic spectra of polyatomic molecules for the elucidation of molecular structures.
subject: CHEM

title: Special Topics in Physical Chemistry
description: May be repeated as content varies by title.
subject: CHEM

title: Biosynthesis of Macromolecules
description: A detailed consideration of the enzymological basis for the synthesis of DNA, RNA, and protein including mechanisms for the regulation of these processes. Focus will be on eucaryotic mechanisms though procaryotic systems will be covered as necessary for background.
subject: CHEM

title: Regulation and Integration of Metabolism
description: Biochemical organization of the cell. Regulation and integration of metabolism. Membrane structure and function. Energy transduction processes.
subject: CHEM

title: Enzymology and Protein Chemistry
description: An analysis of the isolation, composition, structure, and function of enzymes emphasizing their kinetic, mechanistic, and regulatory features. Protein chemistry: amino acid and protein sequence analysis; chemical modification methodologies; analysis of higher order structures of proteins.
subject: CHEM

title: Biomedical Biochemistry I
description: First of a two-semester sequence covering the major areas of biochemistry in a biomedical context. Chemistry of amino acids and proteins, enzymology, metabolism of carbohydrates and lipids. Emphasis is on biomedical research applications. Four lecture hours per week.
subject: CHEM

title: Biomedical Biochemistry II
description: A continuation of CHEM 754 . Topics include nucleic acids and protein biosynthesis, blood chemistry, respiration, acid-base chemistry, metabolism, and nutrition. Four lecture hours per week.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: CHEM 754.

title: Special Topics in Molecular Biochemistry
description: May be repeated as content varies by title.
subject: CHEM

title: Introduction to Research
description: A laboratory and introduction to modern research techniques. Six hours of laboratory per week and individual consultation with instructor.
subject: CHEM

title: Introduction to Research
description: A continuation of CHEM 790 . Six hours of laboratory per week and individual consultation with instructor.
subject: CHEM
prereq: Prerequisites: CHEM 790.

title: Research in Chemistry I
description: Directed laboratory research and readings in chemistry.
subject: CHEM

title: Research in Chemistry II
description: A continuation of CHEM 798 for Ph.D. candidates.
subject: CHEM

title: Introduction to Chinese Calligraphy
description: Five hundred of the most commonly used Chinese characters. Emphasis is on the phonetic and significant elements common to large groups of ideograms.
subject: CHIN

title: Elementary Chinese Mandarin
description: Grammar and practical vocabulary necessary for fundamental communication skills. Assumes no prior experience in the language.
subject: CHIN

title: Basic Proficiency in Mandarin Chinese
description: Practice and further development of essential listening, reading, speaking, and writing skills.
subject: CHIN
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in CHIN 121.

title: Intermediate Mandarin Chinese
description: Continued practice of basic sentence patterns used in modern speech with increased emphasis on reading and acquisition of additional characters.
subject: CHIN

title: Chinese Culture, Tradition, and Modern Societies
description: Introduction to Chinese culture, heritage, and modern societies. Readings selected from printed and online sources. Taught in English.
subject: CHIN

title: Advanced intermediate Mandarin Chinese I
description: Provides advanced intermediate training in spoken and written Chinese. By increasing students’ vocabulary and knowledge of sentence patterns, the course focuses on speaking and writing in coherent, well-formed paragraphs.
subject: CHIN
prereq: Prerequisites: CHIN 222.

title: Advanced Intermediate Mandarin Chinese II
description: Continues advanced intermediate training in spoken and written Chinese. Attention is given to complex grammatical patterns, discourse characteristics, and discussions of cultural topics.
subject: CHIN
prereq: Prerequisites: CHIN 321.

title: Women in China
description: Introduces the connection between gender and the Chinese national imagination. Readings include cultural and historical documents that purport to explain the experience of women in China. Readings in English. Taught in English.
subject: CHIN

title: Introduction to Premodern Chinese Literature
description: An introduction to the most important works, authors, genres, and themes of Chinese literature from the first millennium B.C.E. to 1911.
subject: CHIN

title: Modern Chinese Literature
description: Readings of canonical texts from modern Chinese literature. A focus is on the role of literature and other cultural documents in the imagination of China as a modern nation. Readings and discussion in English.
subject: CHIN

title: Screening China
description: Survey of Chinese language cinema. Chinese film history and vocabulary with which to discuss film texts. Covers classic leftwing cinema, Hong Kong martial arts films, as well as the Hong Kong, Taiwan, and PRC New Waves. Taught in English. Films subtitled.
subject: CHIN

title: Selected Topics
description: Intensive study in selected authors or literary movements of China, including cultural aspects. May be repeated for credit under different titles. Taught in English.
subject: CHIN

title: Independent Study
description: Contract approved by instructor, advisor, and department chair is required for undergraduate students.
subject: CHIN

title: Advanced Chinese I
description: Acquisition of advanced grammar and vocabulary. Emphasis on building oral proficiency in professional settings.
subject: CHIN
prereq: Prerequisites: CHIN 322.

title: Advanced Chinese II
description: Continued acquisition of advanced grammar and vocabulary. Emphasis on expanding communicative abilities in a wider variety of interpersonal situations.
subject: CHIN
prereq: Prerequisites: CHIN 421.

title: Introduction to Chinese Linguistics: Structure of Chinese
description: An overview of the linguistic structure of Mandarin Chinese through investigation of the phonetics, phonology, morphology, syntax, and lexicon as well as some aspects of sociolinguistics of the language.
subject: CHIN

title: Advanced Special Topics in Chinese Studies
description: Advanced special topics in Chinese studies. May be repeated as content varies by title.
subject: CHIN

title: Supervised Instruction in Teaching Foreign Languages in College
description: Supervised direction of foreign language teaching in college. Required of all graduate assistants who are teaching. This course will not count toward the MA or PhD degree.
subject: CHIN

title: Introduction to Classical Mythology
description: Major gods, goddesses, heroes, and heroines of classical mythology as portrayed in major literary works; the function of myth in society and its relevance to modern life.
subject: CLAS

title: Medical and Scientific Terminology
description: Greek and Latin elements in the formation of medical and scientific vocabulary; designed for students intending to enter the scientific and health professions. No previous knowledge of Greek or Latin required.
subject: CLAS

title: Sport and Combat in the Ancient World
description: This course is designed to introduce students to the importance of competition in the military and private spheres of the Greco-Roman world, a dominant legacy of antiquity.
subject: CLAS

title: Ancient Philosophy
description: An introduction to the work of ancient philosophers, with special emphasis on Plato and Aristotle.
subject: CLAS

title: Greek and Roman Philosophy after Aristotle
description: Problems such as hedonism, providence, belief and evidence, and mysticism, as they appear in the writings of Epicureans, Stoics, Skeptics, and Plotinus.
subject: CLAS

title: Greece and Rome in Film and Popular Culture
description: Representations of antiquity in cinema, television, and other contemporary media, with emphasis on Hollywood’s reception of Greek and Roman history.
subject: CLAS

title: Sexuality and Gender in Ancient Greece
description: Gender roles, standards of sexual behavior, evidence for women’s lives, as manifested in ancient Greek literary and archaeological evidence; attitudes toward homosexuality; the modern media’s representation of famous Greeks.
subject: CLAS

title: Sexuality, Gender, and Power in Ancient Rome
description: Sexuality as a social construct exemplified in standards of sexual behavior in ancient Rome and their reinforcement of the ruling ideology; feminine virtue, definitions of manliness, attitudes toward homosexuality.
subject: CLAS

title: Greek Civilization on Site
description: Introduction to the history and culture of ancient Greece, combined with an excursion of Greece. Topics include: Mycenaean Greece and the world of Homer, Archaic Greece, oikos and polis, interaction with the Near East, Athens in the 5th and 4th centruy BCE, Greek religion, ancient Greek society.
subject: CLAS

title: Special Topics in Classical Humanities
description: Intensive study of one topic per semester dealing with ancient contributions to Western civilization. Not for Greek or Latin major credit. In English. May be repeated as content varies by title.
subject: CLAS

title: Classical Roots of US Constitution
description: Study of the impact of the culture of Greece and Rome on the founding fathers of the American Revolution.
subject: CLAS

title: Roman Civilization On Site
description: Introduction to the history and culture of ancient Rome through direct participation in archaeological excavations at select ancient Roman sites in a hands-on learning environment.
subject: CLAS

title: Greek Art and Archaeology
description: A survey of ancient architecture, painting, and sculpture 2000-160 B.C.
subject: CLAS

title: Pompeii: Daily Life in a Roman Town
description: This course uses the rich archaeological resource of the city of Pompeii to explore daily life in Ancient Rome. Using both material and written sources, students will learn about how people of all social classes lived in the public and private spaces of Pompeii.
subject: CLAS

title: Classical Origins of Western Medical Ethics
description: Examination of ancient Greek and Roman philosophical, medical, and literary works (in English) as sources for the origins of medical ethics. Priority enrollment for Medical Humanities students.
subject: CLAS

title: Between Magic and Method: Ancient Medicine
description: Introduction to ancient medicine: science and art, theory and practice, healing and predicting. Topics include Medicine before Hippocrates, Hippocratic medicine, holism, naturalism, medicine, religion and magic, medicine and scientific explanation, Hellenistic medicine and methodology, Galenic medicine.
subject: CLAS

title: Rise and Fall of the Athenian Empire
description: Athenian History from Persian Wars through Peloponnesian War.
subject: CLAS

title: The Caesars: Rome's First Family
description: Study of Rome’s imperial family from Julius Caesar until the death of Nero.
subject: CLAS

title: The Age of Alexander the Great
description: In-depth study of the history, literature and culture of the Greek world era of Alexander the Great.
subject: CLAS

title: The Fall of the Roman Empire
description: Historical, literary, and cultural study of the Roman Empire during its final years of dominance in the West.
subject: CLAS

title: The Ancient Novel
description: Study of the development of the novel as a literary genre in ancient Greece and Rome.
subject: CLAS

title: Plutarch's Lives of Greeks and Romans
description: A study of Plutarch’s biographies of some of the most important historical figures from ancient Greece and Rome.
subject: CLAS

title: Greek and Latin Literature in Translation
description: A comparative survey of Greek and Latin masters.
subject: CLAS

title: Classical Drama
description: Representative plays by Greek and Roman dramatists.
subject: CLAS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ENGL 101 and ENGL 102.

title: Rhetoric and the Ancient Roots of Modern Life
description: Classical rhetoric and its ongoing influence in the modern world, emphasizing how the study and use of language in ancient Greece and Rome continue to shape modern communication.
subject: CLAS

title: Classical Mythology
description: The major Greek and Roman myths, with emphasis on their meaning, functions, and influence on ancient and later Western culture.
subject: CLAS

title: Classics of Western Literary Theory
description: Problems of literary theory in texts from the ancients to the 17th century, with an emphasis on the classical tradition.
subject: CLAS

title: Supervised Instruction in Teaching Foreign Languages in College
description: Supervised direction of foreign language teaching in college. Required of all graduate assistants who are teaching. This course will not count toward the MA or PhD degree.
subject: CLAS

title: Interdisciplinary Special Topics in the Liberal Arts: Social Sciences
description: Interdisciplinary special topics emphasizing the social sciences. May be repeated as content varies by title and section.
subject: COLA

title: Internship: Community Engagement
description: Supervised experience in the United States. Contract approval by instructor, advisor, and Assistant Dean for Academic Studies is required for undergraduate students. Minimum GPA of 2.5 required or permission of instructor of record.
subject: COLA

title: Internship: Global Community Engagement
description: Supervised experience either while student is studying abroad or when offered an internship by an appropriate entity outside the United states. Contract approval by instructor and Assistant Dean for Academic Studies is required. Minimum GPA requirement of 2.5 or higher or permission of the instructor of record.
subject: COLA

title: Interdisciplinary Seminar
description: Advanced reading and research on selected interdisciplinary topics in the liberal arts. Course content varies and will be announced in the schedule of classes by title. Open only to juniors and seniors with consent of instructor.
subject: COLA

title: Interdisciplinary Undergraduate Research
description: Supervised research experience in various interdiscsiplinary topics.
subject: COLA

title: Public Health Perspective in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: Public health issues related to speech, language, and hearing from local, national and global perspective in historical context. Special permission required by department.
subject: COMD

title: Directed Study in Speech and Language Pathology
description: Directed readings and/or research in speech pathology.
subject: COMD

title: Introduction to Speech-Language Pathology and Audiology
description: Human communication disorders with an overview of prevention and treatment programs.
subject: COMD

title: Anatomy and Physiology of Speech and Hearing Mechanisms
description: An intensive study of the anatomy and physiology of the speech and hearing mechanisms.
subject: COMD

title: Anatomy and Physiology of the Auditory and Vestibular System
description: Detailed examination of the anatomy and physiology of the auditory and vestibular system.
subject: COMD
prereq: Prerequisites: COMD 501.

title: Introduction to Clinical Procedures in Speech Pathology
description: Diagnostic and therapeutic programs for the communicatively handicapped will be observed in the public school and various rehabilitative settings. Discussion and study of basic therapeutic theories and procedures utilized in speech therapy. Introduction to phonetics or equivalent or permission of instructor.
subject: COMD

title: Selected Topics
description: Presentation of current experimental or innovative programs in diagnosis and treatment of the communicatively impaired. Course is designed to update the practicing clinician in specific areas of expertise. May be repeated for credit. Individual topics to be announced by title. Permission of instructor.
subject: COMD

title: Disorders of Articulation: Evaluation and Therapy
description: The diagnosis and treatment of articulation problems in children and adults, including analysis of current research in testing and therapy for articulation disorders.
subject: COMD
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in COMD 501 and C or better in COMD 507 or equivalents.

title: Principles of Audiology
description: Basic anatomy and psycho-physics of hearing, the pathologies of hearing loss, introduction to identification procedures including organization of hearing conservation programs and practice in pure-tone audiometry, and impact of hearing loss on preschool and school-age children and educational, psychological, and medical aspects of habilitation.
subject: COMD

title: Observation of Speech Language Pathology
description: Introduction to the clinical process through observation of various diagnostic reports and intervention programs included.
subject: COMD

title: Introduction to Language Development
description: The language acquisition process in normal children, including the development of semantics, morphology, syntax, phonology, and pragmatics; American dialects and bilingualism.
subject: COMD
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in COMD 501 and C or better in COMD 507.

title: Advanced Seminars in Speech Sound Disorders
description: Study of advanced alternative procedures for the evaluation and management of individuals with significant phonological disturbances.
subject: COMD

title: Stuttering: Evaluation and Therapy
description: An introduction to the problem of stuttering; its possible causes; the management and training of clients.
subject: COMD

title: Cleft Palate-Craniofacial Disorders: Evaluation and Management
description: Nature, diagnosis and treatment of cleft palate and craniofacial anomalies.
subject: COMD

title: Genetics of Communication Disorders
description: Genetic factors that contribute to disorders of speech, language, and hearing.
subject: COMD

title: Disorders of Voice: Evaluation and Therapy
description: The diagnosis and treatment of voice disorders in children and adults. The neurological, physiological, and psychological bases of voice disorders will be considered.
subject: COMD

title: Adult Speech and Language Disorders
description: The course is designed to introduce students to the neuropathological bases for acquired communicative, language and motor speech disorders in adults. The course includes information about the differential diagnosis of adult language and cognitive disorders as well as clinical intervention techniques.
subject: COMD

title: Preschool Language Development and Disorders
description: Components of communication, oral language, and speech in preschool children with diverse problems across all aspects of language learning, including factors that serve as precursors to literacy skills as well as evidence-based approaches to language assessment and intervention.
subject: COMD

title: Clinical Evaluation in Communicative Disorders
description: Assigned readings and reports combined with clinical practice in the evaluation of cases in areas such as aphasia, cerebral palsy, voice disorders, articulation problems, stuttering, or cleft palate.
subject: COMD

title: Directed Study in Speech Pathology
description: Directed readings and/or research in speech pathology. May be repeated for credit.
subject: COMD

title: Laryngectomy Clinical Management
description: Pre- and postoperative clinical management of the laryngeal patient with emphasis on communication and related problems.
subject: COMD

title: Selected Topics in Speech Pathology
description: Individually assigned directed readings in speech pathology. May be repeated for credit when the topics covered or subject matter is different.
subject: COMD

title: Seminar in Speech Pathology
description: An in-depth study of selected issues. May be repeated for credit when the topics covered or subject matter is different.
subject: COMD

title: Management and Coordination of Programs in Speech Pathology and Audiology
description: A study of management systems in funding, scheduling, and case load.
subject: COMD

title: Neurophysiology and Motor Speech Disorders
description: Neurocognitive bases for speech and language in the central nervous system, includ-ing anatomy and physiology and clinical management of the neurogenic speech dis-orders. Major emphasis on neuroanatomy and neurophysiology, signs and symptoms, etiology, and the diagnosis and treatment of motor speech disorders.
subject: COMD
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in COMD 501.

title: Gerontology and Communicative Disorders
description: Aging, communicative processes and problems associated with aging, and specific communicative disorders associated with aging. Methods of assessment and rehabilitation will be considered.
subject: COMD

title: Augmentative Communication: Assessment and Intervention
description: A study of historical perspectives, current issues, assessment, intervention techniques, and training strategies in augmentative communication.
subject: COMD

title: Public Health Perspectives in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: Public health issues and historical context related to speech, language, and hearing from local, national and global perspectives.
subject: COMD

title: Professional Issues in Speech-Language Pathology
description: Issues affecting speech-language pathologists including professional education, certification, licensure, legislation, standards, and ethics.
subject: COMD

title: School-Age Language Literacy Development and Disorders
description: The relationship between oral and written language and factors that impact reading and writing (phonological awareness, phonics, vocabulary). Reading (word-level, comprehension, fluency) and writing (composition, spelling) development, assessment, intervention and issues related to delivery of literacy services in the schools.
subject: COMD

title: Cognitive Disorders and Reorganization
description: This course is an introduction to the area of cognitive reorganization after traumatic brain injury as well as in dementia. Assessment of cognitive functioning as well as cognitive reorganization techniques and their applications will be described.
subject: COMD

title: Feeding and Swallowing Disorders: Evaluation and Therapy
description: Anatomical and physiological orientation to typical and disordered feeding and swallowing across the lifespan, including interprofessional education for roles and responsibilities of allied and medical health partners involved in patient care.
subject: COMD

title: Language Disorders in Adolescents
description: Diagnosis and treatment of communication problems in adolescent children, including educational psychosocial sequelae.
subject: COMD

title: Introduction to Medical Speech-Language Pathology
description: Practical application skills for speech-language pathology in the medical setting.
subject: COMD

title: Pediatric Feeding and Swallowing Disorders: Evaluation and Therapy
description: Anatomical and physiological orientation to typical and disordered pediatric feeding and swallowing from birth to 18 years of age, including interprofessional education for roles and responsibilities of allied and medical health partners involved in patient care.
subject: COMD

title: Advanced Cognitive Retraining
description: Issues in traumatic brain injury and implications for speech and language function.
subject: COMD

title: Advanced Study of Literacy for Speech-Language Pathologists
description: Theories of reading development with regard to their implications for assessment and intervention. Connections between oral and written language skills, including vocabulary as a link between word level and text-level skills. Overall framework for thinking about literacy as a multi-component language skill.
subject: COMD

title: Cultural Responsiveness in Clinical Practice
description: Foundations in culturally responsive clinical practice, including frameworks for cross-cultural engagement, evidence-based practice in multilingualism and dialectal variation, neurodiversity, power dynamics and marginalization, and related topics. Content is relevant to assessment and intervention in clinical practice.
subject: COMD

title: Introduction to Autism for the Speech-Language Pathologist
description: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a lifelong neurodevelopmental condition that affects 1 in 44 children in the United States. Speech-language pathologists are key team members in the assessment and treatment of individuals with ASD. This graduate level elective course is an introduction to ASD for the speech-language-pathologist, and is intended to prepare speech-language pathologists to understand and serve this unique population. Content areas will include: diagnostic criteria and assessment, etiology, language and communication strengths and weaknesses, speech and language assessment for the individual with ASD, and ASD interventions targeting language and communication.
subject: COMD

title: Neurocognitive Bases of Language Behavior
description: Neurocognitive bases for speech and language in the central nervous system including anatomy and physiology and theoretical constructs of language.
subject: COMD
prereq: Prerequisites: COMD 501.

title: Introduction to Speech Science and Acoustic Measurement
description: The physical and related psychological attributes of speech. Use of electronic instruments and laboratory practices in measurement of acoustic variables. Introduction to information theory.
subject: COMD
prereq: Prerequisites: COMD 501 and COMD 507.

title: Speech Pathology Management of Patients with Tracheostomy Ventilator Dependency
description: Communication options and dysphagia management for speech-language pathologists working with tracheostomized patients.
subject: COMD
prereq: Prerequisites: COMD 722.

title: Introduction to Audiology and Aural Habilitation
description: Basic anatomy and psycho-physics of hearing, pathologies of hearing loss, introduction to identification procedures including hearing screening and pure-tone audiometry, impact of hearing loss on preschool and school-aged children, and educational, psychological, and medical aspects of auditory habilitation.
subject: COMD
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in COMD 507.

title: Aural (Re)Habilitation of Children and Adults
description: Effects of hearing loss on a child's development and also on adult function and quality of life (linguistic, intellectual, social, and educational). Communication assessment and case management, including parent and patient training, education, and counseling.
subject: COMD

title: Aural Rehabilitation of Adults
description: Comprehensive rehabilitation of hearing-impaired adults with emphasis upon amplification, auditory training, and speech reading in developing communication skills.
subject: COMD

title: Cochlear Implants
description: Anatomy and physiology of the normal cochlea and the eighth cranial nerve. Evoked otoacoustic emissions as a diagnostic medium. Cochlear pathology with emphasis on candidacy for cochlear implantation.
subject: COMD

title: Habilitation of Individuals with Cochlear Implants
description: Clinical techniques for the communication assessment and habilitation of the child post cochlear implantation.
subject: COMD

title: Aural Habilitation: Manual Communication
description: Basic sign vocabulary for speech-language pathologists’ professional use with hearing-impaired clients. Focus on the nature and components of manual language systems.
subject: COMD

title: Practicum in Speech Language Pathology and Audiology
description: Supervised clinical practice in screening, diagnosis, and therapy.
subject: COMD

title: Internship in Speech Pathology
description: Supervised internship in diagnosis and treatment of children and adults with communicative disorders in clinical and public school settings in field situations.
subject: COMD

title: Research and Evidence Based Practice in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: An introduction to research methods applicable to and utilized in speech pathology and audiology. An analysis of basic and applied research.
subject: COMD

title: Research Methodology
description: Preparation of research designs, procedures of sampling and use of statistical measures.
subject: COMD

title: Seminar in Speech Pathology
description: An in depth exploration of problems, theories, and research in a specific area of speech pathology. May be repeated for credit when the topic(s) covered is different. Individual topics to be announced with title.
subject: COMD

title: Advanced Topics in Speech Pathology
description: A series of lectures, presentations, and discussion sessions in a selected area of speech pathology. May be repeated for credit when the topic(s) covered is different. Individual topics to be announced with title.
subject: COMD

title: Contemporary Issues In Speech Pathology
description: Issues of local, state, and national import related to the prevention or solution of problems in speech pathology.
subject: COMD

title: Advanced Study of Clinical Phonology
description: Introduction to nonlinear phonological theory (e.g., autosegmental, metrical) and its application for assessment and intervention of children with phonological disorders.
subject: COMD

title: Advanced Study of Language Disorders in Adults
description: Theories of language processing, language development, and the effects of neural pathology on the normal language process.
subject: COMD
