title: Professional Seminar in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: Seminar-format orientation to key issues related to preparing for academic career including the responsible conduct of research, career development, and skills required for success in academia. Content varies; topics announced in advance. Required to be repeated for 4 hours of credit to fulfill COMD Doctoral Program Curriculum requirements.
subject: COMD

title: Survey of Research in COMD
description: Seminar-format orientation to the breadth of research in COMD. This seminar will introduce students to a variety of lines of research, populations, research designs, methods, and models. Content varies; topics announced in advance. Required to be repeated for 2 hours of credit to fulfill COMD Doctoral Program Curriculum requirements.
subject: COMD

title: Scientific Writing in COMD: Papers Grants
description: This course provides structured training in scientific writing, with emphasis on manuscripts, grant proposals, and article critiques.
subject: COMD

title: Mentored Teaching in COMD
description: Mentored teaching experience emphasizing course design and preparation, student engagement, classroom management, use of learning management systems, and assessment.
subject: COMD

title: Advanced Speech Science
description: Advanced study of the physical and related psychological attributes of sound and measurement of acoustic variables of sound and speech. Review of current research in speech science.
subject: COMD

title: Advanced Hearing Science
description: The normal auditory system; middle ear and cochlear physiology as determiners of auditory psychophysics.
subject: COMD

title: Normal Bases of Speech Production
description: Processes underlying speech production, including neural control, respiration, phonation, and articulation; theories explaining the processes; measurements of physical properties of speech.
subject: COMD

title: Normal Bases of Language
description: Advanced study of the effects of pathology on the normal language processes. Theories of language processing and development over the life span. Effects of focal and diffuse neutral pathologies on language processes.
subject: COMD

title: Connections between Oral and Language Literacy
description: The course focuses on prominent theories and contemporary research on the relation between oral and written language processing in skilled adults, the development of oral and written language abilities in children, and developmental and acquired disorders of language and reading.
subject: COMD

title: Teaching Fundamentals in Communication Sciences and Disorders
description: Seminar-format orientation to key issues related to teaching for doctoral students preparing for an academic career. Topics covered will span from theories and principles of adult learning through the major aspects of course development and teaching.
subject: COMD

title: Introduction to Human Communication
description: Concepts and processes relevant to the study of communication and human behavior and their application in a broad variety of contexts, including interpersonal relationships, small groups, organizations, health communication, social media, and media literacy.
subject: COMM

title: Applied Interpersonal Communication
description: Focus on skills development in goal oriented two person conversations. Topics include an overview of the communication process in employment interviews, informational interviews, survey interviews, performance appraisals; recruiting interviews, persuasive interviews; counseling and health care interviews.
subject: COMM

title: Social Influence and Persuasion
description: Introduction to theoretical and applied issues in interpersonal persuasion and persuasion campaigns. Presents an overview of the area with an emphasis on critical analysis about the creation and consumption of persuasive messages.
subject: COMM

title: Communication in Teams
description: Introduction to the theoretical and applied issues to how individuals communicate in teams, how teams are created and defined through communication, and how teams communicate with other teams across organizations. Crucial to understanding and strengthening future teamwork experiences in organizations.
subject: COMM

title: Conflict and Negotiation
description: Surveys a range of conflict settings, including communication in interpersonal, group, and organizational contexts. Focuses on the application of theory and research to help students develop the skills needed to manage conflict effectively in their personal and professional lives.
subject: COMM

title: Media and Youth
description: A developmental approach to media uses and effects research, considering cognitive, emotional, and social development as a framework for thinking about media effects on youth.
subject: COMM

title: Nonverbal Communication
description: Presents a comprehensive introduction to nonverbal communication. Covers the evolutionary and socio-cultural foundations as well as the basic codes and functions of nonverbal communication as well as the applications of nonverbal communication concepts to everyday interactions.
subject: COMM

title: Gender and Communication
description: Examines how gender influences communication and the multiple relationships among gender, communication and culture. Explores how communication creates gender and shapes relationships and how communication reflects, sustains, and alters cultural views of gender.
subject: COMM

title: Communication Research
description: Fundamentals of communications research methods and applications. Survey, observational and experimental research; primary research data-gathering techniques; secondary research sources; concept of data analysis; message and audience research measures.
subject: COMM

title: Crisis Communications
description: Introduction to crisis communications and management from a strategic, theory-based approach using research from historical and current case studies.
subject: COMM

title: Research Experience in Communication
description: Supervised communications research experience to develop student’s advanced research and analytical skills. Individual research contract developed in consultation with the instructor.
subject: COMM

title: Mass Media Criticism
description: Development of critical thinking skills for analyzing mass media.
subject: COMM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in JOUR 101.

title: Family Communication
description: An introduction to research trends and theory in the scientific study of the family, considering issues related to family interaction, functioning, relationships, and communication.
subject: COMM

title: Communication in Social and Personal Relationships
description: Survey of concepts, theories, and research concerning communication across all phases of interpersonal relationships, focusing on both theoretical and practical applications.
subject: COMM

title: Health Communication
description: Explores the diversity of health experiences and the ways in which health communication affects our lives, whether through interpersonal conversations about health issues, exposure to health information in the media, or through our personal involvement with the healthcare system.
subject: COMM

title: Communication in Complex Organizations
description: Survey of the theoretical and empirical literature dealing with human communication behavior as it occurs within the context of complex organizations. Among the topics covered are superior-subordinate communication, communication networks, message distortion, feedback processes, communication climate, leadership and decision-making.
subject: COMM

title: Special Topics in the Scientific Approach to Media Effects
description: Topics in communication and media effects. Individual topics will vary by title.
subject: COMM
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in COMM 332.

title: Health Communication: The Science and Practice
description: Explores the role of journalism in shaping perceptions of scientific issues and task. Emphasis on methods of effectively communicating about science, health, and the environment.
subject: COMM

title: Environmental Communication: The Science and Practice
description: The role of journalism in shaping perceptions of environmental and scientific issues and tasks. Emphasis on methods of effectively communicating about science and the environment.
subject: COMM

title: Public Opinion and Persuasion
description: Theory and practice of persuasive communication and the role of persuasion in shaping public opinion.
subject: COMM

title: Online Communication and Personal Relationships
description: Examines how people form their identities and manage their personal relationships using new communication technologies (social network sites, online dating, mobile computing, video games). Topics include impression management, deception and trust, self-perception and identity, social support and relationship maintenance.
subject: COMM

title: Communication and Interethnic Behavior
description: The relation of communication processes to interethnic and interracial attitudes and behavior. Psycho-social foundations of interethnic communication and conflict, group identification and communication processes, interpersonal communication and culture, communication about race and ethnicity, mass media content and effects.
subject: COMM

title: Study Abroad in Journalism and Mass Communications
description: Study abroad course will focus on topics in journalism and mass communications and will be taught as a study abroad experience. Individual topics will vary by title.
subject: COMM

title: Business and Legal Issues for Science Managers
description: Survey of skills requisite for careers in domestic or international business: economics, finance, accounting, management, marketing, presentation skills, patent law, regulatory issues, other subjects for managers of science/technology-based businesses.
subject: COSM

title: Scientific and Technological Problems in Business and Industry
description: Seminar course in problem solving, responding to cases with significant technical components, drawn from business/industry. Students analyze cases to propose solutions to problems, integrating the major activities of a technically oriented business.
subject: COSM

title: Internship in Science and Technology Based Business
description: Internship in industry, government agency, or national laboratory. Internship experience culminates in oral and written reports on duties and projects. Internship must include a minimum of 150 hours of experience at the internship site.
subject: COSM

title: Values and Ethics in Literature
description: Analysis of major works of world literature focusing on values, ethics, and social responsibility.
subject: CPLT

title: World Literature
description: Selected masterpieces of world literature from antiquity to present.
subject: CPLT
prereq: Prerequisites: ENGL 101 and ENGL 102 or equivalent.

title: What is Comparative Literature
description: Introduction to ways of reading and comparing literatures drawn from diverse languages and cultures.
subject: CPLT
prereq: Prerequisites: any 200-level literature course.

title: Great Books of the Western World I
description: European masterpieces from antiquity to the beginning of the Renaissance.
subject: CPLT

title: Great Books of the Western World II
description: European masterpieces from the Renaissance to the present.
subject: CPLT

title: Great Books of the Eastern World
description: Classical and contemporary poetry and prose of the Middle and Far East.
subject: CPLT

title: Arab Women Writers
description: Introduction to writings by and about women writers from the Arab world from the 20th and 21st centuries. Taught in English.
subject: CPLT

title: Epic to Romance
description: Comprehensive exploration of medieval and other pre-Renaissance literature using texts representative of the evolution of dominant literary forms.
subject: CPLT
prereq: Prerequisites: ENGL 101 and ENGL 102.

title: The Renaissance
description: Literature of the Renaissance, in its cultural contexts, explored through representative works.
subject: CPLT
prereq: Prerequisites: ENGL 101 and ENGL 102.

title: The Enlightenment
description: Literature of the Enlightenment in its cultural contexts, explored through representative works.
subject: CPLT
prereq: Prerequisites: ENGL 101 and ENGL 102.

title: Romanticism
description: Literature of Romanticism, in its cultural contexts, explored through representative works.
subject: CPLT
prereq: Prerequisites: ENGL 101 and ENGL 102.

title: Realism
description: Literature of Realism in its cultural contexts, explored through representative works.
subject: CPLT
prereq: Prerequisites: ENGL 101 and ENGL 102.

title: Modernism
description: Literature of Modernism in its cultural contexts, explored through representative works.
subject: CPLT
prereq: Prerequisites: ENGL 101 and ENGL 102.

title: Postmodernism
description: Literature of Postmodernism in its cultural contexts, explored through representative works.
subject: CPLT
prereq: Prerequisites: ENGL 101 and ENGL 102.

title: Topics in Comparative Literary Relations
description: Topics involving two or more national literatures. Topics to be announced in master schedule by title.
subject: CPLT

title: Special Topics in Comparative Studies in Film and Media
description: Topics in film and media from an international perspective. National cinematic traditions are compared and contrasted. May be repeated as content varies by title.
subject: CPLT

title: Introduction to Graduate Studies in Languages, Literatures, and Cultures
description: An introduction to graduate studies that includes a survey of contemporary literary theory, an overview of the current state of the profession, and instruction in how to carry out research and write at the graduate level.
subject: CPLT

title: Classics of Western Literary Theory
description: Problems of literary theory in texts from the ancients to the 17th century, with an emphasis on the classical tradition.
subject: CPLT

title: Modern Literary Theory
description: Problems of literary theory from the 18th century to the 1960s.
subject: CPLT

title: Topics in Contemporary Literary Theory
description: Presents an in-depth study of selected schools or trends in contemporary literary theory.
subject: CPLT

title: The Periods of Literature
description: The study of one cultural period as an international movement. Topics will vary.
subject: CPLT

title: The Literary Genre
description: Study of a genre from its inception to the present and its manifestations in the several literatures. Topics will vary.
subject: CPLT

title: Themes in Literature
description: The study of recurrence and mutation in literary themes. Topics will vary.
subject: CPLT

title: Cross-Cultural Literary Relations
description: Topics will vary and will be announced (e.g., Anglo-French literary relations, 1740-1900; 20th-century German-American literary relations).
subject: CPLT

title: Literature and Translation: Theory and Practice
description: A survey of recent translation theory as it relates to literature, combined with translation analysis and actual translation of literary texts.
subject: CPLT

title: Advanced Film Study
description: Methods of film analysis, resources for research, and the major critical theories.
subject: CPLT

title: Supervised Instruction in Teaching Foreign Languages in College
description: Supervised direction of foreign language teaching in college. Required of all graduate assistants who are teaching. This course will not count toward the MA or PhD degree.
subject: CPLT

title: The Teaching of Comparative Literature
description: Open to Ph.D. candidates who have passed their comprehensive examinations.
subject: CPLT

title: The American Criminal Justice System
description: Survey of crime and societal responses to crime, including law enforcement, courts, corrections, and the juvenile justice system.
subject: CRJU

title: Research Methods in Criminology and Criminal Justice
description: Introduction to the practice of social research in criminology and criminal justice settings.
subject: CRJU

title: Ethics in Criminal Justice
description: Examination of ethical issues in the criminal justice system.
subject: CRJU
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CRJU 101.

title: History of Crime and Punishment
description: An exploration of the evolution of global practices related to enforcing conformity with societal norms. Examines legal codes, philosophies, and technological devices associated with crime and punishment throughout history.
subject: CRJU

title: Criminal Procedure
description: Overview of the constitutional restraints on the investigation, detention, prosecution and adjudication of criminal defendants. Coverage of Supreme Court decisions involving the 4th, 5th, and 6th Amendments to the U.S. Constitution.
subject: CRJU

title: Policing
description: Current and historical perspectives on American policing.
subject: CRJU

title: Corrections
description: Current and historical perspectives on incarceration and its alternatives.
subject: CRJU

title: Criminal Courts
description: Structure and organization of the federal and state criminal court systems and personnel.
subject: CRJU

title: Criminal Law
description: Origin and development of criminal law in America. Basic elements of crimes and defenses.
subject: CRJU

title: Drugs and Crime
description: Overview of criminal justice system responses to illegal substances. Relationship between substance abuse and crime.
subject: CRJU

title: Violence in America
description: Historical overview of violence in American society, including theoretical perspectives on the causes and prevention of violence.
subject: CRJU

title: Sociology of Crime
description: Social factors in the development, identification, and treatment of criminals.
subject: CRJU

title: Juvenile Delinquency
description: Social factors in the development, identification, and treatment of delinquents.
subject: CRJU
prereq: Prerequisites: CRJU 101 OR SOCY 101.

title: Independent Study
description: Contract approved by instructor, advisor, and Office of Academic Programs is required for undergraduate students.
subject: CRJU

title: Analyzing Homicide
description: Relationships between patterns and trends in homicide, and theoretical explanations for why offenders engage in homicide.
subject: CRJU

title: Victimization
description: Causes and consequences of criminal victimization and public policy responses to victimization issues.
subject: CRJU

title: Alternatives to Incarceration
description: Correctional alternatives to imprisonment including probation, parole, and various community correctional programs.
subject: CRJU

title: Street Gangs: Structure, Activity, and Response
description: Course covers the theoretical and empirical work on gangs, gang members, and gang activity along with insight on these issues from a practitioner perspective. It then examines the variety of policy responses from government and community organizations.
subject: CRJU

title: Criminal Justice Intelligence
description: An investigation of the motivations to commit crime. The course presents profiles of the targets of crimes and provides strategic and tactical assessments of police investigations and intelligence.
subject: CRJU

title: Hate Crimes
description: An examination of the causes and responses to hate crimes. The course also provides a foundation for understanding crimes motivated by racial, gender, religious, disability, and sexual orientation biases.
subject: CRJU

title: Criminal Justice and Mental Health
description: Interface between the mental health sciences and the criminal justice system.
subject: CRJU

title: Communities and Crime
description: Relationship between criminality and community characteristics, with particular attention to how variation in community structure, organization, and culture impacts crime.
subject: CRJU

title: Homeland Security and Terrorism
description: The nature of terrorism; its history; its methodology; and its application to criminological theory.
subject: CRJU

title: Selected Topics in Criminal Justice Policy
description: Public policy responses to crime, its formation, and its impact on society. Individual topics to be announced by title. May be repeated with consent of advisor.
subject: CRJU

title: Special Topics
description: Topics in criminology and criminal justice. Individual topics to be announced by title. May be repeated once with consent of advisor.
subject: CRJU

title: Internship
description: A supervised experiential course in a criminal justice agency. Contract approved by instructor, advisor, and Office of Academic Programs is required for undergraduate students.
subject: CRJU

title: Critical Incident Management for Criminal Justice
description: Leadership and management strategies for criminal justice agencies during critical incidents and disasters including multi-agency and multi-jurisdictional response.
subject: CRJU

title: Information-Based Management in Criminal Justice
description: The collection and use of information and data-driven analysis in criminal justice organizations.
subject: CRJU

title: Inmates and Prisons
description: Examination of issues affecting prisons and the inmates confined within them. Specific topics of study will include the philosophy and goals of imprisonment, institutional crowding, inmate rights, inmate adaptation, and individual and collective misconduct.
subject: CRJU

title: Criminal Justice and Public Health
description: Exploration of the intersection of criminal justice and public health, including an examination of how policies, practices, and systemic issues within criminal justice impact community health outcomes and the application of public health perspectives to crime prevention.
subject: CRJU

title: Adolescent Mentoring
description: Application of skills and theories of adolescent mentoring taught in the classroom to a supervised, structured mentoring field experience.
subject: CRJU

title: Women, Gender, and Crime
description: Impact of gender-based relations on crime and the criminal justice system.
subject: CRJU

title: Crime Over the Life Course
description: Development of criminal and delinquent behavior over time.
subject: CRJU

title: Race, Crime, and Criminal Justice
description: An historical overview of the intersection between issues of race, crime, and justice. The impact of the criminal justice system on minority groups.
subject: CRJU

title: Organized Crime
description: Origins and modern day activity of organized crime in the United States and internationally will be investigated. Attention is given to problems of criminal activity and the present day transnational character of criminal organizations.
subject: CRJU

title: The Death Penalty
description: Overview of the history and evolution of the death penalty. Identification of key legal developments in death penalty jurisprudence.
subject: CRJU

title: Law and Criminal Justice Policy
description: Legal and policy responses to crime and criminal justice issues.
subject: CRJU
prereq: Prerequisites: CRJU 313 or CRJU 314.

title: Computer Applications in Criminal Justice
description: Computing, database systems, and software applications in research and professional practice.
subject: CRJU

title: Selected Topics in Criminal Justice
description: A seminar for advanced students. Individual topics to be announced by title. May be repeated once with the consent of the advisor.
subject: CRJU

title: Survey of Criminal Justice
description: Classical and recent literature in criminal justice. Trends and issues that transcend criminal justice.
subject: CRJU

title: Law and Justice
description: Examination of law as an instrument of criminal justice policy, social control, and the protection of civil liberties.
subject: CRJU

title: Research Methods in Criminal Justice
description: Scientific methods in criminal justice research to include methods of design, data collection, and interpretation of research findings.
subject: CRJU

title: Quantitative Methods in Criminal Justice
description: Descriptive and inferential statistics and the use of computers in criminal justice.
subject: CRJU

title: Policing
description: Historical and contemporary role of the police, societal expectations, resource allocation, police policies, and the effectiveness of various police strategies in controlling crime.
subject: CRJU

title: Ethics in Criminal Justice
description: Classic and contemporary theories of ethics and their applications to criminal justice decision-making.
subject: CRJU

title: Criminal Courts
description: Historical development, structure, and organization of the federal and state criminal court systems and review of the factors that influence criminal case processing.
subject: CRJU

title: Corrections
description: Historical development of corrections, trends, and changes in the field of corrections and rehabilitation.
subject: CRJU

title: Criminology
description: The major theories of the etiology of criminal behavior, including biological, environmental, and other causative factors.
subject: CRJU

title: Juvenile Justice
description: Historical and contemporary role of the juvenile justice system, evolution of juvenile justice policies, and evaluation of juvenile justice processes and interventions.
subject: CRJU

title: Gender, Crime, and Justice
description: The role of gender in explaining crime, criminal justice system processing, and punishment.
subject: CRJU

title: Race, Crime, and Justice
description: The role of race in explaining crime, criminal justice system processing, and punishment.
subject: CRJU

title: Violence and Victims
description: Causes of, consequences of, and responses to violence and victimization.
subject: CRJU

title: Selected Topics in Criminal Justice
description: Seminar for advanced students. Topics of current importance, such as drugs, judicial reform, or crime prevention. May be repeated for credit up to 6 semester hours with consent of advisor.
subject: CRJU

title: Directed Study in Criminal Justice
description: Independent study for advanced students, under faculty supervision. May be repeated for credit up to 6 semester hours.
subject: CRJU

title: Internship in Criminal Justice
description: Placement in a criminal justice agency under faculty supervision.
subject: CRJU

title: Professional Development in Criminology and Criminal Justice
description: Review of strategies and skills necessary for a successful career after completion of a doctoral degree in criminology and criminal justice.
subject: CRJU

title: Crime, Law, and Public Policy
description: The study of the legal and policy-making processes as they apply to criminology and criminal justice, Examines the interrelationships between law, crime, and public policy and the research methodologies appropriate for the study of crime-related policies.
subject: CRJU

title: Advanced Quantitative Analysis for Criminology and Criminal Justice
description: A detailed treatment of the general linear model and logistic regression analysis with applications in criminology and criminal justice.
subject: CRJU
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in CRJU 705 or equivalent.

title: Research Design in Criminology and Criminal Justice
description: Intensive coverage of the logic and practice of research design and measurement issues commonly encountered in criminology and criminal justice research. Emphasizes the use of experimental research designs as the preferred methodology for making causal inferences.
subject: CRJU

title: Applied Quantitative Data Analysis
description: Review of applied quantitative methodological literature in criminology and criminal justice. Topics can include analysis of count data, data from randomized field experiments, interrupted time-series studies, longitudinal studies, regression discontinuity studies, instrumental variable estimation, treatment probability matching estimators, statistical power analysis, and study planning.
subject: CRJU
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in CRJU 705 and CRJU 812 or equivalents.

title: Qualitative Research Methods and Data Analysis
description: Examination of the qualitative research paradigm and its contribution to social inquiry, including the collection, organization, and analysis of qualitative date. Collection and analytic strategies involve interviewing, observation, and textual analysis.
subject: CRJU

title: Advanced Criminological Theory
description: Advanced coverage of theoretical and developments and empirical research in criminology, with a focus on definitive statements from important theoretical traditions, empirical tests of criminological theories, and the translation of theory into policy.
subject: CRJU

title: Dissertation Preparation
description: Dissertation Preparation.
subject: CRJU

title: Introduction to Computer and Programming Concepts using AI Tools
description: History, application, and social impact of computers; problem-solving, algorithm development, AI, applications software, and programming in a procedural language.
subject: CSCE

title: Web Design and Development using AI Tools
description: Introduction to web design and development. Covers HTML, CSS, JavaScript, AI tools, web design best practices, layout, content management, accessibility, usability, and search engine optimization.
subject: CSCE

title: Program Design and Development
description: Fundamental algorithms and processes used in business information systems. Development and representation of programming logic. Introduction to implementation using a high-level programming language.
subject: CSCE

title: Scientific Applications Programming
description: Introduction to computer applications in science and engineering. Programming exercises in a high-level language.
subject: CSCE

title: Algorithmic Design I
description: Problem-solving, algorithmic design, and programming. Three lectures and two laboratory hours per week.
subject: CSCE

title: Algorithmic Design II
description: Rigorous development of algorithms and computer programs; elementary data structures.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CSCE 145 or CSCE 106; C or better in MATH 111 or higher (or by Math Placement Test score into MATH 115 or higher math).

title: Artificial Intelligence for All
description: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence and its applications. Overview of AI topics and applications, including machine learning, deep learning, natural language processing, knowledge representation, statistical inference, and symbolic manipulation.
subject: CSCE

title: Computing in the Modern World
description: An introduction to the field of computing: trends in computing technology, the profession, and careers; subdisciplines in computing; the nature of research and development.
subject: CSCE

title: Introduction to Computer Security
description: Introduction to the theory and practice of computer security, including security policies, authentification, digital certificates, firewalls, malicious code, legal and ethical issues, and incident handling.
subject: CSCE

title: Business Applications Programming
description: Introduction to computer applications in business. Programming exercises in COBOL.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: MGSC 290 or CSCE 101 or above.

title: UNIX System Administration
description: The Unix programming environment: I/O programming, Unix processes, fork, exec, pipes and signals, and tools.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 145 or CSCE 106.

title: Special Topics in Computer Programming
description: Programming and application development using selected programming languages. Course content varies and will be announced in the schedule of classes by title.
subject: CSCE

title: Computer Hardware Foundations
description: Number representation, data formats, CPU and memory organization, assembly language, I/O and peripherals, computer networks. Students may not apply both CSCE 210 and CSCE 212 to any minor or major program of study.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in CSCE 145, CSCE 104, CSCE 205, CSCE 106, or CSCE 207.

title: Digital Logic Design
description: Number systems, Boolean algebra, logic design, sequential machines.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: MATH 141.

title: Introduction to Computer Architecture
description: Computer architecture, components. and organization; memory addressing; Input/Output; instruction sets; interrupts; assembly-language programming. Students may not apply both CSCE 210 and CSCE 212 to any minor or major program of study.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in CSCE 211 and D or better in either CSCE 145 or CSCE 106.

title: UNIX/Linux Fundamentals
description: UNIX operating system, user-level system commands, and programming tools. UNIX scripting languages.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CSCE 145 or CSCE 106 .

title: Advanced Programming Techniques
description: Pointers; memory management; advanced programming language structures: operator overloading, iterators, multiple inheritance, polymorphism, templates, virtual functions; Unix programming environment.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 215, C or better in CSCE 146.

title: Web Applications
description: Web technologies to support client-server computing. Implementation of client-server applications.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CSCE 146.

title: Object-Oriented Programming Techniques
description: Advanced object-oriented concepts and techniques; multiple inheritance; memory management; operator overloading; polymorphism; performance issues.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CSCE 146.

title: Software Engineering
description: Fundamentals of software design and development; software implementation strategies; object-oriented design techniques; functional design techniques; design patterns; design process; source control; testing.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CSCE 146.

title: Robotic Applications and Design
description: Design and control of robots. Interactions between robots, sensing, actuation, and computation.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CSCE 240.

title: Applied Problem Solving and Programming
description: Systematic problem definition, solution formulation, and computer implementation for business and related areas. Internet and database applications. Programming exercises in a high-level programming language.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 104 or MGSC 298.

title: Operating Systems
description: Operating system structure and function; process implementation, scheduling, and synchronization; memory management; security; naming protection; resource allocation; network file systems.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 240; CSCE 210 or CSCE 212.

title: Embedded Systems
description: Fundamentals of embedded systems: hardware components, software components, hardware/software interface design, and hardware/software co-design.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 211, CSCE 212.

title: Computer Systems Engineering
description: System-level modeling and evaluation of computer systems: requirements elicitation and specification, architectural design, reliability and performance evaluation, Markov modeling, life-cycle cost analysis, project management.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 212, MATH 242, STAT 509.

title: Programming Language Structures
description: Formal specification of syntax and semantics; structure of algorithms; list processing and string manipulation languages; statement types, control structures, and interfacing procedures.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 240; MATH 174 or MATH 374 or MATH 574.

title: Data Structures and Algorithms
description: Techniques for representing and processing information, including the use of lists, trees, and graphs; analysis of algorithms; sorting, searching, and hashing techniques.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in CSCE 240; D or better in MATH 174, MATH 374, or MATH 574; D or better in MATH 141 or MATH 122.

title: Foundations of Computation
description: Basic theoretical principles of computing as modeled by formal languages, grammars, automata, and Turing machines; fundamental limits of computation.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 211, CSCE 212, CSCE 350.

title: Professional Issues in Computer Science and Engineering
description: Professional issues in the information technology professions; history and social context of computing; professional responsibilities; privacy; intellectual property; risks and liabilities of computer-based systems.
subject: CSCE

title: Mainframe Systems
description: Introduction to the large scale computer systems used by businesses to support thousands of simultaneous users and process millions of transactions.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ITEC 352, or C or better in CSCE 146.

title: Introduction to Computer Networks
description: Concepts and components of computer networks and the Internet; network applications; network protocol stack.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 146.

title: Capstone Computing Project I
description: Major team-based software design project to be undertaken in a student’s final year of study; project planning, software requirements analysis, design, and specification. Written reports and oral presentations in a technical setting.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in CSCE 240.

title: Capstone Computer Engineering Project
description: Advanced computer systems engineering. Team projects. Written reports and oral presentations in a technical setting.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in CSCE 240, CSCE 313, CSCE 611.

title: Capstone Computing Project II
description: Continuation of CSCE 490 . Computer system implementation, testing, verification and validation of results. Written reports and oral presentations in a technical setting.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in CSCE 240, CSCE 350, and CSCE 490.

title: Independent Study
description: Individual investigation or study of special topics. At most three credits may be applied toward a degree. Approval of project proposal by instructor and department advisor.
subject: CSCE

title: Computer Programming and Applications
description: Concepts and properties of algorithms; programming exercises with emphasis on good programming habits. Credit may not be received for both CSCE 500 and CSCE 145 . Open to all majors. May not be used for major credit by computer science and engineering majors.
subject: CSCE

title: System Programming
description: System software such as command language interpreters, client-server applications, debuggers; mail systems, browsers, macroprocessors, and revision control systems; file systems, processes, threads, and interprocess communication.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 215, CSCE 240.

title: System Performance Evaluation
description: Measuring, modeling, analyzing, and predicting performance of computer systems and networks; bottleneck analysis; Markovian queuing systems and networks; use of operational and probabilistic models.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 311, STAT 509 or STAT 515.

title: Computer Architecture
description: Design methodology; processor design; computer arithmetic: algorithms for addition, multiplication, floating point arithmetic; microprogrammed control; memory organization; introduction to parallel architectures.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 211, CSCE 212.

title: Computer Network Programming
description: Computer networks and communication protocols; socket programming; interprocess communication; development of network software; case studies.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 311.

title: Computer Networks
description: Structure, design, and analysis of computer networks; ISO/OSI network architecture.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: STAT 509 or STAT 515.

title: Computer Crime and Forensics
description: Methodical approaches for collecting and preserving evidence of computer crimes. Foundational concepts such as file system structures, MAC times, and network protocols; tools for extracting evidence; general legal issues.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CSCE 215.

title: Ethical Hacking
description: Fundamental principles and techniques of ethical hacking, including penetration testing life cycle, planning and scoping, identifying targets and goals, active and passive reconnaissance, enumeration and scanning, exploitation, post-exploitation, and results reporting.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 215 or previous Linux/UNIX experience.

title: Database System Design
description: Database management systems; database design and implementation; security, integrity, and privacy.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CSCE 145, CSCE 104, or CSCE 106; C or better in MATH 142, MATH 170, MATH 172, or MATH 174.

title: Information Security Principles
description: Threats to information resources and appropriate countermeasures. Cryptography, identification and authentication, access control models and mechanisms, multilevel database security, steganography, Internet security, and intrusion detection and prevention.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 146; MATH 374 or MATH 174.

title: Service Oriented Computing
description: Cooperative information systems and service-oriented computing. Techniques for achieving coordinated behavior among a decentralized group of information system components. Distributed databases, multiagent systems, conceptual modeling, Web services, and applications.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 311.

title: Compiler Construction
description: Techniques for design and implementation of compilers, including lexical analysis, parsing, syntax-directed translation, and symbol table management.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 240.

title: Functional Programming
description: Functional programming as a paradigm. History of functional languages from Lisp to the present. Programming in Haskell, ML, or similar language at an intermediate level: recursive and higher-order functions, list comprehensions, types and classes, monads, lazy evaluation, reasoning about programs.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CSCE 330, CSCE 350, or MATH 374.

title: Mobile Application Development
description: Development of mobile applications, including user interface design for mobile, local and cloud data storage techniques, and application architectures.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 240 or previous programming experience with one of the following programming languages (C/C++, Java, Swift, Python, Matlab, Javascript).

title: Windows Programming
description: Object-oriented methods and tools for application programming with graphically interactive operating systems.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 240.

title: Building Secure Software
description: Construction of software systems resistant to vulnerabilities and attacks. Cryptographic tools. Language, operating system, and network security. Case studies. Development of best practices through programming assignments.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 240.

title: Theory of Computation
description: Basic theoretical principles of computing as modeled by formal languages and automata; computability and computational complexity.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CSCE 350 or MATH 300.

title: Computer Game Development
description: Design and development of computer games, with emphasis on the technologies used. Hands-on development of computer games.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 240, CSCE 350.

title: Analysis of Experimental Data in Python
description: Hand-on introduction in Python to the analysis of experimental data (biosignals, sensor readings, etc.), including various aspects such as data wrangling, time-series handling, statistics, classification, and visualization.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CSCE 240 or CSCE 106.

title: Introduction to Cryptography
description: Design of secret codes for secure communication, including encryption and integrity verification: ciphers, cryptographic hashing, and public key cryptosystems such as RSA. Mathematical principles underlying encryption. Code-breaking techniques. Cryptographic protocols.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CSCE 145 or MATH 241, and at least one of CSCE 355, MATH 300 or MATH 374.

title: Numerical Analysis
description: Interpolation and approximation of functions; solution of algebraic equations; numerical differentiation and integration; numerical solutions of ordinary differential equations and boundary value problems; computer implementation of algorithms.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in MATH 520 or in both MATH 242 and MATH 344.

title: Systems Simulation
description: Computer simulation of real systems; principles of system organization; random number generation; programming exercises in a simulation language.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 240, STAT 509 or STAT 515.

title: Computational Science
description: Parallel algorithms; scientific visualization; techniques for solving scientific problems.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: MATH 526, CSCE 146 or CSCE 207 or CSCE 500.

title: Introduction to Computer Graphics
description: Graphics hardware; graphics primitives; two-dimensional and three-dimensional viewing; basic modeling.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 240, MATH 526 or MATH 544.

title: Visualization Tools
description: Scientific visualization tools as applied to sampled and generated data; methods for data manipulation and representation; investigation of visualization techniques.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 145, CSCE 106, or CSCE 207.

title: Parallel Computing
description: Architecture and interconnection of parallel computers; parallel programming models and applications; issues in high-performance computing; programming of parallel computers.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: knowledge of programming in a high-level language; C or better in MATH 344 or MATH 544.

title: Critical Interactives
description: Foundational techniques in multidisciplinary software development, specifically of applications designed to present sensitive, sometimes controversial, materials in ways to engender empathic awareness of the interactor.
subject: CSCE

title: Human-Computer Interaction
description: Interdisciplinary approach to interaction design, user-centered design, human abilities, survey development, experimental study methodology, heuristic evaluations, usability testing, universal design, and accessibility.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: Undergraduate or graduate standing in CSE or permission of the instructor.

title: Robotics
description: Design and application of robotic systems; emphasis on mobile robots and intelligent machines.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 211, CSCE 212, CSCE 240.

title: Text Processing
description: Text and natural language processing; formal models and data structures appropriate for text processing; selected topics in computational linguistics, stylistics, and content analysis.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 330, CSCE 355.

title: Artificial Intelligence
description: Heuristic problem solving, theorem proving, and knowledge representation, including the use of appropriate programming languages and tools.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 350.

title: Trusted Artificial Intelligence
description: AI Trust – responsible/ethical technology, fairness/ lack of bias, explanations (XAI), machine learning, reasoning, software testing, data quality and provenance, tools and projects.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CSCE 240 and CSCE 350.

title: Bayesian Networks and Decision Graphs
description: Normative approaches to uncertainty in artificial intelligence. Probabilistic and causal modeling with Bayesian networks and influence diagrams. Applications in decision analysis and support. Algorithms for probability update in graphical models.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in either CSCE 106 or CSCE 145 and either STAT 509 or STAT 515.

title: Applied Machine Learning Using Python
description: Introduction to fundamentals of machine learning with emphasis on how to use these technologies. Topics include supervised learning, decision trees, neural networks, deep learning.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CSCE 104, CSCE 106, or CSCE 145.

title: Neural Networks and Their Applications
description: Introduction to artificial neural networks, neural network topologies, neural network learning paradigms, training rules, and applications across supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning tasks.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CSCE 240 or CSCE 556; C or better in MATH 241; C or better in MATH 344.

title: Machine Learning Systems
description: Design and implementation of machine learning systems, Deep learning systems stack, machine learning platforms, scalable and distributed machine learning.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CSCE 240 or CSCE 106.

title: Big Data Analytics
description: Foundational techniques and tools required for data science and big data analytics. Concepts, principles, and techniques applicable to any technology and industry for establishing a baseline that can be enhanced by future study.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in STAT 301, STAT 509, STAT 513, or STAT 515.

title: Advanced Machine Learning with Implementation
description: Theory and application of machine learning. Current mainstream programming libraries such as in Python, and implementation of regression, clustering, principal components, and linear discriminant analysis. Detailed coverage of methods such as random forests, support vector machines, and k-nearest neighbors. Introduction to neural networks.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CSCE 106; C or better in STAT 530, CSCE 587, or STAT 587; C or better in MATH 241, MATH 328, MATH 344, or MATH 544.

title: Topics in Information Technology
description: Reading and research on selected topics in information technology. Course content varies and will be announced in the schedule of courses by title. May be repeated for credit as topics vary.
subject: CSCE

title: Strategic Management of Information Systems
description: Strategic management and use of information systems in organizations.
subject: CSCE

title: Advanced Digital Design
description: Design techniques for logic systems; emphasis on higher-level CAD tools such as hardware description languages and functional modeling.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 212.

title: VLSI System Design
description: VLSI design process models, introduction to EDA tools, HDL modeling and simulation, logic synthesis and simulation, benchmark design projects.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 211.

title: Fundamentals of VLSI Chip Design
description: Design of VLSI circuits, including standard processes, circuit design, layout, and CAD tools. Lecture and guided design projects.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: ELCT 371.

title: Advanced Operating Systems
description: Operating system organization and interactive processing systems, multiprogramming systems, process management, task scheduling, resource control, deadlocks.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 311.

title: Advanced Computer Architecture
description: Architecture of high-performance computers, including array processors, multiprocessor systems, data flow computers, and distributed processing systems.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 311 and CSCE 513.

title: Edge and Neuromorphic Computing
description: Artificial Intelligence of Things (AIoT) Systems, Edge AI Hardware, advanced concepts in brain-inspired computing architectures, neuromorphic computing algorithms and hardware, spiking neural networks (SNN), in-memory computing, processing-in-memory architectures, edge computing and neuromorphic computing applications.
subject: CSCE

title: Network Systems Security
description: Analysis of security threats in TCP/IP networks. Design of safeguards. Coverage of security threats at each of the OSI layers. Application of cryptographic protocols for secure communication across a network.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 515 or CSCE 516.

title: Design for Reliability
description: Design of more reliable systems through the application of reliability theory and models; reliability modeling; design techniques; testing; and requirement specifications.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: STAT 509 or STAT 511, or MATH 511.

title: Computer System Performance and Reliability Analysis
description: Evaluation of computer system performance and reliability using reliability block diagrams, fault trees, reliability graphics, queuing networks, Markov models, and Markov reward models.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: STAT 509 or STAT 511.

title: Real-Time Computer Applications
description: Problems of real-time computer applications in process control or similar areas; task scheduling; real-time operating systems; advanced interrupt structures; memory management techniques.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 245, CSCE 311.

title: Security and Privacy for Wireless Networks
description: This course focuses on the security and privacy issues associated with wireless networks. Various attacks against wireless networks and their defense strategies will be analyzed. Students are able to embark in research of wireless network security.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 416.

title: Physical Database Design
description: Components of a database management system; implementation issues; query optimization; file organization; file organizations’ transaction management; fault recovery; security; system performance.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 520.

title: Advanced Database Design
description: Database design methodologies and tools; data models; implementation languages; user interfaces.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 520.

title: Information Warfare
description: Current trends and challenges in information warfare. High-level analysis of information warfare threats, like cyber terrorism, espionage, Internet fraud, intelligence activities, cyber ethics, and law enforcement.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 522.

title: Programming Language Semantics
description: Approaches for specifying programming language semantics, including operational, axiomatic, and denotational specification.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 531.

title: Software Engineering
description: Current practices and research in software development, requirements definition, design, program testing and reliability, maintenance, and management.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 240.

title: Software Process
description: Personal, team, and organizational software processes; personal and organizational maturity; application of software process and management concepts during software development, primarily at the individual level.
subject: CSCE

title: Software Architectures
description: Structural organizations for software systems as collections of interconnected components: formal models and languages; design tools and guidelines.
subject: CSCE

title: Software Requirements
description: Elicitation, analysis, and validation of software requirements, specification of software systems including formal specification methods; CASE tools.
subject: CSCE

title: Object-Oriented Analysis and Design
description: Fundamentals of object-oriented technology; object modeling of structure, function, and time-dependent behavior; system analysis and design.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 350.

title: Object-Oriented Programming Methods
description: Object-oriented programming paradigm, including encapsulation, inheritance, reusable classes, object classification, specialization, and message passing; case studies and applications.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 245.

title: Software Testing and Quality Assurance
description: Structural and functional techniques for testing software; code inspection, peer review, test verification and validation; statistical testing methods; preventing and detecting errors; testing metrics; test plans; formal methods of testing.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 740.

title: Analysis of Algorithms
description: Algorithm design techniques; algorithms and data structures for sets and graphs; time and space complexity; sorting and searching; NP-complete problems.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 350.

title: Computability, Automata, and Formal Languages
description: Formal models of computation, including finite state automata, Turing machines, recursive functions, formal grammars, and abstract complexity theory.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 355 or CSCE 551.

title: Probabilistic System Analysis
description: Application of probability theory and stochastic processes to analyze the dynamic behavior of engineering systems.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: STAT 509 or STAT 511.

title: Numerical Analysis I
description: Numerical solution of equations and systems of linear equations, polynomial approximation, difference calculus, solution of ordinary and partial differential equations, least squares and sets of orthogonal polynomials, Gaussian quadrature.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: MATH 526 or MATH 544.

title: Numerical Analysis II
description: Continuation of CSCE 760 .
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 760.

title: Digital Image Processing
description: Concepts and techniques for digital image processing; emphasis on low-level processes that analyze discrete images at the pixel level.
subject: CSCE

title: Quantum Information
description: Fundamentals of quantum information theory and quantum communications. Topics include: Postulates of quantum mechanics, classical information and entropy, compression of classical information and classical typical sets, quantum entropy and quantum relative entropy, quantum states discrimination, Schumacher's theory of quantum compression and quantum typical subspace, communicating classical information using quantum channels, the Classical Capacity Theorem of a quantum channel.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in MATH 344 or MATH 544 or equivalent, or permission by the instructor; and C or better in MATH 511 or STAT 511 or equivalent, or permission by the instructor; knowledge of quantum mechanics is not required.

title: Computer Graphics System Design
description: Graphics data structures, graphics languages, modeling, raster displays, 3-D shading, hidden surface algorithms.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 565.

title: Scientific Visualization
description: Visualization techniques for scientific computing; interactive steering of calculations; animation and rendering techniques for multivariate data analysis.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 565.

title: Interactive Computer Systems
description: Principles for the design of systems supporting effective human-computer interaction; interaction styles; displays and interactive devices; user assistance; system design and evaluation.
subject: CSCE

title: Pattern Recognition and Classification
description: Bayesian classifiers; optimal risk schemes; error rates; numerical methods; implementation; architectures.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: STAT 509 or STAT 510 or STAT 511.

title: Computational Structural Biology
description: Theoretical concepts and algorithmic tools currently utilized in the field of protein folding such as Xplor-NIH and ROSETTA are presented. Participants are enabled to embark in research of protein folding.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: linear algebra.

title: Computer Processing of Natural Language
description: Computational models for the analysis and synthesis of natural language; representations for syntax and semantics; applications to text-to-speech conversion, speech recognition, and language understanding.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 580.

title: Computer Speech Processing
description: A/D conversion, digital filters, discrete Fourier transform and FFT, acoustics of speech, and synthesis and recognition of speech.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 580.

title: Robotics Systems
description: Design and operation of robot systems; dynamics, control, and motion trajectories of manipulators; visual, auditory, and tactile sensing systems; planning and learning.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 574.

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning
description: Theory and practical implementation of reinforcement learning methods with deep neural networks. Markov decision processes, policy iteration, value iteration, q-learning, SARSA, deep neural networks, approximate value iteration, deep q-networks, policy gradients, model-based reinforcement learning, multi-armed bandits, partial observability.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in CSCE 580 or graduate standing.

title: Knowledge Representation
description: Representation techniques and languages for symbolic knowledge, including predicate calculus, frame-based systems, and terminological systems; computer reasoning using these systems.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 580.

title: Knowledge Systems
description: Expert system domains, knowledge representation techniques, inference engines, and knowledge acquisition methods.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 580.

title: Multiagent Systems
description: Coordinated problem solving by multiple knowledge systems.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 580.

title: Neural Information Processing
description: Mathematical foundations of biological and artificial neural networks, supervised and unsupervised systems, applications.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: MATH 526 or MATH 544.

title: Quantum Computer Science
description: Fundamentals of quantum information processing, including quantum computation, quantum algorithms, quantum cryptography, and basic quantum information theory. Topics include: the quantum circuit model, alternative models, qubits, unitary operators, measurement, entanglement, quantum algorithms for factoring and search, quantum cryptographic key distribution, simulation of physical systems, error-correction and fault-tolerance, quantum channels, complexity of quantum computation, near-term implementations, quantum computer programming.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in MATH 344, MATH 544, or MATH 700, or the equivalent, or instructor permission.

title: Introduction to Fuzzy Logic
description: Principles of fuzzy set theory, fuzzy relations, and fuzzy logic; fuzzy “if-then” rules.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: MATH 174.

title: Topics in Information Technology
description: Reading and research on selected topics in information technology. Course content varies and will be announced in the schedule of courses by title. May be repeated for credit as topics vary.
subject: CSCE

title: Seminar in Advances in Computing
description: Technical writing and presentations in major computing research areas; ethics in research and writing.
subject: CSCE

title: Internship in Software Engineering
description: Work. Written report required. Not auditable.
subject: CSCE

title: Individual Study and Research
description: Individual research to be arranged with the instructor.
subject: CSCE

title: Directed Study and Research
description: Individual research to be arranged with the instructor.
subject: CSCE

title: Internet Security
description: Study security threats and prevention/detection/response techniques on the Internet, including hackers, masqueraders, information spoofing, sniffing, and distribution of damaging software. Security analysis of Web applications.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 522 and CSCE 715.

title: Distributed Systems Security
description: Security mechanisms of distributed software systems, including cryptographic applications. Secure multiparty computation, group-based cryptography, and security mechanisms for emerging distributed architectures.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 522.

title: Computer Communications
description: Contemporary computer communication protocols and network architectures.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 515 or CSCE 516.

title: Top-Down VLSI Design
description: VLSI system design automation, hardware description language-based design, multi-methodology design, and introduction to HDL support tools.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 611 or CSCE 612.

title: Custom VLSI Design
description: Custom design methodology design rules, stick notation, logic synthesis, and circuit layout; symbolic layout languages; introduction to CAD tools.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 611 or CSCE 612.

title: Distributed Database Design
description: Architecture, design, and implementation of distributed database managements systems; data fragmentation, replication, and allocation; query processing and transaction management; distributed object database management systems.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 520.

title: Data Mining and Warehousing
description: Information processing techniques and mathematical tools to assemble, access, and analyze data for decision support and knowledge discovery.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 520.

title: Secure Database Systems
description: Security threats to database systems. Access control models, multilevel security, integrity, Web-based databases, and data inference problem. Formal models of multilevel security, confidentiality versus availability and integrity.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 522.

title: Cooperative Information Systems
description: Strategies for achieving coordinated behavior among a heterogeneous group of information system components; world-wide information networks and applications in health care, logistics, telecommunications, and manufacturing automation.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: CSCE 520, CSCE 580.

title: Software Reliability and Safety
description: Reliability and safety of computer-intensive systems; software reliability models and analysis; operational profiles; hazard analysis using fault trees and event trees; formal verification of safety-critical systems.
subject: CSCE
prereq: Prerequisites: STAT 509 or STAT 510 or STAT 511.

title: Advanced Analysis of Algorithms

title: Introduction to Clinical Medicine I
description: A two-semester, three-credit-hour (DMED D601 - fall) and four-credit-hour ( DMED D602 - spring), first-year course consisting of an introduction to the medical profession and to doctor-patient and doctor-community relationships, community and preventive medicine concepts, information about the components of health care delivery systems, and basic philosophical principles underlying bioethical decision-making. The stages of normal growth and development are also discussed to ensure that students acquire a basis for exploring the life history of the patient with an awareness of issues relevant to different age periods. Information about gender and sexuality is presented, followed by a discussion of sexual disorders and dysfunctions. Students are introduced to the basic skills of medical interviewing and the mental status examination through the use of standardized patients and through participating in a clinical preceptorship. Primary methods of instruction include lecture, problem-based learning, standardized/simulated patients, and small-group discussion. Modes of assessment include multiple choice examinations, clinical preceptorship reflection papers, assessment of performance in the medical interview and the mental status examination, and by participation in small-group discussion and problem-based learning.
subject: DMED

title: Introduction to Clinical Medicine I
description: A two-semester, three-credit-hour ( DMED D601 - fall) and four-credit-hour (DMED D602 - spring), first-year course consisting of an introduction to the medical profession and to doctor-patient and doctor-community relationships, community and preventive medicine concepts, information about the components of health care delivery systems, and basic philosophical principles underlying bioethical decision-making. The stages of normal growth and development are also discussed to ensure that students acquire a basis for exploring the life history of the patient with an awareness of issues relevant to different age periods. Information about gender and sexuality is presented, followed by a discussion of sexual disorders and dysfunctions. Students are introduced to the basic skills of medical interviewing, the mental status examination, and the physical exam through the use of standardized patients and through participating in a clinical preceptorship. Primary methods of instruction include lecture, problem-based learning, standardized/simulated patients, and small-group discussion. Modes of assessment include multiple choice examinations, clinical preceptorship reflection papers, assessment of performance in the medical interview, the mental status examination, the physical exam, and by participation in small-group discussion and problem-based learning.
subject: DMED

title: Introduction to Clinical Medicine II
description: A two-semester, two-credit-hour (DMED D603 - fall) and two-credit-hour ( DMED D604 - spring), second-year course consisting of advanced physical diagnosis and the use of various models to assist in the conceptualization of psychopathological behavior, with an emphasis on the relationship of emotional factors to physical illness. Developmental problems of children, adolescents and adults, addictive problems, sleep disorders, and organic mental disorders are discussed. The course emphasizes the use of population-based data in making sound judgments regarding the clinical care of individual patients and interventions at the community level. Bioethical issues in the care of patients and techniques of prevention are presented and discussed, as are the most frequently encountered primary care clinical problems. The course includes information about frequently used radiologic and clinical laboratory studies and their value and limitations in the context of the pathophysiology of various disease states. The course utilizes an organ system approach and integrates physical diagnosis, ultrasound, and radiology with the medical pathology course. A portion of the spring semester is devoted to a series of problem-based learning exercises designed to integrate information from the Introduction to Clinical Medicine course continuum with knowledge gained from first and second-year basic science courses and to prepare students for the transition to the clinical environment in the third and fourth years. Primary methods of instruction include lecture, problem-based learning, self-instruction modules, standardized/simulated patients, and small-group discussion. Modes of assessment include written multiple choice/essay examination, assessment of participation in small-group discussion and problem-based learning, Objective Structured Clinical Examinations, and the Introduction to Clinical Diagnosis NBME subject examination. Demonstration of mastery of the following minimum clinical skills is required for successful completion of this course: performance of a clinical breast examination; demonstration of basic cardiac life support (BCLS) skills and provision of documentation of same.
subject: DMED

title: Introduction to Clinical Medicine II
description: A two-semester, two-credit-hour ( DMED D603 - fall) and two-credit-hour (DMED D604 - spring), second-year course consisting of advanced physical diagnosis and the use of various models to assist in the conceptualization of psychopathological behavior, with an emphasis on the relationship of emotional factors to physical illness. Developmental problems of children, adolescents and adults, addictive problems, sleep disorders, and organic mental disorders are discussed. The course emphasizes the use of population-based data in making sound judgments regarding the clinical care of individual patients and interventions at the community level. Bioethical issues in the care of patients and techniques of prevention are presented and discussed, as are the most frequently encountered primary care clinical problems. The course includes information about frequently used radiologic and clinical laboratory studies and their value and limitations in the context of the pathophysiology of various disease states. The course utilizes an organ system approach and integrates physical diagnosis, ultrasound, and radiology with the medical pathology course. A portion of the spring semester is devoted to a series of problem-based learning exercises designed to integrate information from the Introduction to Clinical Medicine course continuum with knowledge gained from first and second-year basic science courses and to prepare students for the transition to the clinical environment in the third and fourth years. Demonstration of mastery of the following minimum clinical skills is required for successful completion of this course: performance of a clinical breast examination; demonstration of basic cardiac life support (BCLS) skills and provision of documentation of same.
