title: Capstone: Transition to Residency
description: Capstone is a four-week program of interdepartmental and interdisciplinary material designed to prepare students professionally and personally for the transition to residency training and the practice of medicine through a series of didactic sessions and workshops.
subject: DMED

title: Clinical Informatics
description: Clinical Informatics is a rapidly evolving field in healthcare and information technology. In this course, the student will gain an understanding of the field and the role it plays in improving quality of care, patient engagement and population health. Students will explore the integration of medicine and information technologies in an experiential manner. Interactive as well as observational exposure to informaticists will allow the student to better understand a wide variety of clinical informatics topics to include but not limited to: Clinical decision support, Patient engagement, Interoperability, Physician efficiency, Telemedicine and Quality of care.
subject: DMED

title: QUEST: Quality Improvement Education and Systems Training
description: QUEST is experiential education in quality improvement, a chance to apply principles learned in the classroom in real life with existing healthcare teams. The program is sponsored by the University of South Carolina School of Medicine in partnership with faculty mentors with Prisma Health and McLeod Health. Prior to starting QUEST, students are required to complete the basic certificate in quality and patient safety from the Institute for Healthcare Improvement Open School, a series of thirteen online modules. QUEST begins in the fall with a series of workshops led by system leaders and QI experts. The workshops include interactive sessions to deepen fundamentals of QI and explore applications in our local learning environment. Following the workshops, pairs of students are connected to mentors and QI teams in part based on their areas of interest. Students are generally paired with a learner from a different health profession.
subject: DMED

title: USMLE Step 2 Preparation
description: This course has been specially designed to allow students protected time for USMLE Step 2 preparation. Students are expected to devise and implement their own study plans. A passing score on the first attempt is required in order to receive credit for the course. If a passing score is not achieved on the first attempt no credit will be given for the block.
subject: DMED

title: Ind Study Ultrasound
description: The main objective of this Independent Study is to further develop ultrasound skills. The M4 students will be participating in teaching and supervising M1 and M2 medical students in the scheduled ultrasound labs. There may also be opportunities to assist with ongoing ultrasound related research projects. Students are expected to complete a series of self-directed learning modules. There is a requirement to present an ultrasound topic of the student’s choice towards the end of the rotation.
subject: DMED

title: Medical Education: Learning From Teaching
description: Students must be in good academic standing. Prior to registration, students must meet with the instructor to identify specific teaching/coaching opportunities in which they will be able to participate. During the rotation, M4 students will serve as coaches for M1 and M2 students who are facing academic challenges in a specific course or block or who need targeted assistance to prepare for CBSE and Step 1. Students will receive training as academic coaches related to their teaching responsibilities (five hours). Students will work with instructors to select a minimum of eight (8) teaching contact hours in which to participate. Hours may be 1:1 sessions or small group sessions. Additional hours may be assigned in collaboration with the course director. Grading: P/F
subject: DMED

title: Medical Legal Partnership Elective
description: The medical legal partnership elective involves students working with second and third year law students and graduate students in social work on cases referred to the Law School’s Medical Legal Partnership Clinic from the Children’s Hospital Outpatient and Special Care Centers. The students will work with the law and social work students to identify the social determinants of health, particularly the legal issues affecting health, and will advocate for changes.
subject: DMED

title: Culinary Medicine
description: Each 3 hour session is designed to teach fourth year medical students how to prepare nutritious and affordable meals and to effectively prescribe healthier foods and lifestyles to their patients. Students will spend 30 minutes role playing and discussing patient scenarios that are connected to diet-related diseases. The next 90 minutes will be spent in teams preparing various recipes and 60 minutes reviewing nutritional content of recipes as well as sharing the meal. The curriculum is based on the Mediterranean diet and was designed by Tulane Medical University to be affordable in order to reach lower-income populations which have been particularly susceptible to diet-related chronic disease.
subject: DMED

title: Application of Clinical Evidence I (ACE)
description: ACE will help students to develop the skill set to draw from the best evidence for patient care, to critically consider the literature and begin the process of application of the literature to care decisions at the individual and population levels.
subject: DMED

title: Application of Clinical Evidence II (ACE)
description: ACE will help students to develop the skill set to draw from the best evidence for patient care, to critically consider the literature and begin the process of application of the literature to care decisions at the individual and population levels. Weekly case studies will combine physiology, pathology, microbiology, immunology and pharmacology concepts.
subject: DMED

title: General Principles
description: This course is designed to give students a working knowledge of the general principles of pathology, microbiology, immunology, and pharmacology before they apply that knowledge to the individual organ systems. The course will include an understanding of the basic mechanisms of diseases, the body’s response to these diseases, fundamental aspects of microbiology and immunology as they relate to bacteria, viruses, fungi and parasites and the principles of drug action including pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics.
subject: DMED

title: Cardiovascular
description: This course presents the most common pathological conditions, clinical presentations (signs and symptoms) and diagnostic methods involving the cardiovascular system, with an emphasis on how pathological conditions affect the cardiovascular system and renal system. Treatments for the pathological conditions will also be presented. The goals of the course are for students to learn the pathophysiology, pathology, infectious diseases, clinical presentations of diseases, and pharmacological and other treatments of diseases of the cardiovascular system and renal system.
subject: DMED

title: Renal
description: This course presents the most common pathological conditions, clinical presentations (signs and symptoms) and diagnostic methods involving the renal system, with an emphasis on how pathological conditions affect the renal system. Treatments for the pathological conditions will also be presented. The goals of the course are for students to learn the pathophysiology, pathology, infectious diseases, clinical presentations of diseases, and pharmacological and other treatments of diseases of the renal system.
subject: DMED

title: Pulmonary
description: This course presents the most common pathological conditions, clinical presentations (signs and symptoms) and diagnostic methods involving the renal system, with an emphasis on how pathological conditions affect the pulmonary system. Treatments for the pathological conditions will also be presented. The goals of the course are for students to learn the pathophysiology, pathology, infectious diseases, clinical presentations of diseases, and pharmacological and other treatments of diseases of the pulmonary system.
subject: DMED

Treatments for the pathological conditions will also be presented. The goals of the course are for students to learn the pathophysiology, pathology, infectious diseases, clinical presentations of diseases, and pharmacological and other treatments of diseases of the hematologic system.

The goals of the course are for students to learn the pathophysiology, pathology, infectious diseases, clinical presentations of diseases, and pharmacological and other treatments of diseases of the MS system.

description: This course presents the most common pathological conditions, clinical presentations (signs and symptoms) and diagnostic methods involving the gastrointestinal system, with an emphasis on how pathological conditions affect the gastrointestinal system. Treatments for the pathological conditions will also be presented. The goals of the course are for students to learn the pathophysiology, pathology, infectious diseases, clinical presentations of diseases, and pharmacological and other treatments of diseases of the GI system.
subject: DMED

description: This course presents the most common pathological conditions, clinical presentations (signs and symptoms) and diagnostic methods involving the endocrine/reproductive system, with an emphasis on how pathological conditions affect the endocrine/reproductive system. Treatments for the pathological conditions will also be presented. The goals of the course are for students to learn the pathophysiology, pathology, infectious diseases, clinical presentations of diseases, and pharmacological and other treatments of diseases of the endocrine/reproductive systems.
subject: DMED

title: Neuroscience
description: This course presents the most common pathological conditions, clinical presentations (signs and symptoms) and diagnostic methods involving the central and peripheral nervous systems including psychopathology and neurologic disorders, with an emphasis on how pathologic conditions affect the central and peripheral nervous systems. Treatments for the pathological conditions will also be presented. The goals of the course are for students to learn the, pathophysiology, pathology, infectious diseases, clinical presentations of diseases, and pharmacological and other treatments of diseases of the central and peripheral nervous systems.

title: Comparative Corporate Governance
description: The course provides a theoretical framework for understanding the historical, sociological, political, economic and cultural aspects of each IMBA residency region within the context of globalization. In order to do this, this course introduces you to comparative corporate governance and examines how different players inside and outside the firm work to shape leadership and strategy across various capitalist systems in different regions that are covered in this course. Within the broader context of globalization, liberalization and deregulation, all organizations – from private to public to non-governmental – are currently undergoing significant changes. One of the major dimensions of these changes pertains to the way organizations in general and firms in particular are “governed” and relate to their shareholders. Finally, this course analyses how governance at firm and country level is a core element of competitive advantage for any firm, and how important it is for firm to deploy the most effective governance practice. Through class discussion and case assignment, participants on the International MBA will be encouraged to reflect upon their own futures as executives, board members, and owners as well as on their learning experience from being immersed in their IMBA residency region.
subject: DMSB

title: Introduction to Chemical Engineering

subject: ECHE

subject: ECHE

subject: ECHE

title: Chemical Process Principles

subject: ECHE

title: Introductory Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics
description: First law and second law of thermodynamics. Thermodynamic properties of single component systems. Analysis of power and refrigeration cycles.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ECHE 300.

title: Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics
description: Mass, energy, and entropy balance analysis of chemical engineering systems; evaluation of thermodynamic property changes of pure materials; solution thermodynamics of single-phase multicomponent systems; phase and chemical reaction equilibrium.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ECHE 310, ENCP 290, CHEM 541, BMEN 290, EMCH 290 or PHYS 306.

title: Chemical Engineering Fluid Mechanics
description: Fluid statics and dynamics with emphasis on chemical engineering applications.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in PHYS 211.

title: Heat-Flow Analysis
description: Theory of heat transmission; mechanism, generation, distribution, and measurement; use of theory in practical equipment design.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ECHE 320 or ENCP 360; C or better in MATH 242.

title: Mass Transfer
description: Molecular diffusion in fluids; diffusion in laminar and turbulent flow; momentum, transport analogies; interfacial mass transfer; design applications including humidification and absorption.
subject: ECHE

title: Introduction to Materials
description: Overview of the fundamental chemical aspects of materials; role of materials in applications in modern society by case studies of advances in new materials and processes.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: CHEM 112.

title: Special Topics in Chemical Engineering
description: Course content varies and will be announced in the schedule of classes by title. May be repeated as topic varies.
subject: ECHE

title: Chemical Engineering Kinetics
description: Concepts of chemical kinetics, batch and flow reactors, catalysts and reactor design.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ECHE 311.

title: Separation Process Design
description: Design of stagewise chemical separation cascades; analysis of binary and ternary systems; multicomponent separations, plate and column specification procedures; distillation, crystallization, extraction, and leaching.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ECHE 300.

title: Adsorption Fundamentals and Processes
description: Basic principles of adsorption and adsorption processes including adsorbents, thermodynamics, kinetics, fixed bed adsorption and cyclic adsorption processes.
subject: ECHE

title: Computational Methods for Engineering Applications
description: Introduction to advanced computational tools for the analysis of chemical engineering systems. Initial and boundary value problems related to heat and mass transfer, reaction engineering, and parameter estimation.
subject: ECHE

title: Chemical Engineering Laboratory I

subject: ECHE

title: Chemical Engineering Laboratory II
description: Continuation of ECHE 460 ; topics in mass transfer, kinetics, and process control.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECHE 460.

title: Chemical-Process Analysis and Design I
description: Economics of chemical engineering projects related to typical corporate goals and objectives; process-flowsheet development techniques; review of shortcut design techniques; selection of profitability criteria.
subject: ECHE

title: Chemical-Process Analysis and Design II
description: Continuation of ECHE 465 ; computer-aided design of chemical processes; written and oral presentation of a comprehensive design project.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECHE 430, ECHE 440, ECHE 465.

title: Thesis Preparation
description: Completion of the thesis requirements for the departmental undergraduate research track. A maximum of three credits may be applied toward a degree.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: Three credit hours of ECHE 499, acceptance into the departmental undergraduate research track, and consent of instructor.

title: Topics in Chemical Engineering
description: Reading and research on selected topics in chemical engineering. Course content varies and will be announced in the schedule of classes by title. May be repeated two times as topics vary. Pass-Fail grading.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: upper division standing.

title: Special Problems
description: Individual investigation or studies of special topics. A maximum of six credits may be applied toward a degree. Advance approval of project proposal by advisor and instructor.
subject: ECHE

title: Chemical Engineering Fluid Mechanics
description: Multi-phase pressure drop, phase contacting, flow through porous media, fluidization, mixing, and turbulence.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECHE 320 or ENCP 360.

title: Computational Fluid Dynamics for Engineering Applications
description: Introduction to the use of computational fluid dynamics codes to analyze flow, heat, and mass transfer problems of practical engineering applications.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECHE 320, EMCH 360, ECIV 360, ENCP 360, or AESP 265.

title: Intermediate Chemical Engineering Kinetics
description: Intermediate concepts of chemical kinetics, batch and flow reactors, catalysts and reactor design, including non-ideal systems.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ECHE 311.

title: Intermediate Separation Process Design
description: Intermediate level design of stagewise chemical separation cascades; analysis of binary and ternary systems; multicomponent separations, plate and column specification procedures; distillation, crystallization, extraction, and leaching.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ECHE 300.

title: Chemical-Process Dynamics and Control
description: Fundamental physical and chemical principles in mathematically modeling the dynamic response of chemical processes; feedforward and feedback control systems; design of control schemes for selected chemical processes.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ECHE 300 and MATH 242, or graduate standing.

title: Process Safety, Health and Loss Prevention
description: Reliability, availability, and fault-tree analyses, risk indices, hazard evaluation, vapor cloud modeling, toxicology, material safety classification and regulations, individual/corporate ethical responsibilities.
subject: ECHE

title: Corrosion Engineering
description: Basic principles of corrosion engineering developed from a chemical engineering approach to thermodynamics, kinetics, mass transfer, and potential theory.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECHE 311.

title: Polymer Processing
description: Industrial polymers with emphasis on their characterization and on the modeling of the major polymer fabrication processes.
subject: ECHE

title: Next Energy
description: An examination of energy technologies that will enable society to move from an economy based on fossil fuels to one based on sustainable energy.
subject: ECHE

title: Combustion
description: Fundamental process and applications related to the broad field of combustion and energy generation including emissions control technologies.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECHE 430.

title: Engineering of Soft Materials
description: Introductory overview of fundamental concepts in science and engineering of soft materials; the relation between microstructure and macroscopic behavior in a variety of soft matter systems; key applications in chemical and biomedical engineering.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in MATH 122 or MATH 141.

title: Introduction to Battery Systems
description: A concise introductory guide to modern battery technology; overview of the electrochemical principles of batteries; operational factors of battery systems; materials for modern and emerging battery technologies.
subject: ECHE

title: Special Advanced Topics in Chemical Engineering
description: Course content varies and will be announced in the schedule of classes by title. May be repeated as topic varies.
subject: ECHE

title: Chemical Process Analysis
description: Quantitative analysis of industrial chemical operations. Equilibrium relations, material and energy balances, and reaction kinetics principles are used to analyze a variety of chemical processes and systems.
subject: ECHE

title: Advanced Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics
description: Mass, energy, and entropy balance analysis of complex systems; evaluation of thermodynamic property changes of pure materials; solution thermodynamics of single-phase multicomponent systems; phase and chemical reaction equilibrium.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: ECHE 311.

title: Selected Topics in Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics
description: Special topics in chemical engineering thermodynamics with emphasis on current research.
subject: ECHE

title: Advanced Fluid Flow Analysis
description: Theory and application of fluid flow phenomena; momentum equations, conformal mapping, empirical methods, boundary layers, dimensional analysis.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: ENCP 360 and MATH 242.

title: Advanced Heat Flow Analysis
description: Theory and application of heat flow phenomena; classical techniques and finite-difference numerical methods; conduction, convection, radiation, boiling.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: ECHE 321 and ECHE 720.

title: Advanced Mass Transfer
description: Diffusive and convective mass transfer. Applications of the Stefan-Maxwell equations, prediction of diffusion coefficients, convective mass transport, correlations for mass transfer coefficients, and combined mass transfer and reaction modeling.
subject: ECHE

title: Advanced Computational Fluid Dynamics for Engineering Applications
description: Use of computational fluid dynamics codes to analyze flow, heat, and mass transfer problems of practical engineering applications.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECHE 720 or permission of instructor.

title: Rheology
description: Rheological characteristics of viscous, elastic, viscoelastic, and plastic substances; non-Newtonian fluid flow, viscometry, and rheogoniometry; rheological equations of state; engineering applications.
subject: ECHE

title: Selected Topics in Fluid Mechanics
description: Special topics in fluid mechanics with emphasis on current research.
subject: ECHE

title: Selected Topics in Heat and Mass Transfer
description: Special topics in heat and mass transfer with emphasis on current research.
subject: ECHE

title: Chemical Reator Design
description: Optimum temperature sequencing. Modeling of non-ideal reactors. Theories of catalysis with emphasis on the rate of diffusion. Interpretation of experimental catalytic data and use of these data in reactor design.
subject: ECHE

title: Heterogeneous Catalysis - Fundamentals
description: Fundamentals of heterogeneous catalysis, with emphasis on computational catalysis.
subject: ECHE

title: Heterogeneous Catalysis – Synthesis, Characterization and Evaluation
description: Catalyst synthesis methods; experimental characterization approaches; correlating synthesis/characterization with catalytic performance.
subject: ECHE

title: Industrial Catalysis
description: History of catalysis; industrial applications of catalysis; development of catalytic reactions from lab to pilot to production scale.
subject: ECHE

title: Selected Topics in Kinetics and Reactor Design
description: Special topics in kinetics and reactor design with emphasis on current research.
subject: ECHE

title: Adsorption Fundamentals and Processes
description: Advanced principles of adsorption and adsorption processes including adsorbents, thermodynamics, kinetics, fixed bed adsorption and cyclic adsorption processes.
subject: ECHE

title: Selected Topics in Separations
description: Special topics in separations with emphasis on current research.
subject: ECHE

title: Process Dynamics and Control
description: Advanced topics in chemical process dynamics and control. Multivariate analysis, system identification, sampling, optimal process control.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: ECHE 550.

title: Selected Topics in Process Control
description: Special topics in process control with emphasis on current research.
subject: ECHE

title: Introduction to Fusion Energy and Fusion Materials Science
description: Fundamentals of plasma confinement and fusion reaction mechanisms. Comparative study of magnetic and inertial fusion systems. Materials challenges under extreme temperature, radiation, and particle flux. Evaluation of candidate structural and plasma-facing materials for next-generation fusion reactors.
subject: ECHE

title: Fusion Fuel Cycle and Tritium
description: Fusion fuel cycle fundamentals and tritium breeding strategies. Isotope separation and recovery processes. Tritium handling, containment, and safety technologies. Integration of fuel cycle systems with reactor operation and materials performance.
subject: ECHE

title: Fusion Blanket Technology
description: Principles of fusion breeding blanket design. Neutronics, thermal-hydraulics, and tritium breeding performance. Materials selection for structural and functional components. Integration of blanket systems with reactor operation and safety requirements.
subject: ECHE

title: Fuel Cycle and Blanket Modeling and Control
description: Modeling and simulation of fusion fuel cycle processes and breeding blanket systems. Control strategies for isotope recovery, tritium inventory, and thermal management. Coupling of neutronics, materials behavior, and systems engineering. Computational methods for performance optimization and safety analysis.
subject: ECHE

title: Fuel Cycle Analytical Technologies and Tritium Accountancy
description: Analytical techniques for tritium detection, measurement, and accountancy. Monitoring of fuel cycle processes and inventories. Methods for isotopic analysis, leak detection, and environmental monitoring. Application of analytical data to fuel cycle control and regulatory compliance.
subject: ECHE

title: Tritium Safety, Regulation, Supply, and Nonproliferation
description: Safety principles for tritium handling and storage. Regulatory frameworks governing tritium use and transport. Global tritium supply, demand, and resource management. Nonproliferation concerns and safeguards in fusion fuel cycles.
subject: ECHE

title: Selected Topics in Chemical Engineering Design
description: Special topics in chemical engineering design with emphasis on current research.
subject: ECHE

title: Electrochemical Engineering
description: Electrochemical engineering principles developed from thermodynamic, kinetic, mass transfer, and potential theory. Numerical analysis and design of electrochemical systems. Statistical analysis of experimental data and industrial experimental designs.
subject: ECHE

title: Corrosion Engineering
description: Corrosion engineering principles developed from thermodynamic, kinetic, mass transfer, and potential theory. Numerical analysis of corroding systems, statistical analysis of experimental data, and industrial experimental designs.
subject: ECHE

title: Principles of Polymer Systems
description: Theory and applications of polymer systems. Structure, physical properties, rheological, and mechanical behavior of polymers. Polymerization reactions and industrial polymerization processes. Fabrication techniques.
subject: ECHE

title: Selected Topics in Chemical Engineering
description: Approved for special topic offerings.
subject: ECHE

title: Graduate Seminar in Chemical Engineering
description: Seminar on current topics in chemical engineering. Includes oral presentations by students on research projects.
subject: ECHE

title: Thesis Preparation
description: To be arranged by candidates for the master’s degree with the thesis advisor.
subject: ECHE

title: Chemcial Process Safety and Loss Prevention
description: Chemical process quantitative risk analysis, consequence modeling, risk estimation, and hazards assessment; design principles including inherent safety and mitigation techniques; elements of process safety management.
subject: ECHE
prereq: Prerequisites: ECHE 720.

title: Introduction to Civil Engineering
description: Fundamental concepts in each of the disciplines of civil engineering are discussed. Critical thinking skills are formally fostered by hands-on experiences and group discussions.
subject: ECIV

title: Introduction to Engineering Graphics and Visualization
description: Principles and practice of visualization and graphical representation using modern computer-aided design tools.
subject: ECIV

title: Statics
description: Fundamentals of engineering mechanics. Equilibrium of particles and rigid bodies. Free-body diagrams, analysis trusses and frames. Distributed forces, centroids, centers of gravity, and friction.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in MATH 141.

title: Computational Methods for Civil Engineering
description: The use of computational tools and techniques for solving civil and environmental engineering problems. Overview of numerical methods including roots of equations, systems of linear equations, interpolation, and integration. Analysis of civil and environmental systems.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in MATH 142 and ECIV 200 or ENCP 200.

title: Dynamics
description: Kinematics of particles and rigid bodies. Vector representation of force and motion. Free-body diagrams, application of energy and momentum methods to solve problems. Rigid body and central force motion.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ECIV 200 or ENCP 200; C or better in MATH 142.

title: Mechanics of Solids
description: Concepts of stress and strain; stress analysis of basic structural members. Vectors, free bodies, equilibrium and elastic behavior. Combined stress, Mohr’s circle. Beams, columns, torsion, and rotation.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ECIV 200 or ENCP 200 and in MATH 142.

title: Introduction to Construction Engineering
description: Broad exposure to how construction projects are planned and executed including the use of construction equipment, site planning, quality control, and construction safety.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 101 and C or better in MATH 141.

title: Fundamentals of Civil Engineering Design
description: Introduces students to civil engineering design through projects that address community needs.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in MATH 141.

title: Civil Engineering Measurements
description: Theory and application of plane surveying and mapping techniques. Lecture plus laboratory.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in MATH 241.

title: Civil Engineering Materials
description: Mechanical and thermal properties of mineral aggregates, cements, concrete, timber, asphalt, metals, and plastics.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ECIV 220 or ENCP 260.

title: Civil Engineering Materials Laboratory
description: Experiments, exercises, and demonstrations to accompany ECIV 303 .
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in STAT 509 or STAT 511.

title: Professional Development for Civil Engineers
description: Regulatory permits and scheduling of construction projects. Engineering responsibility and liabilities. Reporting of engineering designs and analysis. Cost estimation of engineering projects including present or future net value.
subject: ECIV

title: Structural Analysis I
description: Equilibrium, shear and moment diagrams, and influence lines for statically determinate trusses, beams, and frames. Energy principles and other methods for displacement calculations. Introduction to indeterminate structural analysis.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in MATH 242; C or better in ECIV 220 or ENCP 260.

title: Structural Steel Design
description: Behavior and design of steel beams, columns, and tension members; strength and stability; design of connections using welded, bolted and riveted construction.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ECIV 320.

title: Reinforced Concrete Design
description: Behavior and design of reinforced concrete beams, columns, continuous beams and one way slabs, and footings.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ECIV 320.

title: Introduction to Geotechnical Engineering
description: Engineering properties of soil and rock; hydraulic conductivity, flow nets, drainage design; consolidation theory, shearing strength of soil.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in either ECIV 220 or ENCP 260.

title: Geotechnical Laboratory
description: Laboratory associated with ECIV 330 . Soil mechanics experiments, exercises, and demonstrations. Three contact hours per week.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in STAT 509 or STAT 511.

title: Introduction to Transportation Engineering
description: Transportation design, planning, and operational analysis, including roadway, airway, and railway systems; transportation elements, including traveled way, vehicle, control, terminals, and advanced technology; traffic data collection, interpretation, and analysis.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 201, ENCP 201, or ECIV 270; D or better in STAT 509 or STAT 511.

title: Transportation Engineering Laboratory
description: Principles of distances, elevations and angles that pertain to roadways, basic theories in engineering measurements and surveying calculations, and an introduction to mapping, for transportation engineering applications. Three contact hours per week.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in STAT 509 or STAT 511.

title: Introduction to Environmental Engineering
description: Concepts of environmental engineering, including air and water pollution, solid and hazardous waste disposal, and noise pollution. Qualitative and quantitative development of engineering techniques for pollution control.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in CHEM 111 or CHEM 141; C or better in Math 142

title: Introduction to Environmental Engineering Laboratory
description: Physical, chemical, and biological analysis of water and wastewater. Three laboratory hours per week.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in STAT 509 or STAT 511.

title: Fluid Mechanics
description: Principles of fluid statics and dynamics. Conservation of mass, momentum, and energy. Similitude and dimensional analysis, open channel flow, lift and drag forces, and introduction to turbulent flow.
subject: ECIV

title: Introduction to Water Resources Engineering
description: Application of fluid mechanic principles to water resources engineering problems; pipe systems, pumps, open channel flow, peak runoff, seepage, hydraulic structures.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in either ECIV 360 or ENCP 360.

title: Introduction to Water Resources Engineering Laboratory
description: Experiments, exercises, and demonstrations on flow in pipes and open channels, pumps, flow measurement, seepage, and infiltration. Three contact hours per week.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in STAT 509 or 511.

title: System Applications in Civil Engineering
description: Systems approach to analysis and design; application of engineering economic principles to the evaluation of design alternatives; deterministic modeling and optimization emphasizing civil engineering applications.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in MATH 142.

title: Structural Design
description: Design of steel structures including elastic and plastic design concepts. Design of concrete structures including continuous members and long columns.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 325 or ECIV 327.

title: Civil Engineering Design
description: Application of hydraulic, geotechnical, and structural principles in design; project scheduling; cost estimation; ethics; environmental and social impact; design drawings; report documents.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 270 and ECIV 307; D or better in two ECIV Distribution Courses.

title: Special Topics
description: Course content varies and will be announced in the schedule of classes by course title. May be repeated as topic varies. A maximum of twelve credits may be applied towards a degree.
subject: ECIV

title: Fundamentals of Engineering Preparation
description: Preparation for the Fundamentals of Engineering Exam. Will cover general engineering and civil engineering specific areas. Restricted to Civil Engineering Seniors. Pass/ Fail Grading.
subject: ECIV

title: Independent Study in Civil and Environmental Engineering
description: Individual investigation or studies of special topics. A maximum of six credits may be applied toward a degree.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: approval of project proposal by instructor; USC/GPA 2.8.

title: Life Cycle Assessment of Engineered Systems
subject: ECIV

title: Structural Modeling and Experimental Methods
description: Introduction of structural modeling; strain gauge instrumentation; force, displacement, acceleration, pressure, temperature measurements; concrete and steel modeling; size effects; analysis of experimental data.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 327.

title: Structural Analysis II
description: Advanced methods of structural analysis with emphasis on matrix methods. Development of the generalized matrix force and matrix displacement methods of static analysis, with applications to trusses and frames.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 320.

title: Numerical Methods in Mechanics
description: Numerical modeling of typical engineering problems. Numerical solution of linear and nonlinear, boundary and initial value problems. Introduction to optimization.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in MATH 242, or departmental approval.

title: Structural Vibrations
description: Response of single- and multiple-degree of freedom structurally dynamic systems to impact, harmonic, wind, and seismic excitations.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 320.

title: Timber and Masonry Design
description: Basic engineering properties of timber and masonry materials, design methods and philosophies for timber and masonry structures. Particular attention is paid to current codes, specifications and analysis.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ECIV 320.

title: Foundation Analysis and Design
description: Subsurface investigation procedures. Theoretical and practical aspects of shallow and deep foundation design.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 330 or equivalent.

title: Design of Earth Structures
description: Geotechnical engineering problems associated with the behavior of earth masses. Soil shear strength, lateral earth pressure, design of retaining structures, slope stability, water flow through soils.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 330.

title: Geosynthetics and Geotechnical Design of Landfills
description: Principles for the design, construction, and performance of waste containment systems. Characterization of barrier materials; geosynthetics; design of liner and leachate collection systems; stability and deformation analyses of landfills.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 330.

title: Pavement Analysis and Design
description: Design and analysis of flexible and rigid pavements. Pavement distress evaluation, subgrade and base course behavior, stress analysis, traffic prediction.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 330.

title: Experimental Methods in Geotechnical Engineering
description: Overview of transducers, signal conditioning and data acquisition; test control methods, data analysis and measurement errors; testing systems to measure soil strength, stiffness, and hydraulic conductivity; laboratory projects and examinations.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 330, D or better in ECIV 330L.

title: Transportation Systems Planning
description: Fundamental interactions between supply and demand in transportation systems. Modeling transportation demand and trip-making behavior. Evaluation of alternatives for decision making.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 340.

title: Highway Design
description: Design of transportation facilities using relevant tools and guidelines with emphasis on physical and operational aspects of arterials, freeways, intersections, and interchanges, including geometry, capacity, control, and safety.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 111 or ENCP 102 and D or better in ECIV 340.

title: Traffic Engineering
description: Capacity analysis of freeways and arterials. Traffic flow characteristics and basic relationships among traffic flow parameters. Signalized and unsignalized intersection control and signal timing design.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 340.

title: Traffic Safety Analysis
description: Research concepts and methodologies to enable students to identify the underlying reasons and factors that contribute to traffic crashes and determine appropriate countermeasures.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 340.

title: Elements of Water and Wastewater Treatment
description: Unit operations and processes employed in the physical, chemical, and biological treatment of water and wastewater. Design of water and wastewater treatment systems.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 350.

title: Principles of Municipal Solid Waste Engineering
description: Fundamentals and engineering principles of solid waste generation, characterization, collection and transport, source reduction and recycling, and physical, chemical, and biological treatment strategies.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 350.

title: Air Pollution Control Engineering
description: Introduction to the sources of air pollution and the engineering principles used for control and prevention.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 350.

title: Sustainable Construction for Engineers
description: Instruction to sustainable engineering design alternatives and principles for construction and site development from preconstruction through design and the construction phase.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 350 and ECIV 570.

title: Environmental Engineering Process Modeling
description: Modeling fate and transport phenomena in environmental processes with applications in engineered unit operators and natural systems.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better ECIV 350 and MATH 242.

title: Open Channel Hydraulics
description: Steady and unsteady flows in single or multiple-channel systems.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 360 or ENCP 360.

title: Engineering Hydrology
description: Applications of hydrologic techniques to design problems; stormwater simulation models; urban stormwater.
subject: ECIV

title: Subsurface Hydrology
description: Hydrologic cycle, subsurface physical properties, equations of groundwater flow, well flow, well design, groundwater resource development, design of dewatering systems, groundwater contamination.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 362 or departmental approval.

title: Land Development for Engineers
description: Fundamentals of designing and permitting the conversion of land to new or altered states, including environmental issues, traffic and parking, utility resources, site engineering, ADA, safety, planning, and zoning requirements.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in, Three from ECIV 320, ECIV 330, ECIV 340, ECIV 350, and ECIV 362.

title: Railway Engineering I
description: Introduction to the analysis and design of the railway infrastructure for freight and passenger systems to include track and track support systems, grade crossings, special trackwork, construction, inspection, assessment and compliance.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 320, ECIV 330, and ECIV 340.

title: Operation and Logistics of Railway Systems
description: Principles of rail operations; Network management; Best practices for train planning, performance management and delivery of service; technical elements of a railway from an operations perspective (train controls, signaling, communications, yards, tractive power etc).
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 340.

title: Design of Railway Bridges and Structures
description: Introduction to railway infrastructure; Structural design considerations and criteria of railway structures; Bridge types and components; Planning and preliminary design of modern railway bridges; Loads and forces; Structural analysis and design of steel railway bridges and components.
subject: ECIV

title: Intermediate Special Topics
description: The content of this course varies, and the topics are selected by the faculty. The aim of this course is to expose upper-level undergraduate students and graduate students to a contemporary issue, not covered in any Civil and Environmental Engineering course. Possible topics include intelligent infrastructure, sustainable construction, and monitoring and improvement of poor and degrading infrastructure.
subject: ECIV

title: Deterministic Civil and Environmental Systems Engineering
description: Planning, design, and operation of large-scale, integrated civil and environmental engineering systems, with applications of mathematical programming and other search models.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 405.

title: Probabilistic Civil and Environmental Systems Engineering
description: Civil and environmental systems engineering under uncertainty, including decision rules, decision theory, uncertainty propagation, stochastic programming, and conservative design.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in STAT 509.

title: Management of Engineering Projects
description: This course focuses in studying the life-cycle of a project using a systems engineering approach. Industry standards for engineering companies as well as practical considerations are studies through the semester.
subject: ECIV

title: Risk Analysis for Engineering Applications
description: Risk analysis is presented in the context of reliability in design including applications to mechanical and electrical systems with discussion of failure modes and life cycle costs.
subject: ECIV

title: Boundary Element Methods in Engineering
description: Introduction to boundary element methods and their computer implementation. Steady-state and transient solutions of two- and three-dimensional problems of elasticity and potential flow.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: ENCP 260, MATH 242.

title: Advanced Structural Mechanics and Analysis
description: Development of concepts and practical applications of the finite element method of structural analysis with emphasis on the displacement method approach. Initial strains, specified displacements, numerical integration, and isoparametric elements are included.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 520.

title: Theory and Design of Plates and Shells
description: Development of the fundamental differential equations for plates. Miscellaneous classic plate solutions. Membrane and bending solutions for shells of revolution, circular cylindrical shells, hyperbolic paraboloid shells and circular cylindrical barrel shells.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in MATH 242.

title: Advanced Analysis and Design in Structural Metals
description: Analysis and behavior of metal structural components under general loading combinations. Buckling phenomena of thin-walled open sections in the elastic and inelastic regions, and correlation with design code criteria. Behavior and design of plate girders.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 325.

title: Repair and Retrofit of Structures
description: Analysis and modeling existing and repaired structures. Selection, modeling, and design of repair and/or retrofit measures.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 520.

title: Advanced Analysis and Design of Reinforced Concrete
description: Design of multistory structures, two-way slabs, joints in buildings, pavement design, and miscellaneous topics.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 327.

title: Prestressed Concrete Analysis and Design
description: Pre-stressing methods and materials; flexural analysis, shear and torsion, design of simple, composite and continuous beams. Deflections, slab design, and study of axially loaded members.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 327.

title: Advanced Soil Mechanics
description: Course covers the mechanical properties of soil; analysis of the field and laboratory tests to determine soil properties required for foundation analysis and design; consolidation theory; and settlement analysis.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 530.

title: Slope Stabilty, Retaining Systems and Lateral Earth Pressure
description: Shear strength of soil and rock under effective stress. Slope stability analysis, tieback and reinforced earth systems. Computation of earth pressures for excavations and tunnels. Dewatering for construction.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 530.

title: Theoretical and Numerical Methods in Geomechanics
description: Constitutive models and their numerical implementation. Elastic and plastic approaches to analysis. Finite element applications to geomechanics problems. Layer analysis, arching, and stability case studies.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 530.

title: Physico-chemical Properties of Soils
description: Soil mineralogy, chemistry, and clay-water systems. Impact of soil fabric and microstructure on engineering behavior. Soil-contaminant interaction. Barriers and liners for containment.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 530.

title: Soil Dynamics and Geotechnical Earthquake Engineering
description: Properties and behaviors of soils under cyclic and dynamic loading. Seismic hazard assessment. Dynamic response analysis. Soil liquefaction.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 530 or ECIV 531.

title: Ground Improvement Techniques
description: Application of soil mechanics principles to improving the engineering characteristics of soil and rock. Topics include mechanisms of soil densification, preconsolidation, grouting, ground freezing, reinforced earth, and soil nailing.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 530.

title: Advanced Foundation Design
description: Application of soil mechanics to design and analysis of foundations. Shallow foundations, bearing capacity, settlement. Deep foundations, axial and lateral loading, wave equation analysis, drilled shafts. Design and construction issues.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 530.

title: Intermodal Freight Transport
description: Marine container terminal design and operations, rail-yard design and operations, cross-dock terminal design and operations, drayage routing and scheduling, and network design. Application of operations research techniques to intermodal transportation.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 705.

title: Discrete Choice Analysis of Travel Demand
description: Individual choice theory; binary choice models; unordered multinomial and multi-dimensional choice models; sampling theory and sample design; ordered multinomial models, aggregate prediction with choice models; joint stated preference and revealed preference modeling, and longitudinal choice analysis; review of state-of-the-art and future directions.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in STAT 509.

title: Flows in Transportation Networks
description: Design, operation, and management of traffic flows over complex transportation networks. Covers two major topics: traffic flow modeling and traffic flow operations. Includes deterministic and probabilistic models, elements of queueing theory, and traffic assignment. Concepts and methods are illustrated through various applications and examples.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 706.

title: Traffic Flow Theory
description: Survey of statistical techniques useful in traffic engineering. Deterministic and stochastic models of traffic flow. Operations research techniques. Simulation by digital computer. Applications to highway problems.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 541, D or better in STAT 509.

title: Principles of Environmental Engineering Process
description: Basic physical, chemical, and biological processes applied to aqueous systems.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in CHEM 112, C or better in MATH 142.

title: Water and Wastewater Treatment Theory I
description: Physical and chemical water and wastewater treatment processes. Topics include mixing, coagulation, sedimentation, filtration, oxidation, absorption, and ion exchange.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 750.

title: Water and Wastewater Treatment Theory II
description: Biological water and wastewater treatment process. Topics include activated sludge, biofilms, nutrient removal, lagoons, and sludge treatment and disposal.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 750.

title: Unit Operations Laboratory for Water and Wastewater Treatment
description: Laboratory experiments in selected processes for water and wastewater treatment.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 350L.

title: Industrial Wastewater Treatment
description: Industrial sources, characteristics, and treatment plant design.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 751 or ECIV 752.

title: Computational Hydraulics
description: Unsteady flow in open channels and pipes: theory, governing equations, and methods for their solution.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 560.

title: Numerical Methods in Subsurface Hydrology
description: Formation of groundwater flow and solute transport problems, theory and practice, numerical methods, solution techniques.
subject: ECIV

title: Advanced Hydrology
description: Advanced theories and techniques used in stormwater modeling; kinematic hydrology; soil physics infiltration; deterministic and parametric stormwater models; stochastic methods.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 562.

title: Unsaturated Flow Theory
description: Moisture content-matric suction relationships, theory of flow in unsaturated soils, governing equations, measurement techniques, computer modeling of flow and transport.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 563.

title: Contaminant Transport
description: Quantitative study of conservative and non-conservative pollutant transport in groundwater. Special topics include: transport processes, field techniques to determine aquifer transport parameters, and computer modeling of flow and transport.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 563.

title: Erosion and Sediment Control
description: Erosion, sediment transport, methods for control, pond hydraulics and performance, nonpoint source pollution, stream water quality.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 562.

title: Fluid Transients
description: Definitions; derivation of governing equations; methods of solution; method of characteristics; transients caused by turbomachinery, and methods for controlling transients.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ENCP 360.

title: Sediment Transport and River Mechanics
description: Sediment properties, review of fluid mechanics of sediment transport as bedload and suspended load, stability analysis of bedforms, alternate bars, growth and migration of meander bends.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 560.

title: Dynamic Analysis of Railway Systems
description: Dynamic characteristics of railway systems and their components; Modeling and simulations of railway systems including trains, track and ballast; Dynamic interaction of components including wheel-rail and train-bridge interaction; Study of environmental vibrations; Advanced topics on infrastructure assessment, infrastructure upgrade and vibration mitigation.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 524.

title: Design Project in Railway Engineering
description: Application of engineering design principles in railway projects; project management; project scheduling; cost estimation; ethics; environmental and social impact; design drawings; report documents.
subject: ECIV
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in ECIV 580 or ECIV 582.

title: Selected Topics in Civil Engineering
description: Individual studies and/or investigations of special topics in the field of civil engineering.
subject: ECIV

title: Research in Civil Engineering
description: Credits to be designated upon registration.
subject: ECIV

title: Seminar in Civil and Environmental Engineering
description: Seminar on current topics in civil and environmental engineering. Includes oral presentations by students on their research projects. Recommended by the department that all graduate students participate each semester the seminar series is offered.
subject: ECIV

title: Thesis Preparation in Civil Engineering
description: To be arranged by candidates for the master’s degree with the instructor under whose direction the master’s thesis is being written.
subject: ECIV

title: The American Economy
description: Basic concepts, institutional foundations, structure of the private and public sector, labor markets; major economic problems.
subject: ECON

title: Principles of Microeconomics
description: The study of supply and demand, pricing and cost concepts, firm and consumer decision-making, market structure, and government policies.
subject: ECON

title: Principles of Macroeconomics
description: The study of gross domestic product, business cycles, economic growth, inflation, unemployment, and monetary and fiscal policy.
subject: ECON

title: Introduction to Economics
description: Introduction to economics principles for non-majors. Basics of supply and demand and government and monetary policy are covered in a non-technical manner. Not open to business or economics students. Credit not granted for both ECON 223 and ECON 221 or ECON 222 .
subject: ECON

title: Introduction to Economics
description: The study of supply and demand, markets, household and firm decision-making, gross domestic product, inflation, unemployment, and government policies. Open to all students except business administration and economics majors.
subject: ECON

title: Money and Banking
description: The role of money in the market economy. Commercial banks, the Federal Reserve System, and monetary policy. Cannot be used to satisfy major requirements.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224.

title: The International Economy
description: Survey of international economic issues and institutions, including trade and protectionism, global and regional trade agreements, trade balances and exchange rates, Japan, NAFTA, and the European Union.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 224.

title: Issues in Economics
description: The nature and causes of major economic problems facing the nation and its communities, and policy alternatives designed to solve them. The philosophy and methodology of economics in social problem solving.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224.

title: Sports Economics
description: Applications of economic theory to sports through hands-on analysis of data from sporting events and other sports markets.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ECON 221 and ECON 222; or C or better in ECON 224.

title: Intermediate Microeconomic Theory
description: Analysis of the economic behavior of households and firms. Production, consumption, price determination, and the degree of competition in markets.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224, MATH 122 or MATH 141.

title: Intermediate Macroeconomic Theory
description: Analysis of the national economy as a whole. Money, output, employment, inflation, and international economic linkages.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224, MATH 122 or MATH 141.

title: American Economic History
description: Growth and development of the American economy; applications of economic theory to economic history.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224.

title: Introduction to Data Science for Economists
description: An introduction to the various types and sources of data used by economists and how to interpret such data to answer economic questions and support economic theory. Includes the uses of survey data, cross-sectional, time series, and panel data.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ECON 221 and ECON 222 or ECON 224.

title: Business Finance
description: The procurement and management of wealth by privately owned profit-seeking enterprises.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221, ACCT 225, and 3 hours of statistics at the 200-level.

title: Financial Institutions
description: A study of the functions and operations of financial institutions and their relationships to the commercial banking system and the general economy. Attention is devoted to savings institutions, insurance companies, rural and urban real estate credit, consumer credit, and associated topics.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224.

title: Government Policy Toward Business
description: An analysis of public policy toward business in the United States. Emphasis is on the desirability of various policies in light of their consequences for the general welfare.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224.

title: Independent Study
description: Contract approved by instructor, advisor, and undergraduate division head is required.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224.

title: Money, Income, and Prices
description: A study of monetary standards, monetary theory, monetary policy, and the mechanism of international payments. Attention is devoted to questions of monetary problems, employment, and fiscal policy.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224.

title: Labor Economics
description: A study of labor market institutions, trends in labor market activity, and the effects of government policy on the labor market. (Not open to majors in economics.)
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224.

title: History of Economic Thought
description: A survey of economics from the ancient philosophers to the present; with emphasis on the mercantilist, physiocratic, classical, Marxian, Austrian, neo-classical, and institutional schools of economics.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224.

title: Economics of American Industry
description: A study of the structure of selected American industries, of the development and concentration of economic power in the American economy, and of public policy toward industry.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224.

title: Business Applications of Economic Forecasting
description: Analysis of business cycles and applications of forecasting techniques to project and interpret economic trends.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224.

title: Engineering Economics
description: Decision making with respect to capital goods, with emphasis on such decision making in governmental activities and public utilities. Intended primarily for engineering students, the course emphasizes the types of investment decisions that engineers are often called upon to make.
subject: ECON

title: Introductory Econometrics
description: The use of statistical techniques to analyze economic relationships. The emphasis is on the application of linear regression to real-world economic data.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 224, or ECON 221 and ECON 222; MGSC 291 or STAT 201; and MATH 122 or MATH 141.

title: Foundations of Capitalism
description: Examines the foundations of capitalism and why it has prevailed over alternative systems. Topics include the justification of private property, distribution of wealth, profit motive, source of wealth creation, and others.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 211 and ECON 222.

title: Internship in Economics
description: Supervised work experience of at least nine hours per week, to include one class meeting a month and individual consultation. Contract approval by instructor, advisor, and department chair is required. Cannot be used to satisfy major requirement.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in both ECON 321 and ECON 322, and cumulative GPA of 2.75.

title: Urban Economics
description: An analysis of economic forces affecting urbanization and the economic processes influencing urban form and structure. Spatial concepts are considered in addition to traditional micro-economic and macro-economic concepts. Topic coverage includes: the economic origin of cities; urban functions and the urban economic base, land-use structure and urban form, and urban efficiency.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224.

title: International Trade Economics
description: Theory of international specialization, commercial policy, customs unions, and the effects of trade liberalization and protectionism; economic growth and multinational enterprises.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 321.

title: International Monetary Economics
description: Exchange rate and balance of payments determination; purchasing-power parity; optimum currency areas, absorption, elasticity, monetary approaches, spot- and forward-exchange markets.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ECON 322.

title: International Development Economics
description: Economic theories of growth in developing countries. Use of factor resources; role of social and economic institutions; use of financial trade policies for growth.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224.

title: Labor Economics and Labor Markets
description: Economics of labor demand, labor supply, wage determination in competitive markets, migration, discrimination, unemployment, and labor unions. Theoretical models and empirical knowledge will be considered.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224; ECON 321.

title: Comparative Economic Systems
description: An analysis of the organization and operation of the world’s major economic systems.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224.

title: Law and Economics
description: Economic analysis and interpretation of the law. The economic effect of current law and optimal design of law to meet social objectives.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224.

title: Economics of Sustainable Development
description: Exploration of the basic theory and practice of sustainable economic development. Topics include: environmental legislation, global agreements, sustainable development indicators, and economic strategies and methods to promote environmentally sound development.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in the following ECON 221 and ECON 222; or ECON 224; MATH 122.

title: Experimental Economics
description: Exploration of the basic theory and techniques of experimental economics. Topics include: basic game theory, experimental design, and elements of behavioral economic thought.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in ECON 321.

title: Senior Seminar in Economics
description: Philosophy and methodology of economics, perspectives on theory and empiricism, economic policy; individualized guided research.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 321, ECON 322, and ECON 436 with grade of C or higher.

title: The Economics of Terrorism
description: Focuses on the following aspects of terrorism: (1) its causes/determinants (historical, social, cultural, economic, political, and religious determinants); (2) the organizational and funding structure of terrorist groups; (3) the tactics and weapons of terrorist groups; (4) mobilization and recruitment within terror networks; and (5) counterterrorism methods. Restricted to: Business Majors and Economics Arts and Sciences Majors.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ECON 321.

title: Industrial Organization
description: This course uses the tools of microeconomics and game theory to examine how firms compete and competition’s impact on industry performance. Topics include: price discrimination, product differentiation, and oligopoly behavior.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 321.

title: Political Economy
description: This course covers fundamental models of collective decision making, studies their empirical relevance, and considers interactions between the economy and politics.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ECON 221 and ECON 222 or C or better in ECON 224.

title: Economic Scholars I
description: This seminar course is designed to introduce students who have been accepted into the Econ Scholars program to economics research and real-world problem solving.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in ECON 321, ECON 322, and ECON 436.

title: Economic Scholars II
description: This seminar course continues the work from Econ Scholars I and is reserved for students who have been accepted into the Econ Scholars program. Students will be placed in teams and partnered with local businesses, government agencies, or economics faculty members to work on real-world projects and academic research.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in ECON 520.

title: Introduction to Mathematical Economics
description: Mathematical formulation of economic theories; the use of mathematics in the development and demonstration of economic relationships.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224; MATH 122, MATH 141, or the equivalent.

title: Essentials of Economics
description: A course designed to acquaint the student with the principles of operation of the American economic system. A survey course for social studies teachers in secondary schools.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224.

title: Managerial Economics
description: A study of the application of the economic theory of profits, competition, demand, and costs to analysis of problems arising in the firm and in decision making. Price policies, forecasting, and investment decisions are among the topics considered.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224.

title: The Economics of Education
description: Investment in human capital; the economic value of schooling; internal efficiency of schools; faculty compensation; equity and efficiency of school finance systems; financing higher education.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224.

title: Health Economics
description: Applications of economic analysis to health care. Structure and behavior of health-care markets. Description of health care policy issues.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224.

title: Research and Policy from Birth to Kindergarten
description: Applications of economic analysis to early childhood. Topics may include research and policy related to families, pregnancy, birth, and early childhood.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in ECON 221, ECON 224, or equivalent; C or higher in STAT 201, STAT 206, or equivalent with a grade of C or higher.

title: Environmental Economics
description: An analysis of the economics aspects of environmental decay, pollution control, and natural resource use. Analysis of the ability of the market system to allocate resources efficiently when economic activity is accompanied by environmental damage. Discussion of alternative public policy approaches to pollution control and natural resource conservation.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ECON 221 and ECON 222 or C or better in ECON 224.

title: Game Theory in Economics
description: Game theory as used to understand decision making in business, economics, politics and other real-world environments. Topics covered include: basic terminology; strategic, extensive, and combinatorial models; and equilibrium strategy.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 321 or MATH 141 and STAT 201 or C or higher in STAT 206.

title: Public Finance
description: Theory and practice of taxation: public revenue, expenditure, and debt.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in ECON 321.

title: Topics in Economics
description: Individual topics to be announced with title.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224.

title: Advanced Econometrics
description: Advanced econometric methods including quasi-experimental methods, applications of machine learning to causal inference and economic forecasting, and related topics.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ECON 436.

title: Survey of Contemporary Economic Theory
description: Neo-classical value and distribution theory combined with income and employment theory.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224.

title: Quantitative Foundations for Business and Economics I
description: Calculus and classical optimization methods applied to problems in business and economic analysis; matrices, derivatives, and integrals in the analysis of both univariate and multivariate business and economic models.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224.

title: Quantitative Foundations for Business and Economics II
description: Statistics and probability theory applied to problems of business and economic analysis.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224; MGSC 690 or ECON 690.

title: Quantitative Methods I
description: Probability and statistics necessary for graduate study in economics and business administration; estimation, hypothesis testing, regression, analysis of variance, and nonparametric methods.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224.

title: Quantitative Methods II
description: A study of decision models useful in business administration. Topics covered include linear programming, sensitivity analysis and duality, network models, integer programming, determinate and stochastic dynamic programming, inventory, and queues.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 221 and ECON 222, or ECON 224; ECON 692, mathematics and computer portion of Fundamental Business Skills or equivalent.

title: Economic Growth and Development
description: Overall view of problems of economic development, including its history and relationship to the modern world. Brief consideration is given to such noneconomic factors as political, sociological, and cultural environments. Basic theories of growth are presented and critically evaluated.
subject: ECON

title: Applied Microeconomics
description: Theory of demand, production, cost, pricing, distribution, and capital. Particular emphasis on applications of the theory to various problems faced by the firm.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 621.

title: Applied Macroeconomics
description: The modern theory of income determination. Inflation, unemployment, and interest rates in an open economy setting. Emphasis on economic policy.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 621.

title: Macroeconomics Analysis and International Economics
description: The modern theory of national employment, output, and the price level. Monetary and fiscal policy. International trade, exchange rates, and international capital markets.
subject: ECON

title: Managerial Economics
description: The application of microeconomic concepts to managerial decisions. The concepts include demand, cost, market structure, pricing, profitability, and strategic behavior.
subject: ECON

title: MA Economics Practicum
description: The MA Econ practicum course involves developing and executing a team-based project in applied economics. The course is broken down into sections to help each team maintain progress on their project and culminates in a day of project presentations open to the public. The instructor will provide general and specific guidance throughout. Team are encouraged to partner with businesses or government in developing their project, but may also choose a topic independently.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in ECON 711, ECON 712, and ECON 736.

title: Applied Microeconomics
description: Theory of demand, production, cost, pricing, distribution, and capital. Particular emphasis on applications of the theory to various problems faced by the firm.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 621.

title: Applied Econometrics
description: The theory and application of linear regression analysis to economic problems. The course will present both finite-sample and asymptotic properties of regression estimators, and address problems that arise in using regression methods with economic data.
subject: ECON

title: Applied Economic Forecasting
description: Examination of a variety of techniques that are used for forecasting and policy simulation purposes. Development of skills that have applications in business, government, and economic research.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 692 or equivalent.

title: International Trade, Theory and Policy
description: Classical and modern models of international trade, evaluation of tariffs and quotas, advantages and disadvantages of international trade agreements.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 711 or ECON 720, with exceptions made for anyone who has taken an undergraduate Intermediate Microeconomics class ECON 321 at USC in the last five years.

title: Health Economics
description: A critical introduction to the application of economic analysis to problems in the health care field. Selective surveys of the related scientific literature will be covered.
subject: ECON

title: Programming Methods
description: Mathematical programming techniques which are useful in business and economics. Topics include: solution techniques and applications of linear programming, duality, theory, parametric programming, the decomposition problem, integer programming, dynamic programming, Lagrange multipliers, Kuhn-Tucker theory, and an introduction to control theory.
subject: ECON
prereq: Prerequisites: ECON 694 or equivalent.

title: Economics Independent Study
description: Directed research on a topic to be developed by MS Economics student and instructor. May be repeated.
subject: ECON

title: Microeconomic Theory I
description: The modern theory of consumer behavior, production, the firm, and market structure.
subject: ECON

title: Microeconomic Theory II
