title: Environment and Obesity
description: Role of the built environment and environmental toxins in the development and progression of obesity and metabolic syndrome from a public health perspective.
subject: ENHS

title: Disasters and Public Health Emergency Management
description: Introduction to emergency preparedness and response in relation to environmental and public health. Historical context for the emergence of public health emergency preparedness and demonstration of articulation with community response partner agencies in the post-COVID era.
subject: ENHS

title: Introduction to Public Health Microbiology
description: Public health microbiology and the intersection between microbial disease, the environment, and health, with a particular focus on critical public health issues in the 21st century.
subject: ENHS

title: Independent Study
description: Enrollment and topic to be approved in advance by advisor and instructor. May be repeated.
subject: ENHS

title: Special Topics in Environmental Health Sciences
description: Issues and emerging themes in environmental health. May be repeated for a total of 9 credit hours as content varies by title.
subject: ENHS

title: Disasters and Public Health Emergency Management
description: Introduction to emergency preparedness and response in relation to environmental and public health. Historical context for the emergence of public health emergency preparedness and demonstration of articulation with community response partner agencies in the post-COVID era.
subject: ENHS

title: Climate Change Impacts on Human Health
description: A detailed exploration of the impacts of climate change on human health and well-being.
subject: ENHS

title: Public Health and Community Disaster Resilience
description: A study of national, community, and organizational multi-hazard resilience and risk management framed in the public and environmental health contexts.
subject: ENHS

title: Crisis Leadership for the Public Health Professional
description: A study in the intersection of public health and crisis leadership to develop the knowledge and skills needed to effectively respond to and recover from emergencies and disasters.
subject: ENHS

title: Advanced Special Topics in Environmental Health
description: Emerging issues and topics concerning environmental health. May be repeated as content varies by title up to a total of 9 credit hours.
subject: ENHS

title: Medical Mycology
description: Advanced study of infectious diseases caused by fungi. Etiology, symptoms, and treatment of fungi related illnesses.
subject: ENHS

title: Concepts of Environmental Health Science
description: Environmental health sciences presenting the earth as a complex system in which people, plants, animals, and non-living physical-chemical components interact.
subject: ENHS

title: Parasitology
description: Parasites of biological, economic, and public health importance. Three lecture and three laboratory hours per week.
subject: ENHS
prereq: Prerequisites: 300 level Biology course or equivalent.

title: Industrial Health Programs
description: Analysis, planning, and implementation of programs to protect workers’ health in industry; legislative and regulatory background.
subject: ENHS

title: Environmental Genomics
description: State of the art molecular techniques that elucidate mechanisms of environmental contaminants in model systems.
subject: ENHS

title: Biofilms in Environmental Health and Disease
description: Effect of bacterial biofilm process on many diverse areas. Recognition, prevention, and control of biofilm-related problems in the environment, health care, industry, and engineering.
subject: ENHS

title: Metals and Human Health
description: Trace metal(loid)s, their fate and transport in the environment and their potential impacts on human health.
subject: ENHS
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 101 or BIOL 110; CHEM 101 and CHEM 102, or equivalent.

title: Environmental Pollutants and Human Health
description: Overview of environmental pollutants and their impact on human health; case studies of environmental catastrophes; principles of ecotoxicology; air, water, and land pollution associated with neurotoxicity, toxicology, and carcinogenesis.
subject: ENHS
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 101 or BIOL 110; CHEM 101 and CHEM 102.

title: From Air to Alveoli: Exposure Science
description: A receptor-oriented approach for assessing human exposure to environmental contaminants by inhalation, dermal and ingestion routes. Covers methods for estimating exposures to protect health and well-being, to relate adverse effects to exposures, and to comply with regulations and guidelines.
subject: ENHS

title: Infectious Disease Ecology
description: Ecological theories as the basis for environmental change and the (re)emergence of infectious agents that ultimately impact human and ecosystem health.
subject: ENHS

title: Occupational Ergonomics I
description: Introduction to ergonomics: hazards identification and analysis; solution design and implementation; human musculoskeletal characteristics, injuries; effects of work on performance, safety, and health. Application to manufacturing and office environments.
subject: ENHS

title: Disasters and Public Health Emergency Management I
description: Introduction to emergency preparedness and response in relation to environmental and public health. Historical context for the emergence of public health emergency preparedness and demonstration of articulation with community response partner agencies in the post-COVID era.
subject: ENHS

title: Disasters and Public Health Emergency Management II
description: The Disasters and Public Health Emergency Management II companion course is designed for public health practitioners regardless of expertise and particular discipline who must be aware of the emergency management functions relating to public health. The course is designed to familiarize students with the fundamental knowledge needed to address natural and man-made disasters and to follow up where the companion course left off with a specific focus on environmental disasters.
subject: ENHS
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in ENHS 715.

title: Environmental Nanoscience
description: Fundamental principles of environmental nanoscience: unique properties of nanomaterials, syntheses and characterization of nanomaterials, and key processes determining their environmental fate and behavior of nanomaterials.
subject: ENHS

title: MPH Integrated Learning Experience (ILE)
description: Synthesis of foundational and ENHS MPH competencies in preparing a high quality grant proposal to address a public health issue.
subject: ENHS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PUBH 724, PUBH 725, PUBH 726, PUBH 730, PUBH 735, ENHS 660, ENHS 771, and ENHS 775 .

title: Marine Conservation and Environmental Health
description: Explores the intersection between conservation and environmental health with a particular focus on coastal and marine case studies.
subject: ENHS

title: Fundamentals of Air Pollution
description: Chemical and physical aspects of air pollution and their regulatory problems. An examination of air pollution sources; physical and chemical processes affecting pollutants after emission; pollutants and their effects and the ultimate fate of pollutants. Attention is also given to the legal, administrative, and technical aspects of air pollution control.
subject: ENHS

title: Ecotoxicology of Aquatic Systems
description: Lethal and sublethal effects of environmental stressors on organisms living in the water column and in sediments of aquatic systems. Practical techniques of aquatic toxicology, risk assessment and modeling.
subject: ENHS

title: Fundamentals of Industrial Hygiene
description: Industrial hygiene, including health effects, occupational health standards, and the evaluation and control of occupational health hazards.
subject: ENHS

title: Medical Aspects of Occupational Health
description: Emphasizes the medical aspects of exposure to hazardous materials, accidents, and mental and physical stresses on the job. Clinical spectrum of occupational illness with some emphasis on industrial toxicology.
subject: ENHS

title: Principles, Methods, and Issues in Air Quality
description: Fate, transformation, and behavior of pollutants in the atmosphere. Exposure and human health impacts of atmospheric particles.
subject: ENHS

title: Applied Research in the Environmental Health Sciences
description: Current and prospective research associated with the multi-disciplinary areas of environmental health sciences. Critical evaluation of scientific research, and technical writing and oral presentations.
subject: ENHS

title: Applied Aquatic Sciences
description: The quantitative application of principles of basic physical, biological, and geochemical principles in assessing and solving environmental problems in lakes, streams, and wetlands. Emphasis on watershed-water quality interactions, trophic state analyses, wasteload impact prediction, toxic chemical fate and transport, wetland values, and classification.
subject: ENHS
prereq: Prerequisites: ENHS 660, pre-calculus math, general ecology.

title: Applied Aquatic Sciences Laboratory
description: Sampling and analysis of the interacting parameters used in assessing water quality and the functioning of aquatic systems.
subject: ENHS

title: Ecological Modeling and Environmental Planning
description: Concepts in systems ecology and ecological modeling. Emphasis on the use of models and computer simulations in examining environmental interactions, predicting environmental impact, and facilitating the process of environmental planning. Lab practice in model development and computer simulation analysis.
subject: ENHS
prereq: Prerequisites: MATH 111 or equivalent, ecology, ENHS 660.

title: Industrial Ventilation and Hazard Control
description: Control of chemical and physical hazards in the occupational environment. Course covers principles and design of health protection systems such as ventilation systems, collection mechanisms, control of physical factors (excluding radioactivity).
subject: ENHS
prereq: Prerequisites: college math and ENHS 762 or consent of instructor.

title: Exposure and Risk Assessment
description: Designing, implementing, and analyzing environmental exposures in the field; error analysis; computation of the value of improved information; hazard identification; dose-response evaluation; and risk characterization.
subject: ENHS
prereq: Prerequisites: MATH 141, CHEM 111 and CHEM 112, and BIOS 700 or STAT 701.

title: Microbial Processes and Pollution
description: Microbial processes which alter the fate, bioavailability, and toxicity of environmental pollutants: biotransformations of metals and organic pollutants; resistance mechanisms and roles of microbial biofilms in toxin transfer.
subject: ENHS

title: Environmental Health Sciences Seminar
description: Environmental Health Sciences Seminar is a one credit course that provides the opportunity for graduate students within the department and other related departments/programs to enhance and broaden their knowledge in environmental health by exploring current research and case studies.
subject: ENHS

title: Human and Ecological Risk Assessment
description: Focuses on history, theory, and practice of predicting, managing, and communicating potential human health and environmental risks of hazardous chemicals. Reviews fundamental components and explores uncertainties, probabilistic approaches, and ‘real-world’ challenges of risk analysis.
subject: ENHS

title: Radiation Health Physics
description: Physics of radiation and associated health hazards; hazard evaluation and measurements; radiation content and protection of the individual. Course covers ionizing radiation, ultraviolet, microwave, lasers, R.F. field, and ultra-sound.
subject: ENHS
prereq: Prerequisites: ENHS 660.

title: Risk Assessment and Interactions of Environmental Toxicants
description: A study of biological interactions and transformation of environmental toxicants at the cellular and subcellular levels, and assessment of cellular damage as it relates to health hazards and risks. Topics to include: environmental toxicants; exposure measurements; factors affecting interactions and toxicity; metabolism of xenobiotics: types and levels of effects and interactions; and human health risks.
subject: ENHS
prereq: Prerequisites: ENHS 660.

title: Resource Management and Environmental Impact Assessment
description: Review of ecological principles as applied to environmental impact assessment. Study of the mandates of the National Environmental Policy Act of 1969. Analysis of several impact assessment methodologies.
subject: ENHS
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOL 102 and BIOL 570.

title: Environmental Regulation and Planning
description: Introduction to environmental planning. Survey of major federal environmental legislation. Review of processes and techniques of environmental planning including zoning, permits, management plans, assessments, and evaluation methods. Case studies of significant environmental projects.
subject: ENHS

title: Radiation Biology
description: Fundamentals on the biological effects of ionizing radiation on living systems, especially man; basic biological mechanisms which bring about somatic and genetic effects.
subject: ENHS
prereq: Prerequisites: ENHS 660, ENHS 773.

title: Air Pollution Monitoring and Modeling
description: Sources, sinks, transport, and transformation of air pollutants. Health effects that occur directly or by intermediate transport. Current monitoring methods and modeling techniques for air pollution.
subject: ENHS
prereq: Prerequisites: one year each of general chemistry and physics.

title: Applied Environmental Physiology
description: Lecture and laboratory investigations concerning sublethal and lethal physiological responses of aquatic organisms to a variety of environmental pollutants. Stresses the in-depth understanding of the effects of: bacterial and thermal pollution, pesticides/herbicides, industrial chemicals, hazardous materials, and petroleum hydrocarbons on different physiological mechanisms.
subject: ENHS
prereq: Prerequisites: ENHS 660, ENHS 761.

title: Advanced Seminar in Environmental Modeling
description: A critical review of recent advances and case histories in the formulation and use of ecological/ environmental models. Ecosystems analysis and environmental planning.
subject: ENHS

title: Occupational Ergonomics II
description: Literature reviews and applications in evaluation of hazards and design of ergonomic interventions including human factors in information processing, design of displays and controls, vibration, macroergonomics, fatigue, and shiftwork.
subject: ENHS
prereq: Prerequisites: ENHS 681.

title: Analytical Concepts for Environmental Health Sciences
description: Physical and chemical principles of environmental qualitative and quantitative analysis with emphasis on atmospheric, aquatic, and terrestrial samples. Includes use and limitations of instrumental techniques, sampling strategies, data management and reduction, and quality assurance programs.
subject: ENHS

title: Concepts of Hazardous Materials Management I
description: Chemical and physical principles of multimedia contaminant transport, environmental effects of hazardous materials, statutes and regulations classification, treatment and disposal of hazardous materials.
subject: ENHS

title: Concepts of Hazardous Materials Management II
description: Chemical and physical properties of hazardous materials; use and storage; disposal options; transportation requirements; site safety considerations; management systems involving hazardous materials.
subject: ENHS
prereq: Prerequisites: ENHS 788.

title: Special Topics in Environmental Health Sciences
description: Content varies by title. Course may be repeated for a total of 6 credit hours.
subject: ENHS

title: Introduction to Environmental Science Research
description: The course is intended to develop theoretical and practical knowledge in environmental science research. The learning formats will permit focus on areas of interest as a means to develop the research skills for later projects. Guided by focus, students may work in the laboratory, field, and/or use existing data.
subject: ENHS

title: Issues in Coastal Environmental Health
description: Problems associated with coastal population growth and development. Emphasis is on the working group approach to ameliorating impacts on ecosystem and human health.
subject: ENHS

title: Introduction to Nanoanalytics
description: Laboratory based course aimed at developing theoretical and practical knowledge in regards to nanoscience in toxicology and in the environment. Students will perform nanoparticle syntheses, characterization, fate and behavior studies or toxicology exposures. Learning formats will permit focus on areas of interest aimed at developing research skills.
subject: ENHS

title: Global Environmental Health and Food Security
description: Global environmental health with a focus on food security in developing nations, including crop responses to warming, soil changes, more variable precipitation inputs and expanding geographical range of pests.
subject: ENHS

title: Public Health Practice
description: Performance of a limited work or service project in a public need setting, pursuit of planned learning objectives related to previously identified aspects of the student’s chosen role. Self-monitoring and regular seminars focusing on learning accomplishments.
subject: ENHS
prereq: Prerequisites: 9-10 hours of specified courses including BIOS 700, EPID 700.

title: Advanced Research Approaches and Applications in Environmental Health Sciences
description: This course is intended for Doctor of Philosophy (PhD) graduate students to enhance their knowledge of research methods and approaches to address contemporary environmental health sciences issues. The learning formats will allow examination and focus on areas of scientific inquiry and interest as a means to develop the research skills for subsequent research. Guided by focus and instructor guidance through lecture and individual mentoring, students may conduct preliminary pilot research in the laboratory, field, and/or from a review of existing scientific data. Development of the basic scientific background literature review and formulation of the research questions to be tested as part of the final dissertation will be the focus of this research. Further development and enhancement of technical writing, oral communication and research translation skills will be gained.
subject: ENHS

title: Environmental Radiation Surveillance
description: Technical coverage relevant to a practical evaluation of radiation sources and contaminants in the environment.
subject: ENHS
prereq: Prerequisites: ENHS 773.

title: Aerosol Science
description: Physical and chemical principles applied to the behavior and properties of particles suspended in air. Course covers motion under applied forces, electrical properties, diffusion, removal from gas, cloud dynamics, and optical properties.
subject: ENHS

title: Special Research Topics in Environmental Health Sciences
description: Discussion and/or laboratory participation involving techniques used in multidisciplinary research areas of environmental health sciences that have not been covered by other courses. May be repeated for credit on different topics.
subject: ENHS
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOS 700, EPID 700, ENHS 660.

title: Advanced Topics in Environmental Planning
description: Detailed analyses of techniques, especially computer simulation modeling, used in environmental assessment and planning. Emphasis will be on the prediction of the ecological effects of development projects. Students will collectively construct a simulation model for the purpose of environmental assessment.
subject: ENHS
prereq: Prerequisites: ENHS 767, ENHS 775.

title: Survey in Multi-Disciplinary Research of Environmental Health Sciences
description: This course is intended for Doctor of Philosophy (PhD) graduate students to enhance their knowledge of current research approaches and to address contemporary environmental health sciences issues. The learning format will allow students to examine the potential of current research and be exposed to a variety of environmental health sciences issues that will enable students to develop foundational research knowledge needed for their subsequent dissertations. In ENHS 864, students will be presented with a variety of environmental health sciences research and topics in a survey fashion. Students will be presented with lectures covering various scientific research including primary laboratory and field research as well as secondary research analyzing existing scientific data. Students will then select a scientific topic in Environmental Health Sciences to lead a class discussion in which they will also provide an overview of the research and will critically examine the strengths and weaknesses of the research methods, data analysis, results, discussions and conclusions. The final section of the course will include lectures on research translation and how to develop a Community Engagement component to their research. This will entail essentially communication elements with the public to develop prevention/restoration strategies that will improve human and/or ecosystem health and well-being.
subject: ENHS

title: Community-Based Approaches for Precision Environmental Public Health Assessment
description: A comprehensive overview of community-based approaches for environmental public health assessment. Emphasis of this course is placed on introducing the rapidly evolving field of wastewater-based epidemiology (WBE) where wastewater-derived measurements of human health biomarkers indicative of various aspects of human and environmental health (infectious disease, pollutant exposures, nutritional status, etc.) offer as an inclusive and minimally invasive source of information in support a precision-based model for community-level environmental public health assessment.
subject: ENHS

title: Ethics Research Prep
description: Overview of skills and standards, including ethics and research preparation, for Environmental Health Sciences doctoral students.
subject: ENHS

title: Independent Study
description: Directed research on a topic to be developed by doctoral student and instructor. May be repeated.
subject: ENHS

title: Dissertation Preparation
subject: ENHS
prereq: Prerequisites: one full year (18 hrs) of graduate study beyond the master’s level.

title: Entrepreneurism and Free Enterprise
description: This course is designed to help you develop an understanding of how to start, grow, and manage innovations and new ventures successfully and how the free enterprise system works. In addition to covering essential information about new venture development, this course will address skills of entrepreneurial management and strategic thinking.
subject: ENTR

title: Special Topics in Entrepreneurship
description: Entrepreneurial opportunities giving insight into the real world of entrepreneurship.
subject: ENTR

title: Startup Finance, Legal Structures, and Business Systems
description: This course is designed to help you develop an understanding of how to develop, analyze, and manage financial, legal, and other systems needed to build and manage a business. The successful development of a business depends on effectively managing a range of systems and processes, from sales funnels to financial reports and legal structures. This course focuses on the practical mechanics of managing an organization from a few people to many thousand people.
subject: ENTR

title: Applied Entrepreneurship
description: This course is designed to help you develop an understanding of how entrepreneurs start, grow, and manage innovations and new ventures successfully and how the free enterprise system works. In addition to covering essential information about new venture development, this course will address skills of entrepreneurial management and strategic thinking.
subject: ENTR
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ENTR 201, MGMT 373, or MUSC 580; comparable experience may be considered.

title: Independent Study in Entrepreneurship
description: This course is an independent study for advanced undergraduate majors and graduate students in entrepreneurship. An approved independent study contract is required for enrollment.
subject: ENTR

title: Introduction to the Environment
description: Analysis of environmental issues and the role of science in their identification and resolution.
subject: ENVR

title: Introduction to the Environment Lab
description: Demonstrations, field trips, data analyses, and discussion relating to environmental issues, such as sustainability, resource management, and pollution control.
subject: ENVR

title: Green Explorations
description: Interdisciplinary seminar combining the intellectual exploration of ecological perspectives with the physical exploration of the local environment. First-year students only.
subject: ENVR

title: Green Engagements
description: Interdisciplinary seminar on designing, researching, and implementing collaborative projects to promote ecological sustainability. First-year students only.
subject: ENVR

title: Natural History of South Carolina
description: General review of plants, animals, and geological features of South Carolina, with an emphasis on connections to the natural world.
subject: ENVR

title: Environmental Science and Policy I
description: Introduction to interdisciplinary and multidisciplinary perspectives on environmental issues. Required for majors in the Environment and Sustainability Program. Integrative case studies address ways of understanding nature. Sophomore Standing.
subject: ENVR

title: Environmental Science and Policy II
description: Continuing interdisciplinary and multidisciplinary exploration of relations between environment and society for majors in the Environment and Sustainability Program. Case studies raise issues, challenges, and strategies to achieving sustainability.
subject: ENVR

title: Introduction to Sustainability Management and Leadership
description: Introduction to development, establishment, and implementation of sustainability management systems and organizational leadership for achieving environmental, social, and economic goals.
subject: ENVR

title: Green Technology in Germany
description: Examination of roots and culture of environmentalism and related technological innovation in Germany. Comparison of green practices around the world to practices within Europe and U.S.
subject: ENVR

title: Surface and Subsurface Hydrology
description: Environmental considerations of the hydrologic cycle. Occurrence and movement of surface water and groundwater as they relate to water budgets, water supply, and ecosystems. Geochemistry of natural waters, water quality, and pollution.
subject: ENVR
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in MATH 122 or MATH 141; C or higher in GEOL 101, GEOL 103, GEOL 201, GEOG 104, GEOG 201, ENVR 101, ENVR 201, ECIV 101 or MSCI 101.

title: Marine Organic Pollution
description: An exploration of the science behind oil spills, toxic chemicals, microplastics, and other organic pollutants impacting our oceans, from coastlines to the deep-sea. Emphasis on how these contaminants move, transform, and affect marine life and human health, and how cutting-edge science drives legal action and policy change.
subject: ENVR

title: Environmental Pollution and Health
description: A survey of pollution (chemical, biological, physical) effects on environmental quality and public health with emphases on how each pollutant class behaves and affects individual and community health over acute to chronic exposure periods.
subject: ENVR

title: Environmental Ethics
description: Examination of principles and arguments surrounding moral issues involving the environment.
subject: ENVR

title: Global Environmental Health
description: Concerns in global environmental health, with a focus on toxic pollution and disease burden in developing countries. Investigation of international treaties, corresponding environmental pollution processes, and human health effects.
subject: ENVR

title: Integrating Sustainability
description: Multidisciplinary approach to interrelated environmental, economic and social problems facing humans at local, regional and global scales.
subject: ENVR

title: Environmental Anthropology: Cross-cultural Perspectives on Environmental Change
description: Cross-cultural perspectives on environmental issues.
subject: ENVR

title: Energy, Society and Sustainability
description: The role of energy in shaping society and geographic settings, as well as how energy production and consumption are shaped by the societal values and norms in which it is extracted, produced, and consumed.
subject: ENVR

title: SEOE Peer Mentorship, Leadership, Professional Development
description: Exploration of post-graduation opportunities in academia and industry. Interactive discussion of topics including helping skills, active listening, oral communication, goal setting, interviewing, and building a strong professional portfolio.
subject: ENVR

title: Data Science in Earth, Ocean and Environmental Science
description: Computational analysis of earth, marine, and environmental datasets applying time series analysis, regression, filtering, and statistical analysis.
subject: ENVR
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CSCE 106 or higher; C or better in MATH 122 or higher.

title: Independent Study
description: Contract approved by instructor, advisor, and dean of the School of the Environment is required for undergraduate students.
subject: ENVR

title: Congaree National Park: Field Investigations in Environmental Science
description: Field research practices and analysis techniques in environmental science. Combines inquiry-based field investigations at Congaree National Park and laboratory sample analysis with integration of data and public policy concepts. Daily field trips required.
subject: ENVR
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ENVR 201 and ENVR 202.

title: Capstone Seminar in Environmental Science and Environmental Studies
description: Collaborative study of a contemporary environmental issue. Field trips may be required.
subject: ENVR

title: Special Topics in Sustainability and the Environment
description: Current developments in sustainability and global environmental issues selected to meet faculty and student interests. May be repeated as content varies.
subject: ENVR

title: Internship in the Environment
description: Supervised internship experience in a professional environment within the field of the environment and/or related environmental sciences or studies. Undergraduate internship contract required with approval of instructor, advisor, and department head before registration.
subject: ENVR
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ENVR 201 and ENVR 202.

title: Research in Environmental Science
description: Independent student research in collaboration with faculty mentors. Contract approved by instructor, advisor, and department chair is required.
subject: ENVR

title: Environmental Practicum
description: Multidisciplinary research projects related to University or community environmental problems (e.g., energy, water conservation, solid waste, recycling).
subject: ENVR

title: Special Topics in the Environment
description: An in-depth analysis course of a specific interdisciplinary environmental topic. Course content varies and will be announced in the schedule of classes by title.
subject: ENVR
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ENVR 101 or ENVR 201 or graduate standing.

title: Environmental Microbiology
description: An overview of the microbial world including a survey of the distribution, functioning, and diversity of microorganisms in natural systems. Discusses the crucial roles that microorganisms play in ecosystem function, biogeochemical cycles, and environmental quality.
subject: ENVR
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in MSCI 102 or BIOL 102; C or better in CHEM 112.

title: Climate Geoengineering
description: This course will discuss the urgent need for deploying solar radiation and carbon dioxide removal approaches at scale, including potential benefits and risks of these options. It will also discuss regulatory and governance considerations at both the national and international level and strategizes to incentivize large-scale adoption of these approaches.
subject: ENVR

title: Coastal Nature-Based Solutions
description: Application of the principles of ecology with the methods of engineering to design sustainable ecosystems to better integrate human society with the coastal environment.
subject: ENVR

title: Socionatural Coastlines in Global Perspective
description: A discussion-based seminar course that examines nature-society relations in coastal regions globally. The course will use social theory to understand how uneven development processes shaped – and continue shaping – current coastlines. We will explore key topics including coastal capitalism, delta ecologies, and climate justice via several global case studies.
subject: ENVR

title: Sustainability Management and Leadership Strategies
description: Integrated management system principles and advanced leadership strategies to create sustainable development initiatives.
subject: ENVR

title: Sustainability Projects Course
description: Research, development and implementation of sustainability projects throughout the campus and community.
subject: ENVR

title: Water and Sanitation in Global Perspective
description: Interdisciplinary examination of the global policy challenge of ensuring equitable access to water and sanitation services for all.
subject: ENVR

title: Global Food Politics
description: Political, social, and cultural landscapes of food and farming around the world; issues of agricultural production, trade, consumption, and food security.
subject: ENVR

title: Decolonizing the Environment: Race, Nature, Power
description: Critical examination of the ways ideas about nature and racial difference are conceptually and materially entwined with the production of social and environmental inequalities.
subject: ENVR

title: Environmental Economics
description: An analysis of the economics aspects of environmental decay, pollution control, and natural resource use. Analysis of the ability of the market system to allocate resources efficiently when economic activity is accompanied by environmental damage. Discussion of alternative public policy approaches to pollution control and natural resource conservation.
subject: ENVR
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ECON 221 and ECON 222 or C or better in ECON 224.

title: Conservation Biology
description: Principles of conservation biology. Importance of biodiversity, causes of decline and extinction, and restoration and conversation policy in terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems.
subject: ENVR
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 301.

title: Freshwater Ecology
description: Quantitative study of the population, community and evolutionary ecology of freshwater habitats (lakes, ponds, rivers, streams, wetlands). Includes mandatory fieldtrips.
subject: ENVR
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOL 301.

title: Current Topics in Environmental Studies
description: Current issues, policies, and regulations pertaining to environmental studies. Emphasizes integrated multidisciplinary approaches toward identification, evaluation, preservation, mitigation, and/or utilization of environmentally sensitive material and sites.
subject: ENVR

title: Coastal Nature-Based Solutions
description: Application of the principles of ecology with the methods of engineering to design sustainable ecosystems to better integrate human society with the coastal environment.
subject: ENVR

title: Marine Data Science with R
description: This course provides a hands-on, project-oriented investigation of current approaches for research in marine science, ecology and environmental science. Components of the course will include exploratory data analyses, statistics, graphics and the R programming language. Prior programming experience is beneficial, but not required.
subject: ENVR

title: International Environmental Management Systems
description: International environmental management systems standards will be integrated with business planning to provide students with the best strategies for future growth in today’s environmentally sensitive global economy.
subject: ENVR

title: Directed Individual Studies
description: Directed research topics to be individually assigned.
subject: ENVR

title: Environmental Internship Preparation
description: Preparation and presentation of a capstone project plan for conduct of multidisciplinary environmental research addressing public/private/non-profit sector issues through an internship in government agencies, NGOs or private industry.
subject: ENVR
prereq: Prerequisites: One semester full-time graduate enrollment or equivalent.

title: Environmental Internship
description: Environmental internship in government agencies, NGOs, or private industry, culminating in a project deliverable. Typically includes data analyses/metrics, resource management options, and/or internal outreach education, with final assessment. Restricted to graduate students in the MEERM program.
subject: ENVR
prereq: Prerequisites: 3 credits of ENVR 795; successful completion of MEERM comprehensive examination.

title: Thesis Preparation
description: Thesis Preparation.
subject: ENVR

title: Seminar in Environmental Studies
description: Examination of the effectiveness of environmental policies and methods relative to current issues and needs.
subject: ENVR

title: Environmental Policy and Management
description: An examination of issues related to environmental policy making, implementation and management.
subject: ENVR

title: Environmental Advocacy Seminar
description: This seminar is designed to explore and develop practical advocacy skills in the area of environmental representation and to provide an understanding of advocacy in administrative, legislative, and litigation arenas.
subject: ENVR

title: Seminar in Environmental Ethics
description: Examination of the intellectual, cultural, and ethical frameworks within which environmental problems arise and are solved.
subject: ENVR

title: Infectious Disease Epidemiology
description: This introductory course will review the history of infectious diseases, principles of infectious disease transmission, relevant study design and analysis techniques, and the clinical epidemiology of specific pathogens by transmission route categories. This course requires a prerequisite of a solid understanding of basic science and public health curricula, such as molecular biology or anatomy-physiology; or permission from instructor.
subject: EPID

title: Special Topics in Epidemiology
description: Novel and emerging themes in epidemiology. Content varies by instructor and title. May be repeated for a total of 9 credit hours.
subject: EPID

title: Principles of Epidemiology
description: Introduction to descriptive and analytical epidemiology. Topics will include the distribution and determinants of disease, surveillance, outbreak investigations, measures of association, screening tests, bias, and causal reasoning.
subject: EPID

title: Independent Study
description: Enrollment and topic to be approved in advance by advisor and instructor. May be repeated.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: Instructor Permission.

title: Global Health Epidemiology
description: This course will introduce epidemiologic concepts and methods using cases studies examining current global health challenges. Students will gain an understanding of the role of epidemiology in understanding the distribution of disease and risk factors, and developing, implementing and evaluating public health interventions globally.
subject: EPID

title: Special Topics in Epidemiology
description: This course will introduce epidemiologic concepts and methods using cases studies examining current global health challenges. Students will gain an understanding of the role of epidemiology in understanding the distribution of disease and risk factors, and developing, implementing and evaluating public health interventions globally.
subject: EPID

title: Parasitology
description: Parasites of biological, economic, and public health importance. Three lecture and three laboratory hours per week.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: 300 level Biology course or equivalent.

title: Introduction to Epidemiology
description: Principles of epidemiology with examples of selected health problems. Health status of populations and conceptual tools for translating epidemiologic findings into public health action. May not be used as part of a degree program in epidemiology or biostatistics.
subject: EPID

title: Concepts and Methods of Epidemiology
description: Conceptual foundation of epidemiologic research, quantitative methods, and epidemiologic study design. Intended for those who will be involved in epidemiologic research.
subject: EPID

title: Clinical and Population Research Protocol Development and Implementation
description: The purpose of this course is to develop applied research skills related to the development of appropriate data collection protocols for a given public health issue and context.
subject: EPID

title: Scientific Writing and Appraisal of Epidemiologic Studies
description: This course will familiarize students with techniques used to critically assess, interpret, evaluate, and synthesize epidemiologic literature. Students will be introduced to research databases, reference management software, reporting guidelines, and methods for systematic reviews. Students will learn how to effectively communicate research finding via manuscript and oral or poster format.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 701, PUBH 725 or equivalent. The instructor reserves the right to waive course requirements or consider

title: Biologic Basis of Public Health
description: Survey of the biology of human disease processes at cellular, tissue and body system levels: application of biological principles to contemporary public health problems.
subject: EPID

title: Public Health Surveillance Systems
description: Introduction to the concepts, implementation, and evaluation of surveillance systems to monitor the health of human populations.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 701, PUBH 725, or equivalent course.

title: Intermediate Epidemiologic Methods
description: Application of epidemiologic methods to current health problems through analysis of secondary data. Strategies for investigating etiologic hypotheses, assessment and control of confounding.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 701, PUBH 725, or equivalent.

title: Nosocomial Disease Control
description: Specialization in the identification of potential or existing health hazards in institutional settings of the health care system; and includes instruction in the application of scientific knowledge to the daily routines in the implementation of appropriate control behaviors. Two lecture and three laboratory hours per week.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOS 700, EPID 700, EPID 742.

title: Cardiovascular Disease Epidemiology
description: Epidemiology of selected groups of cardiovascular diseases (CVD) including etiology, pathophysiology, identification and description of events of CVD, and outcomes.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 701, PUBH 725.

title: Seminar in Epidemiology
description: Analysis of current and prospective issues in epidemiology, including historical foundations. Includes student exploration and critical consideration of current research and unsolved problems in epidemiology.
subject: EPID

title: Cancer Epidemiology
description: Epidemiology of selected cancers in humans, including etiology, pathophysiology, identification and description of events of cancer and outcomes.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 701, PUBH 725 or equivalent. The instructor reserves the right to waive the prerequisite or consider

title: Environmental Epidemiology
description: Emphasis on the epidemiology of selected environmental factors which may affect human health including the identification of health hazards and methods of investigation. Two lecture and three laboratory hours per week.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 700, BIOS 700.

title: Infectious Diseases Epidemiology
description: Introduction to Principles of Epidemiology and Control of Infectious Disease. Focus will be on selected bacterial, viral, parasitic, and fungal diseases of public health importance locally, nationally and globally.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 701, PUBH 725, or equivalent course; BIOS 700/BIOS 701 or consent of instructor. The instructor may waive course requirements or consider the need for

title: Methods in Infectious Disease Epidemiology
description: Quantitative methods for the study of infectious disease dynamics, including study design and analysis, mathematical modeling, computer simulation, and phylogenetic inference.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 741 and EPID 749.

title: Sexually Transmitted Diseases: Their Epidemiology and Control
description: A study of the epidemiology of the various sexually transmitted diseases and their complications, with emphasis on their prevention and control.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 700 and BIOS 700.

title: Epidemiology and Control of Parasitic Diseases of Public Health Importance
description: Study of major parasitic diseases of public health importance. Emphasis on epidemiologic principles and patterns of human morbidity and mortality. Analyzes and evaluates various approaches in prevention and control programs.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 700, BIOS 700, and ENHS 661.

title: AIDS: Epidemiology and Control
description: A study of the epidemiology of Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome (AIDS) and its various implications and issues with emphasis on its prevention and control.
subject: EPID

title: Emerging Infectious Diseases: Epidemiology and Pathobiology
description: Principles and factors in emerging infectious diseases with emphasis on epidemiology, pathobiology, prevention, and control.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 749.

title: Epidemiologic Applications to Occupational Health
description: Introduction to clinical and epidemiologic aspects of occupational health and recognition and prevention of occupational diseases and injury. Epidemiologic applications to occupational health are highlighted and stressed, including design and implementation.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 700 or EPID 701.

title: Nutritional Epidemiology
description: Covers methodology for investigating nutrition's role in health, including nutritional assessment and the design and interpretation of research studies. Substantive issues emphasize major public health concerns of the 21st century.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 701, PUBH 725, or equivalent.

title: Reproductive and Perinatal Epidemiology
description: Epidemiology of reproductive and perinatal health with emphasis on current research, controversial issues and methodological approaches.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 701, PUBH 725, or equivalent course, BIOS 701 .

title: GIS and Public Health Applications
description: Principles and application of basic and intermediate-level GIS technologies in public health practice and research.
subject: EPID

title: Psychiatric Epidemiology
description: Methodologic issues in the epidemiologic study of psychiatric disorder, the epidemiology of mayor psychiatric outcomes, and issues in the study of special populations.
subject: EPID

title: Social Epidemiology
description: Influence of social factors and the distribution of those factors on patterns of health and disease. Including individual-level examinations of the role of social determinants in producing health, as well as more macro-level examinations of patterns of social disparities in health status.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 701, PUBH 725, or equivalent course.

title: Fundamentals of Genetic Epidemiology
description: This course is an introduction to the field of genetic epidemiology, providing students with an understanding of: 1) basic genetics, 2) the tools used by geneticists and genetic epidemiologists, and 3) the integration of genetic data into traditional epidemiologic study designs. This course includes application of epidemiologic and computational software tools used to analyzed genetic data.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 701 or PUBH 725; BIOS 701.

title: Practical Methods for Secondary Data Analysis
description: Introduction to data sources and methods commonly used by epidemiologists and health analysts in state or federal health departments and research settings. Methods include data management and analysis using SAS, data interpretation, survey designs, and innovative record linkages.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: PUBH 725 and BIOS 709 or EPID 701 and BIOS 701 and BIOS 709 or BIOS 710, Recommended: BIOS 757, BIOS 758, or BIOS 754; and EPID 741 or equivalent research methods course.

title: Independent Study
description: Directed research on a topic to be developed by M.P.H. or M.S.P.H. student and instructor. May be repeated.
subject: EPID

title: Selected Topics in Epidemiology
description: Content varies by title. Course may be repeated for a total of 6 credit hours.
subject: EPID

title: Integrated Learning Experience
description: Demonstrate synthesis of MPH foundational and concentration competencies to address a public health issue in the form of a high-quality written product.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PUBH 724, PUBH 725, PUBH 726, PUBH 730, PUBH 735, and EPID 741 or permission of the program director.

title: Epidemiology Applied Practicum
description: Apply and test public health concepts, theories, and analytical tools learned in the classroom to real-world public health issues outside of the classroom in any one of a variety of settings.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PUBH 724, PUBH 725, PUBH 726, and PUBH 730 or permission of MPH Program Director.

title: Advanced Methodological Theory in Epidemiology
description: Advanced epidemiologic methods in the design of epidemiologic studies, with emphasis on causal inference. Theories and frameworks of causation and interactions between causes and graphical visualization tools.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 741.

title: Advanced Analytic Methods in Epidemiology
description: Extension of research design and development issues with focus on grant writing.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 800.

title: Grant Writing
description: Extension of research design and development issues with focus on writing a major research grant application.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 741.

title: Introduction to Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analysis
description: The major goal for all students taking this course is to provide an overview of how to conduct systematic reviews and meta-analyses from published scientific literature. Emphasis is placed on developing a working knowledge of how to conceptualize a literature review, keyword search for relevant articles through online databases, define inclusion and exclusion criteria, develop coding schemes for study information, and the calculation of effect sizes using published data. For students taking more than 1-credit the additional goals are to provide hands-on experience in conducting a systematic review and meta-analysis, calculating effect sizes, and learning to use meta-analysis software.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOS 700 and BIOS 701 or their equivalent; C or better in BIOS 757 or equivalent is strongly recommended.

title: Seminar in the Epidemiology of Health Effects of Physical Activity
description: Seminar presentation and group discussion on the major issues in the study of physical activity and exercise and their impact on health.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 700.

title: Seminar in the Epidemiology of Aging
description: Exploration in depth of theories, current health problems, research, and methodological issues in the epidemiology of aging.
subject: EPID

title: Advanced Cardiovascular Disease Epidemiology: Evidence Synthesis and Evaluation
description: Epidemiology of cardiovascular disease and its risk factors, with a focus on evidence synthesis through systematic reviews.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 701.

title: Doctoral Seminar
description: May be repeated for credit.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: complete at least one semester of course work.

title: Advanced Environmental Factors and Human Health
description: Advanced methods encompassing the investigation of environmental factors and how they affect human health. Emphasis on reading and interpreting the peer reviewed scientific literature and developing a systematic literature review and grant proposal.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 700 EPID 701 and BIOS 700 BIOS 701.

title: Fundamentals and Applications of Translational Science
description: The course is divided into two parts. Part I, the Fundamentals of Translational Science, will provide students with foundational information to understand the basic principles of translational science. Part II of the course, Applications of Translational Science, will provide students with real-world examples of translational science research.
subject: EPID

title: Methods in Reproductive Perinatal Epidemiology
description: This course provides an overview of reproductive and perinatal epidemiology and the applications in the field of Maternal and Child Health. It covers the current and emerging topics in this area. Designed for doctoral students with interests in conducting research related to reproductive and perinatal epidemiology.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 700 EPID 701 and BIOS 700 BIOS 701.

title: Geographic Information Systems for Public Health Research
description: Principles and application of basic and intermediate-level GIS technologies in public health research. Designed for doctoral students with interest in conducting health-related research using GIS methods.
subject: EPID

title: Clinical Effectiveness
description: Clinical Effectiveness is a broad term that includes clinical trials and interventional study designs. The purpose of this course is to develop skills in the application of epidemiologic methods to clinical effectiveness research, by conceptualizing and designing an intervention study. Students will actively participate in teaching and learning through in-class activities and developing a protocol to test an intervention in a clinical trial design.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 701, PUBH 725, or equivalent course.

title: Advanced Methods and Concepts in Nutrition Research
description: Advanced Methods and Concepts in Nutrition Research addresses aspects of nutrition ranging from nutritional biochemistry to dietetics and community nutrition education. It covers disciplinary breadth encompassing the study of effects of dietary exposures on inflammation, epigenetics, immune function, psychological states and traits, physiologic states, and pathophysiologic processes, including carcinogenesis.
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: EPID 763.

title: Independent Study
description: Directed research on a topic to be developed by doctoral student and instructor. May be repeated.
subject: EPID

title: Selected Topics in Epidemiology for Doctoral Students
description: Variable credit doctoral level epidemiology course (1-3). The specific epidemiologic topic to be taught is determined by the course instructor in consultation with the department.
subject: EPID

title: Dissertation Preparation
subject: EPID
prereq: Prerequisites: one full year (18 hrs) of graduate study beyond the master’s level.

title: Introduction to European Studies
description: Team-taught interdisciplinary seminars, lectures, and readings with guest lecturers.
subject: EURO

title: Senior Seminar
description: Topics in contemporary European studies. Applicable to EURO major only.
subject: EURO

title: Senior Thesis
description: Approval of topic by EURO advisor(s).
subject: EURO

title: Holistic Health and Fitness
description: Focusing on the mind-body-spirit connection, individuals assume responsibility for their health through everyday choices. Students learn about and expand on the pillars of holistic health.
subject: EXSC

title: Holistic Health and Fitness Lab
description: Lab focusing on how to implement and practice techniques that promote health and well-being through the holistic practices.
subject: EXSC

title: Physical Activity and Health
description: Concepts of exercise, nutrition, behavior changes, and skills to promote lifelong physical activity and health.
subject: EXSC

title: Introduction to Sports Medicine and Athletic Training
description: Examination of careers and specific practices within sports medicine, specifically athletic training.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in EXSC 223 and EXSC 223L.

title: Foundations of Physical Therapy
description: Introduction to the profession and practice of physical therapy detailing the functions, disorders, and therapies of the major organ systems in applied context.
subject: EXSC

title: Effective Learning Strategies
description: Students enrolled in this course will discuss and learn how to apply evidence-based learning strategies that will increase the likelihood of success in the pursuit of a college degree.
subject: EXSC

title: Anatomy and Physiology I
description: The structure and functions of the human body: tissues, integument, skeletal, muscular, respiratory, and reproductive systems, and regulation of eating and metabolism.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: ENGL 102; BIOL 102; CHEM 111; MATH 122 or MATH 141.

title: Anatomy and Physiology I Laboratory
description: Hands-on activities covering micro- and macroscopic anatomical topics including identification of tissues, bones and markings of the skeletal system, the joints, and the skeletal muscles of the body.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: ENGL 102; BIOL 102, CHEM 111; MATH 122 or MATH 141.

title: Anatomy and Physiology II
description: The structure and functions of the human body: nervous, cardiovascular, digestive, immune, urinary, and endocrine systems.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in EXSC 223 and EXSC 223L.

title: Anatomy and Physiology II Lab
description: Hands-on activities covering the gross anatomy nervous, cardiovascular, digestive, and muscular systems.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in both EXSC 223 and EXSC 223L.

title: Functional Musculoskeletal Anatomy
description: Human anatomy for allied health professions. Focus on anatomy relevant to providing health services; knowledge and skills of orthopedic anatomy relative to muscle, ligament, and tendon; muscle origins, insertions, innervations, and actions pertaining to joint motion.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: EXSC 223 and EXSC 223L.

title: Functional Musculoskeletal Anatomy Lab
description: Clinical application of human anatomy for allied health care professions using discussion, models, and charts. Anatomy relevant to providing health care to individuals.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: EXSC 223 and EXSC 223L.

title: Perceptual-Motor Development
description: Theoretical foundations and observation of growth and motor development of children, age birth to 10 years. Observation will be provided via video and live subjects provided by the instructor.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in both EXSC 224 and EXSC 224L.

title: Exercise Physiology
description: The individual and combined roles of the major organ systems of the body in maintaining homeostasis during muscular exercise.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in EXSC 224 and EXSC 224L.

title: Exercise Physiology Lab
description: Laboratory procedures in exercise physiology; measurement of physical fitness components.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: EXSC 224 and EXSC 224L.

title: Biomechanics of Human Movement
description: Kinetic and kinematic principles governing efficient human movement. Selected methods of analyzing human movement will be reviewed.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in EXSC 224, EXSC 224L, PHYS 201 and PHYS 201L.

title: Health Fitness Practicum
description: First hour of a supervised practicum in a clinical setting for the Health Fitness Track.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: EXSC 223, EXSC 224.

title: Acquisition of Motor Skills
description: Scientific and behavioral foundation of the learning and performance of motor skills.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in EXSC 224 and EXSC 224L.

title: Special Topics in Exercise Science
description: Novel and emerging themes in exercise science. Content varies by instructor and title. May be repeated for a total of 6 credit hours as content varies by title.
subject: EXSC

title: Research Seminar in Exercise Science
description: The research process in exercise science; participation in, presentation and discussion of current research.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: EXSC 223, EXSC 224.

title: Practicum Preparation
description: Integration of major program of study and general education; issues of transition into senior practicum - EXSC 444 .
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in EXSC 330.

title: Calling Bull in the Age of Big Data, Social Media, and False Claims
description: Introduction to the large scientific and philosophical literature base related to bull, with a focus on the occurrence of bull in exercise science. Defining, identifying, and refuting bull when it is encountered in scientific literature, the news, social media, and casual conversation.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in EXSC 330 and EXSC 330L; C or better in STAT 201 or STAT 205.

title: Psychology of Physical Activity
description: Introduction to psychosocial factors in physical activity. Topics include mental health effects of exercise, behavior change theories applied to physical activity, and physical activity determinants and interventions.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PSYC 101.

title: Exercise Science Practicum
description: Supervised experience in a field, clinical, or research setting. EXSC majors only.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: EXSC 401.

title: Health/Fitness Programs
description: Design and implementation of health/fitness programs.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: EXSC 223, EXSC 224.

title: Conditioning Methods in Athletic Performance
description: Performance of pre-exercise assessments, development of appropriate exercise training programs based on these assessments, and development and conduct of safe and effective methods of exercise by the application of the primary theories and principles of exercise science.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in both EXSC 224 and EXSC 224L.

title: Exercise Testing and Prescription
description: The art of exercise testing and prescription for athletes, general and special populations through classroom and hands on experience.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in EXSC 330 and EXSC 330L.

title: Independent Study
description: Enrollment and topic to be approved in advance by advisor and instructor.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: EXSC 223, EXSC 224.

title: Exercise, Sport, and Nutrition
description: The relationship between exercise, sport performance, and nutrient metabolism.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: EXSC 223, EXSC 224, EXSC 330, EXSC 330L.

title: Clinical Exercise Physiology
description: Scientific bases of clinical exercise programming. The fitness instructor’s role in encouraging changes in exercise behavior.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: EXSC 223, EXSC 224, EXSC 330, EXSC 330L.

title: Clinical Exercise Physiology Lab
description: Prerequisite: EXSC 223 , EXSC 224 , EXSC 330 , EXSC 330L .
subject: EXSC

title: Physiological Basis for Strength and Conditioning
description: Investigation on the physiological basis for strength and conditioning. Principles of strength and conditioning through lecture based learning, demonstrations, and through laboratory activities.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in EXSC 330.

title: Current Topics in Exercise Science
description: Content varies by title. Course may be repeated for a total of 6 credit hours.
subject: EXSC

title: Impairments of the Human Motor System
description: Role of motor development in the growth and development of individuals exhibiting impaired motor control.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: biology, anatomy, physiology, or the equivalent.

title: Physical Activity and the Physical Dimensions of Aging
description: The effects of age and physical activity on physical and motor functions of elderly individuals.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: EXSC 223, EXSC 224, EXSC 351, EXSC 330, EXSC 330L.

title: Women’s Health and Physical Activity
description: Sex differences in diseases, physiological function of sex hormones, hormonal changes in a woman’s life, specific women’s health issues, and role of physical activity and exercise in prevention and treatment of conditions and diseases specific to women or related to sex hormones. Restricted to 30 students, Special Permission by Instructor.
subject: EXSC

title: Apps, Wearables and Technology for Lifestyle Behavior Change and Weight Loss
description: The course will increase students’ understanding of the theoretical foundations, scientific evidence and practical application of technology-assisted lifestyle interventions, with an emphasis on behavioral weight control for adults.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in EXSC 410.

title: Neuroscience of Human Performance
description: Application of neuroscientific theories and measurements to human performance and expertise.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in EXSC 330, EXSC 330L, EXSC 351, and EXSC 410.

title: Nutrition and Immunology
description: Examination of the interrelationships that link human nutrition to the immune system in health and disease. Topics will include basic immunology, overview of nutritional sources, deficiencies and excesses, and the impact on public health issues such as exercise, disease and aging.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: EXSC 330.

title: Neuromuscular Basis of Functional Strength Training
description: The aim of this course is to acquire a fundamental understanding of how concepts from motor learning, neurophysiology, and muscle physiology are applied to functional strength training.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in EXSC 330, EXSC 330L, EXSC 351.

title: Analysis of Everyday Motor Behavior
description: Students in this course will analyze everyday activities to gain insight into how humans plan, initiate, execute and refine motor skills. Students will also learn how to evaluate research on motor behavior and how to create novel studies aimed at advancing our understanding of everyday motor behavior.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in EXSC 351.

title: Exercise Oncology
description: Physiological and psychosocial impact of cancer and its treatments on individuals, and how this impacts the design and delivery of exercise programs before, during and after cancer treatments.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in EXSC 330 and EXSC 330L.

title: Environmental Exercise Physiology
description: This course is designed to provide students a survey of physiological responses to a variety of environments, such as heat, cold, altitude, and microgravity environments, and how the body acclimatizes to these environments with regards to exercise training.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in EXSC 330 and EXSC 330L.

title: Cardiorespiratory Exercise Physiology
description: Examination of the anatomy and function of the cardiovascular and respiratory systems of the exercising human organism, including acute adjustments and chronic adaptations to the systems.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in EXSC 330.

title: Skeletal Muscle Physiology: Form and Function
description: Skeletal muscle physiology and exercise through select laboratory experiences and discussion of related research literature.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in both EXSC 330 and EXSC 330L.

title: Research Methods Design for the Exercise Sciences
description: A comprehensive overview of the methods and skills needed to design and interpret research including developing a research question, writing a research proposal, designing appropriate protocols to test the research question, conducting ethical research, and professional presentation and dissemination of research.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in EXSC 224 and EXSC 224L; C or better in STAT 201, STAT 205, or STAT 206.

title: Writing and Presenting in Research
description: The research process in Exercise Science through participation, presentation, and discussion of current research.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: EXSC 224.

title: Physical Activity and Health: Epidemiology, Research and Practice
description: An introduction to exercise science with emphasis on the relationships between exercise and health for promotion of physical activity in clinical and public health settings.
subject: EXSC

title: Assessment of Motor Behavior
description: Assessment of infant, child, adolescent, and adult motor behavior.
subject: EXSC

title: Behavioral Aspects of Physical Activity
description: Psychosocial and behavioral factors in physical activity. Topics include mental health effects of exercise, behavior change theories applied to mental health effects of exercise, behavior change theories applied to physical activity, and physical activity determinants and interventions.
subject: EXSC

title: Ecological Momentary Assessment in Health Behavior Research
description: This course is designed to introduce students to Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) or Experience Sampling Methods (ESM) for health behavior research. EMA/ESM is a real-time data capture strategy that can be assisted by mobile technologies to assess behaviors, experiences, and exposures in real-world settings. This course will provide an overview of the EMA literature and introduce different EMA approaches and designs to collect data on individuals’ health determinant(s) and/or outcomes. The primary goal of the course is to enable students to apply course concepts and learned skills to develop and implement EMA/ESM in their fields of study.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: Students should have an introductory understanding of research design and quantitative methods; for example, students are expected to complete at least one graduate-level statistics and methods course (examples: EXSC 787, HPEB 707, BIOS 700, BIOS 757, EDRM 711).

title: Genetics in Health Sciences
description: The part lecture and part discussion course will explore genetic research in the health sciences, with emphasis on human genetic association studies, clinical utility for personalized medicine, direct-to-consumer genetic testing, and ethical issues. Students will receive hands-on experience searching, interpreting, and summarizing genetic studies on a topic of their choice.
subject: EXSC

title: Controlled Trials in Exercise Science
description: This course covers planning, organizing and implementing randomized controlled trials of physical activity or exercise interventions. It is primarily aimed to meet the needs of graduate students in exercise science and others in related fields.
subject: EXSC

title: Mechanisms of Motor Skill Performance
description: A study of theories and mechanisms involved in human movement. Focus is on analysis of principles and systems of gross motor control and learning.
subject: EXSC

title: Measurement of Body Composition and Associated Health Behaviors
description: Overview of measurement theory and measures to assess body composition and associated health behaviors (i.e., physical activity, sedentary behavior, sleep, diet).
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOS 700, BIOS 701, or PUBH 725; for MPH-PAPH students, EXSC 700.

title: Applied Human Biomechanics
description: This course focuses on fundamentals of biomechanics emphasizing measurement of human movement and motor control. Content presented is essential to understanding human movement, exercise training, movement impairment and injury. Utilization and interpretation of instrumentation for capturing, describing and quantifying human movement and motor control will be covered (e.g., electromyography, kinematics).
subject: EXSC

title: Clinical Exercise Testing
description: Study of the procedures involved in screening and testing persons with varying levels of functional work capacity.
subject: EXSC

title: Community-Based Physical Activity Interventions
description: Role of the physical activity specialist within the community health department. Development, initiation, and evaluation of campaigns, resources, community capacity building, and coalitions to promote physical activity.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: EXSC 700 or HPEB 700.

title: Special Topics in Exercise Science
description: A study of selected issues in exercise science. Content varies by title.
subject: EXSC

title: Neural Basis of Skilled Motor Behavior
description: Current and historical perspectives on the neural basis of skilled motor behavior.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: EXSC 731.

title: Ergogenic Aids: Science vs. Myth
description: Examine the current evidence related to popular nutritional and pharmacological ergogenic aids.
subject: EXSC

title: Endocrinology of Exercise and Health
description: The course examines the endocrine system, its interaction with the nervous system, and how they affect human biology before, during, and after exercise. Special attention will be paid to this system’s influence on the relationship between physical activity and health.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: At least one undergraduate or graduate course in statistics and molecular or cellular biology.

title: Exercise and Childhood Obesity
description: Causes and treatment of childhood obesity with special reference to the role of exercise in prevention and early intervention.
subject: EXSC

title: Physiology of Exercise
description: Physiological responses to exercise: skeletal muscle structure and function, cardiorespiratory function, physiological determinants of exercise performance, and training adaptations. Didactic and laboratory included.
subject: EXSC

title: Physiology, Exercise, and Disease
description: The input and response to exercise in diseased populations. Diseases to be examined include cardiovascular disease, age-related diseases, pulmonary, renal, and other conditions.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: EXSC 780.

title: Research Seminar in Exercise Physiology
description: Presentation and discussion of current research topics in exercise physiology.
subject: EXSC

title: Cardiovascular/Pulmonary Testing and Programming
description: Techniques used in exercise testing (including principles of electrocardiology) and in design and delivery of exercise programs for enhancing the health of normal and cardiopulmonary-diseased populations.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: EXSC 781.

title: Research Methods and Design for Exercise Science
description: The major goal of this course is to provide an in-depth examination of: research concepts, terminology, experimental, non-experimental, and epidemiological designs, internal and external validity, methods for establishing causality investigating associations, and application of designs to test hypotheses in research of exercise science-related outcomes.
subject: EXSC

title: Independent Study
description: Topics to be assigned and approved by advisor, graduate director, and department head.
subject: EXSC

title: Internship in Exercise Science
description: Clinical practice in an applied area of exercise science. Requirements include at least 20 hours fieldwork per week with intensive supervision.
subject: EXSC

title: Physical Activity Integrative Learning Experience
description: This course is designed to provide students with a culminating seminar focused on the synthesis of foundational and MPH-PAPH competencies in preparing a high quality grant proposal to address a public health problem.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PUBH 724, PUBH 725, PUBH 726, PUBH 730, and PUBH 735; B or better in EXSC 700, EXSC 710, and EXSC 780.

title: Physical Activity Applied Practice Experience
description: The focus of this course is the performance of a limited work or service project in an approved public need setting and the demonstration of at least 5 competencies related to previously identified aspects of the student’s chosen role.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PUBH 724, PUBH 725, PUBH 726, PUBH 730, and PUBH 735; B or better in EXSC 700, EXSC 710, and EXSC 780.

title: Project in Exercise Science
description: Independently executed project designed to expand the student’s knowledge of exercise science.
subject: EXSC

title: Ethical Conduct in Public Health Research
description: The course will provide an overview of ethical issues scientists encounter conducting and disseminating public health research. Topics include the history of ethics in public health, working with human participants, conflicts of interests, spin, and creating safe and healthy workplaces.
subject: EXSC

title: Predoctoral Fellowship Writing Course with Special Emphasis on NIH F31
description: The course is designed to enable predoctoral students to gain grant writing experience, develop into productive, independent research scientists, and to obtain mentored research training while conducting dissertation research.
subject: EXSC

title: Introduction to Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analysis
description: The major goal for all students taking this course is to provide an overview of how to conduct systematic reviews and meta-analyses from published scientific literature. Emphasis is placed on developing a working knowledge of how to conceptualize a literature review, keyword search for relevant articles through online databases, define inclusion and exclusion criteria, develop coding schemes for study information, and the calculation of effect sizes using published data. For students taking more than 1-credit the additional goals are to provide hands-on experience in conducting a systematic review and meta-analysis, calculating effect sizes, and learning to use meta-analysis software.
subject: EXSC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in BIOS 700 and BIOS 701 or their equivalent; C or better in BIOS 757 or equivalent is strongly recommended.

title: Neuro Repair - Rehabilitation

subject: EXSC

subject: EXSC

title: Physical Activity and Health: Epidemiology and Research Methods

subject: EXSC
