subject: FREN

title: French Seminar
description: May be repeated with approval of advisor.
subject: FREN

title: Special Projects in French
description: Directed research and reading in subjects to be individually assigned. Prior written approval of professor required. May be repeated once for credit.
subject: FREN

title: Clinical Genetics
description: This medical student rotation in Clinical Genetics is largely observational and will provide an introduction to this specialty that focuses on the genetic disorders that impact the physical, emotional, and/or developmental well-being of infants, children, adolescents, and adults. The student will observe and participate in the evaluation and management of individuals with known or suspected genetic disorders. The student will have the opportunity to interact with members of the multidisciplinary team that includes physicians, genetic counselors, genetic service coordinators, and genetic clinic coordinators. There will also be an opportunity to learn about the outreach efforts and collaborative projects with community and state agencies that are focused on promoting healthy child development and supporting families with children who have special needs.
subject: GENE

title: Clinical Genetics
description: The purpose of this course is to provide the learner with an exposure to the clinical, professional, and lifestyle aspects of Genetics. Students will be exposed to a breadth of medical conditions. Clinical experiences will occur at a GHS Hospital or affiliate under the direct supervision of attending and resident (if applicable) physicians. This medical student rotation in Clinical Genetics is largely observational and will provide an introduction to this specialty that focuses on the genetic disorders that impact the physical, emotional, and/or developmental well-being of infants, children, adolescents, and adults. The student will observe and participate in the evaluation and management of individuals with known or suspected genetic disorders. The student will have the opportunity to interact with members of the multidisciplinary team that includes physicians, genetic counselors, genetic service coordinators, and genetic clinic coordinators. There will also be an opportunity to learn about the outreach efforts and collaborative projects with community and state agencies that are focused on promoting healthy child development and supporting families with children who have special needs.
subject: GENE

title: Genetics Away Rotation
description: The student will complete an intensive study of Genetics at another hospital or practice. This course must be approved through the VSAS application service or office of the M3/M4 Director.
subject: GENE

title: Foundations of Geography
description: A survey of the principles and methods of geographic inquiry. Not required for the geography major.
subject: GEOG

title: Introduction to Physical Geography
description: Basic concepts of landform geography, climatology and meteorology, and biogeography.
subject: GEOG

title: The Digital Earth
description: Introduction to geographic data; use of digital maps and aerial/satellite images as means of Earth observation; basics of spatial data analysis; location-based Web APPs; digital map services.
subject: GEOG

title: Geographies of Global Change
description: Introduction to processes of political, economic, social, and environmental change around the world; topics include geopolitical transitions, nationalism, trade, uneven development, conflict, environmental geopolitics, and demographic change, with an emphasis on non-U.S. contexts.
subject: GEOG

title: Landform Geography
description: Hydrology, soil science, and interpretation of physical features formed by water, wind, and ice, with emphasis on environmental change. Three hours of lecture and one two-hour laboratory per week.
subject: GEOG

title: Weather and Climate
description: Processes that influence weather and climate patterns on the earth. Three lectures and one two-hour laboratory per week.
subject: GEOG

title: Peoples, Places, and Environments
description: Basic principles of human geography.
subject: GEOG

title: Geography of South Carolina
description: An intensive regional analysis of South Carolina. Selected phenomena such as urbanization, industrialization, land use, the physical environment, and their interrelationships.
subject: GEOG

title: Geography of Latin America
description: Physical and human geography of Latin America.
subject: GEOG

title: Geography of North America
description: Physical and human geography of North America with emphasis on the United States.
subject: GEOG

title: Geography of Europe
description: Physical and human geography of Europe.
subject: GEOG

title: Geography of the Middle East
description: A regional geographic approach to the environmental, social, economic, and political aspects of the Middle East (Southwest Asia and north Africa) with emphasis on contemporary problems.
subject: GEOG

title: Geography of Russia and Central Asia
description: A geographical investigation of the historical, cultural, political, and economic processes that shape Central Asian states and societies from antiquity, through periods of colonialism and empire, the formation of the Soviet Union, and into the current context of globalization and international development.
subject: GEOG

title: Geography of Sub-Saharan Africa
description: A regional approach to the physical, social, economic, and political aspects of Sub-Saharan Africa with emphasis on contemporary problems.
subject: GEOG

title: Geographic Information Systems
description: Introduction to principles and methods of geographic information systems including discussion of computers, spatial data, analysis, and display. Includes discussion of applications and hands-on experience.
subject: GEOG

title: Introduction to Drones for Airborne Spatial Data
description: This course is an introduction to the use of small unmanned aerial systems (UAS) in collecting/processing imagery for mapping/information analysis. Course content includes UAS characteristics, small camera considerations, project planning and processing, and legal requirements in the United States and selected European countries.
subject: GEOG

title: Global Migration
description: Global patterns and processes of migration through multi-disciplinary perspectives, addressing the types, causes, and effects of migration. Possible topics include labor migration, refugees and asylum, climate migration, migration controls, and processes of inclusion and exclusion.
subject: GEOG

title: Topics in Geography
description: Selected topics of special interest in geography. May be repeated as content varies by title.
subject: GEOG

title: Landscape and Cultural Geography
description: The temporal-spatial relationship between humans and the natural environment with emphasis on the role through time of human activity in changing the face of the earth.
subject: GEOG

title: Geography and Global Geopolitics
description: Geographic perspectives on problems in international relations. Political geographic analysis of contemporary world problems.
subject: GEOG

title: Economic Geography
description: Spatial interrelation and linking of economic activities and how location affects the nature of economic systems.
subject: GEOG

title: Geopolitics and Refugees
description: An overview of conflict, displacement, and humanitarianism in the context of the modern interstate-system. Topics include refoulment, border externalization, transit, resettlement, and asylum politics.
subject: GEOG

title: Cities in a Changing World
description: An overview of historical and contemporary urban development and growth, with an emphasis on post-colonial and non-Western contexts.
subject: GEOG

title: Global Health Geographies
description: Investigation of human-environment interactions and their effects on global population health and health disparities through the lens of Health and Medical geography.
subject: GEOG

title: Sustainable Cities
description: Overview of the relationships between urbanization and environmental processes and an exploration of pathways to greater sustainability.
subject: GEOG

title: Landscapes of the United States
description: Geographic change through time in the United States, with emphasis on evolution of the American landscape. Physical environment as modified by human intervention over time within a regional framework.
subject: GEOG

title: Disasters
description: The study of disasters, their triggering mechanisms (natural, human, technological), their spatial distributions from local to global scales, and associated human responses.
subject: GEOG

title: Cartography
description: Introduction to the theory and principles of map construction including discussions of equipment and materials, lettering and symbolization, scale and generalization, data manipulation and representation. Presentation of geographic information on maps.
subject: GEOG

title: Environment and Society
description: A geographic consideration of the interactions between environment and society. The ways in which social, economic, and cultural processes interact across local to global scales and influence environmental practices, policies, and patterns of change will be emphasized.
subject: GEOG

title: Geographies of American Cities
description: Overview of the development of American cities from industrial period to the present. Special attention given to the political, economic, social processes that shape urban space and urban ways of life.
subject: GEOG

title: Introduction to Remote Sensing
description: Basics of remote sensing and image interpretation in aerial photography, drone technology and satellite imagery for earth observations. Emphasis on competent use of remote sensing data for a variety of geographic and multidisciplinary applications.
subject: GEOG

title: Climate and Society
description: Major theories and methodologies for studying the relationship between climate and society.
subject: GEOG

title: Water as a Resource
description: Introduction to spatial and institutional aspects of water availability, demand, and quality. Water storage/conveyance strategies and facilities. Real and perceived flood, drought hazards.
subject: GEOG

title: Biogeography
description: Spatial distributions of plants and animals as they relate to historical biogeographic patterns and human impact on the biosphere.
subject: GEOG

title: Cartographic Animation
description: Introduction to theories and principles of cartographic animation.
subject: GEOG
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOG 341 or GEOG 263.

title: Geography of Wind
description: Fundamental principles of wind formation, measurement, and its impacts on the natural and human environment – landscape, human settlement and health, transportation, and energy.
subject: GEOG

title: Hurricanes and Tropical Climatology
description: Atmospheric circulation, structure, and processes of tropical climates and hurricanes. Forecasting and model simulations of hurricanes. Temporal variations of tropical climate change and hurricanes.
subject: GEOG

title: America’s National Parks
description: Resource, managerial, and recreational-use components of the national park system; contemporary issues, problems, and managerial alternatives.
subject: GEOG

title: Air Pollution Fundamentals
description: Fundamentals, processes, and issues that relate to air pollution. Emphasis is on the role of the atmosphere. Additional topics include pollution sources, environmental and health effects of air pollution, air quality measurements, urban smog, and ozone depletion, regulations, and societal impacts.
subject: GEOG

title: Global Human Rights
description: Geopolitical, historical, cultural, and economic geographies of human rights around the world; issues of development, environment, health, inequality, violence.
subject: GEOG

title: Independent Study
description: Contract approved by instructor, advisor, and department chair is required for undergraduate students.
subject: GEOG

title: Seminar in Geography
description: Research methods and projects; restricted to students with at least 15 hours of credit in geography. Restricted to students with at least 15 hours of credit in geography.
subject: GEOG

title: Special Topics: Service Learning in Geography
description: Service learning experience in conjunction with designated Geography courses. Direct, hands-on service experience with an agency, voluntary organization, or community-based project. May be repeated, as content varies by title. Students enrolled in designated Geography courses by Instructor permission.
subject: GEOG

title: Undergraduate Research
description: Research on a significant geography problem in the local environment. Emphasis will be on the development of relatively individualized experiences in scientific investigation.
subject: GEOG

title: Senior Thesis
description: Senior research thesis on a problem of fundamental geographic significance, supervised by faculty member; must include a written final project report.
subject: GEOG

title: Special Topics in Geographic Research
description: Selected topics of special interest in geography. May be repeated as content varies by title.
subject: GEOG

title: Planning and Locational Analysis
description: Scientific approaches to locational problems in urban and regional planning, including regional growth and decline, land use control, public facility location and provision, and locational efficiency.
subject: GEOG

title: Migration and Globalization
description: A survey of the political, economic, and social causes and consequences of migration. Topics include immigration policy, border control, settlement patterns, transnationalism, multiculturalism, and integration. Selected contemporary and historical cases.
subject: GEOG

title: Coastal Zone Management
description: Analysis of the competing demands for limited resources in the coastal zone with emphasis on the role of management in the resolution of conflicts over resource use.
subject: GEOG

title: Socionatural Coastlines in Global Perspective
description: A discussion-based seminar course that examines nature-society relations in coastal regions globally. The course will use social theory to understand how uneven development processes shaped – and continue shaping – current coastlines. We will explore key topics including coastal capitalism, delta ecologies, and climate justice via several global case studies.
subject: GEOG

title: Landscapes of South Carolina
description: An examination of the factors responsible for creating the contemporary South Carolina cultural landscape.
subject: GEOG

title: Geographical Analysis of Transportation
description: Analysis of transportation systems and the application of geographic tools to transportation planning.
subject: GEOG

title: Environmental Hazards
description: Human and environmental contributions to the generation and management of hazards originating from extreme natural events to technological failures. Contemporary public policy issues at the national and international level.
subject: GEOG

title: Quantitative Methods in Geographic Research
description: A survey of basic quantitative approaches for handling and interpreting geographically related data; univariate and bivariate procedures applicable to a variety of problems.
subject: GEOG

title: Hazards Analysis and Planning
description: Examination of the geo-spatial aspects of hazards analysis and planning with specific reference to disaster preparedness, recover, mitigation, and resilience.
subject: GEOG
prereq: Prerequisites: GEOG 363 and GEOG 530, or equivalents.

title: Global Food Politics
description: Political, social, and cultural landscapes of food and farming around the world; issues of agricultural production, trade, consumption, and food security.
subject: GEOG

title: Advanced Cartography
description: Planning, compiling, constructing, and evaluating thematic maps. Theory and practice in scribing, separation and screening, color proofing, and map reproduction. Discussions of the process of map communication and the ways the cartographer can improve that communication.
subject: GEOG
prereq: Prerequisites: GEOG 341.

title: Dynamic Cartography
description: Theories and principles of interactive and animated cartographic design.
subject: GEOG
prereq: Prerequisites: GEOG 341.

title: Geography of the City
description: The influence of political boundaries, historical forces, settlement patterns, and transportation processes on urban life.
subject: GEOG

title: Weather Analysis and Forecasting
description: Principles and controls of weather and climate as they occur at the regional/synoptic scale. Background on the critical tools and concepts used for daily weather forecasting by the National Weather Service and prominent government agencies, and used by broadcast meteorologists.
subject: GEOG
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOG 202.

title: Applied Climatology
description: Analysis of climate applications in natural and human-modified environments. Content may include water resources, solar energy, urban planning, air quality, agriculture, and tourism. Course work includes lab and field experimentation.
subject: GEOG

title: Landscape Ecology
description: Analysis of emergent patterns in landscape structure and its linkage to ecological processes; application of relevant principles, methodologies, and geospatial technologies to solving real-world challenges focused on species and ecosystem conservation.
subject: GEOG

title: Water and Watersheds
description: Spatial variation of hydrology, water quality, and water-related hazards, including runoff generation, soil erosion, sedimentation, and flood hazards. Emphasizes a watershed perspective using geographic data and methods.
subject: GEOG
prereq: Prerequisites: GEOG 347, GEOL 371, or ECIV 360.

title: Remote Sensing of the Environment
description: Computer-assisted digital image analysis; Image correction; Spatial/spectral transformation; image classification and change detection; Use of remote sensing for solving real-world environmental problems.
subject: GEOG

title: LiDARgrammetric and Photogrammetric Digital Surface Mapping
description: Introduction to fundamental concepts used to map topographic and planimetric Earth surface features using digital LiDAR (LiDARgrammetric) and digital soft-copy photogrammetry (Photogrammetric).
subject: GEOG
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOG 263, GEOG 341, GEOG 345, GEOG 551, GEOG 563, or graduate student standing.

title: Spatial Programming
description: Computer programming of spatial problems; spatial statistical analysis, interactive graphics, and computer maps.
subject: GEOG

title: WebGIS
description: Web-based Geographic Information Systems (WebGIS), including concepts and principles of WebGIS, web programming fundamentals, web-based mapping techniques, and developing WebGIS applications.
subject: GEOG
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOG 263 or graduate student standing.

title: Contemporary Issues in Geography Education
description: Key concepts of geography and current approaches to teaching geography with specific attention to classroom materials, curriculum reform, cross-curricular integration, learning theory, and the use of geospatial/instructional technology.
subject: GEOG

title: Satellite Mapping and the Global Positioning System
description: Technology and use of Global Positioning Systems (GPS). GPS space segment, receiver technologies, range observables, and positioning accuracy. Applications to large/medium scale mapping, remote sensing, and aerial photography.
subject: GEOG
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOG 345, GEOG 263, or GEOG 551 or graduate student standing.

title: Advanced Geographic Information Systems
description: Theory and application of geographic information systems including discussions of automated input, storage, analysis, integration, and display of spatial data. Use of an operational geographic information system.
subject: GEOG
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOG 263 or graduate student standing.

title: GIS-Based Modeling
description: Geographical information systems for modeling physical/human processes in space and time using raster and vector data. Cartographic modeling concepts, embedded models, and GIS-model coupling.
subject: GEOG
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOG 263 or graduate student standing.

title: Geographic Information System (GIS) Databases and Their Use
description: Representation, construction, maintenance, and analysis of spatial data in a geographic information system (GIS) database.
subject: GEOG
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOG 263 or GEOG 341 or GEOG 551 or GEOG 563 or graduate student standing.

title: Climate Adaptation and Community Resilience Planning
description: The processes of climate adaptation planning and management from central concepts in adaptation to issues such as projecting impacts, vulnerability assessment, coping with uncertainty, and decision making.
subject: GEOG

title: Human Dimensions of Global Environmental Change
description: Consequences of increasing anthropogenic changes on environmental systems including the sources of change, regional impacts, and social and policy responses.
subject: GEOG
prereq: Prerequisites: GEOG 343.

title: International Development and the Environment
description: Intersections of international development and environmental change; study of general theoretical perspectives balanced with case studies from the Global South.
subject: GEOG

title: Geography of Public Land and Water Policy
description: Geography of public land, water, and related public trust resources (wildlife, timber, minerals, fuels, recreation, wetlands, coastal zones, wilderness); historical geography of policy; spatial aspects of current research and management.
subject: GEOG

title: Microclimatology
description: Field techniques and processes in the atmospheric boundary layer including radiation, soil heat fluxes, turbulence, momentum, latent and sensible heat fluxes, moisture, and evaporation.
subject: GEOG
prereq: Prerequisites: GEOG 202.

title: Climatic Change and Variability
description: Observations and theories of climatic change and variability as they occur at different space and time scales. Projections of future climates. Techniques used in climatic change research and impact analysis.
subject: GEOG
prereq: Prerequisites: GEOG 202 or equivalent.

title: Globalization and Cultural Questions
description: This course examines cultural understandings of and responses to globalization, examining topics such as its history and theories, migration, economic integration and inequality, identity, social movements, and the environment.
subject: GEOG

title: Beach-Dune Interactions
description: Influence of wind on coastal systems, with emphasis on nearshore currents, sediment transport and bedforms, aeolian transport, and dunes. Minimum Junior standing required.
subject: GEOG

title: Internship in Geography
description: Internship in government agencies, private-sector businesses, and non-profit organizations under the joint supervision of sponsor and departmental. A maximum of three credits may be applied to undergraduate Geography major or to Geography master's degree. May be repeated to a maximum of six credits.
subject: GEOG

title: History of Geographic Thought
description: A survey of the development of geographic philosophy and an analysis of geographic methodology.
subject: GEOG

title: Directed Individual Studies in Geography
description: Directed research topics individually assigned and supervised by graduate faculty. May be repeated for credit.
subject: GEOG

title: Selected Topics in Cartography and Remote Sensing
description: Special topics are offered in the form of short courses, seminars, and workshops. Students may take these offerings, by permission of the instructor, for variable credit. The course may be taken more than once.
subject: GEOG

title: Women Explorers and Travelers
description: Examines in geographical and historical contexts the activities of various women travelers and explorers.
subject: GEOG

title: Seminar in Geography Education
description: Review of recent literature on geography education with an emphasis on the national geography standards, spatial thinking and the use of geospatial technology in pedagogic contexts.
subject: GEOG

title: Urban Geography
description: An investigation into the concepts of the urban field and the urban region.
subject: GEOG

title: Advanced Economic Geography
description: Investigation into the locational aspects and the spatial systems of selected economic activities, from both regional and systematic viewpoints.
subject: GEOG

title: World Regional Geography for Teachers
description: The physical and human geography of major world regions with emphasis on basic principles of regional geography. Cannot be used in M.A., M.S., or Ph.D. programs in geography.
subject: GEOG

title: Seminar in Systematic Geography
description: Studies of the characteristics, processes, and distributions over the world of the different cultural and physical environmental elements, such as economic, political, or social activities, climate and landforms.
subject: GEOG

title: Seminar in Environmental Geography
description: Review of recent geographic literature on nature-society interactions with an emphasis on identifying research themes and methodologies employed by contemporary geographers.
subject: GEOG
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in GEOG 530, GEOG 568, or equivalents.

title: Seminar in Quantitative Analysis in Geography
description: Advanced quantitative approaches for handling and interpreting geographically related data. Multivariate procedures applicable to a variety of problems will be presented. For each topic the students will analyze data relating to their individual interests.
subject: GEOG
prereq: Prerequisites: GEOG 531 or equivalent.

title: Field Seminar in Third World Development Projects
description: The student works in a developing country for two to four months on projects designed by instructor and funded by the host country.
subject: GEOG

title: Seminar in Political Geography
description: Intensive discussion of classic and contemporary works in the field of political geography.
subject: GEOG

title: Seminar in Spatial Cognition
description: Selected topics in spatial cognition.
subject: GEOG

title: Research Trends in Geography
description: Seminar on research trends and writing research proposals in geography.
subject: GEOG

title: Seminar in Cartography
description: A seminar to familiarize students with current experimental techniques, literature, and research topics in cartography.
subject: GEOG

title: Seminar in Climatology
description: Major theories, measures of climatic change and variability, climate models, statistical analysis, and climate impacts.
subject: GEOG

title: Seminar in Physical Geography
description: Investigation of physical systems and processes at the earth’s surface. Topics vary; landforms, hydrology, pedology, biogeography, quaternary science, human impacts on physical systems.
subject: GEOG

title: Digital Techniques of Remote Sensing
description: Introduction to the fundamental principles and methods of digital image processing of remotely sensed data. Algorithms are discussed for preprocessing, enhancement, and classification mapping of digital data for agricultural, urban, geological, and environmental problems.
subject: GEOG
prereq: Prerequisites: GEOG 551 and course in computer programming.

title: Remote Sensing Modeling and Analysis
description: Satellite-based information extraction; programming skills for digital image processing; self-developed modeling approaches; quantitative analysis of remote sensing data.
subject: GEOG
prereq: Prerequisites: GEOG 575 or equivalent.

title: Seminar in Geographic Information Systems
description: Theory and application of modern automated approaches to handling geographic data. Includes computer oriented procedures for the input, analysis and display of spatial data.
subject: GEOG
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in GEOG 563 or equivalent.

title: Historical and Contemporary Geographic Thought
description: A survey of (1) the philosophical and intellectual foundations of Geography as a discipline, and (2) contemporary ideas and debates in major subfields of geographic research.
subject: GEOG

title: Advanced Directed Individual Studies in Geography
description: Advanced directed research by a PhD student on geographical topics to be individually supervised by graduate faculty. This course may be taken for 1-3 credit hours of independent study by a student working closely with a faculty member on a specific research project to be defined and agreed upon between the student and a supervising faculty member.
subject: GEOG

title: Advanced Seminar in Human Geography
description: Reading intensive seminar focused on conceptual frontiers and methodological debates in contemporary human geography with a secondary emphasis on intradisciplinary and cross-disciplinary affinities.
subject: GEOG
prereq: Prerequisites: any 700-level GEOG seminar course.

title: Advanced Seminar in Regional Geography
description: Advanced reading and discussion of the physical, economic, social and/or cultural geography of major selected world regions.
subject: GEOG

title: Advanced Seminar in Environmental Geography
description: A research seminar where students critically evaluate relevant literature, develop a research proposal, and complete a related research project in environmental geography.
subject: GEOG
prereq: Prerequisites: GEOG 730.

title: Advanced Seminar in Cartography
description: A topic central to cartography will be studied. Students will critically evaluate pertinent literature, develop a research proposal, and complete a related research project.
subject: GEOG

title: Advanced Seminar in Physical Geography
description: Research and discussion on various topics in physical geography. Literature varies with seminar topic but will include prevailing theories, data types, and modeling strategies in climatology, meteorology, hydrology, biogeography, soils, or geomorphology.
subject: GEOG
prereq: Prerequisites: GEOG 547 or GEOG 746.

title: Advanced Seminar in Remote Sensing
description: Advanced reading and discussion in the following areas - 1) the theoretical bases of remote sensing; 2) remote sensing of biophysical variables such as plant and soil temperatures and moisture content; 3) advanced principles of optical and digital image processing; and 4) economic aspects of remote sensing of the environment.
subject: GEOG

title: Advanced Seminar in Geographic Information Systems
description: A research seminar in which students conduct a detailed analysis of specific aspects of geographical data handling. This will include the design, implementation, and management of an operational geographical information system.
subject: GEOG

title: Introduction to the Earth
description: Origin and nature of the earth with emphasis on internal processes and phenomena such as earthquakes, volcanoes, and mountain building; surface processes, including landform evolution. Three lectures and three laboratory hours each week.
subject: GEOL

title: Fossils and the Evolution of Life on Earth
description: Basic overview of fossils, including dinosaurs, and their importance for understanding earth history and the evolution of life. Three lectures and three laboratory hours each week.
subject: GEOL

title: Environment of the Earth
description: Analysis of basic energy cycles of the earth. Interaction of human activity with earth processes to affect the environment. Three lectures and three laboratory hours each week. Field trips required.
subject: GEOL

title: Cultural Geology
description: The growth of geological concepts, scientific and non-scientific. The impact of geological factors on human affairs. The role of time and evolution (biological and physical). Restricted to non-science majors.
subject: GEOL

title: The Water Planet
description: Introduction to the origin of water and water properties, water in the universe, water in the solar system, water on Earth and its geological history.
subject: GEOL

title: Observing the Earth
description: An introduction to study of the earth through observation of ancient and modern earth systems in a field setting. Field trips required.
subject: GEOL

title: Earth Resources
description: Mineral, energy, and water resources with emphasis on geological processes governing their distribution. Intended for non-science majors. Three lecture hours each week with occasional field trips.
subject: GEOL

title: Coastal Environments of the Southeastern U.S.
description: Coastal zones of South Carolina and neighboring states, including geologic history, geomorphology, stratigraphy, hydrogeology, shoreline processes, environmental issues, and effects of man. Not available for geological science or marine science major credit. Three lecture hours each week plus optional field trips.
subject: GEOL

title: Coastal Environments of the Southeastern U.S. (Laboratory)
description: Exercises examining coastal ecology, geomorphology, hydrogeology, shoreline processes, environmental issues, and human impact. Not available for marine science major credit. Two laboratory hours per week. Scheduled field trips required.
subject: GEOL

title: Geology of the National Parks
description: Examination of the geologic setting and scientific significance of selected National Parks. Three lecture hours.
subject: GEOL

title: Rocks and Minerals
description: Chemical and physical processes of mineral formation in earth systems including an overview of igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic rock-forming processes. Includes laboratory. Field trips required.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOL 101, GEOL 103, or GEOL 201; CHEM 111 recommended.

title: Earth Systems through Time
description: Survey of earth history, the evolution of continents and oceans, the history of life, and geological dating methods. Includes laboratory and recitation. Required field trips. Taught alternate years.
subject: GEOL

title: Surface and Subsurface Hydrology
description: Environmental considerations of the hydrologic cycle. Occurrence and movement of surface water and groundwater as they relate to water budgets, water supply, and ecosystems. Geochemistry of natural waters, water quality, and pollution.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or higher in MATH 122 or MATH 141; C or higher in GEOL 101, GEOL 103, GEOL 201, GEOG 104, GEOG 201, ENVR 101, ENVR 201, ECIV 101 or MSCI 101.

title: Surface and Near Surface Processes
description: Overview of groundwater, surface water hydrology, sediment transport, river systems, and coastal processes. Includes laboratory and recitation. Required field trips.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in PHYS 201 or PHYS 211.

title: Field Studies in Geology
description: Directed field studies of extraordinary geological locations in North America. Requires a seven- to nine-day field trip during spring break.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOL 101, GEOL 103, or GEOL 201 and consent of instructor.

title: Stratigraphy and Sedimentary Basins
description: Overview of sedimentary basins, sediment transport, sedimentation, depositional environments, stratigraphy, seismic stratigraphy, eustacy, and sedimentary petrology. Includes laboratory and recitation. Required field trips.
subject: GEOL

title: Processes of Global Environmental Change
description: The science of global change, its relation to the hydrosphere, atmosphere, lithosphere, and biosphere. Global system science, biogeochemical cycles, paleoclimatology, glaciation, and eustacy.
subject: GEOL

title: Igneous and Metamorphic Processes
description: Origin and significance of igneous and metamorphic rocks, and relation to tectonic processes. Mineralogy, geochemistry, volcanism, plate tectonics, isostasy, heat flow. Includes laboratory and recitation. Required field trips.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOL 302; D or better in MATH 122 or MATH 141.

title: Structural Geology and Tectonics
description: Geologic structures and deformation of Earth materials. Stress and strain, deformation mechanisms, P-T-t paths, geologic maps, and structural regimes in plate tectonics. Includes laboratory and recitation. Required field trips.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOL 302; D or better in PHYS 201 or PHYS 211.

title: SEOE Peer Mentorship, Leadership, Professional Development
description: Exploration of post-graduation opportunities in academia and industry. Interactive discussion of topics including helping skills, active listening, oral communication, goal setting, interviewing, and building a strong professional portfolio.
subject: GEOL

title: Data Science in Earth, Ocean and Environmental Science
description: Computational analysis of earth, marine, and environmental datasets applying time series analysis, regression, filtering, and statistical analysis.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CSCE 106 or higher; C or better in MATH 122 or higher.

title: A View of the River
description: Introduction to terrestrial and tidal river morphology and processes, with case studies of South Carolina. Field trips required.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOL 101, GEOL 103, or GEOL 201.

title: Independent Study
description: Contract approved by instructor, advisor, and department chair is required for undergraduate students.
subject: GEOL

title: Internship in the Geological Science
description: Supervised internship experience in a professional environment within the field of geology and/or related geological sciences. Undergraduate internship contract required with approval of instructor, advisor, and department head before registration.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOL 101, GEOL 103 or GEOL 201; C or better in one other GEOL course at or above the 300 level.

title: Undergraduate Research
description: Student research on problems of regional and fundamental significance, supervised by a faculty member of the student’s choice. Emphasis is on the development of critical thinking and lucid scientific report writing.
subject: GEOL

title: Undergraduate Research
description: Student research on problems of regional and fundamental significance, supervised by a faculty member of the student’s choice. Emphasis is on the development of critical thinking and lucid scientific report writing.
subject: GEOL

title: Field Geology
description: Geological field techniques including the use of field instruments and the preparation of geologic maps. Written and oral reports required.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOL 325 and GEOL 355.

title: Introduction to Field Geology
description: Introduction to geological field techniques and review of core field geology concepts in preparation for an extended field experience.
subject: GEOL

title: Principles of Geomorphology
description: The process of earth denudation with emphasis on chemistry of weathering, stream and erosion hydraulics, quantitative analysis of land form evolution.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOL 101.

title: Principles of Coastal Geomorphology
description: Geological and physical controls on the morphology, development, and stability of coastlines. Analysis of waves and erosional processes, and coastal zone morphodynamics. Several required field trips.
subject: GEOL

title: Regional Stratigraphy and Biostratigraphy of North America
description: Sedimentologic, biostratigraphic, and tectonic history of North America, approached from paleogeographic considerations with emphasis on the Atlantic Coastal Plain and Continental Margin. Three hours lecture and three hours recitation per week. Required field trips.
subject: GEOL

title: Climate Geoengineering
description: This course will discuss the urgent need for deploying solar radiation and carbon dioxide removal approaches at scale, including potential benefits and risks of these options. It will also discuss regulatory and governance considerations at both the national and international level and strategizes to incentivize large-scale adoption of these approaches.
subject: GEOL

title: Advanced Paleontology
description: Systematic, ecologic, biogeographic, and evolutionary aspects of paleontology; lectures, practical exercises, field trips.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOL 305.

title: Marine Micropaleontology
description: Marine microfossils; distribution, ecology, paleoecology, and biostratigraphy; use of microfossils in marine sediments to study oceanographic history. Three lectures and two laboratory hours per week.
subject: GEOL

title: Sedimentology
description: Modern concepts of sediment composition, sedimentary facies, depositional environments, and stratigraphy. Includes laboratory.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOL 325.

title: Surface to Subsurface Stratigraphy
description: Surface to subsurface stratigraphic interpretation and techniques; litho- and biostratigraphy; geophysical log interpretation and subsurface presentation.
subject: GEOL

title: Isotope Geology and Geochronology
description: Dating techniques for Pleistocene deposits, sediments, archaeological materials, igneous and metamorphic rocks.
subject: GEOL

title: Introduction to Geochemistry
description: Investigation of low temperature chemical reactions controlling the geochemistry of the earth’s surface. Emphasis on CO2, carbonates, oxidation-reduction, thermodynamics, isotopes, biogeochemistry.
subject: GEOL

title: Environmental Radioisotope Geochemistry
description: Introduction to radioactivity and the use of radionuclides to study environmental processes, including age-dating and biogeochemical cycling in aquatic systems.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CHEM 111, CHEM 112 and MATH 141.

title: Igneous Petrology
description: Petrography and petrogenesis of igneous rocks; evolution of contrasting petrotectonic terranes. Three lectures and three laboratory hours per week.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOL 302.

title: Metamorphic Petrology
description: Petrography and petrogenesis of metamorphic rocks in orogenic belts. Three lectures and three laboratory hours per week.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOL 302.

title: Plate Tectonics
description: Geological and geophysical evidence for plate tectonics, detailed development of the plate tectonics model, and present areas of research, including measurements of plate motion using satellite geodesy.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in two GEOL courses numbered 300 or above, or consent of instructor.

title: Field Methods in Geophysics
description: Application of two or more geophysical field methods to a current geological problem. Independent study contract required.
subject: GEOL

title: Earth Science for Teachers I
description: Origin, internal structure and internal processes of the earth, including plate tectonics, earthquakes, volcanoes, and mountain building. Required field trips, two lectures, and three lab hours per week. Cannot be used in MS or PhD programs in geology.
subject: GEOL

title: Earth Science for Teachers II
description: Surface processes acting on the earth; introduction to weather and climate, weathering, erosion, and sedimentary processes; land form evolution; ocean currents and tides, near-shore geologic processes. Required field trips, two lecture, and three lab hours per week. Cannot be used in MS or PhD programs in geology.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in EDSE 548 or C or better in GEOL 540.

title: Methods in Geoscience Education Research
description: Introduction to methods used in discipline-based education research and their application to research questions in the geosciences.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in least one course in GEOL, ENVR, MSCI or GEOG.

title: Geological Oceanography
description: A comprehensive study of the origin and development of the major structural features of the ocean basins and the continental margins. Discussion of the techniques used in obtaining geologic data and the interpretation of sedimentary processes, vulcanism, and the stratigraphy of the ocean basins.
subject: GEOL

title: Marine Geophysics
description: Introduction to the nature and structure of the ocean floor as revealed by geophysical techniques. Two hours lecture and three hours laboratory.
subject: GEOL

title: Environmental Geophysics
description: Practical geophysical techniques for exploring the shallow subsurface. Seismic, resistivity, well log, gravity, magnetic method. Includes lectures and field exercises to collect and analyze data.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in MATH 141; D or better in PHYS 201 or PHYS 211.

title: Sedimentary Simulations and Sequence Stratigraphy
description: Problems of sequence stratigraphy resolved with graphic computer simulations. Sedimentary fill of basins by carbonates and/or clastics tracked as a function of rate of sediment accumulation, tectonic behavior, and sea level. Includes laboratory.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOL 325.

title: Marine Sediments
description: Marine sedimentary environments; physical/biological factors which control the formation and distribution of modern marine sediments.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOL 516.

title: Applied Seismology
description: Theory of seismic wave propagation. Seismic reflection data acquisition, processing, and interpretation.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in MATH 141; D or better in PHYS 201 or PHYS 211.

title: Elementary Seismology
description: Basic elements of seismology. Mathematical development of seismic wave equations; measurement, description, and interpretation of seismic data.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in MATH 241.

title: Seismic Reflection Interpretation
description: The interpretation of geologic structure using seismic sections. Recognition of apparent structure caused by velocity anomalies, multiples, and complex reflector geometry. Application to hydrocarbon exploration.
subject: GEOL

title: Coastal Processes
description: Physical and geological processes controlling the formation and evolution of beach, barrier, and nearshore environments, including discussion of coastal management issues.
subject: GEOL

title: Earth Resource Management
description: An approach to problems of resource management by lecture and seminar using case studies in mineral, energy, hydrogeological, and environmental science.
subject: GEOL

title: Environmental Field Geology
description: An introduction to field methods in sedimentology, structural geology, hydrogeology and geophysics with special reference to geological hazards and environmental problems.
subject: GEOL

title: Long-Term Environmental Change
description: Climatic changes of the past and their impact on the physical landscape, with an emphasis on the Quaternary period.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in a 200-level course in physical geography or geology or equivalent.

title: Introduction to Micrometeorology
description: Small-scale processes in the atmospheric boundary layers, including energy budget, radiation, soil heat transfer, humidity, viscous flows, turbulence, momentum and heat exchanges, evaporation, and marine atmospheric boundary layer.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PHYS 201 and MATH 141.

title: Environmental Hydrogeology
description: Environmental considerations of the hydrologic cycle, occurrence and movement of ground water, aquifer analysis, and water well emplacement and construction. Water quality, pollution parameters, and the geochemistry of selected natural systems. The effects of environmental problems, waste disposal, and urban development upon the aqueous geochemical regime.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOL 101 and D or better in CHEM 111 or their equivalents.

title: Soil Hydrology
description: Saturated and unsaturated water flow through soils, pore pressure development, runoff generation, and watershed response to rainfall. Three lecture and three laboratory hours per week.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in PHYS 202 and MATH 142.

title: Numerical Modeling for Earth Science Applications
description: Finite difference and finite element methods for solving the diffusion equation and advection-dispersion equation, with applications in hydrogeology, geophysics, geology, and marine science.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in MATH 142; MATH 241 is recommended.

title: Air-Sea Interaction
description: The physical mechanism responsible for interaction between the ocean and the atmosphere and the influence of air-sea interaction on atmospheric and oceanic dynamics and thermodynamics on a wide variety of spatial/temporal scales.
subject: GEOL

title: Satellite Oceanography
description: This course provides knowledge of various techniques used in satellite remote sensing of the oceans. Key skills will be developed in satellite data processing, image analysis, and hands-on research.
subject: GEOL

title: Estuarine Oceanography
description: Estuarine kinematics and dynamics; classification of estuaries; estuarine circulation and mixing. Scheduled field trips are required.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in MSCI 314.

title: Marine Hydrodynamics
description: Basic principles of fluid statics and dynamics. Conservation of mass, momentum, and energy; viscosity, vorticity, and boundary layers with examples from the marine environment. Applications to and analysis of ocean currents and waves.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in MATH 241 and C or better in either PHYS 201 or PHYS 211.

title: Geology and Geochemistry of Salt Marshes
description: Geological and geochemical processes in salt marshes. Methods of geological research in marshes including instrumental techniques, sampling design, and data analysis. Two lectures per week plus four weekends of project oriented fieldwork and/or equivalent lab work. Scheduled field trips are required.
subject: GEOL

title: Topics in Geological Science
description: Current developments in geological science selected to meet faculty and student interests. Course content varies and will be announced by title in schedule of courses.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in GEOL 101, GEOL 103 or GEOL 201 or graduate student standing in the School of the Earth, Ocean and Environment (SEOE).

title: Senior Seminar in Geology and Geophysics
description: Advanced research topics in geology and geophysics; critical reading of literature, technical presentations, and written reports. Senior standing.
subject: GEOL

title: Electron Microscopy and Microanalysis
description: SEM, ESEM, TEM, and EMPA, WDS quantitative analysis, EDS semi-quantitative analysis, EBSD, methods of sample preparation, and applications in varieties of disciplines. Two lecture and three laboratory hours per week.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in CHEM 111 or equivalent.

title: Senior Thesis
description: Senior capstone experience, research on a problem on fundamental significance, supervised by faculty member; must include field study component, written final project report, and oral presentation at departmental seminar.
subject: GEOL

title: Geology of South Carolina
description: Survey of the surficial, coastal, and bedrock geology of South Carolina, its regional physiographic and tectonic setting, and the natural resources of the state.
subject: GEOL

title: Environmental Earth Science for Teachers
description: The hydrologic cycle in geologic settings of this region, and the effects of urbanization and industrialization on groundwater, rivers, and coasts. The vulnerability of urban and industrial systems to natural geologic processes. Two lecture and three laboratory hours per week. Not available for graduate credit for students in M.S. or Ph.D. programs in geological sciences.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: introductory course in any of the earth sciences.

title: Field Studies in Pleistocene and Holocene Geology for Teachers
description: Two weekend field courses dealing with Pleistocene and Holocene coastal geology, plate tectonics, sea-level change, global circulation patterns, shoreline change since 1850, and nearshore processes.
subject: GEOL

title: Field Studies for Teachers in Natural and Altered Barrier Island Systems
description: Two weekend field courses dealing with barrier island and associated marsh environments, marsh productivity, the dune-beach-bar system, shoreline stabilization, and nearshore processes on natural and armored shorelines.
subject: GEOL

title: Paleoclimatology
description: An overview of Earth’s climate history during Cenozoic. Emphasis will be placed on Pleistocene glacial-interglacial climate variability and understanding the proxies used to reconstruct past climate changes.
subject: GEOL

title: Stable Isotope Geochemistry
description: Introduction to the analysis of stable isotopes of hydrogen, oxygen, carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur using mass spectrometry. Emphasis will be on the use of these isotopes in geological problems.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: GEOL 521.

title: Eustasy and Global Variations in Sequence Stratigraphy
description: Relationship of sequence stratigraphy to sea level variations, tectonics and sedimentation. Construction and analyses of paleogeographic maps, regional cross-sections, and chronostratigraphic charts.
subject: GEOL

title: Organic Geochemistry
description: Sources, transport, and fate of organic matter in natural environments including soils, riverine, estuarine, coastal and open ocean sediments and waters.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: GEO 521L/MSCI 521.

title: Crystal Chemistry and Mineral Structure
description: Principles of atomic structure and chemical variation of minerals.
subject: GEOL

title: Aqueous Geochemistry
description: This course was not found in the supplied content but was listed in the program requirements. If possible, please provide us with the correct information.
subject: GEOL

title: Solid Earth Processes
description: Examination of the structure and dynamics of the Earth’s interior combining perspectives from geophysics and geochemistry. Focus on the lithospheric cycle.
subject: GEOL

title: Igneous Processes and Crustal Genesis
description: An investigation of igneous processes and their role in crustal genesis and evolution.
subject: GEOL

title: Advanced Structural Geology
description: A study of the deformation of the earth’s crust including mechanics of folding, faulting, jointing, and cleavage formation. Consideration of current theories of orogenesis in relation to geophysical evidence for the structure of the earth’s crust, mantle, and core.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: GEOL 331 and GEOL 536.

title: Rock Mechanics
description: Behavior of rocks and minerals up to 10kb, 8000°C. Role of internal pore pressure and time. Interplay of theory and empiricism.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: MATH 300.

title: Regional Tectonics
description: Integrated analysis (from both model and case history approaches) of the regional structural geology of selected classic areas and analysis of the interaction of tectonic and sedimentary processes in the production of the sedimentary sequences of selected geosynclines and basins. Weekend field trips.
subject: GEOL

title: Decision Making in Environmental Resource Management
description: Environmental project planning and management. Types and magnitudes of environmental problems; environmental pathways; environmental data acquisition and analysis; protection versus restoration; risk assessment; site assessment.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: GEOL 560.

title: Decision Making in Energy Resource Management
description: An integrative seminar for science managers. Consideration of the technical, managerial, and financial aspects of decision making in geologic enterprises, with emphasis on hydrocarbon exploration.
subject: GEOL

title: Petroleum Geology
description: An introduction to exploring for oil and natural gas; concentration on specific regions with energy resources.
subject: GEOL

title: Basin Analysis Seminar
description: Development of the stratigraphic systems; detailed analysis of the aims, working methods, and relations between litho-, bio-, and chronostratigraphy. Three lecture hours per week with occasional field trips.
subject: GEOL

title: Carbonate Petrology
description: Detailed analysis of the processes and products of carbonate sedimentation, diagenesis, and lithification, with special emphasis upon the role of organisms in forming carbonate sediments and sedimentary rocks. Three lecture hours per week with occasional field trips.
subject: GEOL

title: Sandstone Petrology
description: Sandstone properties as a response to geologic processes. Relationships between the porous microstructure of sandstones and fluid transport. Automated petrography using image analysis and pattern recognition procedures.
subject: GEOL

title: Oceanographic Techniques
description: Shipboard experience with basic techniques used by geological, physical, chemical, and biological oceanographers.
subject: GEOL

title: Environmental Measurements and Analysis
description: A field and laboratory course designed to acquaint students with basic techniques needed to measure and analyze various biotic and abiotic environmental parameters in estuarine and shallow water habitats. One lecture and six laboratory hours per week.
subject: GEOL

title: Analysis of Geological Data
description: Principles used in processing, smoothing, correlating and contouring geological data and simulating geologic processes.
subject: GEOL

title: Seismic Reflection Interpretation
description: The interpretation of regional stratigraphy and structure using seismic sections. Recognition of stratigraphic sequences, sedimentary facies, and extensional and compressional structures. Application to hydrocarbon exploration.
subject: GEOL

title: Advanced Seismology
description: Advanced treatment of elastic wave propagation, ray theory, normal modes, and free oscillations; applications to determine earth structure, modeling of earthquakes.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: GEOL 555 or equivalent.

title: Ground Water Geology
description: The evaluation of aquifer characteristics by flow nets, Theis equation and graphic solution technique for water table and artesian conditions. Methodology of pumping tests and data collection. Prediction of aquifer response through time. Analog and computer analysis and interpretation of data.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: GEOL 570 or equivalent.

title: Topics in Hydrogeology
description: Selected topics germane to the qualitative and quantitative aspects of the hydrologic cycle.
subject: GEOL

title: Geologic Theories
description: Survey of the origin and development of geologic principles.
subject: GEOL

title: Water Quality and Pollution
description: The nature of water; physical, chemical, and biological quality parameters. Techniques of quantitative analysis, methods of water quality control, and pollution abatement. Hydrogeochemical interactions and effects on water quality and waste disposal.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: GEOL 570 or equivalent.

title: Solute Transport in Geologic Media
description: Processes influencing conservative and reactive transport of solutes through porous media. Geochemistry of natural waters; transport processes for geologic and environmental/contaminant problems; mathematical equations; numerical methods; field techniques.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: GEOL 570 or ECIV 563.

title: Numerical Methods in Subsurface Hydrology
description: Formation of groundwater flow and solute transport problems, theory and practice, numerical methods, solution techniques.
subject: GEOL

title: Physical Oceanography
description: Geographic and hydrodynamic aspects of oceanography, with emphasis on estuaries. Physical properties of sea water and theories and methods involved in ocean currents, air-sea interaction, waves, and tides.
subject: GEOL

title: Chemical Oceanography
description: Chemical characteristics of sea water, distribution of properties, and chemical processes in the oceans, with emphasis on estuaries.
subject: GEOL

title: Oceanographic Time Series Analysis
description: Techniques in the analysis of oceanographic data sequences, including filtering techniques, fast Fourier transformers, and empirical orthogonal functions.
subject: GEOL

title: Geophysical Fluid Dynamics
description: Equations governing the large-scale dynamics of the atmosphere and ocean, rotational influence, shallow water equations, vorticity, quasi-geostrophic dynamics, Rossby waves, energy and enstrophy, and geostrophic turbulence.
subject: GEOL
prereq: Prerequisites: MATH 241 or ECIV 360 or GEOL 582/MSCI 582 or GEOL 781/MSCI 781.

title: Atmospheric Dynamics
description: Elementary applications of the basic equations, scale analysis, planetary boundary layer, atmospheric oscillations, synoptic and mesoscale systems, hydrodynamic instability, cyclogenesis, frontogenesis, energy cycle, momentum budget, and tropical motion systems.
subject: GEOL

title: Directed Individual Studies in Geology
description: Directed research topics to be individually assigned and supervised by graduate faculty.
subject: GEOL

title: Seminar (General Geology)
description: Required of all graduate students.
subject: GEOL

title: Seminar in Paleobotany
description: Readings and discussions on current topics.
subject: GEOL

title: Seminar in Stratigraphy
description: Critical analysis of recent papers dealing with the reconstruction of marine paleoenvironments based on deep sea sediments. Emphasis will be placed on specific intervals of geologic time. Two discussion hours per week.
subject: GEOL

title: Seminar in Earth and Ocean Science Education
description: Interactive community outreach and middle school geoscience education for graduate students interested in outreach at the K-12 level.
subject: GEOL

title: Seminar in Geophysics
description: Seminar related to current topics in geophysics.
subject: GEOL

title: Seminar in Tectonophysics
description: Readings and discussion on current tectonophysical problems.
subject: GEOL

title: Data Presentation Workshop
description: Preparation and presentation, oral and written, of geological data, discussed via examples from students’ own work and from published material.
subject: GEOL

title: Beginning German I

Restricted to those who have never studied German or who have placed by examination into GERM 109. Credit may be received only for one of the following GERM 109; GERM110; GERM 111 ; GERM 121 .
subject: GERM

title: Beginning German II
description: Introduction to grammar and practical vocabulary necessary for fundamental communication skills. Credit may be received only for one of the following: GERM 109 ; GERM 110; GERM 111 ; GERM 121 .
subject: GERM

title: Intensive Beginning German
description: Intensive introduction to grammar and practical vocabulary necessary for fundamental communication skills. Admission only to highly motivated beginning students who obtain permission of the department. Credit may be received only for one of the following: GERM 109 and GERM 110 ; GERM 111; GERM 121 .
subject: GERM

title: Elementary German
description: Grammar and vocabulary necessary for fundamental communication skills. Assumes prior experience in German. Admission only by proficiency examination. Credit may be received for only one of the following: GERM 109 ; GERM 110 ; GERM 111 ; GERM 121.

title: Basic Proficiency in German

prereq: Prerequisites: GERM 122, or satisfactory score on Basic Proficiency Phase II placement test.

description: Reading and translation of texts in of the Old Germanic languages: Old High German 711H, Old Saxon 711S, Old Norse 711N, or Gothic 711G. May be repeated for credit with a different letter suffix from options noted above. Reading knowledge of Moderna German required.
subject: GERM

description: Content Varies.

title: German Seminar
description: Content Varies.

subject: GLST

subject: GLST

subject: GLST

subject: GLST

subject: GLST

subject: GLST

subject: GLST

title: Integrated Practice of Medicine Ia
description: The first half of the M1 IPM Course (IPM-1a): The Integrated Practice of Medicine modules are part of a longitudinal multi-year curriculum that prepares students for the multi-faceted aspects of clinical medicine including application of basic science knowledge, clinical proficiency in physical diagnosis, diagnostic testing and fundamental therapeutic procedures, patient interactions, population health, effective communication skills, behavioral and social considerations, team-based health care delivery, ethical issues, professionalism, life-long learning, research and evidence-based practice. In the IPM-1a module, weekly clinical material is presented in a “Case of the Week” format, and provides an introduction to history taking, physical examination skills, clinical reasoning, and patient-centered care. The structure of this module focuses on a team-based approach to clinical problem-solving that includes the application of basic science knowledge and social science concepts to patient care. Weekly, student teams will focus on a clinical case and identify the problem, formulate hypotheses, and develop a comprehensive approach to the case. Students will examine the role of psychological, social, behavioral, economic, and environmental factors in the health of individuals and populations. Through this process, IPM-1a will promote an understanding of patients in their varying contexts: as individuals, in a societal context, within the healthcare delivery system, and as members of a larger population. The premise of the material is that an understanding of the multifactorial aspects of health and the need to understand patients as individuals is necessary for the provision of high quality, patient-centered care. Students will learn to recognize opportunities for personal growth as well as self- identify signs of stress and identify skills of managing stress. Throughout the year, students will learn effective history taking communication skills and to perform physician examinations. Students will begin to develop strategies to formulate differential diagnoses and explain the underlying basic science principles that lead to their hypotheses. Students will begin to develop the foundation for clinical research literacy and practice evidence-based strategies to support their differential diagnoses for each case.
subject: GMED

title: Integrated Practice of Medicine Ib
description: The second half of the M1 IPM Course (IPM-1b): The Integrated Practice of Medicine modules are part of a longitudinal multi-year curriculum that prepares students for the multi-faceted aspects of clinical medicine including application of basic science knowledge, clinical proficiency in physical diagnosis, diagnostic testing and fundamental therapeutic procedures, patient interactions, population health, effective communication skills, behavioral and social considerations, team-based health care delivery, ethical issues, professionalism, life-long learning, research and evidence-based practice. In the IPM-1b module, weekly clinical material is presented in a “Case of the Week” format, and provides an introduction to history taking, physical examination skills, clinical reasoning, and patient-centered care. The structure of this module focuses on a team-based approach to clinical problem-solving that includes the application of basic science knowledge and social science concepts to patient care. Weekly, student teams will focus on a clinical case and identify the problem, formulate hypotheses, and develop a comprehensive approach to the case. Students will examine the role of psychological, social, behavioral, economic, and environmental factors in the health of individuals and populations. Through this process, IPM-1b will promote an understanding of patients in their varying contexts: as individuals, in a societal context, within the healthcare delivery system, and as members of a larger population. The premise of the material is that an understanding of the multifactorial aspects of health and the need to understand patients as individuals is necessary for the provision of high quality, patient-centered care. Students will learn to recognize opportunities for personal growth as well as self- identify signs of stress and identify skills of managing stress. Throughout the year, students will learn effective history taking communication skills and to perform physician examinations. Students will begin to develop strategies to formulate differential diagnoses and explain the underlying basic science principles that lead to their hypotheses. Students will begin to develop the foundation for clinical research literacy and practice evidence-based strategies to support their differential diagnoses for each case.
subject: GMED

title: Emergency Medical Technician
description: The Emergency Medical Technician (EMT) module is a minimum state-required 200-hour program that teaches the practice of emergency healthcare delivery to patients who become unexpectedly ill or injured. The module focuses on the role and competencies of the prehospital care provider and exposes the student to the many psychological, social, behavioral, economic, environmental, and lifestyle-related factors in the health of individuals and populations. Following successful module completion, students will take a National Registry of Emergency Medical Technicians (NREMT) psychomotor skills and written examination and become certified as a South Carolina EMT.
subject: GMED

title: Foundations in Medicine I
description: The Foundations of Medicine I module presents initial scientific and laboratory methods to explore the biochemical, molecular, and genetic basis of health and disease. Molecular, genetic, and genomic testing modalities will be discussed, with initial application to associated disorders provided.
subject: GMED

title: Foundations of Medicine II
description: The Foundations of Medicine II module continues to build upon Foundations of Medicine I by incorporating additional core concepts essential to understanding the development, organization, and structural elements of the human body from a clinically relevant perspective. Students will develop a clear understanding of the dynamic relationship between function and structure by examining the molecular and cellular interactions required for tissue formation and function and the differentiation of tissues into organ systems, adding additional principles integrating cell biology, immunology, histology, microbiology, lifestyle behaviors, and introductory pathophysiology and pathology material, providing the basis of disorders to be covered in the organ systems, as well as basic concepts of pharmacology to introduce students to the therapeutics of disease. Students will also acquire core knowledge in population health, biostatistics, and epidemiology to promote the application of evidence-based medicine and lifelong learning.
subject: GMED

title: Integrated Practice of Medicine IIa
description: The first half of the M2 IPM course (IPM-2a): The Integrated Practice of Medicine modules are part of a longitudinal multi-year curriculum that prepares students for the multi-faceted aspects of clinical medicine including application of basic science knowledge, clinical proficiency in physical diagnosis, diagnostic testing and fundamental therapeutic procedures , patient interactions, population health, effective communication skills, behavioral and social considerations, team-based health care delivery, ethical issues, professionalism, life-long learning, research and evidence-based practice. In the IPM-2a module weekly clinical material is presented in a “Case of the Week” format, and provides an introduction to history taking, physical examination skills, clinical reasoning, and patient-centered care including cost effective knowledge of appropriate diagnostic studies and fundamental therapeutic procedures. The structure of this module focuses on a team-based approach to clinical problem-solving that includes the application of basic science knowledge and social science concepts to patient care. Weekly, student teams will focus on a clinical case and identify the problem, formulate hypotheses, and develop a comprehensive approach to the case. Students will examine the role of psychological, social, behavioral, economic, and environmental factors in the health of individuals and populations. Through this process, IPM-2a will promote an understanding of patients in their varying contexts: as individuals, in a societal context, within the healthcare delivery system, and as members of a larger population. The premise of the material is that an understanding of the multifactorial aspects of health and the need to understand patients as individuals is necessary for the provision of high quality, patient-centered care. Students will learn to recognize opportunities for personal growth as well as self- identify signs of stress and identify skills of managing stress. Students will learn the role other professionals play in coordinated patient care. Throughout the year, students will learn to perform physician examinations understand the role of appropriate diagnostic studies and procedures including fundamental therapeutic procedures. Students will begin to develop strategies to formulate differential diagnoses and explain the underlying basic science principles that lead to their hypotheses. Students will build on their foundation for clinical research literacy and practice evidence-based strategies to support their differential diagnoses for each case.
subject: GMED

title: Integrated Practice of Medicine IIb
description: The second half of the M2 IPM course (IPM-2b): The Integrated Practice of Medicine modules are part of a longitudinal multi-year curriculum that prepares students for the multi-faceted aspects of clinical medicine including application of basic science knowledge, clinical proficiency in physical diagnosis, diagnostic testing and fundamental therapeutic procedures , patient interactions, population health, effective communication skills, behavioral and social considerations, team-based health care delivery, ethical issues, professionalism, life-long learning, research and evidence-based practice. In the IPM-2b module weekly clinical material is presented in a “Case of the Week” format, and provides an introduction to history taking, physical examination skills, clinical reasoning, and patient-centered care including cost effective knowledge of appropriate diagnostic studies and fundamental therapeutic procedures. The structure of this module focuses on a team-based approach to clinical problem-solving that includes the application of basic science knowledge and social science concepts to patient care. Weekly, student teams will focus on a clinical case and identify the problem, formulate hypotheses, and develop a comprehensive approach to the case. Students will examine the role of psychological, social, behavioral, economic, and environmental factors in the health of individuals and populations. Through this process, IPM-2b will promote an understanding of patients in their varying contexts: as individuals, in a societal context, within the healthcare delivery system, and as members of a larger population. The premise of the material is that an understanding of the multifactorial aspects of health and the need to understand patients as individuals is necessary for the provision of high quality, patient-centered care. Students will learn to recognize opportunities for personal growth as well as self- identify signs of stress and identify skills of managing stress. Students will learn the role other professionals play in coordinated patient care. Throughout the year, students will learn to perform physician examinations understand the role of appropriate diagnostic studies and procedures including fundamental therapeutic procedures. Students will begin to develop strategies to formulate differential diagnoses and explain the underlying basic science principles that lead to their hypotheses. Students will build on their foundation for clinical research literacy and practice evidence-based strategies to support their differential diagnoses for each case.
subject: GMED

title: Hematology and Oncology
description: The Hematology and Oncology module provides an overview of major diseases and abnormalities of red and white blood cells, lymph nodes, spleen and thymus, bleeding and clotting disorders, and therapeutic agents for neoplastic processes. Basic principles and characteristics of neoplasia, genetic, viral, and lifestyle-related disease etiology, clinical presentation, and evidence-based clinical prevention, treatment, and management will be emphasized. Gross and microscopic images will be examined and correlated with clinical findings. Laboratory studies pertinent to diagnosis, prognosis, and therapy selection will be discussed. Imaging techniques will be applied, as applicable, and compared with respect to the information they provide in the clinical analysis and treatment of these diseases and abnormalities.
subject: GMED

title: Molecular and Cellular Foundations of Medicine
description: This instructional module integrates the disciplines of molecular biology, genetics, cell biology, and biochemistry as the foundations of understanding the human body at the molecular and cellular level. Students will be introduced to cellular structure and its influence on cellular communication, organization and integration of cellular functions, utilization of biomolecules in metabolic pathways, and processes of cell division and transmission of genetic information. The goal of this module is to gain an understanding of the biology and biological processes of the human body from the molecular to cellular scale, processes and patterns of inheritance, and the role of the human genome and epigenome in medicine.
subject: GMED

title: Musculoskeletal, Dermatology, and Rheumatology
description: The Musculoskeletal, Dermatology and Rheumatology module provides the students with knowledge about the structure and diseases of the musculoskeletal system, the skin, and initial discussion of rheumatologic disorders. Cadaveric dissection and correlation with radiologic findings allow for intensive study of normal anatomy and correlation with abnormal processes. Students will be presented with the effects of lifestyle on musculoskeletal health, and the major diseases and illnesses affecting these systems, including infectious, neoplastic, environmental, and immunologic; as well as their signs and symptoms, histopathologic and clinical laboratory characteristics, and rationale for current therapeutic interventions.
subject: GMED

title: Biomedical Science Inquiry and Analysis
