prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in MATH 142 and D or better in one of the following: CSCE 102, CSCE 106, CSCE 145, CSCE 146, ENCP 201, ECIV 201, EMCH 201, ITEC 104, CSCE 104, or ITEC 352.

title: Independent Study
description: Individual investigation or studies of special topics. Requires contract approval.
subject: INDE

title: Quality Engineering
description: Quality tools and techniques employed to help prevent defects in engineered products, and to avoid problems when delivering solutions or services to customers.
subject: INDE
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in STAT 509 or higher; D or better in INDE 391.

title: Facilities Planning Material Handling
description: Methods to analyze and optimize facilities layout and the arrangement and movement of physical resources to support the production and distribution of goods and services.
subject: INDE
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in INDE 391.

title: Industrial Engineering Capstone Project
description: Open-ended team design experiences that develop the ability to develop, implement, and improve integrated systems that include people, materials, information, equipment, and energy. Real-world experiences and business perspectives.
subject: INDE
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in INDE 291, INDE 391, and INDE 392.

title: Special Topics in Industrial Engineering
description: Content varies and will be announced in the schedule of classes by section title. May be repeated for different topics.
subject: INDE

title: Smart Manufacturing
description: Advanced concepts of smart manufacturing: hardware infrastructure, cyber infrastructure, data infrastructure, industrial Internet of things, machine to machine network, machine vision, manufacturing event understanding.
subject: INDE
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in INDE 291 or EMCH 377 .

title: Supply Chain Engineering
description: Engineering analysis of the movement, production, and storage of raw materials, work-in-process inventory, finished goods, and services from point of origin to point of consumption or use.
subject: INDE
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in INDE 392.

title: Systems Simulation
description: Discrete event simulation methodology emphasizing the statistical basis for simulation modeling and analysis. Overview of computer languages and simulation design applied to various industrial situations.
subject: INDE
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in INDE 392.

title: Independent Study in Industrial Engineering
description: Individual investigation or studies of special topics. Requires contract approval.
subject: INDE

title: Advanced Special Topics in Industrial Engineering
description: Current developments in industrial engineering research and practice. Course content varies and will be announced in the schedule of classes by title.
subject: INDE

title: Study Abroad – USC Exchange
description: Keeps a USC student active while on a pre-approved USC exchange program.
subject: INTL

title: Study Abroad–Non-USC Program
description: This course keeps a USC student active while on a pre-approved non-USC study abroad program.
subject: INTL

title: Study Abroad–Through Another U.S. Institution
description: This course keeps a USC student active while on a pre-approved non-USC study abroad program through another U.S. institution of higher education.
subject: INTL

title: Data, Information Society
description: Major concepts, principles, theories, issues, and trends in the development of Information Science.
subject: ISCI

title: Information Literacy and Technology
description: An introduction to the basic information technologies used in all types of information organizations and the essential concepts and skills needed for information literacy.
subject: ISCI

title: AI: Cognitive Aspects and Social Impacts
description: Artificial intelligence (AI) focusing on the futurology of superior intelligence, misinformation, cognitive-bias, cyber decision-making, and other information science and communication concepts.
subject: ISCI

title: Ethics in the Era of Big Data
description: Ethical issues surrounding the use of data and information at the individual and organizational levels.
subject: ISCI

title: Principles of User Experience and Design (UX/UI)
description: An exploration of theoretical and practical foundations of user-centered design from both the design and research perspectives.
subject: ISCI

title: Information Design
description: Overview of responsive website design, development and basic content management systems. Examine the current tools and standards and learn how they function together in a modern web environment. Emphasis on the myriad of viewing devices and specific reference to the unique needs of information intensive institutions.
subject: ISCI

title: User Experience Research Methods and Tools
description: User behavior and priorities; gather, analyze, and communicate key insights to develop effective products and services.
subject: ISCI

title: Battling Misinformation, Deepfakes, and Conspiracy Theories
description: Relevant history and practical coverage of the news media; digital participation and democratic citizenship; systems of power and oppression; how individuals and groups interpret media frames and arguments.
subject: ISCI

title: Text Mining in Big Data Analytics
description: Introduction to the concepts, issues, theories, and techniques of information storage and retrieval systems.
subject: ISCI

title: Information Science Data Analysis and Evaluation
description: Overview of major types of research methods and techniques within the field of information science. Methods of data analysis, evaluation of published research, and ethical principles.
subject: ISCI

title: Cyberethics and Information Policy
description: Problems and ethical issues that arise in the development and implementation of information policies in Information Science.
subject: ISCI

title: Blockchain and Cryptocurrency
description: Introduces blockchain technology and its applications in various professional areas, businesses, and industries. No prior blockchain knowledge is required to be successful in this foundational course.
subject: ISCI

title: Blockchain, Privacy, and Decentralization
description: Introduces bitcoin, cryptocurrency, data privacy, self-sovereign identity, and other blockchain concepts and applications with in the context of professional areas, businesses, and industries. No prior blockchain knowledge is required to be successful in this foundational course.
subject: ISCI

title: Children’s Literature
description: A study of materials for children from birth through elementary school (age 13) with emphasis on the evaluation, selection, and use of those materials to meet the educational, cultural, and recreational needs of children.
subject: ISCI

title: Introduction to Computer Technology Applications for Info Env
description: The basic information technology concepts and applications relevant to library and related information environments. Unique information technology needs and applications of information-intensive organizations.
subject: ISCI

title: Cyber Security and Information Science
description: This course provides students with essential skills and training in information and cyber security leading to the option to complete the CCSA certification process through Check Point Software Technologies.
subject: ISCI

title: Special Topics in Information Science
description: Examination of selected current and emerging topics in the field of information science and data analytics. May be repeated for credit as topics vary.
subject: ISCI

title: Management in Data Driven Organizations
description: History, development, and implementation of theories and practices associated with managing information environments.
subject: ISCI

title: Knowledge Work as an Organizational Asset
description: Introduction to the background, principles, practices, and technologies of knowledge management for library and information professionals.
subject: ISCI
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ISCI 301.

title: Social Issues in Information and Communications Technologies
description: Examines the design, uses, and effects of information and communication technologies (ICTs) from the standpoint that society and technology mutually shape one another.
subject: ISCI

title: Information and Communication Needs and Assessment
description: An overview of the communication models, major concepts, trends, and other related issues of information transfer with a focus on information seeking and use in digital age.
subject: ISCI
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ISCI 201 or JOUR 101.

title: Information Management for Journalists
description: Online resources specific to mass communications, research strategies, organization and creation of digital information.
subject: ISCI

title: User-Centered Information Architecture
description: Processes and techniques for designing user-centered information systems on the Web. Issues of needs analysis, content development, cognitive models, human-computer interaction, interface design, and usability testing.
subject: ISCI
prereq: Prerequisites: ISCI 202.

title: Introduction to Knowledge Discovery
description: The students will review knowledge discovery basics concepts, techniques, tools, and applications. This course is project based and the students will develop new Wikipedia pages by reading papers in a selected domain.
subject: ISCI
prereq: Prerequisites: MATH 122 or MATH 141 or MATH 142 or MATH 170 or MATH 172 or STAT 515 or STAT 201 or STAT 205.

title: Planning and Sustaining Digital Projects
description: Theoretical and technological foundations of building the digital information infrastructure. Emphasis on technical aspects of managing digital assets for Intranet and Internet use.
subject: ISCI
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ISCI 202, ISCI 402.

title: Using Python for Information Science
description: Emerging concepts and techniques of information science using the Python programming language.
subject: ISCI
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in CSCE 104 or CSCE 106.

title: Competitive Intelligence
description: Strategies and techniques for locating competitive intelligence information.
subject: ISCI
prereq: Prerequisites: ISCI 201, ISCI 202, ISCI 301, ISCI 402.

title: Information Issues in Community Institutions
description: Problem of identifying or defining cultural heritage and the issues and problems in preserving, accessing, and managing cultural heritage information. Issues such as copyright/ownership, technical problems of preservation and intellectual access, and the different ways in which libraries, archives, museums, zoos and other cultural heritage institutions operate.
subject: ISCI

title: Emerging Topics in Information Science
description: Examination of selected current and emerging topics in the field of information science. May be repeated once for credit as topics vary.
subject: ISCI

title: Independent Study in Information Science
description: Independent study in an area of information science relevant to the students professional goals. May be repeated once for credit as topics vary.
subject: ISCI
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in any ISCI course.

title: Internship in Information Science
description: Supervised field experience in an information agency relevant to students professional goals. Approval of the appropriate internship application must be submitted early in the semester preceding enrollment.
subject: ISCI
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in any ISCI course.

title: Teaching and Training in Distributed Environments
description: Knowledge and skills for applying complementary technologies for learning in distributed learning environments (Pre-K-lifelong) through lecture, demonstration, and discussion.
subject: ISCI

title: Materials for Early Childhood
description: Media resources and techniques for children from birth to 9 years. Reading interests and developmental needs of young children. Authors, illustrators, indexes, bibliographic tools, evaluation sources, and professional literature. Not open to students enrolled in M.L.I.S. program.
subject: ISCI

title: Materials for Children
description: Media resources for children. Reading interests of children and their curricular and independent needs for information. Authors, illustrators, indexes, bibliographic tools, and sources of evaluation of materials for children. Techniques and literature for read-aloud programs and storytelling. Not open to students enrolled in M.L.I.S. program.
subject: ISCI

title: Materials for Adolescents
description: Media resources for adolescents. Reading interests of adolescents and their curricular and independent information needs. Study of relationships of media to information needs and critical comparison between classic and contemporary materials for adolescents. Indexes, bibliographic tools, and sources of evaluation of materials. Not open to students enrolled in M.L.I.S. program.
subject: ISCI

title: Special Topics in Library and Information Studies
description: Specific topics of current concern to the library, information, and media professions to be identified by title. Not open to students enrolled in M.L.I.S. program.
subject: ISCI

title: Applications of Information Technology and the Infrastructure
description: Introductory knowledge for school library media specialists, teachers, administrators, parents, and other citizens interested in practical applications of information technology to support learning, decision making, and community building.
subject: ISCI

title: Knowledge Discovery Techniques
description: Knowledge discovery techniques and applications.
subject: ISCI
prereq: Prerequisites: ISCI 434 for Undergraduate Students.

title: Data Visualization
description: Foster theoretical insights about data visualization. Prepare temporal, geospatial, and textual data for visual representations. In hands-on sessions, students practice data visualization techniques and apply them in a wide range of real-world scenarios.
subject: ISCI

title: Storytelling: Theory, Practice, and Development
description: Storytelling methods, techniques, and materials encompassing heritage, art, literature, and programming.
subject: ISCI

title: Information Behavior and Practices
description: Focuses on theories, models, and concepts of information behavior. Emphasizes information seeking and use practices and activities in relation particular communities, channels and barriers to information, and the impacts of technology. Provides an introduction to methods that can be used to study information needs, information seeking behavior, and related phenomena.
subject: ISCI

title: Ethics, Values, and Foundational Principles of Library and Information Science Professions
description: Introduction to the issues and core values of library and information professions, including equity of access, literacy and learning, information policy, collaboration, service, professional growth and development, and culturally responsive practice.
subject: ISCI

title: Community Engagement and Service
description: Explores the role of library and information organizations in communities, with a focus on building community relationships, engagement, and outreach.
subject: ISCI

title: Reference and Instruction
description: Introduction to the design and delivery of instructional services and assistance on the use of information resources to promote information literacy and informed decision-making.
subject: ISCI

title: Leadership in Information Organizations
description: Introduction to the nature, development, roles, and fundamental issues of leadership in library and information organizations.
subject: ISCI

title: Research Design and Evaluation
description: Introduces the research process as applied to library and information science topics with an emphasis on research methods, critical evaluation, and the practical application of research.
subject: ISCI

title: Information Organization and Access
description: Explores the design, use, and evaluation of information organization and retrieval systems to support digital curation and preservation, metadata generation, and information-seeking.
subject: ISCI

title: Information Organization and Retrieval
description: Issues and techniques of knowledge representation and information organization, information retrieval systems, and users’ information seeking behavior.
subject: ISCI

title: Advanced Reference Across the Disciplines
description: Exploring reference principles when applied to various disciplines, populations, and contexts. Focus on topics related to information practices and behaviors of different populations, information seeking in various contexts, as well as sources of information that respond to specific subject matter, topics, or formats dictated by specific disciplinary fields.
subject: ISCI
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ISCI 703.

title: Introduction to Archival Records Studies
description: Nature and use of archives and records; functions of archives and records professionals; and legal, ethical, and political issues in archives and records.
subject: ISCI

title: Crisis Management and Communication in Libraries
description: Exploring the role libraries play in mitigating crises, with an emphasis on crisis communication and response. Analyze real-world case studies involving libraries in contexts ranging from community-wide emergencies to individual trauma, and examine strategies organizations can use to develop effective crisis management plans. Resources and infrastructure libraries need for competent response, as well as the impact on staff, highlighting the importance of well-being in supporting communities.
subject: ISCI

title: Trauma Informed Librarianship
description: Critical analysis of current library practices through a trauma-informed lens and develop skills to advocate for organizational change that centers patron wellbeing. Special attention is given to serving vulnerable populations including children, homeless individuals, survivors of domestic violence, refugees, and those experiencing mental health challenges.
subject: ISCI

title: Introduction to Bibliography and Textual Studies
description: Introduction to analytical, descriptive, and textual bibliography, and to the principles and practice of editing.
subject: ISCI

title: Special Collections Librarianship
description: Introduction to the missions, professional standards and best practices of special collections librarianships. Topics include access and acquisitions, colletion assessment and development, collection management and maintenance, donor relations, public programming and current issues and trends. Restricted to ISCI graduate students.
subject: ISCI

title: History of Children’s and Young Adult Literature
description: Historical overview of the literary content, illustration, and social values of children’s and young adult literature written in English. Examines the influence of movements such as Romanticism, Rationalism, and postmodernism, as well as changing trends over time.
subject: ISCI

title: Preservation Planning and Administration
description: The planning and administration of preservation programs in libraries, archives, records centers, and manuscript depositories.
subject: ISCI

title: Seminar in School Library Programs
description: Problems relating to contemporary school media programs will be identified and analyzed by students, drawing from their own experiences, pertinent literature in the field, and field investigations.
subject: ISCI
prereq: Prerequisites: ISCI 701, ISCI 703, ISCI 705, ISCI 707, ISCI 720.

title: Special Libraries
description: Rotating topics that provide an overview of organizational characteristics, governance, services, and distinctive features of special library types including, but not limited to, government, law, business, presidential, music, and medical libraries. Simulation of information management problems in these organizations.
subject: ISCI

title: Digital Libraries
description: History and current state of digital records, including their storage, organization, and preservation in digital libraries.
subject: ISCI

title: Leading Knowledge-Intensive Organizations
description: Overview of the creation, evaluation, documentation, sharing, and application of knowledge in information organizations.
subject: ISCI

title: Public Library Systems
description: Course focuses upon three topics: 1) organizational patterns for various library operations, local and regional; 2) the political environment of the public library; and 3) major problems confronting public library systems.
subject: ISCI

title: Academic Libraries
description: An analysis of the historical development and current issues in academic libraries.
subject: ISCI

title: Cataloging Information Materials
description: An in-depth study of current descriptive cataloging standards covering both print and nonprint materials; searching, creating, editing, and updating encoded bibliographic records; introduction to authority records and control, subject headings, and classification.
subject: ISCI

title: Subject Analysis and Classification
description: Study of major classification and subject authority systems. Emphasizes the understanding and application of these systems in information agencies.
subject: ISCI

title: E-Resources Management
description: An in-depth study of current descriptive cataloging standards covering both print and nonprint materials; searching, creating, editing, and updating encoded bibliographic records; introduction to authority records and control, subject headings, and classification.
subject: ISCI

title: Metadata
description: Examination of metadata definition, selection and applications; Role of metadata in information discovery, acquaintance with various metadata schemes and standards for libraries, museums, archives and info centers.
subject: ISCI

title: Project Management for Library Collections
description: How to identify critical problems and propose initiatives related to library collections management, lead investigations into potential solutions, and develop project plans for implementation.
subject: ISCI

title: Teaching for Academic Libraries
description: Explore current trends and methodologies in academic library instruction to design, implement, and evaluate effective library instruction sessions.
subject: ISCI
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ISCI 703.

title: Curricular Role of the School Librarian
description: Role of the school library media specialist in integrating the school library media program into a K-12 standards-based curriculum, including best practices, needs assessment, collaboration, instructional design, and resource provision.
subject: ISCI
prereq: Prerequisites: ISCI 701, ISCI 706, ISCI 703 preferred and required education courses (for initial certification candidates).

title: Health Sciences Information Resources
description: Characteristics and use of print and computer-based materials in the health sciences and for general reference librarians.
subject: ISCI

title: Information and Records Management
description: An introduction to the role and functions of the information manager in organizations with emphases on use, retention, and management of information and records.
subject: ISCI

title: Libraries, Literacy, and Literature
description: Ways in which libraries and librarians become more effective providers and partners in the literacy movement.
subject: ISCI

title: Diversity in Libraries
description: Nontraditional library users in all types of libraries. Literacy programs, disabled and/or institutionalized persons, older adults, and members of selected ethnic groups.
subject: ISCI

title: Library Programming for Children and Young Adults
description: The nature, philosophy, and development of non-curricular programs for children and young adults in the school and public library. Among the types of programs to be discussed are storytelling, film programs, reading programs, programs for parents, and other activities associated with library service to young people. Students will study the principles and problems involved in designing, implementing, and evaluating programs of this nature.
subject: ISCI

title: Page-Turners and Programs: Engaging Adult Readers in Libraries
description: Materials popular with adult readers, the principles and practices of reader’s advisory, and programs utilizing those materials. Emphasis on extensive reading, advisory techniques, and experience in planning and presenting engaging programs for diverse adult audiences.
subject: ISCI

title: Children’s Materials
description: A study of materials intended for children of elementary school age (6-13) with emphasis on the process of evaluating them to meet the educational, cultural, and recreational needs of children.
subject: ISCI

title: Young Adult Materials
description: A study of materials for young adults (13-19) with emphasis on the process of evaluating them to meet the educational, cultural, and recreational needs of young adults.
subject: ISCI

title: Consumer Health Resources and Information Services
description: Concepts and current trends in the creation, implementation, and evaluation of adult consumer health resources and services, including consumer health informatics and e-health.
subject: ISCI

title: Materials for Early Childhood
description: A study of picture books and audiovisual materials intended for the very young child through age 9 with emphasis on the process of evaluating these materials to meet the educational, cultural, and recreational needs of very young children.
subject: ISCI

title: Materials and Services for Latino Youth
description: Introduces a wide range of print and nonprint materials appropriate for Latino youth. Provides resources for librarians and educators serving young Latinos literacy needs.
subject: ISCI

title: Information Technologies in the School Library Program
description: Technology management, use of technology and nonprint resources, and their integration into the K-12 curriculum.
subject: ISCI
prereq: Prerequisites: ISCI 706.

title: Universal Design and Accessibility for Youth
description: This course will offer an introduction to the process of selecting and evaluating materials for children and youth who are differently-able, or who have disabilities. It will also offer an introduction to the process of planning and implementing programming for these populations. Special emphasis will be placed upon Universal Design and creating inclusive environments and service learning opportunities.
subject: ISCI

title: Planning Library Facilities
description: An introduction to the process of planning new and renovated spaces and facilities. Content covers roles of participants in planning preparation of building program and examines examples of recent buildings.
subject: ISCI

title: Management of Public Library Youth Services
description: Planning, implementation, and evaluation of public library services for children and young adults.
subject: ISCI

title: Challenges in Information Intensive Organization Administration
description: Examines in detail frequently occurring problems that require decision activity by information intensive organization managers, directors, and other administrators.
subject: ISCI

title: Copyright in the Digital Environment: Issues for Information Professionals and Institutions
description: Copyright policy issues affecting information professionals and institutions in the digital environment. Copyright basics, limitations and exceptions (Section 108), fair use, The Marrakesh Treaty Implementation Act (MTIA), orphan works, digital rights management, controlled digital lending (CDL), the Digital Millennium Copyright Act (DMCA), licensing, and online learning.
subject: ISCI

title: Design and Management of Databases
description: Databases used in libraries and other information agencies, including operational and functional design. Extensive hands-on evaluation of selected database software packages.
subject: ISCI

title: Web Technologies for Information Specialists
description: Evaluation and programming of Web technologies and related issues in libraries and in other information agencies.
subject: ISCI

title: Design and Management of Digital Image Collections
description: This course presents introductory concepts related to the creation, manipulation, and implementation of visual collections in various online environments. It identifies resources, procedures, and skills needed to successfully design, implement, and manage digital image collections in a collaborative environment.
subject: ISCI

title: Seminar in Information Science
description: A critical examination of the principles, trends, and issues of modern information systems design and use.
subject: ISCI

title: Social Informatics
description: This course examines the design, uses, and effects of information and communication technologies (ICTs) from the standpoint that society and technology mutually shape one another.
subject: ISCI

title: Information Networks
description: Identification and evaluation of information networks in libraries and other information agencies. The nature of networks, including hardware and software applications.
subject: ISCI

title: Critical Cultural Information Studies
description: Examines how issues of diversity, social justice, race, gender and sexuality are represented in the information professions and will study how these social imperatives affect, and are affected by, information technologies.
subject: ISCI

title: Social Justice Storytelling and Advocacy
description: Conceptualize, critique, and reformulate social justice as an outcome while working towards a better understanding of how their social identities and systems of oppression contribute to and/or work against the social justice process.
subject: ISCI

title: Disinformation and the Literacy Landscape
description: Addresses the renewed phenomenon of fake news, misinformation/disinformation, and its related concepts; then focuses more explicitly on the affective information behaviors that influence our interactions with information and help us intellectually thrive in a post-truth society.
subject: ISCI

title: Health Informatics for Information Professionals
description: Provides foundational knowledge and practical skills in the exploration and application of evidence-based health information systems within health information environments. Covers the principles and practices of managing health-related data, including data literacy, curation, organization, and ethical stewardship.
subject: ISCI

title: Leadership and Ethics in Information Security
description: Critical and analytical study of cyber-related leadership, information security, and information policy and ethical issues at the individual, institutional, and international levels.
subject: ISCI

title: Seminar in Applied Information Systems for Information Specialists
description: Application, management and evaluation of information systems for libraries and other information agencies, including emerging technical, administrative and management issues related to these systems.
subject: ISCI

title: Library and Information Science Study Away
description: An intensive experience examining information systems, data management practices, and knowledge organization across different cultural and technological contexts. Students visit libraries, archives, research institutions, technology companies, and government information agencies to explore global approaches to information access, digital preservation, privacy policies, and information equity. Through site visits and fieldwork, students analyze comparative information infrastructures, digital governance models, and cross-cultural information behaviors
subject: ISCI

title: Internship in Library and Information Science
description: Supervised field experience in library, media center, or other information agency relevant to student’s professional goals.
subject: ISCI
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ISCI 701 and ISCI 705.

title: Special Topics in International Information Issues
description: Discussion and critical examination of selected topics of current international debate regarding information and related technologies. Topics vary by title. May be repeated a maximum of 3 times.
subject: ISCI

title: Independent Study in Library and Information Science
description: Independent study in an area of library and information science relevant to the student’s professional goals.
subject: ISCI

title: Selected Topics in Librarianship and Information Services
description: Discussion and investigation of selected topics of current concern to the library and information profession. Specific topics to be identified by title.
subject: ISCI

title: Theoretical Issues in Library and Information Science
description: Seminar examining a range of issues, theories, and research questions that currently shape thinking and discourse in library and information science.
subject: ISCI

title: Research Methods and Design in Information Science
description: Seminar overviewing research methods and design, including quantitative, qualitative, and multi-method approaches.
subject: ISCI

title: Information and Society
description: Seminar examining the historical and intellectual foundations of library and information science in relation to the nature and current roles of information organization and information transfer in societies.
subject: ISCI

title: Preparation for Academic Careers in Library and Information Science
description: Seminar examining the history, trends, and current status of academic careers in library and information science, emphasizing knowledge and skills needed for successful teaching, scholarship, and service.
subject: ISCI

title: Information Policy and Ethics
description: Seminar in the critical and analytical study of information policy and ethical issues at the individual, institutional, and international levels. Not auditable.
subject: ISCI

title: Communication Processes and Information-Seeking Behavior
description: Seminar examining the characteristics of communication, human information interaction, and information-seeking behavior, with emphasis on social network models, and the relationship between information-seeking behaviors and the design of communication and information systems and services. Not auditable.
subject: ISCI

title: Planning and Evaluating Colloquia In Library and Information Science
description: Involves students in planning, managing, and evaluating colloquia, including recruiting speakers, scheduling venues, attracting audiences, conducting the sessions, and evaluating the results. May be repeated up to three times for credit.
subject: ISCI

title: Human Information Interactions and Cultural Institutions
description: Explores libraries and other cultural institutions as lifelong educational environments where complex human interactions take place. Over the past two years, the promising intersection of cultural organizations has been a topic of strong interest to practitioners and scholars associated with these institutions. This course introduces the interdisciplinary framework, social perspectives, and research methods required to development an understanding of this intersection, the changes that will be required as new technologies alter the way that people engage with these institutions, and the challenges that have begun to emerge as their boundaries become less defined. This type of interdisciplinary framework is needed to address these topics and address both the human information needs that impel cultural institutions and the steps and strategies by which these needs may be recognized and resolved in these information rich environments.
subject: ISCI

title: Technologies in Cultural Institutions
description: This course provides an opportunity for doctoral students to explore the issues associated with the implementation, evaluation and management of various technologies found in cultural institutions. Students will gain practical experience working with different technologies through class demonstrations and will be exposed to different technical environments via class field trips.
subject: ISCI

title: Elementary Italian
description: Grammar and practical vocabulary necessary for fundamental communication skills.
subject: ITAL

title: Basic Proficiency in Italian
description: Practice and further development of essential listening, reading, speaking, and writing skills.
subject: ITAL
prereq: Prerequisites: ITAL 121.

title: Accelerated Basic Proficiency in Italian
description: Accelerated development of essential listening, reading, speaking, and writing skills to obtain the Basic Proficiency Level in Italian. Credit may be received only for one of the following: ITAL 121 and ITAL 122 , or ITAL 130.
subject: ITAL

title: Intermediate Proficiency in Italian I
description: Practice and rapid development of accurate skills in speaking, listening, reading and writing. Features BBC television course.
subject: ITAL
prereq: Prerequisites: ITAL 122.

title: Intermediate Proficiency in Italian II
description: Practice and further rapid development of accurate skills in speaking, listening, reading and writing. Features BBC television course.
subject: ITAL
prereq: Prerequisites: ITAL 221.

title: Accelerated Intermediate Italian
description: Accelerated development of intermediate level listening, reading, speaking, and writing skills in Italian. Credit may be received only for one of the following: ITAL 221 and ITAL 222 , or ITAL 230.
subject: ITAL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ITAL 122 or ITAL 130.

title: Italian Conversation
description: Oral practice with advanced protocols of Italian conversation, focusing on perfecting rhythms and tonalities, and on a clear presentation of meaning.
subject: ITAL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ITAL 222 or ITAL 230 or by placement.

title: Writing in Italian
description: Introduction to letter, short essay, and creative writing, and to newspaper reports and selected essays as models of self-expression.
subject: ITAL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ITAL 222 or ITAL 230.

title: TEATROMANIA: Italian Theater Practicum
description: Project work in the production of plays in Italian. Includes readings in and public performance of Italian theatre and related materials.
subject: ITAL

title: The Other Italians: Emigrants and Immigrants
description: Cultural analysis of the issues facing immigrants and their descendants in Italy, as well as the development of Italian American Culture in the United States. Through films, original texts, secondary literature, and contemporary media will be explored. Taught in English.
subject: ITAL

title: Advanced Italian Study Abroad
description: Intensive language practice, emphasizing oral proficiency skills and advanced conversational protocols. Classroom instruction by native speakers, extensive contact with native environment, field trips. May be repeated for credit by permission.
subject: ITAL
prereq: Prerequisites: ITAL 122.

subject: JSTU

title: Convergence and Divergence in African American and Jewish Relations: Historical and Contemporary
description: An examination of African American and Jewish American inter-ethnic, historical and contemporary connections and disconnections. Implications for educational, social, and social settings are considered.
subject: JSTU

title: Introduction to Judaism
description: Overview of Jewish experiences, beliefs, practices from a contextual point of view.
subject: JSTU

title: Language and Humor at the Intersections of Religion, Race, Ethnicity, and Gender
description: Explores language and humor, looking at (i) the linguistic structure and psychology of humor, (ii) changing societal standards for humor, (iii) racial, ethnic, religious, and gender-based humor, and (iv) socio-political questions surrounding the use of these.
subject: JSTU

title: Hebrew Bible (Old Testament)
description: Modern study of the Hebrew Bible from historical, literary, and archeological points of view. Reading and analysis of texts in translation.
subject: JSTU

title: Law and Religious Traditions
description: The study of the role of law, legal argumentation, and legal contexts in one or more religious traditions.
subject: JSTU

title: Literature and Film of the Holocaust
description: Film, poetry and literature created in response to the Holocaust as the means for a decades long cultural discussion, in European and American societies, of the moral and religious implications of the Holocaust on our self-understandings as religious and moral beings.
subject: JSTU
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ENGL 101 and ENGL 102.

title: Life and Legacy of Anne Frank
description: Introduction to the life and legacy of Anne Frank (1929-1945). Analysis of her diary and other texts as historical documents related to WWII and the Holocaust.
subject: JSTU

title: Jewish History I: Late Antiquity to 1500
description: The religious, cultural, social, and political conditions that shaped the Jewish experience in the Near East and Europe from Late Antiquity to 1500.
subject: JSTU

title: Jewish History II: 1500 to the Present
description: Case studies of Jewish history in Europe, America, and the land of Israel, 1500 to the present.
subject: JSTU

title: Jews and Muslims
description: Jewish-Muslim relations in the Near East and the US; an exploration of Jewish-Muslim encounters, issues of religious law, politics, radical religious ideologies, and their repercussions for today.
subject: JSTU

title: Kabbalah: Science, Religion and Nature in Western History
description: Historical investigation of kabbalah, a philosophical system and mystical current common to Judaism, Islam, and Christianity, from the medieval period to the present; focus on its formative role in early modern Western cosmology and science, and its ecological implications today.
subject: JSTU

title: American Jewish History
description: Examination of experiences of Jews in the United States from Colonial Period to late 20th century, especially Jewish immigration, political behavior, social mobility, religious affiliation, group identity formation, and meaning of Anti-Semitism in American and global contexts.
subject: JSTU

title: Visions of Apocalypse
description: Symbolic visions, tours of heaven and hell, cosmic battles, divine judgment, messianic figures, prophecy, or other forms of revelation as found in literature, art, or social movements from diverse geographical and historical locations.
subject: JSTU

title: Special Topics in Jewish Studies
description: Intensive study of special topics in Jewish Studies; may emphasize interdisciplinary themes. Maybe be repeated as content varies by title.
subject: JSTU

title: History of the Holocaust
description: Introduction to Nazi Germany’s systematic mass-murder of Europe’s Jews and other minorities during war. Examination of forces that led to the Holocaust, including scientific racism, Nazi policy implementation, and dynamics of annihilation during war.
subject: JSTU

title: Elementary Korean
description: Grammar and practical vocabulary necessary for fundamental communication skills. Assumes no prior experience in the language.
subject: KORE

title: Basic Proficiency in Korean
description: Practice and development of essential listening, reading, speaking, and writing skills.
subject: KORE
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in KORE 121 or by foreign language placement test.

title: Intermediate Korean I
description: Review and continuation of fundamentals of the language; development of oral and reading skills.
subject: KORE
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in KORE 122.

title: Intermediate Korean II
description: Increased emphasis on written and oral expression in Korean.
subject: KORE
prereq: Prerequisites: KORE 221.

title: Supervised Instruction in Teaching Foreign Languages in College
description: Supervised direction of foreign language teaching in college. Required of all graduate assistants who are teaching. Will not count toward the 30-hour M.A. or M.A.T. degree.
subject: KORE

title: Nursing Care Management I
description: This course focuses on the knowledge, skills, and abilities that are fundamental to nursing practice with application in acute or extended care settings.
subject: LANU
prereq: Prerequisites: Admission to the nursing program

title: Pharmacologic Basics
description: This introductory course outlines the basic concepts of pharmaceutics, pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, and pharmacotherapeutics. The process of clinical calculations is introduced, as well as the major drug classifications.
subject: LANU
prereq: Prerequisites: Admission to the nursing program; LANU 159, LANU 211

title: Nurse Care Management II
description: Focuses on the delivery of nursing care to an increasing number of individuals experiencing health problems emphasizing selected physiological systems.
subject: LANU
prereq: Prerequisites: LANU 104, LANU 106, LANU 206

title: Clinical Skills Application
description: Involves the application of knowledge, skills, and abilities in a clinical setting.
subject: LANU
prereq: Prerequisites: Admission to the nursing program

title: Care of the Childbearing Family
description: This course facilitates the application of the nursing process to assist in meeting the needs of the childbearing and child-rearing family. Focus is on both normal and abnormal aspects.
subject: LANU
prereq: Prerequisites: LANU 104, LANU 106, LANU 206

title: Mental Health Nursing
description: This course facilitates the utilization of the nursing process to assist in meeting the needs of patients with common mental health problems. Focus is on the dynamics of human behavior, ranging from normal to extreme.
subject: LANU
prereq: Prerequisites: LANU 229

title: Nursing Management and Leadership
description: This course prepares the student for the professional nursing role through the introduction of management skills required to care for small groups of individuals and to function as a leader of a nursing team.
subject: LANU
prereq: Prerequisites: LANU 214

title: Nursing Care Management IV
description: This course focuses on the delivery of nursing care to clients throughout the lifespan who are experiencing complex, multi-system health problems.
subject: LANU
prereq: Prerequisites: LANU 209

title: Latin American, Caribbean, and Latino/a Cultures through Film
description: Interdisciplinary introduction to the diversity of landscapes and political and social issues in Latin America’s history. Films, lectures, and readings provide an overall comprehensive perspective. Lectures by faculty in Spanish, Portuguese, anthropology, and history.
subject: LASP

title: Interdisciplinary Study of Latin America
description: Anthropology, geography, history, politics, language, and culture of Latin America.
subject: LASP

title: Working with Spanish-Speaking Communities
description: Crosscultural approaches to interactions with persons of Hispanic origin in a variety of professional settings. Readings, speakers, media. Taught in Spanish. Departmental permission required for transfer students.
subject: LASP

title: Latin American Cultures
description: Comparative study of selected Latin American cultures with emphasis on their significance for a broader anthropological theory.
subject: LASP

title: South American Indian Cultures
description: An examination of ethnographic data on South American Indians, emphasizing methods used to acquire those data and their applications to theoretical considerations.
subject: LASP

title: Mesoamerican Prehistory
description: Cultural development and variation in Mesoamerica from the first arrival of man to the arrival of Europeans. Particular attention to cultural continuities from prehistoric times.
subject: LASP

title: Prehistoric Civilizations of the New World
description: Study of Mesoamerican and South American civilizations, particularly the Mayan, Aztec, and Inca states. Processes of state formation as reflected in archaeological data.
subject: LASP

title: Geography of Latin America
description: Physical and human geography of Latin America.
subject: LASP

title: Colonial Latin America
description: The establishment and consolidation of the Spanish and Portuguese empires in the Western hemisphere; interaction of Indians, Africans, and Iberians, and the formation of social, economic, and political traditions in Latin America; political independence.
subject: LASP

title: Modern Latin America
description: Traditional society in the area and problems arising from social, economic, and political changes since independence; comparative studies of national responses to these problems.
subject: LASP

title: Politics and Governments of Latin America
description: The development, principles, political thought, and politics of the several Latin American states.
subject: LASP

title: Latin American Culture
description: Survey of the cultures of Latin America via readings, visual culture, and discussion. Department permission required for transfer students.
subject: LASP
prereq: Prerequisites: Placement at 300-level on Phase II placement exam or C or better in SPAN 303.

title: Literary Tendencies and Masterpieces of Spanish America
description: A survey of the masterworks and literary tendencies of Spanish America.
subject: LASP
prereq: Prerequisites: SPAN 312.

title: Special Topics in Latin American Studies
description: Selected Topics on Latin America. May be repeated for credit as content varies by title.
subject: LASP

title: Independent Study
description: Contract approved by instructor, advisor, and department chair is required for undergraduate students.
subject: LASP

title: Prehistoric Archaeology of South America
description: Prehistoric archaeology of the South American continent.
subject: LASP

title: Social and Economic History of Latin America
description: The evolution of social groups and changes in economic patterns in Latin America from pre-Columbian times to the present.
subject: LASP

title: History of Mexico
description: Mexico from the pre-conquest period to the present, with an emphasis on modern Mexico.
subject: LASP

title: International Relations of Latin America
description: Contemporary international relations among Latin American states, including economic and political security and relations with the United States.
subject: LASP

title: Contemporary Spanish-American Literature
description: Survey of the most significant work from 1960 to the present.
subject: LASP

title: Senior Seminar
description: Study in depth of a topic on Latin America.
subject: LASP

title: Contemporary Latin America
description: Analysis and discussion of contemporary Latin American history and the sociocultural forces that have contributed to define this area's national identities. Taught in Spanish.
subject: LASP
prereq: Prerequisites: Placement at 300-level on Phase II placement exam or C or better in SPAN 303. Department permission required for transfer students.

title: Colonial Spanish-American Literature to Neoclassicism
description: Survey of pre-Columbian poetry and of texts dating from the time of Columbus to the end of the Colonial period.
subject: LASP

title: Art of the U.S. South and Afro-Caribbean
description: An expansive study of art, material culture, and popular culture by people of African descent in the U.S. South, and of Afro-Caribbean lineage, which understands these two regions as importantly intertwined.
subject: LASP

title: Beginning Latin I
description: Introduction to grammar and practical vocabulary necessary for fundamental reading skills. Admission to 109 restricted to those who have never studied Latin, who have not studied Latin in the previous five years, or who have a score of L-1 on the placement test.
subject: LATN

title: Beginning Latin II
description: Introduction to grammar and practical vocabulary necessary for fundamental reading skills. Credit may not be received for both LATN 109 /LATN 110 and LATN 121 .
subject: LATN
prereq: Prerequisites: LATN 109.

title: Elementary Latin
description: Grammar and vocabulary necessary for fundamental reading skills. Assumes prior experience in Latin. Admission only by proficiency examination. Credit may be received for only one of the following: LATN 109 / LATN 110 or LATN 121.
subject: LATN

title: Basic Proficiency in Latin
description: Practice and further development of essential reading skills.
subject: LATN
prereq: Prerequisites: LATN 110 or LATN 121.

title: Advanced Readings in Latin Literature
description: A survey of Latin literature designed for the student who wishes to develop a major or cognate in Latin.
subject: LATN
prereq: Prerequisites: LATN 122.

title: Virgil
description: Readings from the Aeneid.
subject: LATN

title: Latin Literature of the Golden Age
description: Selected readings in prose and poetry of representative authors.
subject: LATN

title: Latin Composition
description: A study of Latin syntax in order to translate English prose into Latin. Instruction is individualized.
subject: LATN

title: Independent Study
description: Contract approved by instructor, advisor, and department chair is required for undergraduate students.
subject: LATN

title: Latin Drama
description: Selected plays of Plautus and Terence.
subject: LATN

title: Cicero
description: Readings from a variety of Cicero’s works to gain a concept of the man as a humanist.
subject: LATN

title: Horace
description: Readings from the Odes.
subject: LATN

title: Ovid
description: Selected readings from the Metamorphoses.
subject: LATN

title: Tacitus
description: Agricola or selections from the Annales.
subject: LATN

title: Livy
description: Readings from Ab Urbe Condita.
subject: LATN

title: Roman Satire
description: Readings in Horace, Juvenal, and Petronius.
subject: LATN

title: Latin Erotic Poetry
description: Readings from the elegies of Catullus, Tibullus, Propertius, and Ovid.
subject: LATN

title: Lucretius
description: Readings from the De Rerum Natura.
subject: LATN

title: Renaissance Latin
description: An examination of several genres of Latin writing from Europe during the period 1400-1600, emphasizing, but not limited to, Italian writers.
subject: LATN

title: Latin Seminar
description: Seminar on various Latin authors. Topics vary by semester.
subject: LATN
prereq: Prerequisites: D or better in LATN 122 or graduate student standing.

title: History of Latin Literature from the Origins to the Golden Age
description: Readings from the Twelve Tables to Virgil, supplemented by readings in history and scholarship. Designed to prepare majors and honors students for further study.
subject: LATN

title: History of Latin Literature in the Silver Age
description: Readings from Ovid to Ammianus, supplemented by readings in history and scholarship. Designed to prepare majors and honors students for further study.
subject: LATN

title: Independent Study
description: Special projects for independent study and research.
subject: LATN

title: Independent Study
description: Special projects for independent study and research.
subject: LATN

title: Teaching Advanced Latin in Secondary School
description: Methods and materials for teaching the Latin Advanced Placement courses in secondary school.
subject: LATN

title: Intensive Grammar Review in Latin
description: Intensive grammar review for non-majors; designed as preparation for LATN 615 .
subject: LATN

title: Intensive Readings in Latin
description: Intensive reading for non-majors. Graduate students fulfill their foreign-language reading requirement with successful completion of the course. Undergraduates may take the course as an elective only.
subject: LATN

title: Medieval Latin
description: Survey of the survival of the classical tradition in the middle ages from the birth of Ammianus Marcellinus to the fall of Constantinople (A.D. 330-1453).
subject: LATN

title: Coastal Law
description: This course explores legal approaches to avoiding and resolving conflicts between human use of coastal areas and the ecological integrity of coastal systems. We will cover relevant South Carolina and Federal law, looking at issues both above and below the tide line. Course readings represent a variety of disciplines, including law, economics, and the natural sciences.
subject: LAWG

title: Principles of Leadership Management: Legal, Ethical, and Organizational Considerations
description: This course develops essential leadership and management skills for effective public safety administration, covering legal, ethical, and organizational responsibilities. Students will learn to navigate moral dilemmas, promote integrity, optimize operations, and manage risks. They will also explore the role of agency culture and how to foster a positive environment, ensuring they are prepared to lead with vision and excellence.
subject: LAWG

title: Evidence-Based Policing Public Safety
description: This course emphasizes evidence-based practices, teaching students to integrate research-informed and data-driven insights into public safety operations and evaluate impacts. It covers the latest research, analytical techniques, and the importance of collaborative research partnerships. Students will learn to cultivate a culture of continuous learning to address challenges, enhance public trust, and drive positive change through evidence-based practices.
subject: LAWG

title: Civil Rights: Practices for Public Safety Leaders
description: This course provides public safety leaders with a legal and practical understanding of advancing civil rights through effective administration. It covers the origins of law enforcement authority, civil rights litigation, and key issues like racial profiling and protest response. Students will develop their own leadership approach to promote justice, respect, and equal protection under the law.
subject: LAWG
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better LAWG 701 and LAWG 702.

title: Sound Agency Management: Law, Policy, and Strategy
description: This course covers best practices and legal requirements for human resource management in public safety agencies. Students will learn to navigate organizational change, leadership roles, and strategies for improving hiring, assessment, and retention of qualified officers. By the end of the course, students will be equipped to handle complex HR challenges, promote a positive culture, and ensure a fair working environment.
subject: LAWG
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better LAWG 701 and LAWG 702.

title: Co-Producing Public Safety: Building Trust and Centering Community Needs
description: This course equips students to understand crime drivers and develop community-centered solutions, leveraging public partnership for safety. Students will learn social determinants of public safety, multi-disciplinary interventions and co-responder models, preparing them as executives to advance collaborative, community-focused solutions.
subject: LAWG
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better LAWG 701 and LAWG 702.

title: Critical Incidents: Responding to and Learning from Near Misses and Unintended Outcomes
description: This course teaches public safety leaders to use near misses and unintended outcomes to improve future performance. Through case studies of critical incidents and sentinel event reviews, students will learn to identify system weaknesses and develop, implement, and assess recommendations for improvement. By course end, students will be equipped to lead organizations in learning from negative outcomes and conducting comprehensive, forward-looking reviews for better performance.
subject: LAWG
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better LAWG 701 and LAWG 702.

title: Collaborative Communications in Public Safety
description: This course develops skills for fostering strong relationships with staff, communities, media, and policymakers through effective communication. Students will gain practical experience in applying communication principles to support trust and collaboration among key stakeholders.
subject: LAWG
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in LAWG 701 and LAWG 702.

title: Directed Study in Policing and Public Safety
description: This course helps public safety executives apply their knowledge to real-world challenges through individualized research and practical projects, developing actionable strategies to enhance public safety and leadership. This capstone experience bridges academic learning with practical implementation, preparing graduates to advance effective public safety services.
subject: LAWG
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better LAWG 701 and LAWG 702.

title: Administrative Law
description: Government agencies regulate almost every area of our lives. Many lawyers work for government agencies and many other lawyers work for people whose lives are affected by these agencies. Virtually every lawyer needs to know how government agencies operate. That is the subject of this course. The course is recommended for students interested in substantive areas in which agencies play an important role, such as environmental law, health-care law, and securities law (to name a few).
subject: LAWG

title: Budgeting, Fiscal Management, and Strategic Planning for Public Safety Leaders
description: This course explores essential financial principles for public safety administrators, including budgeting, revenue streams, financial reporting, and legal obligations, to optimize resource allocation and maintain public trust. Students will learn to conduct cost-benefit analyses, develop long-term financial strategies, and create strategic budgets and plans that align with agency objectives and community needs.
subject: LAWG
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better LAWG 701 and LAWG 702.

title: Legal and Ethical Considerations in Public Safety: Today, Tomorrow, and Beyond
description: This course equips public safety leaders with the knowledge and tools to navigate complex ethical dilemmas through ethical theories, case studies, and real-world scenarios. Students will develop a strong legal and ethical framework to address rapidly evolving challenges, including technology, artificial intelligence and cybercrime, ensuring transparency, privacy, and public trust in their decision-making.
subject: LAWG
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better LAWG 701 and LAWG 702.

title: Crime Prevention: Law, Research, Strategies, and Tactics
description: This course examines evidence-based approaches to crime prevention, focusing on crime patterns, social determinants, and community engagement. Students will learn to design locality-specific strategies using data, technology, and collaboration with communities and interagency partners to develop and evaluate comprehensive crime prevention plans.
subject: LAWG
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better LAWG 701 and LAWG 702.

title: Leadership for Officer Health, Safety, and Wellness
description: This course equips public safety leaders with strategies to prioritize the physical, mental, and emotional well-being of their personnel, addressing stress, burnout, and resilience. Students will learn legal requirements and evidence-based approaches to enhance officer wellness, fitness, and support, ultimately fostering a supportive and resilient work environment.
subject: LAWG
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better LAWG 701 and LAWG 702.

title: Environmental Law Policy
description: This is an introductory course in environmental law. The purpose is to give interested students a background in a number of federal environmental statutes, including NEPA (National Environmental Policy Act) CERCLA (Comprehensive Environmental Response, Compensation and Liability Act) ESA (Endangered Species Act) and CWA (Clean Water Act).
subject: LAWG

title: Climate Change Seminar
description: An exploration of the uniquely difficult challenges climate change creates for institutions, society, and the legal system. Global and domestic legal and regulatory options for addressing global climate change, including international legal and quasi-legal agreements such as the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change, the Kyoto Protocol, the Copenhagen Accord, and the Clean Water Act will also be discussed.
subject: LAWG

title: Environmental Justice Seminar
description: An introduction to the environmental justice movement, including the history and theory of environmental justice in the United States, its application to a range of case studies, and its intersections with other emerging fields of justice like climate change justice, energy justice, and food justice. There will be a particular focus on people and communities that bear disproportionate environmental burdens and the ways that race, ethnicity, and class have shaped the challenges, tools, and politics of environmental justice.
subject: LAWG

title: Conservation Law in South Carolina
description: The history of land conservation and environmental advocacy, with a specific focus on South Carolina laws and practices. Instruction on real estate laws applicable to acquiring lands outright, tax laws applicable to conservation easements, wetlands mitigation, heirs' property, and the practicalities of coordinating and funding a conservation project will also be provided.
subject: LAWG
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in LAWH 700.

title: Environmental Compliance and Enforcement
description: Introduction to environmental compliance and enforcement from the initial stages of a complaint through the administrative, civil and criminal processes.
subject: LAWG
prereq: Prerequisites: C+ or better in LAWH 700 and LAWG 731.

title: Energy Law
description: This course provides an introduction to the law and regulation of energy resources, primarily in the United States, focused on three core areas within the field. The first part of the course will cover extraction of energy resources, primarily coal, oil, and natural gas. The second part will cover regulation of the electricity generation and distribution system, including public utility and rate regulation, transmission, and relevant environmental regulations. The final part of the course will address legal and regulatory issues specific to nuclear and renewable energy, with a particular focus on the Southeast. Throughout, the course will focus on the ability (or inability) of legal and regulatory regimes to keep pace with rapid change in the energy sector.
subject: LAWG

title: Legal Foundations of Health Care Systems
description: Holistic understanding of the various aspects of the healthcare system related to sources of care, sources of payment, and regulatory and other legal systems that constrain and control the behavior of these sources and how these sources impact each other.
subject: LAWH
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in LAWH 700.

title: Introduction to Healthcare Compliance
description: This course introduces students to the complex world of healthcare compliance. It will prepare students to develop and maintain compliance programs for medical facilities by teaching them the pertinent federal laws and regulations, providing an overview of enforcement and regulatory agencies, and developing ethical protocols.
subject: LAWH
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in LAWH 700 and LAWH 701.

title: Risk Management, Patient Safety and Quality
description: This course will utilize reading materials, case studies, and hospital risk management scenarios. Through these materials, students will learn to identify legal, ethical, administrative, compliance, risk management and patient safety issues and reach resolutions for the problems presented. Students will also learn how risk management has changed since the 1998 IOM Report and how the changes impact patients and providers.
subject: LAWH
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in LAWH 700 and LAWH 701.

title: Organizational Leadership and Professional Development for Healthcare Professionals
description: This course introduces healthcare professionals enrolled in the Law School's Health law master's or certificate programs to basic leadership principles, and to the hard and soft skills needed to effectively lead healthcare organizations.
subject: LAWH
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in LAWH 700 and LAWH 701.

title: Ethics in Health Care Systems
description: This course considers and explores the bioethical, medical, and organizational ethical issues encountered in delivering healthcare services. This course also introduces learners to the legal, regulatory and analytical framework guiding and informing healthcare leaders while navigating ethical responsibilities.
subject: LAWH
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in LAWH 700, LAWH 701, LAWH 716, LAWH 722, LAWH 736, LAWH 738.

title: Health Care Contracting (Applied Learning)
description: Health care contracts.
subject: LAWH
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in LAWH 700, LAWH 701, LAWH 716, LAWH 722, LAWH 736, LAWH 738.

title: Medicare Compliance for Medical Facilities
description: Medicare reporting and structural requirements for hospitals.
subject: LAWH
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in LAWH 700, LAWH 701 (These prerequisites may be waived for a student currently enrolled in the JD program at the University of South Carolina).

title: Employment Law
description: Identification, prioritization, and management of various risks faced by health care professionals and institutions. (These prerequisites may be waived for a student currently enrolled in the JD program at the University of South Carolina).
subject: LAWH
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in LAWH 700 and LAWH 701.

title: Billing Compliance and Healthcare Fraud Prevention I
description: Understanding of the requirements for properly billing third-party payers and survey of federal laws and regulations that prohibit healthcare fraud and self-dealing.
subject: LAWH
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in LAWH 700, LAWH 701. (These prerequisites may be waived for a student currently enrolled in the JD program at the University of South Carolina).

title: Billing Compliance and Healthcare Fraud Prevention II
description: Advanced issues in bill coding and justification and complexities of fraud and self-dealing prohibitions.
subject: LAWH
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in LAWH 700, LAWH 701, LAWH 716, LAWH 722, LAWH 736, LAWH 738 (The prerequisite of LAWH 700 may be waived for a student holding a JD degree or equivalent).

title: Healthcare Privacy Security
description: Requirements for organizations that handle protected health information, including information about which entities need to comply with laws protecting such information and to what extent. (These prerequisites may be waived for a student currently enrolled in the JD program at the University of South Carolina).
subject: LAWH
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in LAWH 700 and LAWH 701.

title: Directed Independent Study
description: A supervised research paper or project on a topic related to health systems compliance or other law related issues affecting a health care system. Study culminates in a 30-50 page paper or equivalent research project.
subject: LAWH
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in LAWH 700, LAWH 701, LAWH 716, LAWH 722, LAWH 736, LAWH 738.

title: Introduction to the Legal Profession
description: This course provides an overview of the different roles in which lawyers serve and the different work environments in which lawyers are employed. Students will meet members of the legal profession, hear about the daily work of lawyers in different settings, receive information about handling the responsibilities of law practice, learn about the range of lawyers’ duties and to whom those duties are owed, and be introduced to the basic principles of professionalism. Graduation Requirement: Course required for graduation. Basis of Grade: Class attendance and participation; project report. Form of Grade: Pass/Fail.
subject: LAWS
prereq: Prerequisites: None.

title: Master the Bar Exam
description: The purpose of this course is for students to engage in and commit to completing a commercial Bar Prep program while simultaneously participating in assessments to test the efficacy of their learning. Students will participate in a commercial Bar Prep program approved by the Law School and allow the Law School to access individual progress-tracking reports to ensure timely completion of the program and its components. Students will be monitored by Academic Success Faculty on a weekly basis to determine if they are meeting the benchmarks of their commercial Bar Prep program. In addition to meeting the required benchmarks, students must participate in assessments provided by the commercial Bar Prep program, including graded essays and MBE practice exams. Students will also participate in weekly assessments to be conducted by Academic Success Faculty on Fridays beginning the first Friday after the commercial Bar Prep program begins, and Academic Success Faculty will follow up with students who are not meeting performance measures on the assessments. Such assessments may include a mini Bar Exam. Course requirements: Students must be in their final semester of law school and registered to take a Bar Exam following their graduation. Students must also be enrolled in an approved commercial Bar Prep program and allow the Law School to access individual progress-tracking reports to ensure timely completion of the commercial program and its components.
subject: LAWS

title: Contract Law
description: An introduction to the law governing contracts, both common law and the Uniform Commercial Code. Topics covered include the agreement process, requirements for enforceability, interpretation and meaning, defenses, and remedies. Graduation Requirement: Course required for graduation. Basis of Grade: Examination. Form of Grade: Letter.
subject: LAWS
prereq: Prerequisites: None.

title: Contracts I
description: An introduction to the common law and Uniform Commercial Code relating to the agreement process, including the requirements of offer, acceptance, and consideration for the formation of contractual relationship. Problems on the interpretation of the contract, negotiation, drafting, and legal planning are analyzed and discussed. Basis of Grade: Final examination. Form of Grade: Letter Grade.
subject: LAWS

title: Contracts II
description: Continuation of Contracts I. Basis of Grade: Final examination. Form of Grade: Letter Grade.
subject: LAWS
prereq: Prerequisites: LAWS 504.

title: Property
description: In this course, students are introduced to major concepts of property law, including the historical development of private property rights. The course will focus primarily upon the acquisition, characteristics, and transferability of property interests, as well as the relationship between privately held property interests in land and government regulation of that land for public purposes. Topics covered in the course will typically include adverse possession, estates, future interests, landlord tenant, easements, covenants, purchase sale, deeds, and financing. Graduation Requirement: Course required for graduation. Basis of Grade: Examination Form of Grade: Letter.
subject: LAWS
prereq: Prerequisites: None.

title: Property I
description: In this course, students are introduced to major concepts of personal and real property law, including the historical development of private property rights. The course will focus primarily upon the acquisition, characteristics, and transferability of property interests, as well as the relationship between privately held property interests in land and government regulation of that land for public purposes. Topics covered in the course will typically include personal property (gifts, bailments, lost found), adverse possession, estates, future interests, landlord tenant, easements, covenants, purchase sale, deeds, financing, and zoning. Basis of Grade: Final examination. Form of Grade: Letter Grade.
subject: LAWS

title: Property II
description: Continuation of Property I. Basis of Grade: Final examination. Form of Grade: Letter Grade.
subject: LAWS
prereq: Prerequisites: LAWS 510.

title: Constitutional Law
description: A study of the structure of the Federal Government, the function of the Supreme Court in constitutional government, and the provisions of the United States Constitution that guarantee and protect individual rights against governmental encroachment. Topics include judicial review, sources and limits of congressional power, presidential power, equal protection, substantive due process and identification of unenumerated fundamental rights, freedom of speech, and the religion clauses. Graduation Requirement: Course required for graduation. Basis of Grade: Examination. Form of Grade: Letter.
subject: LAWS
prereq: Prerequisites: None.

title: Criminal Law
description: This course provides an introduction to the substantive law of crimes. The primary emphasis is on those rules, principles, and doctrines applicable to most or many crimes. These doctrines include actus reus (What is a criminal act?), mens rea (What states of mind are criminal?), and the defenses of insanity, intoxication, impossibility, mistake, duress, necessity, and self-defense. Some attention is also given to several specific crimes and to theories of punishment. The primary materials are selected appellate court opinions and the Model Penal Code. Graduation Requirement: Course required for graduation. Basis of Grade: Final examination. Form of Grade: Letter Grade.
subject: LAWS
prereq: Prerequisites: None.

title: Constitutional Law I
description: A study of the Structure of the Federal Government and the function of the Supreme Court in constitutional government. Topics include principles of justiciability and standing; the development of federal power through the commerce clause and limits on state power flowing from the commerce and contract clauses; executive powers and the practice of separation of powers. The course will also generally consider the effect of the Fourteenth Amendment upon federalism, and conclude with the concept of state action as a limitation on the reach of the Amendment’s guarantees. Basis of Grade: Final examination. Form of Grade: Letter Grade.
subject: LAWS

title: Constitutional Law II
description: The second-half of the required year-long Constitutional Law sequence. This class focuses on provisions of the United States Constitution that guarantee and protect individual rights against government encroachment. Among the topics covered are: equal protection, due process, freedom of speech, and freedom of religion. Registration: Rising 2Ls have registration priority. Subject to block registration, required for graduation. Basis of Grade: Final examination. Form of Grade: Letter Grade.
subject: LAWS
prereq: Prerequisites: LAWS 525.

title: Products Liability Problem Research Seminar I
subject: LAWS

title: Products Liability Research Problems Seminar II
subject: LAWS

title: Torts
description: The legal protection afforded in civil proceedings against interference by others with the security of one’s person, property, or intangible interests; the analysis of intentional interference, negligence, and strict liability in the context of recognized categories of tort liability. Graduation Requirement: Course required for graduation. Basis of Grade: Examination. Form of Grade: Letter.
subject: LAWS
prereq: Prerequisites: None.

title: Torts I
