title: Clinical Seminar
description: Regularly scheduled, formal, oral presentations with audiovisuals on controversial or new areas of pharmacotherapeutics, pharmacoeconomics, pharmaceutics, medicinal chemistry or pharmacy practice. A follow-up question and answer period examines the student’s ability to substantiate his/her conclusions. Content and presentation technique will be critiqued.
subject: PHMY
prereq: Prerequisites: fourth professional year standing.

title: Physiology for Health Sciences
description: Major organ systems with emphasis on basic physiological processes and control systems. Primarily for health sciences graduate students.
subject: PHPH

title: Human Neuroanatomy
description: Lecture, laboratory, and independent study devoted to the structure and function of the human nervous system. Emphasis on those features of the nervous system of contemporary research interest.
subject: PHPH

title: Biomedical Pharmacology
description: Lectures and conference discussions covering principles of drug action; autonomic (adrenergic/cholinergic), cardiovascular, renal, central nervous system, endocrine and antimicrobial pharmacology, cancer chemotherapy, and toxicology.
subject: PHPH
prereq: Prerequisites: PHPH 701 or PHPH 720.

title: Graduate Physiology Lecture and Laboratory
description: Lecture, discussions, and laboratory covering: cell, muscle, respiratory, circulatory, gastrointestinal, metabolism, endocrinology, nervous system, and reproduction. Students will conduct physiology experiments to gain knowledge of experimental techniques and data collection.
subject: PHPH

title: Autonomic Pharmacology
description: Functional regulation of biosynthesis, release and reuptake of neurotransmitters as well as the role of transmitters in regulating physiological processes will be presented. Emphasis will be placed on experimental techniques used in this area of pharmacology.
subject: PHPH
prereq: Prerequisites: graduate physiology, pharmacology, and biochemistry.

title: Seminar in Neuroanatomy
description: Advanced study of selected topics in neuroanatomy.
subject: PHPH

title: Cardiovascular Pharmacology
description: An in-depth examination of the cardiovascular system with an emphasis on hemodynamic principles, cardiac regulation, and the use of drugs in various pathological states of the mammalian heart.
subject: PHPH

title: Drug Action on Ion Movements
description: The actions of several classes of drugs that alter ion movements in excitable tissues will be explored in depth with emphasis on models of drug actions and altered responses in hereditary and disease states.
subject: PHPH
prereq: Prerequisites: PHPH 705.

title: Neuroscience
description: Cellular and molecular principles of neurobiology and neuroscience topics from a research-oriented perspective.
subject: PHPH
prereq: Prerequisites: BMSC 754 or CHEM 754.

title: Special Topics in Neuroscience
description: Tutuorial instruction and group discussion of select topics in neuroscience involving neuroendocrinology, neuropharmacology, developmental neurobiology, or neuropsychobiology. Content varies by title and may be repeated up to a maximum of 6 credits total.
subject: PHPH

title: Neuroscience Seminar
description: Presentation and group discussion of research articles in neuroscience. Focus on improvement of critical thinking and scientific writing skills, as well as development of research ideas.
subject: PHPH

title: Scientific Communication
description: This course is focused on the principles of effective scientific communication. The major focus of instruction is on developing and giving high-quality scientific presentations to scientists and non-scientists.
subject: PHPH

title: Grant Writing
description: This course is focused on developing the skills required to navigate the grant writing process, with an emphasis on developing a competitive pre-doctoral grant application for PhD candidates in Neuroscience or related fields.
subject: PHPH

title: Neurophysiology
description: Generation and transmission of excitation in the mammalian nervous system. Integrated function of the nervous system with emphasis on interactions of autonomic, neuroendocrine, and behavioral mechanisms contributing to homeostatic regulation.
subject: PHPH

title: Manuscript Writing
description: This course covers the fundamentals of scientific manuscript writing with a focus on neuroscience topics. Students will develop skills for writing effective publication-quality manuscripts and will focus on improving clarity, concision, and accuracy of scientific reporting. Lectures will cover ethical issues and navigating the submission, revision, and resubmission process.
subject: PHPH

title: Career Development
description: This course is focused on career and professional development for PhD candidates in neuroscience or related fields.
subject: PHPH

title: Fundamental Neuroscience I
description: Integrated foundation in neuroanatomical and neurophysiological principles from a research-oriented perspective.
subject: PHPH

title: Fundamental Neuroscience II
description: Integrated foundation in neurochemical and neuropharmacological principles from a research-oriented perspective. Includes aspects of molecular neuroscience and systems analyses.
subject: PHPH

title: Neurobiology Basics Module: Neuroanatomy
description: Principles of neuroanatomy necessary for research in neurobiology-related disciplines. Each 5-week module provides fundamentals of particular aspects of neurobiology, including neuroanatomy, neurophysiology, neurochemistry, molecular neurobiology, and neuropharmacology, or skills necessary for research in neurobiology-related disciplines.
subject: PHPH

title: Neurobiology Basics Module: Neurochemistry–Fundamental Concepts
description: Principles required for understanding chemical and cellular processes in the nervous system. Each 5-week module provides fundamentals of particular aspects of neurobiology, including neuroanatomy, neurophysiology, neurochemistry, molecular neurobiology, and neuropharmacology, or skills necessary for research in neurobiology-related disciplines.
subject: PHPH

title: Neurobiology Basics Module: Neurophysiology
description: Principles required for understanding neurophysiological processes and integrated nervous system functions. Each 5-week module provides fundamentals of particular aspects of neurobiology, including neuroanatomy, neurophysiology, neurochemistry, molecular neurobiology, and neuropharmacology, or skills necessary for research in neurobiology-related disciplines.
subject: PHPH

title: Neurobiology Basics Module: Physiology for Neurobiologists
description: Principles of physiological processes and the nervous system control of such processes. Each 5-week module provides fundamentals of particular aspects of neurobiology, including neuroanatomy, neurophysiology, neurochemistry, molecular neurobiology, and neuropharmacology, or skills necessary for research in neurobiology-related discipline.
subject: PHPH

title: Neurobiology Basics Module: Neuropharmacology
description: Principles of neuropharmacology and how drugs act on the nervous system. Each 5-week module provides fundamentals of particular aspects of neurobiology, including neuroanatomy, neurophysiology, neurochemistry, molecular neurobiology, and neuropharmacology, or skills necessary for research in neurobiology-related disciplines.
subject: PHPH

title: Neurobiology Basics Module: Molecular Neurobiology
description: Principles related to cellular and molecular control of neurobiological processes. Each 5-week module provides fundamentals of particular aspects of neurobiology, including neuroanatomy, neurophysiology, neurochemistry, molecular neurobiology, and neuropharmacology, or skills necessary for research in neurobiology-related disciplines.
subject: PHPH

title: Neurobiology Basics Module: Quantitative Methods in Neurobiology
description: Principles for quantifying changes related to neurobiological research. Each 5-week module provides fundamentals of particular aspects of neurobiology, including neuroanatomy, neurophysiology, neurochemistry, molecular neurobiology, and neuropharmacology, or skills necessary for research in neurobiology-related disciplines.
subject: PHPH

title: Neurobiology Basics Seminar
description: Novel topics in neurobiology offered as the field progresses. Each 5-week module provides fundamentals of particular aspects of neurobiology, including neuroanatomy, neurophysiology, neurochemistry, molecular neurobiology, and neuropharmacology, or skills necessary for research in neurobiology-related disciplines.
subject: PHPH

title: Neurobiology of Disease Module: Stress, Anxiety Disorders, and the Amygdala
description: Team taught modules integrate the clinical, basic, technical, and social issues surrounding the neurobiological basis of anxiety. Research-oriented and literature-based approaches integrate the molecular, cellular, systems, and translational levels of stress, the amygdale, and anxiety disorders. Team taught, 5-week modules integrate the clinical, basic, technical, and ethical issues surrounding a specific neurobiological disease. Modules focus on research oriented and literature-based approaches to the molecular, cellular, systems, and translation.
subject: PHPH

title: Neurobiology of Disease Module: Stress, Depression, and Hippocampus
description: Team-taught modules integrate the clinical, basic, technical, and social issues surrounding the neurobiological basis of depression. Research-oriented approach integrates the molecular, cellular, systems, and translational levels of stress, the hippocampus, and depression. Team taught, 5-week modules integrate the clinical, basic, technical, and ethical issues surrounding a specific neurobiological disease. Modules focus on research oriented and literature-based approaches to the molecular, cellular, systems, and translation.
subject: PHPH

title: Neurobiology of Disease Module: Degenerative Diseases of Old Age
description: Team-taught modules integrate the clinical, basic, technical, and social issues surrounding the neurodegenerative diseases associated with aging. Research-oriented approaches integrate the molecular, cellular, systems, and translational levels of disorders such as Parkinsons, Alzheimers, and stroke. Team taught, 5-week modules integrate the clinical, basic, technical, and ethical issues surrounding a specific neurobiological disease. Modules focus on research oriented and literature-based approaches to the molecular, cellular, systems, and translation.
subject: PHPH

title: Neurobiology of Disease Module: Chronic Pain and Analgesia
description: Team-taught modules integrate the clinical, basic, technical, and social issues surrounding the neurobiological basis of pain. Research-oriented and literature-based approaches integrate the molecular, cellular, systems, and translational basis of chronic pain and its treatment. Team taught, 5-week modules integrate the clinical, basic, technical, and ethical issues surrounding a specific neurobiological disease. Modules focus on research oriented and literature-based approaches to the molecular, cellular, systems, and translation.
subject: PHPH

title: Neurobiology of Disease Module: Schizophrenia, Prefrontal Cortex, and Executive Function
description: Team-taught modules integrate the clinical, basic, technical, and social issues surrounding the neurobiological basis of disorders of cognition such as schizophrenia. Research-oriented approaches integrate the molecular, cellular, systems, and translational aspects of prefrontal cortex control of executive function and its role in schizophrenia. Team taught, 5-week modules integrate the clinical, basic, technical, and ethical issues surrounding a specific neurobiological disease. Modules focus on research oriented and literature-based approaches to the molecular, cellular, systems, and translation.
subject: PHPH

title: Neurobiology of Disease Module: Epilepsy
description: Team-taught modules integrate the clinical, basic, technical, and social issues surrounding the neurobiological basis of epilepsy. Research-oriented and literature-based approaches integrate the molecular, cellular, systems, and translational levels of seizure disorders and their treatment. Team taught, 5-week modules integrate the clinical, basic, technical, and ethical issues surrounding a specific neurobiological disease. Modules focus on research oriented and literature-based approaches to the molecular, cellular, systems, and translation.
subject: PHPH

title: Clinical Problems in Anesthesia
description: Tutorial instruction in anesthesia. This course may be repeated up to a maximum of 6 credits total.
subject: PHPH

title: Principles and Practice of Anesthesia 1
description: Foundational and basic concepts of anesthesia practice integrated into supervised clinical training to reinforce principles of anesthesia care.
subject: PHPH

title: Principles and Practice of Anesthesia 2
description: Foundational and advanced concepts of anesthesia practice integrated into supervised clinical training to reinforce principles of anesthesia care.
subject: PHPH
prereq: Prerequisites: PHPH 761.

title: Tutorials in Pharmacology and Physiology
description: Tutorial instruction in pharmacology and physiology. This course may be repeated up to a maximum of 6 credits total.
subject: PHPH

title: Seminar in Pharmacology and Physiology
description: Group discussions by students and staff based on literature surveys and current research in pharmacology and physiology.
subject: PHPH

title: Seminar in Anesthesia
description: Group discussions by students and staff based on literature surveys and current research in anesthesia.
subject: PHPH

title: Health Assessment in Anesthesia
description: Preoperative assessment, intraoperative management and postoperative management of patients receiving anesthesia. Development of cognitive and psychomotor skills needed to perform an advanced health assessment for patients undergoing anesthesia.
subject: PHPH

title: Practicum I in Nurse Anesthesia
description: Introduction to the cognitive and clinical skills necessary to perform effective anesthesia in a supervised clinical setting.
subject: PHPH

title: Pract II Nurse Anesthesia
description: Supervised clinical practicum in nurse anesthesia for students beginning their second year of training.
subject: PHPH
prereq: Prerequisites: PHPH 775.

title: Practicum III Nurse Anesthesia
description: Advanced, supervised clinical practicum in nurse anesthesia for students in their second year of training.
subject: PHPH
prereq: Prerequisites: PHPH 777.

title: Research in Pharmacology and Physiology
description: Graduate research designed by student in conjunction with research advisory committee.
subject: PHPH

title: Practicum IV Nurse Anesthesia
description: To instruct nurse anesthesia students entering their third year of training to the advanced cognitive and clinical skills necessary to perform effective anesthesia in a supervised clinical setting.
subject: PHPH
prereq: Prerequisites: PHPH 779.

title: Principles of Anesthesia I
description: Pre and post-operative evaluation, principles, and techniques of anesthesia, and the use of the anesthesia machine, ventilators, and monitoring equipment used in the administration of anesthesia. Course can be taken up to 5 total credits.
subject: PHPH

title: Principles of Anesthesia II
description: Continuation of PCOL 791 . Anesthetic techniques for specialty surgery including neurological, cardiovascular-thoracic, pediatric, and obstetrical surgery. May be repeated for a total of up to 5 credits.
subject: PHPH

title: Physical-Chemical Basis of Anesthetic Action
description: Physical and chemical concepts and their relationships to the principles of anesthesia. Includes the behavior of gases and the gas laws, chemical composition of anesthetic agents and drugs.
subject: PHPH

title: Professional Aspects of Nurse Anesthesia
description: An overview of the professional, practical, and educational issues of the practice of nurse anesthesia. Includes the history of anesthesia practice, psychological, and ethical issues, legal aspects of anesthesia practice, and current trends in anesthesia practice.
subject: PHPH

title: Biomedical Sciences for Nurse Anesthesia
description: Human anatomy, biochemistry, and pathological processes necessary for the practice of the health-related professions.
subject: PHPH

title: Medical Physiology
description: Medical physiology is a 7 credit course covering the principles and mechanisms involved in human physiology. With the exception of the central nervous system, all organ systems are covered. Emphasis is on the mechanisms that elicit and protect homeostasis of the human body. This course covers how cells function and integrates this cellular function to relate it to whole body function. Students learn to analyze and interpret data to understand the physiologic underpinnings maintaining homeostasis of the human body.
subject: PHPH

title: The Physics of How Things Work I
description: A practical introduction to physics and science in everyday life-from concrete examples to basic physical principles.
subject: PHYS

title: The Physics of How Things Work I Lab
description: Experiments, exercises, and demonstrations to accompany PHYS 101 .
subject: PHYS

title: The Physics of How Things Work II
description: A continuation of PHYS 101 with emphasis on electricity, magnetism, optics, and atomic physics.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PHYS 101.

title: The Physics of How Things Work II Lab
subject: PHYS

title: Physics in the Arts
description: The physics of sound, color, illumination; musical instruments and photographic processes. Credit may not be received for both PHYS 151 and PHYS 153 or both PHYS 151 and PHYS 155 .
subject: PHYS

title: Physics in the Arts Laboratory
description: Laboratory work on wave motion, including acoustic, optical, photographic, and electronic measurements. Credit may not be received for both PHYS 151L and PHYS 153L or both PHYS 151L and PHYS 155L .
subject: PHYS

title: Physics in the Visual Arts
description: Principals of optics: video, and photography, eye and vision, color, polarization, lasers, and holography. Credit may not be received for both PHYS 153 and PHYS 151 .
subject: PHYS

title: Physics in the Visual Arts Laboratory
description: Laboratory work in geometrical and wave optics. Credit may not be received for both PHYS 153L and PHYS 151L .
subject: PHYS

title: Musical Acoustics
description: The principles of musical and architectural acoustics, waves and vibrations, digital techniques for generating and recording sound, perception and measure of sound (psychoacoustics). Credit may not be received for both PHYS 155 and PHYS 151 .
subject: PHYS

title: Acoustics Laboratory
description: Laboratory work in musical and architectural acoustics. Credit may not be received for both PHYS 155L and PHYS 151L .
subject: PHYS

title: Measurement and Analysis in Physics
description: Measurements in classical and modern physics are performed, and the analyzed results are compared with basic principles. Four hours of mixed lecture and laboratory per week.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in MATH 115 or equivalent or higher.

title: General Physics I
description: First part of an introductory course sequence. Topics include mechanics, and selections from wave motion, sound, fluids, and heat. No previous background in physics is assumed.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in MATH 111, MATH 111I, MATH 112, MATH 115, MATH 116, MATH 122, MATH 141, or by placement score into MATH 122, MATH 141, or higher.

title: General Physics Laboratory I
description: Experiments, exercises, and demonstrations to accompany PHYS 201 .
subject: PHYS

title: General Physics II
description: Continuation of PHYS 201 ; includes electromagnetism, and selected topics from optics, relativity, quantum physics, atomic and nuclear physics.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PHYS 201.

title: General Physics Laboratory II
description: Experiments, exercises, and demonstrations to accompany PHYS 202 .
subject: PHYS

title: Essentials of Physics I
description: Classical mechanics and wave motion. Calculus-level course for students of science and engineering.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in MATH 141.

title: Essentials of Physics I Lab
description: Experiments, exercises, and demonstrations to accompany PHYS 211 .
subject: PHYS

title: Essentials of Physics II
description: Classical electromagnetism and optics.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PHYS 211 and MATH 142.

title: Essentials of Physics II Lab
description: Experiments, exercises, and demonstrations to accompany PHYS 212 .
subject: PHYS

title: Einstein’s Relativity: Understanding by Example
description: Special theory of relativity. Algebra-based course for students of all majors.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in MATH 115 or equivalent.

title: Principles of Physics III
description: Wave motion, optics, and thermodynamics. Calculus-level treatment; a continuation of PHYS 212 .
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PHYS 212 and MATH 142.

title: Introduction to Modern Physics
description: Experimental foundations and general concepts of quantum theory and special relativity; with selected applications from atomic, condensed matter, and nuclear physics.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PHYS 212 and MATH 241.

title: Intermediate Experimental Physics
description: Descriptive statistics, scientific ethics, and design, construction, and reporting the results of experiments.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PHYS 212 and STAT 509 or STAT 515.

title: Introduction to Applied Numerical Methods
description: Introduction and application of linear algebra and numerical methods to the solution of physical and engineering problems. Techniques include iterative solution techniques, methods of solving systems of equations, and numerical integration and differentiation.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: MATH 141.

title: Introduction to Relativistic Astrophysics
description: Final states of stellar evolution; white dwarfs, neutron stars, black holes. Cosmology.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ASTR 201; C or better in MATH 115; C or better in PHYS 202 or PHYS 212.

title: Independent Study
description: Contract approved by instructor, advisor, and department chair is required for undergraduate students.
subject: PHYS

title: Senior Thesis
description: An individual investigation in the library or laboratory or both under supervision of the major professor. The preparation of a scientific report is an integral part of the work.
subject: PHYS

title: Undergraduate Research
description: Introduction to and application of the methods of research. A written report on work accomplished is required at the end of each semester.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PHYS 310.

title: Quantum Physics I
description: A self-contained treatment of quantum theory and its applications, beginning with the Schrodinger equation.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PHYS 307 and MATH 242 or MATH 520.

title: Quantum Physics II
description: Advanced topics in quantum physics, plus topics in special relativity, high-energy physics, and cosmology.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PHYS 501.

title: Mechanics
description: Classical mechanics of particles, systems, and rigid bodies; discussion and application of Lagrange's equations, introduction to Hamiltonian formulation of mechanics.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PHYS 306 or PHYS 307 and MATH 242 or MATH 520.

title: Electromagnetic Theory
description: Field theory of electric and magnetic phenomena; Maxwell's equations applied to problems in electromagnetism and radiation.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PHYS 503.

title: Thermal Physics and Statistical Mechanics
description: Principles of equilibrium thermodynamics, kinetic theory, and introductory statistical mechanics.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PHYS 306, PHYS 307, MATH 241 and MATH 242 or MATH 520.

title: Nuclear Physics
description: An elementary treatment of nuclear structure, radioactivity, and nuclear reactions. Three lecture and three laboratory hours per week.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PHYS 501.

title: Mathematical Physics I
description: Analytical function theory including complex analysis, theory of residues, and saddlepoint method; Hilbert space, Fourier series; elements of distribution theory; vector and tensor analysis with tensor notation.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in MATH 242 or MATH 520.

title: Mathematical Physics II
description: Group theory, linear second-order differential equations and the properties of the transcendental functions; orthogonal expansions; integral equations; Fourier transformations.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PHYS 515.

title: Computational Physics
description: Application of numerical methods to a wide variety of problems in modern physics including classical mechanics and chaos theory, Monte Carlo simulation of random processes, quantum mechanics and electrodynamics.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PHYS 212 and MATH 142.

title: Introduction to Biological Physics
description: An introduction to the physical principles that underlie a variety of important biological and biophysical phenomena. Combines both physical and biological perspectives to explore a wide range of topics not possible in stand-alone undergraduate physics- or biological-science courses. Designed to be suitable for both bio-oriented and physics-oriented students.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PHYS 201 or PHYS 211; C or better in MATH 141; C or better in BIOL 101.

title: Advanced Experimental Physics I
description: Continuation of PHYS 310 . Optical apparatus (telescope, microscope, interferometer) and advanced project planning including equipment design and budgeting.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PHYS 310.

title: Advanced Experimental Physics II
description: Continuation of PHYS 541 . Study of topics from advanced optics, astronomy, biophysics, digital electronics, nuclear/particle physics, or solid state physics, plus conduction of a physics experiment, including a written paper and an oral presentation.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PHYS 541.

title: Introduction to Astrophysics
description: The basics of observational techniques, structure and evolution of stars, interstellar medium and star formation, structure and properties of the Milky Way and nearby galaxies, and generation and transfer of radiation in astrophysical environments.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PHYS 307 or graduate student standing.

title: Seminar on Teaching Learning in Physics Astronomy
description: Understanding physics students; current teaching methods supported by research in physics, physics education, education, psychology, and cognitive science.
subject: PHYS

title: Classical Mechanics
description: Generalized coordinates, Lagrangian and Hamiltonian formulations, variational principles, transformation theory, and Hamilton-Jacobi equation.
subject: PHYS

title: Classical Field Theory I
description: Development of classical fields; Maxwell's equations; boundary value problems; radiation theory.
subject: PHYS

title: Classical Field Theory II
description: A continuation of PHYS 703 .
subject: PHYS

title: Statistical Thermodynamics
description: Statistics of Boltzmann, of Fermi and Dirac, and of Bose and Einstein, with applications.
subject: PHYS

title: General Relativity and Cosmology
description: Introduction to the basic concepts of general relativity and a discussion of problems of current interest.
subject: PHYS

title: Quantum Mechanics I
description: A development of non-relativistic quantum mechanics.
subject: PHYS

title: Quantum Mechanics II
description: A continuation of PHYS 711 .
subject: PHYS

title: Advanced Quantum Theory
description: Second Quantization. Relativistic formulations of quantum mechanics.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: PHYS 712.

title: Quantum Field Theory
description: Theory of quantized fields. Introduction to renormalization. A continuation of PHYS 713 .
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: PHYS 713.

title: Many-Body Quantum Theory
description: Effective field theory, particle -hole, quasiparticles.
subject: PHYS

title: Subatomic Physics
description: Nuclear physics, mainly from the experimental standpoint.
subject: PHYS

title: Elementary Particles I
description: Introduction to elementary particles. The quark model. Symmetry principles and conservation laws. Calculation of cross sections and decay rates using Feynman rules. Accelerators, particle detectors, and experiments.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: PHYS 701, PHYS 703, PHYS 711.

title: Elementary Particles II
description: Experimentally accessible processes and their description using the framework developed in PHYS 723 . Gauge theories and the standard model. Particle experiments for the next decade and their underlying physics descriptions.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: PHYS 723.

title: Solid State Physics
description: The crystalline state of matter and its main characteristics. Electric and magnetic properties of metals, semiconductors, and insulators.
subject: PHYS

title: Superconductivity
description: Theory and description of conventional and high temperature superconductors and their properties.
subject: PHYS

title: Magnetic Resonance
description: Basic theory. Electron spin resonance. High resolution and wideline nuclear magnetic resonance. MÃƒÂ¶ssbauer effect. Magnetic resonance and dielectric relaxation.
subject: PHYS

title: Quantum Optics - Understanding Light-Matter Interactions
description: Semi-classical and fully quantum-mechanical treatments of interactions between matter and electromagnetic fields on the microscopic level.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: Undergraduate quantum mechanics.

title: Applied Group Theory
description: Groups and representations. Full rotational group. Angular momentum. Ligand field theory. Application to atomic, molecular, and nuclear physics.
subject: PHYS

title: Graduate Seminar
description: Presentation by the student of a designated topic. May be repeated for credit.
subject: PHYS

title: Extragalactic Astrophysics
description: Extragalactic astrophysics, including nearby and distant galaxies, active galaxies, galaxy clusters, large-scale structure, galaxy formation/evolution, scale structure, galaxy formation/evolution, basics of cosmology, cosmic radiation backgrounds, and observation constraints on cosmological models.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: PHYS 701, PHYS 703, and ASTR 211 or equivalent.

title: Graduate Research Laboratory
description: Obtain necessary skills and habits needed to work in any research group by performing hands-on experiments including finding a collaborator, researching the literature, performing the experiment, analyzing data, interpreting/understanding the results, and writing a report.
subject: PHYS

title: Selected Topics in Physics
description: Course content varies and will be announced in the schedule of classes by title.
subject: PHYS

title: Quantum Computer Science
description: Fundamentals of quantum information processing, including quantum computation, quantum algorithms, quantum cryptography, and basic quantum information theory. Topics include: the quantum circuit model, alternative models, qubits, unitary operators, measurement, entanglement, quantum algorithms for factoring and search, quantum cryptographic key distribution, simulation of physical systems, error-correction and fault-tolerance, quantum channels, complexity of quantum computation, near-term implementations, quantum computer programming.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in MATH 344, MATH 544, or MATH 700, or the equivalent, or instructor permission.

title: Topics in Nuclear Physics
description: Course content varies and will be announced in the schedule of classes by title.
subject: PHYS

title: Principles of Astrophysics
description: This is an astrophysics course for physics graduate students. The course will cover the basics of observational techniques, structure and evolution of stars, interstellar medium and star formation, structure and properties of the Milky Way and nearby galaxies, and generation and transfer of radiation in astrophysical environments.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C+ or better in PHYS 307, PHYS 503, PHYS 506.

title: Topics in Solid State Physics
description: Course content varies and will be announced in the schedule of classes by title.
subject: PHYS

title: Topics in Theoretical Physics
description: Course content varies and will be announced in the schedule of classes by title.
subject: PHYS

title: Research
description: Introduction to and the application of the methods of research.
subject: PHYS

title: Research
description: Introduction to and the application of the methods of research.
subject: PHYS

title: Quantum Information
description: Fundamentals of quantum information theory and quantum communications. Topics include: Postulates of quantum mechanics, classical information and entropy, compression of classical information and classical typical sets, quantum entropy and quantum relative entropy, quantum states discrimination, Schumacher's theory of quantum compression and quantum typical subspace, communicating classical information using quantum channels, the Classical Capacity Theorem of a quantum channel.
subject: PHYS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in MATH 344 or MATH 544 or equivalent, or permission by the instructor; and C or better in MATH 511 or STAT 511 or equivalent, or permission by the instructor; knowledge of quantum mechanics is not required.

title: Human Musculoskeletal and Gross Anatomy
description: Intensive study and analysis of the morphologic, functional and imaging anatomy of the human body. Emphasis on the relationship of form and function as a basis for understanding of human movement.
subject: PHYT

title: Musculoskeletal and Gross Anatomy Dissection
description: In depth, hands on study and analysis of the major gross anatomical structures, their relationships to each other and the clinical importance through cadaveric dissection.
subject: PHYT

title: Fundamentals of Physical Therapy
description: An overview of the basic skills needed by the physical therapist related to patient evaluation and management.
subject: PHYT

title: Health Promotion Wellness in Physical Therapy
subject: PHYT

title: Mechanisms of Motor Skill Performance
description: A study of theories and mechanisms involved in human movement. Focus is on analysis of principles and systems of gross motor control and learning.
subject: PHYT

title: Professional Issues in Physical Therapy
description: PHYT 740 is designed to allow you to enhance patient interaction through the development of professional communication skills, to explore professional ethics and the APTA Core Values, and to foster a professional identity.
subject: PHYT

title: Clinical Documentation in Physical Therapy
description: Enhance patient interaction through the development of professional communication skills, to develop appropriate documentation skills, and to foster a professional identity.
subject: PHYT

title: Professional Issues in Physical Therapy II
description: Examination of the physical therapist's role within healthcare systems and professional organizations, with focus on professional communication, interprofessional practice, public policy, and advocacy.
subject: PHYT

title: Professional Issues in Physical Therapy III
description: Preparation for entry into the physical therapy profession through exploration of licensure exam preparation, post-professional opportunities, and development of essential career skills.
subject: PHYT

title: Orthopedic Physical Therapy Interventions
description: Theoretical and practical approaches to physical therapy treatment of orthopedic diagnoses including therapeutic exercise, therapeutic activities, mobility, joint and soft tissue mobilization and manipulation, and functional task practice.
subject: PHYT

title: Orthopedic Physical Therapy I
description: Fundamental principles of physical therapy examination and evaluation for people with orthopedic disorders with an emphasis on lumbar spine and lower extremities.
subject: PHYT

title: Orthopedic Physical Therapy II
description: Fundamental principles of physical therapy examination and evaluation for people with orthopedic disorders with an emphasis on thoracic and cervical spine and upper extremities.
subject: PHYT

title: Orthopedic Integration in Physical Therapy
description: An advanced course to enhance physical therapy students’ knowledge of orthopedic examination tests and manual therapy interventions. Emphasis will be placed on diagnostic accuracy of special tests (sensitivity, specificity, likelihood ratios, reliability and validity), interpretation of the results, proper execution, and integration of manual techniques into clinical practice.
subject: PHYT

title: Research Proposal Development
description: Fundamentals of developing a research proposal.
subject: PHYT

title: Manual Therapy I
description: Joint and soft tissue mobilization and manipulation techniques.
subject: PHYT

title: Manual Therapy II
description: Techniques will include spinal mobilization, mobilization with movement, manipulation, muscle energy, taping, soft tissue mobilization and integration of techniques into clinical practice.
subject: PHYT

title: Integumentary Physical Therapy
description: Physical therapy management of patients with integumentary disorders.
subject: PHYT

title: Pharmacology for the Physical Therapist
description: Management of the physical therapy patient on selected medications.
subject: PHYT

title: Patient Education in Physical Therapy
description: Role of the physical therapist in professional and patient education.
subject: PHYT

title: Therapeutic Exercise
description: Therapeutic exercise for the orthopedic patient: selection of techniques, rate of progression and modification.
subject: PHYT

title: Orthotics and Prosthetics
description: Management of patients with amputations, prosthetics, and orthotics.
subject: PHYT

title: Pain Mechanisms and Treatment
description: This course will address the theoretical models for understanding the basis for pain. Pain assessment and physical therapy pain management will be addressed. Emphasis will be placed on the development of clinical decision-making and problem solving.
subject: PHYT

title: Biophysical Agents in Physical Therapy
description: This course studies theoretical and practical applications for safe, effective use of biophysical agents commonly used in physical therapy settings using evidence based practice.
subject: PHYT

title: Cultural Competence in Health Care
description: Cultural competencies necessary for the delivery of health care to patients of diverse ethno-cultural heritages.
subject: PHYT

title: Geriatric Physical Therapy
description: Fundamental principles for assessment, treatment and overall foundations of geriatric physical therapy based upon the best available evidence.
subject: PHYT

title: Essentials of Cardiopulmonary Physical Therapy
description: Physical therapy management of patients/clients with acute and chronic cardiac and/or pulmonary dysfunction.
subject: PHYT

title: Acute Care Physical Therapy
description: Cognitive and motor skills required in the management of the acute care physical therapy patient.
subject: PHYT

title: Introduction to Examination, Evaluation, and Intervention I
description: Fundamental theory and practical implications of examination for the clinical physical therapy patient.
subject: PHYT

title: Introduction to Examination, Evaluation, and Intervention II
description: Fundamental theory and practical implications of examination, evaluation and intervention for the clinical physical therapy patient with special emphasis on therapeutic exercise for lumbar and lower extremity.
subject: PHYT

title: Special Topics in Physical Therapy
description: A study of selected issues in the field of physical therapy. Course content varies by title. May be repeated for credit as topics vary for a maximum of 6 credit hours total.
subject: PHYT

title: Functional Anatomy
description: Biomechanical principles underlying motor control and selected techniques used to quantify human movement.
subject: PHYT

title: Evidence-Based Practice in Physical Therapy I
description: Application of evidence-based practice to the clinical physical therapy setting with a focus on asking focused clinical questions, searching for the best available evidence, research design and ethics.
subject: PHYT

title: Seminar in Physical Therapy
description: Critical review of professional literature pertaining to clinical practice in physical therapy. Repeatable for credit.
subject: PHYT

title: Research Seminar in Physical Therapy
description: Presentation and discussion of current research topics in physical therapy.
subject: PHYT

title: Seminar and Research in Physical Therapy
description: Exploration of contemporary physical therapy practice, the application of research evidence to physical therapy practice, and interprofessional and professional service activities.
subject: PHYT

title: Evidence-Based Practice in Physical Therapy II
description: Application of evidence-based practice to the clinical physical therapy setting with a focus on statistical methods and interpretation, critical appraisal of the evidence, and clinical decision making.
subject: PHYT

title: Independent Study
description: Topics to be approved by advisor and graduate director. Pass/fail grading.
subject: PHYT

title: Differential Diagnosis and Clinical Reasoning for Physical Therapists
description: Students will gain knowledge and expertise to competently screen for systemic diseases, interpret clinical findings and differentially diagnose movement related impairments from medical conditions that can mimic symptoms of neuromusculoskeletal problems. Emphasis will be placed on Skills necessary to identify patient/client problems that require referral to another health professional.
subject: PHYT

title: Neuroplasticity and Genetics in Physical Therapy
description: A study of neuroplasticity and genetics in relation to motor rehabilitation. Focus is on the analysis and application of key constructs in the design and implementation of rehabilitation interventions in individuals with neurologic diagnoses.
subject: PHYT

title: Neuro Repair - Rehabilitation
description: Examination of neural repair and rehabilitation from a clinical perspective.
subject: PHYT

title: Neuromuscular Assessment and Treatment I
description: Neurological conditions and interventions commonly used in physical therapy practice.
subject: PHYT

title: Neuromuscular Assessment and Treatment II
description: Neurological conditions and interventions commonly used in physical therapy practice.
subject: PHYT

title: Pediatric Physical Therapy
description: Assessment and evaluation of selected pediatric conditions.
subject: PHYT

title: Integrated Patient Management
description: Application of evidence-based clinical reasoning to complex case scenarios for a variety of body systems and physical therapy patient populations.
subject: PHYT

title: Management of Physical Therapy Practice
description: Theory and application of management supervision and leadership skills necessary for the practice of physical therapy.
subject: PHYT

title: Clinical Experience in Physical Therapy I
description: A 320 hour clinical education experience to develop physical therapy management skills of orthopedic patients.
subject: PHYT

title: Clinical Experience in Physical Therapy II
description: A 320-hour clinical education experience to develop physical therapy management skills of acute care patients.
subject: PHYT

title: Clinical Experience in Physical Therapy III
description: Supervised clinical experience in a physical therapy setting.
subject: PHYT

title: Clinical Experience in Physical Therapy IV
description: Supervised clinical experience in a physical therapy setting.
subject: PHYT

title: Clinical Experience in Physical Therapy IVa
description: An 80 hour clinical education experience to demonstrate professional behaviors while safely managing a partial caseload.
subject: PHYT

title: Clinical Experience in Physical Therapy IVb
description: A 400 hour clinical education experience to develop physical therapy management skills in a setting preferred by the student.
subject: PHYT
prereq: Prerequisites: PHYT 860.

title: Evidence Based Project in Physical Therapy
description: Evidence based clinical project in physical therapy.
subject: PHYT

title: Research Project in Physical Therapy
description: Clinically based research project in physical therapy.
subject: PHYT

title: Dissertation Preparation
subject: PHYT
prereq: Prerequisites: consent of instructor.

title: Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation Elective
description: This two-week clerkship provides students exposure to the scope of services provided by physical medicine and rehabilitation specialists. One week is spent on an inpatient service with exposure to spinal cord injury, brain injury, stroke, etc. and another week is spent in the outpatient clinic with exposure to musculoskeletal/sports, spasticity, and electrodiagnositcs. On the inpatient service, students will be exposed to all aspects of rehabilitation, including physical, occupational, speech, and psychological therapies and assistive devices. On the outpatient side, students will work one-on-one with an attending and observe outpatient diagnostics and treatment. There is one 30 minute required case presentation at the end of the rotation.
subject: PMDR

title: Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation Elective
description: After consultation with the Clerkship Director and Clerkship Coordinator, the student will be assigned to the area of practice to which he/she needs the most exposure before entering a residency program. Options include: time in a rehabilitation hospital working with patients who have spinal cord injuries, brain injuries, stroke, etc., and/or time in outpatient clinics gaining exposure to patients with chronic and acute musculoskeletal pain, sports medicine, interventional spine procedures and electrodiagnostics. On the inpatient service, students will be exposed to all aspects of rehabilitation, including physical, occupational, speech, and psychological therapies and assistive devices. On the outpatient side, students will work one-on-one with an attending and observe outpatient diagnostics and treatment.
subject: PMDR

title: Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation Away Rotation
subject: PMDR

title: Nutrition Through the Life Cycle
description: Examination of nutritional concerns, requirements, and metabolism from pre-conception through the aging process; analysis of cultural, environmental, psychosocial, physical, and economic factors affecting nutritional status through the life cycle; and methods for assuring adequate nutrition through dietary selection, promotion of healthy eating throughout the life cycle and nutritional assessment for each state of the life cycle.
subject: PNDI

title: Applied Nutrition Research
description: Application of the scientific method in nutrition research and practice including research design, execution, dissemination, and translation of findings.
subject: PNDI

title: Performance Nutrition
description: Nutritional needs, nutrition strategies, and nutrition-related factors affecting athletic performance in a variety of sports and performance activities.
subject: PNDI

title: Performance Nutrition: Approaches and Advocacy
description: Unique topics specific to the approaches within and advocacy of performance nutrition and sports dietetics.
subject: PNDI
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in PNDI 701.

title: Medical Nutrition Therapy I
description: Application of nutrition and dietary interventions in health and disease using the principles of medical nutrition therapy and the Nutrition Care Process. Students will learn about the pathophysiology of nutrition-related diseases, nutrition assessment, diagnosis, intervention, monitoring, and evaluation to manage disease conditions, recovery, and/or improve the quality of life.
subject: PNDI
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in PNDI 620 or HPEB 620; B or better in PNDI 700 .

title: Performance Nutrition Dietetics Supervised Experience Level 1
description: Foundational supervised experience in the field of nutrition and dietetics that applies knowledge about life cycle nutrition, general nutrition and health, nutrition research, and performance nutrition in practice settings.
subject: PNDI
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in PNDI 620 or HPEB 620; B or better in PNDI 700; B or better in PNDI 701 .

title: Performance Nutrition Dietetics Supervised Experience Level 2
subject: PNDI

title: Performance Nutrition Dietetics Supervised Experience Level 3
description: Supervised experience in the field of nutrition and dietetics with an emphasis on introductory nutrition therapy, nutrition counseling, and nutrition education in various practice settings.
subject: PNDI
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in PNDI 752 or HPEB 752; B or better in PNDI 705; B or better in PNDI 710; B or better in PNDI 730.

title: Performance Nutrition Dietetics Supervised Experience Level 4
description: Upper-level supervised experience in the field of nutrition and dietetics with an emphasis on nutrition therapy, services, and management in therapeutic and performance settings.
subject: PNDI
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in PNDI 710, PNDI 730, and PNDI 770 .

title: Performance Nutrition Dietetics Supervised Experience Level 5
description: Final supervised experience with preceptors in advanced therapeutic and managerial settings within the field of nutrition and dietetics.
subject: PNDI
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in PNDI 730, PNDI 740, PNDI 750, PNDI 760, and PNDI 770.

title: Nutrition Counseling Education
description: Educational and counseling components of nutrition interventions within nutrition care plans. Both psychological counseling and educational pedagogy will be emphasized.
subject: PNDI
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in PNDI 620 or HPEB 620; B or better in PNDI 700 .

title: Medical Nutrition Therapy II
description: Application of nutrition and dietary interventions in illness and disease management using the principles of medical nutrition therapy and the Nutrition Care Process. Students will learn individualized nutrition care planning for patients with complex medical conditions.
subject: PNDI
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in PNDI 710.

title: Performance Nutrition Therapy
description: Intersection of performance nutrition and medical nutrition therapy in which students learn to provide performance nutrition services to performers, athletes, teams, and athletic organizations.
subject: PNDI
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in PNDI 701, PNDI 710, and PNDI 730.

title: Nutrition and Public Health
description: A study of the relationship of human nutrition to public health and the potential for risk reduction through health education.
subject: PNDI
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in HPEB 502 or equivalent.

title: Leadership and Practice in Performance Nutrition and Dietetics
description: Students will learn to apply leadership, management, and business principles to guide practice in the field of nutrition and dietetics.
subject: PNDI
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in PNDI 620 or HPEB 620; B or better in PNDI 700; B or better in PNDI 701; B or better in PNDI 752 or HPEB 752.

title: Food Systems Management
description: The application of food systems principles and management skills to ensure safe delivery of nutritious food.
subject: PNDI

title: Performance Nutrition and Dietetics Capstone
description: Students will summarize the ways in which they are becoming professionally competent in the field of nutrition and dietetics.
subject: PNDI
prereq: Prerequisites: Instructor Permission.

title: Introduction to Global Politics
description: Introduction to theories about global politics. Issues and controversies central to global politics.
subject: POLI

title: Controversies in the Politics of Global Regions: Africa
description: Social, cultural, and historical forces underlying contemporary political controversies in Africa POLI 103A, Asia POLI 103B , Europe POLI 103C , Latin America POLI 103D , and the Middle East POLI 103E ; region will be identified by title.
subject: POLI

title: Controversies in the Politics of Global Regions: Asia
description: Social, cultural, and historical forces underlying contemporary political controversies in Africa POLI 103A , Asia POLI 103B, Europe POLI 103C , Latin America POLI 103D , and the Middle East POLI 103E ; region will be identified by title.
subject: POLI

title: Controversies in the Politics of Global Regions: Europe
description: Social, cultural, and historical forces underlying contemporary political controversies in Africa POLI 103A , Asia POLI 103B , Europe POLI 103C, Latin America POLI 103D , and the Middle East POLI 103E ; region will be identified by title.
subject: POLI

title: Controversies in the Politics of Global Regions: Latin America
description: Social, cultural, and historical forces underlying contemporary political controversies in Africa POLI 103A , Asia POLI 103B , Europe POLI 103C , Latin America POLI 103D, and the Middle East POLI 103E ; region will be identified by title.
subject: POLI

title: Controversies in the Politics of Global Regions: Middle East
description: Social, cultural, and historical forces underlying contemporary political controversies in Africa POLI 103A , Asia POLI 103B , Europe POLI 103C , Latin America POLI 103D , and the Middle East POLI 103E; region will be identified by title.
subject: POLI

title: Introduction to Politics
description: Concepts and problems involved in human relationship with governments, the nation-state, and political change.
subject: POLI

title: Controversies in Political Theory
description: An introduction to the analysis of disputes about the nature of politics and of political ideas such as freedom, equality, and justice.
subject: POLI

title: Controversies in Public Policy
description: An introduction to the analysis of contentious public policy questions in contemporary American society, such as welfare, gun control, health care financing, immigration, affirmative action, and/or abortion.
subject: POLI

title: Controversies in American Politics
description: An introduction to the analysis of key issues in contemporary American politics focusing on the arguments, the groups involved, and the political factors that influence the outcome of the debate.
subject: POLI

title: Green Explorations
description: Interdisciplinary seminar combining the intellectual exploration of ecological perspectives with the physical exploration of the local environment. First-year students only.
subject: POLI

title: Green Engagements
description: Interdisciplinary seminar on designing, researching, and implementing collaborative projects to promote ecological sustainability. First-year students only.
subject: POLI

title: American National Government
description: The formation and development of the national government, its organization and powers. Overlay Course.
subject: POLI

title: Policies and Functions of American Government
description: The policies and functions of the American national government directed to the public issues and problems of contemporary America.
subject: POLI
prereq: Prerequisites: POLI 201.

title: Introduction to Leadership Studies
description: Conceptions and models, values and pitfalls, strategies and skills of leadership and of leaders in diverse contexts
subject: POLI

title: Language Conflict and Language Rights
description: Examination of linguistic conflict and rights, as well as centrality of language rights to human rights and personal/cultural identity. Basic facts about language related to identity, culture, attitudes, dialects, bilingualism. Case studies (local, national, international) with particular attention to nationalism, language revitalization, language planning.
subject: POLI

title: Social and Political Philosophy
description: An overview of major themes in political philosophy such as the nature of politics, obligation, community, representation, freedom, equality, and justice.
subject: POLI

title: The Political Science Discipline
description: The history and development of approaches, methods, and fields of study in political science.
subject: POLI

title: Classical and Medieval Political Theory
description: Political theories from the Greeks to the Renaissance.
subject: POLI

title: Modern Political Theory
description: Political theories from the Renaissance to the 19th century.
subject: POLI

title: Race, Class, Gender, and Sexuality
description: Historical and contemporary power relationships in race, social class, gender, and sexual orientation.
subject: POLI

title: Feminist Theory
description: Historical development of feminist theory and contemporary debates within feminism.
subject: POLI

title: International Relations
description: International political behavior and institutions.
subject: POLI

title: Comparative Politics
description: Comparative approaches to political systems, behavior, and institutions.
subject: POLI

title: Comparative Early Childhood Policy in Industrialized Nations
description: An exploration of how countries around the world are working to improve the welfare of children, and what we in U.S. can learn from their policy innovations.
subject: POLI

title: International Organization
description: An introduction to the structure and functions of international political and economic organizations. Particular attention to the United Nations and its specialized agencies, and to emerging regional communities.
subject: POLI

title: The Conduct and Formulation of United States Foreign Policy
description: An analysis of how contemporary United States foreign policy is made and conducted.
subject: POLI

title: Contemporary United States Foreign Policy
description: A critical analysis of selected problems of United States foreign policy.
subject: POLI

title: National Security Policies of the United States
description: Formulation and implementation of contemporary United States defense and security policies.
subject: POLI

title: Public Opinion and Politics
description: A broad survey of the role and development of public attitudes toward political problems in a democracy. Emphasis on the origins, manifestations, and consequences of public opinion in American politics.
subject: POLI

title: Gender and Politics
description: Impact of gender on the distribution of power in society; foundations for intersections of gender, race, social class, and sexuality and their economic, social, and political concomitants.
subject: POLI

title: Introduction to U.S. Racial and Ethnic Politics
description: Survey of theories of the impact of race, ethnicity, and racism on American politics, and analysis of major policies and racial group experience regarding American citizenship.
subject: POLI

title: Film, Politics, and Social Change
description: Critical analysis of film as expression and agent of political cultural, ideology, and change.
subject: POLI

title: American Political Parties
description: A broad survey of the role of political parties in the American political system. Following an examination of the historical evolution of party systems in the United States, primary attention is given to three aspects of contemporary political parties: the party as an organization, the party as an electorate, and the party as a governing elite.
subject: POLI

title: Elections and Voting Behavior
description: An analysis of elections and the voting process. Topics include candidate selection, campaigning, and the conduct of elections as well as public opinion, voting behavior, and the role of elections in the democratic political system.
subject: POLI

title: Politics and the Mass Media
description: Survey of the role in American politics of mass communications media, including the press and electronic news reporting; influence of mass media on the conduct of political campaigns, political leadership style, and public opinion.
subject: POLI

title: Southern Politics
description: Selected political patterns and trends within the 11 states of the American South. Historical developments with the central focus on Southern politics since 1950.
subject: POLI

title: African-American Politics
description: African-American politics from the colonial period to the present. Emphasis on voting rights and strategies to advance black representation.
subject: POLI

title: State Government
description: A study of state-federal relations, relations among states, state constitutions, and the structure and functions of the three branches of government. Emphasis is given to South Carolina.
subject: POLI

title: Interest Groups and Social Movements
description: The mobilization, organization, tactics, and results of group-based politics, including latent interests and the suppression of interests.
subject: POLI

title: Introduction to Public Administration
description: A study of the basic principles and theory of administrative structure, responsibility, and control in relation to policy making in the modern state.
subject: POLI

title: Politics of Taxing and Spending
description: Principles and practices of financial administration, including organization, budgeting, assessment, treasury management, and debt.
subject: POLI

title: Regulatory Policies
description: Types and limits of powers exercised by regulatory agencies; procedural law and remedies against administrative action.
subject: POLI

title: Public Policy
description: Process of and major approaches to making public policy particularly, in the United States. Case study materials will focus on such major policies as welfare, health care, national security, and resource management.
subject: POLI

title: State Government Internship
description: A course designed to complement internship work in state/local government by introducing students to key concepts in state and local governance and politics to best facilitate the internship experience.
subject: POLI

title: Public Affairs Internship
description: A course designed to complement internship work in state/local government by introducing students to key concepts in state and local governance and politics to best facilitate the internship experience.
subject: POLI

title: Comparative Politics of Developing Countries
description: A comparative analysis of the political problems confronting new nations, the political consequences of the breakdown of traditional society and the problems of developing new institutional forms and procedures.
subject: POLI

title: Comparative Politics of Industrialized Countries
description: Introduction to the development, structure, and functioning of government and politics in Western Europe, the former Soviet states, and other selected industrialized countries.
subject: POLI

title: Genocide: A Comparative Perspective
description: An analysis of the causes of genocide and the application of those insights to explain how genocide has occurred repeatedly at various times and places across the globe.
subject: POLI

title: Topics in Political Science
description: May be repeated once as topics change.
subject: POLI

title: Race and Science Fiction
description: Draws on science fiction to understand the contemporary history of American racial and ethnic politics and to speculate about the significance of race in America’s political future.
subject: POLI

title: Independent Study/Political Science
description: Note: Prior approval of an individualized contract by the director of undergraduate studies in political science and the instructor who will supervise the project required.
subject: POLI

title: Independent Study in Political Science
description: Prior approval of and individualized contract by the director of undergraduate studies in political science and the instructor who will supervise the project required.
subject: POLI

title: Independent Study in International Studies
description: Prior approval of an individualized contract by the director of undergraduate studies in international studies and the instructor who will supervise the project required.
subject: POLI

title: Selected Topics in Political Theory
description: Intensive analysis of a particular topic or topics. To be identified by title each semester.
subject: POLI

title: Selected Thinkers in Political Theory
description: Intensive analysis of particular theorist or theorists. To be identified by title each semester.
subject: POLI

title: African American Political Thought
description: Survey of many of the major schools of historic and contemporary African American political thought.
subject: POLI

title: Democratic Theory
description: An introduction to contemporary theories and practices with focus on Western, especially American, experience.
subject: POLI

title: The State of American Politics
description: Major factors that affect the state of contemporary American politics, including the Constitution, the Congress, the courts, the presidency, the states, federalism, political parties, special-interest groups, and the electoral process.
subject: POLI

title: Revolution and Political Violence
description: Forms, causes, and consequences of domestic political violence with special attention to revolution.
subject: POLI

title: Theories of War in International Relations
description: The contributions of the social sciences and social theorists to an understanding of the causes of war.
subject: POLI

title: International Law
description: The origin, development, and principles of the international law of peace and the enforcement of these principles, the law of war and pacific settlement of disputes.
subject: POLI

title: Law and Contemporary International Problems
description: The growth of law in several areas of increasing international concern: environmental protection, expropriation, outer space, individual rights and obligations, conservation of resources, state responsibility, and terrorism.

subject: POLI

subject: POLI

subject: POLI

subject: POLI

title: Politics and Government of Japan

subject: POLI

subject: POLI

subject: POLI

subject: POLI

subject: PSYC

title: Independent Study or Research Experience for Non-Psychology Majors
description: Reading-based course, closely supervised project, or research experience for psychology minors and other non-psychology majors. Focus on a psychological issue of personal interest or to assist with ongoing research projects under the supervision of a psychology faculty member. 3-4 hours per week for each enrolled credit hour. A written product and/or oral presentation is required. Approved contract required. May be repeated for up to six credits. Not for psychology major credit.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PSYC 101.

title: Survey of Learning and Memory
description: Research and applications concerning the acquisition of new behavior and knowledge, including accounts based on classical and instrumental conditioning and on information-processing models.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: PSYC 101.

title: Cognitive Psychology
description: Research and theories on sensory memory, attention, short-term and working memory, human learning and forgetting, imagery, long-term memory, speech perception, reading, language, thinking and problem solving, and decision making.
subject: PSYC

title: Behavioral and Mental Disorders
description: Covers the classification, diagnosis, etiological theories, and treatments of the major mental and emotional disorders.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: PSYC 101.

title: Survey of Social Psychology
description: Introduction to theory and research in social psychology from a psychological viewpoint. Topics include social perception, social cognition, attitudes, interpersonal relationships, aggression, prosocial behavior, and group processes.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: PSYC 101.

description: This course provides an overview of psychological, social, physical, and emotional issues related to men’s lives. Major topics include: gender construction, men and work, men and health, men in relationships, male sexualities, men in families, and masculinities in the media and popular culture.
subject: PSYC

title: Survey of Personality
description: Covers the major theories and research on personality and the dynamics of human motivation.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: PSYC 101.

title: Sensation and Perception
description: Processing of information from the environment. Physiological, physical, psychological, and contextual determinants of perception.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: PSYC 101.

title: Introduction to Neuroscience
description: Function of the brain including basic neuroanatomy, neurophysiology and neurochemistry, neural systems, and psychopharmacology as it relates to behavior.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PSYC 101.

title: Brain and Behavior
description: How the brain mediates simple and complex behavior and how we can apply basic research about the brain to real world problems.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: PSYC 101.

title: Health Psychology
description: Application of psychological theories and assessment and treatment methodologies for health maintenance and the diagnosis and treatment of illness.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: PSYC 101.

title: Introduction to Language Sciences
description: Introduction to the linguistic component of human cognition. Properties of speech, the organization of language in the mind/brain, cross-linguistic universals, child language acquisition, and aspects of adult language processing.
subject: PSYC

title: Cognitive and Social Aspects of Bilingualism
description: Bilingual language development, social and cultural aspects of bilingualism, the bilingual brain, bilingualism throughout the lifespan.
subject: PSYC

title: Psychology of Sleep
description: Introduction to theory and research in sleep science from a psychological viewpoint. Topics include sleep, dreaming, consciousness, health, and sleep disorders.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in NSCI 300, PSYC 455, or PSYC 460.

title: Multi-Cultural Psychology
description: This course provides an introduction to theories and research in the study of psychosocial issues of racial, ethnic and cultural groups.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: PSYC 101.

title: Community Psychology
description: Application of knowledge from other areas of psychology to the study of the role of the individual in the community.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: PSYC 101 and at least 3 hours in psychology at 400 level or above.

title: Community Psychology Practicum
description: Supervised, structured field experience in a community agency, applying psychological principles, theory, and research. May be repeated once for credit.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: 15 hours in psychology.

title: Assistantship in Teaching of Psychology (AToP)
description: An undergraduate service-learning class focused on teaching assistantships in the Psychology Department, which applies research methods, cognitive and educational psychology concepts to student experience.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: B+ or better in PSYC 101.

title: Internship in Psychology
description: A supervised experiential course in psychology. Contract approved by instructor, advisor, and Office of Academic Programs is required for undergraduate students.
subject: PSYC

title: Research Experience
description: Closely supervised project or research experience in psychology; assist with ongoing research projects. 3-4 hours per week for each enrolled credit hour. A written product and/or oral presentation is required. Approved contract required. May be repeated for up to six credits.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in 9 hours of Psychology coursework (PSYC 101-PSYC 699).

title: Independent Study
description: Reading-based course. Focus on a psychological issue of personal interest under the supervision of a psychology faculty member. 3-4 hours per week for each enrolled credit hour. A written product and/or oral presentation is required. Approved contract required. May be repeated for up to six credits.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PSYC 101 and 9 hours of additional Psychology coursework (PSYC 101-PSYC 699).

subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PSYC 405.

subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: PSYC 420 or PSYC 410.

subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: PSYC 420.

subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PSYC 420 or graduate standing.

subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: PSYC 420.

description: Psychological, social, and biological phenomena associated with maturity and aging.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: PSYC 420.

subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: PSYC 410 or PSYC 420 or equivalent.

subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PSYC 420 or PSYC 520.

description: Children with average/above average intelligence and specific learning impairments; diagnostic and remedial techniques. Offered by both the College of Education and the Department of Psychology.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: EDEX 523 or PSYC 528.

title: Advanced Social Psychology
description: Intensive study of topics selected from the field of social psychology.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: PSYC 430.

title: Advanced Sensation and Perception
description: Intensive study of topics selected from the field of sensation and perception.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: PSYC 450.

title: Advanced Topics in Neuroscience
description: Intensive study of topics selected from the field of neuroscience. Course may be repeated for credit under different topics.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in NSCI 300, PSYC 455, or PSYC 460, for undergraduates.

title: Neuroscience Laboratory
description: Practice in the methods of scientific inquiry and the dissemination of research in neuroscience.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in NSCI 300, PSYC 455, or PSYC 460.

title: Cognitive Psychology Laboratory
description: Practice in the experimental techniques used in the study of cognitive psychology. Two lectures and one three-hour laboratory per week.
subject: PSYC

title: Sensation and Perception Laboratory
description: Concepts and principles in the study of sensation and perception in the laboratory. Two lectures and one three-hour laboratory per week.
subject: PSYC

title: Developmental Psychology Laboratory
description: Methods of observation and experimentation on human psychological development. Two lectures and one three-hour laboratory per week.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in PSYC 220 and PSYC 221.

title: Psychology of Sleep Laboratory
description: Practice in the methods of scientific inquiry and the dissemination of research in sleep science.
subject: PSYC

subject: PSYC

description: Introduction to the theory and practice of measuring psychological attributes. Emphasis on test construction in a laboratory setting. Hands-on experience in designing, administering, and analyzing psychological tests and measures.
subject: PSYC

title: History and Systems of Psychology
description: Systematic approaches to psychology.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: 9 hours in psychology at 400 level or above.

title: Selected Topics in Psychology
description: Course content varies and will be announced in the schedule of classes by title.
subject: PSYC

title: Individual Research
description: Planning and execution of supervised research in psychology on a project with significant original contributions. Not intended as a research assistant position to assist professors or graduate students in their research. 3 hours per week for each enrolled credit hour. A written product and/or oral presentation is required. Approved contract required. May be repeated for up to six credits.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in 15 hours Psychology coursework (PSYC 101-PSYC 699), or departmental approval.

description: Planning and execution of supervised research in psychology on a project with significant original contributions. Not intended as a research assistant position to assist professors or graduate students in their research. May be a continuation of a project from PSYC 598 . 3 hours per week for each enrolled credit hour. A written product and/or oral presentation is required. Approved contract required.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in 15 hours of Psychology coursework (PSYC 101-PSYC 699), or departmental approval.

subject: PSYC

subject: PSYC

description: Functional organization of the nervous system; research and theories of the neurological bases of behavior.
subject: PSYC

title: Basics of Cognitive Psychology
description: Research and theories on attention, memory, language, thinking, and other cognitive processes.
subject: PSYC

title: Basics of Developmental Psychology
description: Research and theories of psychological development across the lifespan.
subject: PSYC

subject: PSYC

description: This course covers basic principles of sound experimental design, including such topics as internal and external validity, subject selection factors, and techniques for reducing sampling error and minimizing bias. In addition, the course will discuss practical limitations to ideal experimental design and have students identify the design conventions specific to their field of study.
subject: PSYC

description: Research and theories of cognitive neuroscience.
subject: PSYC

description: Research and theories of cognitive development.
subject: PSYC

description: Presentation of research and theories that cross different areas within psychology.
subject: PSYC

description: Offered for an interdisciplinary student clientele interested in group processes and structures. Training is provided in observational methods and techniques of group assessment. Laboratory and field study required.
subject: PSYC

title: Psychological Systems and Theories
description: Contemporary trends in systematic approaches and behavior theories.
subject: PSYC

title: Seminar in Judgment and Decision Making
description: Research and theories of processes in judgment, choice, and decision making.
subject: PSYC

description: Quantitative methods for graduate students in psychology and other behavioral sciences. Three lecture/discussion hours and a one-hour scheduled lab per week.

description: A continuation of PSYC 709 . Three lecture/discussion hours and a one-hour scheduled lab per week.
subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: PSYC 709.

subject: PSYC

subject: PSYC
prereq: Prerequisites: 18 hours in psychology.

subject: PSYC
