subject: PSYC

title: Internship in School Psychology
description: Two semesters required for Ph.D. Student is registered upon recommendation of the student’s advisory committee. Will be graded as satisfactory (S) or unsatisfactory (U).
subject: PSYC

title: Selected Topics in Psychology
description: Intensive study in an advanced area in psychology. May be repeated for credit.
subject: PSYC

title: Independent Advanced Research
description: Doctoral-level research additional to that involved in the doctoral dissertation.
subject: PSYC

title: Introduction to Public Health
description: An introduction to the history, theory, and practice of public health. Emphasis will be on the population perspective and the ecological model including the population impacts of health care systems.
subject: PUBH

title: Independent Study in Public Health
description: Contract approved by instructor, advisor, and dean of the Arnold School is required for undergraduate students. May be repeated for up to 6 credits.
subject: PUBH

title: Special Topics in Public Health
description: Issues and emerging themes in public health. May be repeated for a total of 9 credit hours as content varies by title.
subject: PUBH

title: Public Health Capstone Seminar
description: Synthesis and application of BS/BA public health program content and competencies in a practice setting with emphasis on student identified areas for professional growth.
subject: PUBH
prereq: Prerequisites: PUBH 302.

title: Transforming Health Care for the Future
description: Foundation for beginning health professions students to gain an understanding of the complexities of the health care system through experiential activities conducted in interprofessional teams and the importance of interprofessional collaboration in order to improve the system.
subject: PUBH

title: Perspectives in Public Health
description: Lecture-format orientation to history, mission, and core services and disciplines of public health to develop understanding of current public health practice and how many health-related disciplines contribute to achieving public health goals.
subject: PUBH

title: Ethics and the Health Sciences
description: Students are introduced to formal and informal codes of professional conduct of various health science disciplines and understand the implications of these distinctions for interdisciplinary research, clinical practice, and administration.
subject: PUBH

title: Quantitative Methods for Public Health Practice I
description: Integrated review of quantitative methods to use in public health practice. Includes concepts from epidemiology, biostatistics, and environmental health used to calculate and interpret health indicators for describing the populations’ health.
subject: PUBH

title: Quantitative Methods for Public Health Practice II
description: Introduction to epidemiology and biostatistics and their application to public health issues and practice. Covers basic epidemiologic, biostatistical, and data management techniques used to analyze and interpret data in the field of public health.
subject: PUBH
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in PUBH 724.

title: Qualitative Methods for Public Health Practice
description: An introductory course on why, when, and how to apply qualitative data collection and analysis methods to public health activities with a focus on practice-based application. Identifying, working with, and communicating with a range of community stakeholders are also discussed.
subject: PUBH

title: Public Health Systems, Policy, and Leadership
description: A course on public health management and policy process and advocacy to develop effective public health leaders. Included is an emphasis on the relationships between people and their environment and the impact of the environment on human health and well-being.
subject: PUBH
prereq: Prerequisites: PUBH 725 and PUBH 726.

title: Practical Applications of Public Health Planning
description: A practical approach to planning public health programs through the application of planning frameworks and the exploration of fundamental issues in planning, implementing and evaluating programs in various settings addressing diverse populations and issues.
subject: PUBH
prereq: Prerequisites: PUBH 725 and PUBH 726.

title: Foundations of Health Disparities
description: Evolution of health disparities from historical, public, social, economic, and political perspectives.
subject: PUBH

title: Special Topics Public Health
description: Content varies by title.
subject: PUBH

title: Public Health Practice
description: Limited work experience or service project in an approved public health setting.
subject: PUBH
prereq: Prerequisites: BIOS 700 or BIOS 701 and EPID 700 or EPID 701.

title: Ethics in Public Health Research and Practice
description: Foundations of public health ethics with application to practice and to responsible conduct of research in public health disciplines. Enrollment restricted to Doctoral students post-docs, master’s students by permission of instructor.
subject: PUBH

title: Radiology
description: This course will provide instruction and the opportunity for the student to participate in routine diagnostic imaging procedures. The course will include instruction in the principles of image production and quality control. Instruction will also be given in basic radiation safety. At the completion of the course, the student will have participated in the operation of a diagnostic radiology department. The student will have been made aware of the usefulness and limitations of routinely used procedures in diagnostic radiology.
subject: RADI

title: Radiation Oncology
description: The student will participate in the physical diagnosis, treatment planning and treatment delivery of patients receiving therapeutic doses of radiation. The student will attend regularly scheduled rounds to discuss patients currently under treatment and will be encouraged to participate in the group discussion. Students will also attend any regularly scheduled Oncology conferences. Reading assignments will be given.
subject: RADI

title: Clinical Nuclear Medicine
description: The course will give the student instruction and experience in the clinical use and performance of nuclear medicine procedures. At the completion of the course, the student will have participated in the performance of all routine nuclear medicine procedures and will have gained some understanding of their clinical usefulness. The chemical and physiologic principles of the various procedures will have been presented and the students' knowledge of radiation detection and the safe handling of radioactive materials will have been extended.
subject: RADI

title: Introduction to Radiology
description: During your four week rotation, you will have an introduction to radiology that will take you from the basics of x-rays to the uses of advanced imaging modalities. You will gain an understanding of what a radiologist does and you will have the opportunity to observe a variety of radiological procedures. At the completion of the course, you will have a better understanding of diagnostic and therapeutic options available in radiology.
subject: RADI

title: Radiology Elective
description: This clerkship is designed to impart a broad basic understanding of the role of radiology in diagnosis and management. Emphasis is placed on terminology, proper workup, the fundamentals of diagnostic image interpretation, and clinical indications for imaging examinations and interventional radiology procedures. Instruction occurs through didactic lectures, problem-based learning exercises, computer tutorials, conferences, self-learning exercises, observation of film interpretation, and observation of special procedures. The indications and contraindications for radiologic examination as well as the benefits and limitations of radiological methods will be presented. In addition, the student has the opportunity of to work closely with attending radiologists, observing and participating in all phases of their daily work.
subject: RADI

title: Radiology Elective
description: This clerkship is designed to impart a broad basic understanding of the role of radiology in diagnosis and management. Emphasis is placed on terminology, proper workup, the fundamentals of diagnostic image interpretation, and clinical indications for imaging examinations and interventional radiology procedures. Instruction occurs through didactic lectures, problem-based learning exercises, computer tutorials, conferences, self-learning exercises, observation of film interpretation, and observation of special procedures. The indications and contraindications for radiologic examination as well as the benefits and limitations of radiological methods will be presented. In addition, the student has the opportunity of to work closely with attending radiologists, observing and participating in all phases of their daily work.
subject: RADI

title: Interventional Radiology
description: The M4 Interventional Radiology Elective will introduce the medical students to the most common interventional procedures in special procedures. The students will learn the pertinent arterial and venous anatomy. The students will participate in the assessment, diagnosis, management and treatment of patients for interventional services. Students will observe and participate in IR procedures, imaging interpretation and interactions with patients and referring physicians.
subject: RADI

title: PCAT Radiology Longitudinal
description: In this course, students will gain a broad and practical understanding of the role of radiology in the diagnosis and management of patients. Emphasis will be placed on mastering radiologic terminology, professional workflow, the fundamentals of diagnostic image interpretation, and the clinical indications for imaging examinations and interventional radiologic procedures. Instruction will be delivered through didactic lectures, problem-based learning exercises, computer tutorials, conferences, self-directed learning, and direct observation of film interpretation and specialized procedures. The course will also address the indications and contraindications of radiologic examinations, as well as the benefits and limitations of various imaging methods. Additionally, students will have the opportunity to work closely with attending radiologists, observing and participating in all phases of their daily practice.
subject: RADI

title: Radiology Away Rotation
description: The student will complete an intensive study of Radiology at another hospital or practice. This course must be approved through the VSAS application service or office of the M3/M4 Director.
subject: RADI

title: Helping Relationships: Fundamentals of Counseling Practice
description: Development of communication skills necessary to foster and develop effective counseling relationships.
subject: RCON

title: Culture and Disability
description: Concepts and procedures relating to disability and culture, covering relevant issues affecting racially and culturally diverse individuals with disabilities, as well as promoting sensitivity and competence. Includes implications for an array of helping professions.
subject: RCON

title: LGBT Issues Counseling and Rehabilitation
description: Contemporary issues related to the provision of effective counseling and rehabilitation services with the lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender (LGBT+) population. The focus will be on attaining a level of applied knowledge and awareness commensurate with professional practice.
subject: RCON

title: Human Development and Disability Across the Lifespan
description: Theories and principles of human development as applied to working with people with disabilities and disadvantagement across the lifespan. Focus on evaluation of the effects of service delivery systems and principal contexts (e.g., the family, peer groups, and culture) on individuals. Application of developmental assessment strategies and interventions that helping professionals can utilize to address clients’ needs and enhance positive potentials.
subject: RCON

title: Foundations of Clinical Rehabilitation Counseling
description: Origin, evolution, and future of the rehabilitation counseling profession. Role and functions, scope of practice, and practice settings of rehabilitation counselors.
subject: RCON

title: Foundations in Addictions and Treatment in Counseling
description: Examines the structure and application of theories of addiction, techniques, strategies, and resources for dealing with a broad range of addictive behavior, including substance related and process, or behavioral, focused, and the environmental, social, and cultural contexts related to addiction.
subject: RCON

title: Introduction to Rehabilitation Research and Program Evaluation
description: Foundations underlying research and assessment methodologies and their application to counseling. Research design, program evaluation, ethical principles in research, the scholarly research process, and statistical software packages.
subject: RCON

title: Foundations in Career Counseling
description: Introduction to the origin and evolution of career counseling as well as the social, cultural, and political issues, trends, and organizational variables that impact the field. Presents the role and functions, scope of practice, and practice settings of career counselors.
subject: RCON

title: Ethics in Rehabilitation Counseling and Helping Relationships
description: Examines contemporary ethical concerns, issues, decision-making models, and best practices in rehabilitation counseling and the helping professions. Knowledge and awareness appropriate for master's-level professionals with a particular focus on working with people with disabilities or disadvantagement. Application of knowledge across a range of helping relationships.
subject: RCON

title: Rehabilitation Counseling Theories and Practice
description: Individual counseling theories and techniques applied to a wide range of persons, including persons with a disability (emotional, psychosocial, mental, and physical) and disadvantaged persons.
subject: RCON
prereq: Prerequisites: RCON 601 or equivalent.

title: Addiction and the Family
description: Examines theories and principles of family systems and systems-based approaches as applied to working with people with substance use disorders and/or addictive behaviors and their families.
subject: RCON

title: Rehabilitation Assessment
description: Assessment in clinical rehabilitation, including assessment instruments, methods, materials, and interpretation as applied to a variety of rehabilitation clients. Concepts, skill development, and application of clinical rehabilitation assessment.
subject: RCON

title: Group Counseling in Rehabilitation Settings
description: Principles and practice of group counseling techniques applied to a wide range of persons, including persons with a disability (emotional, psychosocial, mental, and physical) or disadvantaged persons.
subject: RCON
prereq: Prerequisites: RCON 601 and RCON 711 or equivalents.

title: Career Counseling Theories and the World of Work
description: Career counseling theories, processes, and techniques, with emphasis on persons with disabilities and vocational placement. Identification of values, interests, abilities, and methods for obtaining, organizing, and utilizing career information to enable career success of persons with disabilities and disadvantagement.
subject: RCON

title: Medical and Psychosocial Aspects of Disability
description: Medical and psychosocial aspects of chronic illness and disability, including physical, psychological, social, and educational impacts of disability. Functional limitations and impact on employment, family/social life, and daily living. Approaches to treatment, prevention, and an overview of adaptive measures to enhance functioning and overall quality of life.
subject: RCON

title: Professional Issues in Clinical Rehabilitation Counseling
description: Professional issues and functions within the clinical rehabilitation process. Includes a detailed exploration of the case management process, independent provider status, expert witness status, forensic rehabilitation, life care planning, and managed care systems. Addresses emerging professional issues.
subject: RCON

title: Leadership in Rehabilitation and the Helping and Healthcare Professions
description: Explores leadership skills, traits, and theories, and their relevance in helping and health professions and rehabilitation.
subject: RCON

title: Advanced Career Counseling and the World of Work
description: Examines career development, theory, and strategies for advanced career counseling practice. Focus on employment trends and labor market information and resources to address the unique needs of individuals across the lifespan. Explores organizational behavior to understand the role individuals and groups have on behavior within organizations and its application in career counseling.
subject: RCON
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in RCON 725.

title: Practicum in Counseling and Rehabilitation
description: Supervised counseling experience in an approved institution or agency.
subject: RCON
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in RCON 720.

title: Internship in Clinical Rehabilitation Counseling
description: Supervised counseling experience in a work setting suitable for a clinical rehabilitation counselor.
subject: RCON
prereq: Prerequisites: RCON 880.

title: Internship in Clinical Addiction Counseling
description: Supervised counseling experience in a work setting suitable for an addiction counselor.
subject: RCON
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in RCON 880.

title: Internship in Career Counseling
description: Supervised counseling experience in a work setting suitable for a career counselor.
subject: RCON
prereq: Prerequisites: B or better in RCON 880.

title: Exploring Religion
description: Beliefs and practices of the world's religions and the methods scholars use to study them.
subject: RELG

title: Comparative Religion
description: Issues, theories, and debates that shape global religious traditions, cultures, and communities; examination of historical contexts and development, applying social scientific inquiry and methods to analyze relevant current circumstances and concerns.
subject: RELG

title: Religion and Culture
description: Exploration of the dynamic relationships between selected religions and cultures.
subject: RELG

title: Introduction to Reason and Faith
description: Critical study of the intellectual strands leading to Western disconnections between reason and faith; the search for balance between belief and reason with emphasis on contemporary developments.
subject: RELG

title: Introduction to Religions in America
description: The diversity of religious traditions in America.
subject: RELG

title: Morality, Ethics, and Religion
description: Values and ethics as developed, contested, and transmitted through a variety of religious practices.
subject: RELG

title: History of the Devil
description: A survey of the beliefs and practices associated with the demonic and the Devil from c 500 B.C.E. to the 20th century.
subject: RELG

title: Introduction to African American Religions
description: The variety of religious traditions of African Americans, with emphasis on the contexts in which they developed.
subject: RELG

title: Biblical Archaeology
description: The fundamental elements of human culture as it relates to biblical archaeology. The defining characteristics of different kinds of society through interdependency of language and culture. The affects of modern world interests in defining / redefining this area.
subject: RELG

title: History of Magic
description: History of magic as theory and practice from antiquity to modernity and beyond. Chronological, geographical, and thematic range stretches from ancient Mesopotamia, Greece, and Rome; to the Jewish, Christian, and Islamic worlds; to early modernity and the modern period in Africa, Asia, Europe, and the Americas.
subject: RELG

title: Introduction to Hinduism
description: An interdisciplinary examination of the complexity of the Hindu religious and philosophical traditions covering such topics as deity, self, cosmos, body ritual, karma, and yoga.
subject: RELG

title: Ethics and the Good Life in Asian Religions
description: Fundamental questions of ethical and moral inquiry in the religious traditions of Asia.
subject: RELG

title: Introduction to Buddhism
description: An introduction to Buddhism from a social historical perspective that examines Buddhist religious goals, ethics, values, and practices in the local contexts of India, Sri Lanka, Tibet, China, and Japan.
subject: RELG

title: Buddhist Meditation in Theory and Practice
description: Buddhist methods of meditation, asceticism, and similar disciplinary practices for personal and social transformation. Examination of classic Buddhist works from diverse cultures with attention to modern American practices.
subject: RELG

title: Introduction to Judaism
description: Overview of Jewish experiences, beliefs, practices from a contextual point of view.
subject: RELG

title: Introduction to Christianity
description: Introduction to the Christian religion, with emphasis on the history of the major traditions and movements that have shaped the multicultural practices and social impact of modern global Christianity.
subject: RELG

title: Introduction to Islam
description: Interpretation of primary materials reflecting many dimensions of the Islamic religious tradition, such as the Qur'an, Hadith, legal, and theological and mystical writings, art, rituals, and contemporary Muslim voices.
subject: RELG

title: Anthropology of Magic and Religion
description: A comparative examination of such topics as ritual, cosmology, revitalization movements, magic, witchcraft, myth, and possession.
subject: RELG

title: Global Human Religiosity
description: The human experience and expression of what it means to be religious.
subject: RELG

title: Religion and the Arts
description: Literary, visual, and/or performance art associated with religious discourse and practice.
subject: RELG

title: Special Topics in Religious Studies
description: Special topics in Religious Studies. May be repeated as content varies by title.
subject: RELG

title: Hebrew Bible (Old Testament)
description: Modern study of the Hebrew Bible from historical, literary, and archeological points of view. Reading and analysis of texts in translation.
subject: RELG

title: New Testament
description: Historical and critical study of the New Testament writings, with emphasis on origins, production, and transmission.
subject: RELG

title: Paul and the Philosophers
description: Paul’s teachings and practices, as shown in his letters and how these resemble those of various cultural formations of his time, with emphasis on moral teachings and schools of Hellenistic philosophy.
subject: RELG

title: Gospel Literature and the Formation of Christianity
description: Gospels about Jesus from the 1st and 2nd centuries CE; analysis with attention to canonical texts as well as those not contained in today’s major canonical collections; assessment of gospel literature in competing configurations of Christianity during its formative years.
subject: RELG

title: The Life and Letters of Paul
description: A critical study in the life and thought of Paul, his letters to the early Christian churches, his role in the expansion of the Christian movement, and his continuing influence today.
subject: RELG

title: Religion and Culture
description: The impact of religion on modern Western culture and, in turn, of culture on religion. Selected topics: Holocaust, Puritanism, fundamentalism, Islam, Freud, love wisdom tradition, civil religion.
subject: RELG

title: Early Christianity
description: Christianity in the 1st through 5th centuries; its formation as seen through the literature of early Christians and their detractors.
subject: RELG

title: Imagining Jesus: Antiquity to Present
description: Conceptions and representations of Jesus in antiquity up to the present; including the gospel traditions as well as literature, art, and film.
subject: RELG

title: The Greek New Testament
description: Readings in the Gospels and Epistles.
subject: RELG
prereq: Prerequisites: GREK 121 and GREK 122.

title: Prophets and Prophecy
description: Prophets, prophetic speech, and prophetic literature from the Hebrew Bible until today; their literary, sociological, and historical contexts; and the reception and interpretation of prophecy.
subject: RELG

title: Christian Theology
description: Basic Christian teachings concerning God, creation, sin, the person and work of Christ, and life after death.
subject: RELG

title: Sex, Gender, and Religion
description: Gender and sexuality in the shaping of social and individual identity in religious contexts.
subject: RELG

title: Asian Religious Philosophy
description: A historical overview and critical introduction to the philosophical practices of Asian religions; an examination of the basic worldviews, thought frameworks, and foundational questions of the main schools of premodern Asian religious philosophy.
subject: RELG

title: Christian Ethics
description: Basic Christian teachings concerning human nature and conduct; historical foundations and contemporary applications.
subject: RELG

title: Social Justice Religion
description: Historical, contextual, and developmental aspects of social justice as a significant function of religion.
subject: RELG

title: Religion and Politics
description: Relationships between religion and the structure, institutions, and content of a nation’s political processes.
subject: RELG

title: Religion and Sociology
description: Religion isn’t just personal—it shapes politics, communities, health, and relationships. Examine how religion interacts with culture and society, and how society shapes religion.
subject: RELG

title: Law and Religious Traditions
description: The study of the role of law, legal argumentation, and legal contexts in one or more religious traditions.
subject: RELG

title: God and the Gods
description: The worship of Yahweh and other deities in ancient Israel with special attention to the evolution of monotheism.
subject: RELG

title: Religions of the African Diaspora
description: Explore development/theologies of African/African Diaspora religions; examine misunderstandings; arrive at a more sophisticated and nuanced vision of these religions and the people who hold them.
subject: RELG

title: Hindu Devotional Traditions
description: One of the main paths to the divine in the Hindu tradition; deep devotion to a god or goddess, or bhakti, with expressions in art, poetry, mythology, theology, and ascetic fervor.
subject: RELG
prereq: Prerequisites: At least one 100- or 200-level Religious Studies course.

title: Tantra: Sex, Power, and Bliss in South Asian Religions
description: Investigation of the Buddhist and Hindu religious ideas and practices known as tantra. Topics include tantric views of the human body, freedom, and consciousness; tantric use of sec, imagination, visualization, and manipulation of bodily energy; role of tantric traditions in south Asian religions and cultures.
subject: RELG

title: Buddhist Stories, Poetry, and Films
description: Buddhist stories, poetry, novels, and films from various cultures and times examined for how they creatively convey their religious ideals. Study of great works of Buddhist writing, in English translation, analyzing their techniques and examining how Buddhists use film today for similar aims.
subject: RELG

title: Yoga: The Art of Spiritual Transformation
description: Examination of major South Asian religions--Hinduism, Jainism, Buddhism, and Islam, emphasizing the historical context for changing religious ideals, and the commingling of traditions.
subject: RELG

title: Religions of East Asia
description: Expansion of Buddhism beyond India, development of Confucianism, Taoism, Shinto, and other national religious expressions in China and Japan.
subject: RELG

title: Islamic Institutions and Traditions
description: The religious, political, social and economic institutions and intellectual and scholarly traditions developed by Muslim societies throughout Afro-Eurasia from late antiquity to the present.
subject: RELG

title: The Qur'an and Hadith
description: Intensive study of the Qur'an and Hadith: its major themes and literary quality, with attention to a range of classical and contemporary discourses about the Qur'an, both Islamic and Western.
subject: RELG

title: Islamic Theology and Philosophical Thought
description: Close reading and discussion of primary texts (the Qur'an, Hadith, creeds, classical theological arguments, and modern writings) on major theological problems such as salvation, God, revelation, and religious pluralism.
subject: RELG

title: Psychology of Religion
description: The development of the religious consciousness and its various expressions, the psychological dynamics of growth and conversion, response to crisis, and the relation of spiritual practice to health and wholeness.
subject: RELG

title: American Islamic Thought
description: Examines the work of a range of living Muslim-American thinkers.
subject: RELG

title: Sufism
description: A survey of Islamic mysticism, its foundation in the Quranic revelation doctrines and practices, subsequent development, significance within Islamic civilization, and role in the contemporary world, both Islamic and non-Islamic.
subject: RELG

title: Messiahs, Mystics and Rebels in the Islamic World
description: Representative messianic movements, millenarian visionaries and apocalyptic imaginings in the Islamic world from the 7th century to the present, with attention to related developments in the Jewish and Christian traditions over the last two millennia.
subject: RELG

title: Islamic Law
description: Close reading and discussion of primary texts (scriptural, classical, and modern) and accounts of court cases, focuses on one aspect of Islamic law such as equity, violence, authority, or gender.
subject: RELG

title: Religion and Existentialism
description: Existentialist thought as adapted by theologians to interpret religious experience and the biblical message. The movement from philosophical protest against essentialism into imaginative description of existence revealed under stress.
subject: RELG

title: Literature and Film of the Holocaust
description: Film, poetry and literature created in response to the Holocaust as the means for a decades long cultural discussion, in European and American societies, of the moral and religious implications of the Holocaust on our self-understandings as religious and moral beings.
subject: RELG
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ENGL 101 and ENGL 102.

title: Religion in the South
description: Regional faith traditions in Southern historical-cultural context: evangelical piety, denominational tradition, revival, African-American church, Lost Cause idealism.
subject: RELG

title: Religion and Literature
description: Classic literary works from one or more religious traditions which have shaped and/or expressed the core ethos of a religious tradition or of the more general human concern for the religious and spiritual; and/or general literature (fiction, poetry, plays, essays, non-fiction) which incorporates religious or spiritual references, ideas, symbolism, allusions.
subject: RELG

title: Jewish History I: Late Antiquity to 1500
description: The religious, cultural, social, and political conditions that shaped the Jewish experience in the Near East and Europe from Late Antiquity to 1500.
subject: RELG

title: Jewish History II: 1500 to the Present
description: Case studies of Jewish history in Europe, America, and the land of Israel, 1500 to the present.
subject: RELG

title: Jews and Muslims
description: Jewish-Muslim relations in the Near East and the US; an exploration of Jewish-Muslim encounters, issues of religious law, politics, radical religious ideologies, and their repercussions for today.
subject: RELG

title: Kabbalah: Science, Religion and Nature in Western History
description: Historical investigation of kabbalah, a philosophical system and mystical current common to Judaism, Islam, and Christianity, from the medieval period to the present; focus on its formative role in early modern Western cosmology and science, and its ecological implications today.
subject: RELG

title: Theories of Religion
description: A historical overview of major theories and approaches in the academic study of religion.
subject: RELG

title: Independent Study
description: Contract approved by instructor, advisor, and department chair is required for undergraduate students.
subject: RELG

title: Origins of Western Morality
description: Greek and Roman ethical theory, its adaptation into Judean and Christian traditions and impact on Western models of morality.
subject: RELG

title: Faith, Doubt, and God
description: Judeo-Christian views of God; modern criticism and contemporary responses.
subject: RELG

title: Race and Religion
description: Conceptualization of race and its transformative impact on the study of religion; genealogical, historical, and theoretical parameters of the intersection between race and religion; recent scholarship that combines the categories of race and religion in the context of various religious communities.
subject: RELG

title: Religion and Science: Human Questions
description: Relationships between religion and science, especially considering impacts of mutual responses and questions with respect to human life in the 21st century.
subject: RELG

title: Religions, Medicines, and Healing
description: Inter-relationships between religion, medicines, and healing; examining perspectives and practices, interfaces and influences across cultures.
subject: RELG

title: Spiritual Lives
description: Contemporary and historical life-stories about spiritual or religious figures as presented in various forms such as biography, autobiography, hagiography, art, and/or film; explores both the specific issues within unique accounts and idealized, general models for spiritual lives.
subject: RELG

title: Visions of Apocalypse
description: Symbolic visions, tours of heaven and hell, cosmic battles, divine judgment, messianic figures, prophecy, or other forms of revelation as found in literature, art, or social movements from diverse geographical and historical locations.
subject: RELG

title: Perspective in Religious Studies
description: Build an understanding of the contexts of religious studies; participate in ongoing scholarly discussions; and expand the serious student’s skills in critically analyzing religions.
subject: RELG

title: Advanced Special Topics in Religious Studies
description: Advanced special topics in Religious Studies. May be repeated as content varies by title.
subject: RELG

title: Special Topics in Research in Religious Studies
description: Focused research on special topics in Religious Studies. May be repeated as content varies by title.
subject: RELG

title: Advanced Project
description: A supervised research project or other creative work, required of intensive majors, to be completed in the senior year.
subject: RELG

title: Tradition and Transformations in Islamic Cultures
description: Islam as a dynamic cultural tradition: emphasis on the tension between Islamization and the larger Islamic tradition.
subject: RELG

title: Problems in the Comparative Study of Religion
description: Introduction to formative thinkers in the discipline of religious studies, with attention to the methodological problems of comparison.
subject: RELG

title: Foundational Readings
description: Foundational texts appropriate to the student's area of specialization and required for advanced course work and thesis preparation.
subject: RELG

title: The Christology of the New Testament
description: Examination of the four major New Testament titles: Son of Man, Christ, Lord, and Son of God. Attention also given to some lesser-known titles and to Christology in context.
subject: RELG

title: Religion and Politics
description: Religion as a factor in the comparative politics and international relations of states and societies.
subject: RELG

title: Israelite Religion
description: Beliefs and practices in ancient Israelite religion, with particular attention to the emergence of monotheism. Comparison with other ancient Near Eastern religions.
subject: RELG

title: Religion and Literature
description: Introduction to historical and contemporary approaches to the cross-disciplinary study of the inter-relations of the religious and the literary imaginations.
subject: RELG

title: Black Christianity in America
description: Elements in the religion of the black slave in early America, the development of black churches and theological movements.
subject: RELG

title: Black and Liberation Theology
description: Tenets, themes, and representative figures in black and liberation theology in the United States and in Central and South America.
subject: RELG

title: Nineteenth-Century American Evangelical Thought
description: The diverse heritage of American evangelism, with special emphasis on its socio-political and theological origins in the late 18th and 19th centuries.
subject: RELG

title: Twentieth-Century Christology
description: Various 20th-century christological perspectives, with special emphasis on the person and work of Jesus as bases for addressing life/death and hope/despair issues.
subject: RELG

title: World Spirituality
description: An examination of the perennialist approach to the mystical and contemplative teachings of the major religious traditions.
subject: RELG

title: Seminar in Philosophical Theology
description: Examination of contemporary problems in the philosophical foundations of religion.
subject: RELG

title: Special Topics in Texts and Traditions
description: Topics related to the study of texts in the life of specific religious communities. Course content varies; individual topics will be announced.
subject: RELG

title: Special Topics in Theology and Religious Thought
description: Topics related to the study of religious doctrines, cosmologies, spiritual practices, and ethics. Course content varies; individual topics will be announced.
subject: RELG

title: Special Topics in Religion and Society
description: Topics examining religious institutions, practices, and experiences in relation to other cultural forms. Course content varies; individual topics will be announced.
subject: RELG

title: Fashion History: A Global View
description: Examination of influences on fashion throughout history both domestically and globally.
subject: RETL

title: Fashion Through the Ages: 1800 A.D. to Present
description: Introduction to the history of fashion from 1800 A.D. to the present.
subject: RETL

title: Exploration of Retail Management and Fashion Merchandising Industries
description: Exploration of retail management and fashion merchandising curriculum and careers.
subject: RETL

title: History of Designers
description: Survey of influential fashion designers since 1857, examining their design influences and their contributions to fashion.
subject: RETL

title: The Changing Consumer Marketplace
description: The examination of shopping habits and patterns for U.S. consumers in today’s changing marketplace. Effective strategies for reaching various segments of the population from a retailing perspective.
subject: RETL

title: HRSM Professional Communications
description: Theory, processes, and applications of business communications.
subject: RETL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in ENGL 101 and ENGL 102.

title: Principles of Accounting I
description: A study of the accounting cycle with emphasis on preparation and analysis of financial statements.
subject: RETL

title: Principles of Accounting II
description: A study of the preparation and interpretation of corporate financial statements with an emphasis on analysis and decision making techniques.
subject: RETL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in RETL 261.

title: Principles of Retailing
description: Management methods, location analysis, store organization, personnel, planning, buying and pricing techniques, and customer service policies for retail firms.
subject: RETL

title: Principles of Fashion Merchandising
description: The place of fashion in buying, selling, and promoting merchandise. Meets the needs of individuals in retail organizations from entry level to buyer.
subject: RETL

title: Retailing Practicum
description: Supervised work experience in an area of the retail industry, selected by the student and approved by the instructor.
subject: RETL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in RETL 265.

title: Digital Retailing
description: Development of a comprehensive plan for implementing a retailing business online via digital technology.
subject: RETL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in RETL 265.

title: Asset Protection for Retailers
description: Examination of asset protection and risk management issues which affect the retailing industry, such as retail risk assessment and response, loss prevention, employee-related risks, facility security, crisis management, and intellectual property protection.
subject: RETL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in RETL 265.

title: Sustainability in Fashion and Retail
description: Examination of concepts, practices, and opportunities of fashion sustainability (such as social and environmental responsibility) available to designers, developers and consumers.
subject: RETL

title: Human Resource Management in Hospitality, Retail, and Sport Management
description: Recruitment, selection, utilization, and development of human resources; role of supervisors in management and personnel administration.
subject: RETL

title: Digital Innovations in Fashion Merchandising
description: Examination of transformative impact of digital innovations on the fashion industry.
subject: RETL

title: Sales Strategies
description: Theories, principles, and techniques of personal selling with application to different buyer-seller situations.
subject: RETL

title: Retail Entrepreneurship
description: Essentials of creating and operating a new retail venture in physical and virtual environments.
subject: RETL

title: Principles of Customer Service
description: Essential skills necessary to manage successful service operations, including retail, e-commerce, hospitality/tourism, food/beverage, and sports/event organizations.
subject: RETL

title: Visual Merchandising and Store Design
description: Displays and visual merchandising strategies.
subject: RETL

title: Retail Buying
description: Planning, purchasing, and controlling inventories.
subject: RETL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in RETL 261.

title: Fashion Product Analysis
description: Analysis of fashion products with emphasis on textile selection, product construction, life cycle, cost elements, and the changing demographics of the fashion consumer.
subject: RETL

title: Retail Promotion
description: Planning and executing retail promotion strategies.
subject: RETL

title: Global Luxury Fashion Management
description: The course covers the historical context of luxury, the psychology of the consumer, and the core principles of brand building within the fashion and accessories sectors. Topics include Sustainability, Artificial Intelligence, Marketing, and Financial Management in Fashion.
subject: RETL

title: Advanced Retail Accounting
description: Accounting topics related to retail establishment with emphasis on managerial interpretation and use.
subject: RETL

title: Fashion Forecasting
description: Forecasting fashion trends to impact retail merchandising performance.
subject: RETL

title: Special Topics in Retailing
description: Current topics and trends in retail management and/or fashion merchandising. Content varies by title. May be repeated once.
subject: RETL

title: Retailing and Fashion Merchandising Field Study
description: Introduction to international/domestic manufacturing, retail, wholesale and ancillary businesses focused on retail and fashion merchandising. May be repeated once for credit.
subject: RETL

title: Independent Study
description: Contract approved by instructor, advisor, and department head is required for undergraduate students.
subject: RETL

title: Retail Finance
description: Retail Finance provides a link between accounting and finance emphasizing the various financial aspects needed to operate a successful retail venture. Emphasis is placed on corporate finance. Also, introduces financial institutions, investment fundamentals, time value of money, and capital budgeting techniques.
subject: RETL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in RETL 262.

title: Customer Experience Management
description: Study of customer trends and experience management.
subject: RETL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in RETL 265.

title: Retail Branding Strategies
description: Overview of retail branding strategies with emphasis on implications of the development of brand equity towards increasing customer loyalty.
subject: RETL

title: Space Optimization for Merchandise Management
description: The knowledge and essential transferable skills to optimize retail space for strategic merchandise management.
subject: RETL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in RETL 366.

title: Global Sourcing in Retail and Fashion
description: Exploration of theoretical, political, economic, social, and environmental implications of global sourcing decisions in retail and fashion.
subject: RETL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in RETL 261 and RETL 265.

title: Category Management
description: Application of category management principles and models to competitive behavior in retailing with a focus on product category issues. Case-based analysis and/or JDA computer software will be applied to industry-specific problems related to inventory management.
subject: RETL
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in RETL 265.

title: Multi-National Retailing
description: Retail operations within foreign environments.
subject: RETL

title: Retail Management Strategies
description: Application of strategic management principles and models to competitive behavior in retailing.
subject: RETL

title: Retailing Internship
description: Supervised work experience within the retail industry that links classroom learning and student interest with the acquisition of knowledge in an applied work setting.
subject: RETL
prereq: Prerequisites: RETL 295.

title: Legal Aspects of Entrepreneurship and E-Commerce
description: Examination of domestic and international laws affecting retail entrepreneurship and online commerce, such as data privacy and breach response, intellectual property protection, sales tax, advertising and unfair trade practices, consumer protection laws, employment laws, and legal obligations involving physical locations.
subject: RETL
prereq: Prerequisites: SPTE 240 or equivalent.

title: Fashion and the Law
description: Examination of domestic and international laws which affect the fashion industry, such as intellectual property protection, licensing agreements, operational and marketing issues, and international trade.
subject: RETL
prereq: Prerequisites: SPTE 240 or equivalent.

title: Retail Logistics
description: Examination of the flow of retail inventory from initial production to final purchase. Meets the needs of individuals in retail organizations from entry-level sales floor personnel to buyers. Students must be qualified to enroll in a 500 level course at The University of South Carolina.
subject: RETL

title: Retail and Fashion Business Planning
description: Essential skills for building a new or expanding an existing retail or fashion business in both brick-and-mortar and online venues by developing a marketing plan and corresponding e-Commerce website for a business or fashion organization.
subject: RETL
prereq: Prerequisites: RETL 351.

title: Advanced Merchandising Management Strategies
description: The analysis of assortment planning and inventory management of apparel products utilizing merchandising principles and industry software.
subject: RETL

title: Advanced Retail Promotion and Social Media Analytics
description: Essential principles and analytical tools used in retail promotion; appraisal of methods and outcomes via field experiences, visuals, and simulations.
subject: RETL

title: Special Topics in Retail Management
description: Course content varies. May be repeated once under a different title.
subject: RETL

title: Fundamentals of Omni-Channel Retailing
description: Exploration of the fundamentals of Omni-Channel Retailing.
subject: RETL

title: Personnel Development Relations Management
description: Advanced examination of human resource management within retail organizations.
subject: RETL

title: Customer Relationship Management for the Retail Industry
description: The analysis of customer relationship management for retailers utilizing merchandising principles and industry software.
subject: RETL

title: Advanced Omni-Channel Retailing
description: Advanced examination of Omni-channel retailing.
subject: RETL

title: Retailing E-Commerce
description: Examination of e-commerce elements and retailer implications.
subject: RETL

title: Customer Experience Optimization in the Retail Environment
description: Study of customers’ needs, activities, and trends to aid retail strategy formulation for enhancing customer experiences in retail environments.
subject: RETL

title: Retail Loss Prevention
description: The analysis of current retail loss prevention issues from the perspective of the business and customer. Meets the needs of individuals in retail organizations from entry level sales floor personnel to senior management.
subject: RETL

title: Leadership for Retailers
description: The course addresses both leadership theory and practice in the evolving domain of retail and fashion merchandising businesses, utilizing a combined theoretical and practical case-based approach to problem solving.
subject: RETL

title: Omni-Channel Workforce Management
description: Advanced examination of workforce management for Omni-channel retailers.
subject: RETL

title: International Retailing
description: Broad overview of retail marketing theories, principles, and methods for international operations focusing on the cultural, economic, and regulatory environments.
subject: RETL

title: Competitive Strategies in Retailing
description: Fundamentals of strategic decision-making and performance measurement within the retail organization.
subject: RETL

title: Advanced Retail Space Management
description: Advanced examination of retail space allocation and management of merchandise via retail analytics and JDA software.
subject: RETL

title: Advanced Category Management
description: Advanced examination of category management strategies.
subject: RETL

title: Advanced Sales Strategies for Retail
description: Advanced strategic decision-making theories, principles, and techniques used in different buyer-seller situations by a retail organization.
subject: RETL

title: Special Topics in Marketing Education
description: Contemporary topics, trends, and issues in marketing education. Individual topics may be announced by titles.
subject: RETL

title: Financial Management for Retailers
description: This course examines financial information management necessary to perform the usual duties and responsibilities associated with the retail industry.
subject: RETL

title: Directed Study in Retailing
description: Independent study for advanced students under faculty supervision.
subject: RETL

title: Assistive and Adaptive Technology
description: The use of Assistive Technology as it relates to employment, education, communication, recreation, and mobility for individuals with disabiliities. Explores types of assistive technologies, functional assessments, and resources through hands-on application, lectures, and discussions.
subject: RHAB

title: Introduction to Rehabilitation Research and Assessment
description: Foundations underlying research and assessment methodologies and their application to counseling. Research design, program evaluation, ethical principles in research, the scholarly research process, and statistical software packages.
subject: RHAB

title: Ethics in Rehabilitation Counseling
description: Examines contemporary ethical concerns and issues in rehabilitation counseling profession. The focus will be on attaining a level of applied ethical knowledge and awareness for master’s-level professionals.
subject: RHAB

title: Culture and Disability
description: Concepts and procedures relating to disability and culture, covering relevant issues affecting racially and culturally diverse individuals with disabilities, as well as promoting sensitivity and competence.
subject: RHAB

title: Medical Aspects of Rehabilitation
description: This course is concerned with imparting medical terminology, the muscular, skeletal, and neurological systems, and common diagnostic categories encountered in rehabilitation counseling. Understanding and utilization of symptomology, treatment, and other management aspects of physical medicine are emphasized. The major outcome is directed toward developing the counselor’s ability to interpret medical information meaningfully to a plan of action for the client’s rehabilitation.
subject: RHAB
prereq: Prerequisites: RHAB 570.

title: Rehabilitation Counseling Practice II
description: Individual counseling theory and technique applied to persons with a disability (emotional, psychosocial, mental, and physical) and disadvantaged persons.
subject: RHAB
prereq: Prerequisites: RCON 601.

title: Occupational Analysis and Placement in Rehabilitation
description: Sequential set of opportunities to acquire and apply knowledge, skills, and insights pertaining to the employment of persons with disabilities.
subject: RHAB
prereq: Prerequisites: RCON 700, RCON 702.

title: Career Development and Counseling in Rehabilitation
description: Career development theories and their relevance to persons with disabilities. Identification of values, interests, abilities, and methods for obtaining, organizing, and utilizing career information to enable career success.
subject: RHAB

title: Rehabilitation Assessment
description: Vocational assessment instruments, methods, materials, and interpretation are applied to a variety of rehabilitation clients. Concepts, skill development, and application of vocational assessment.
subject: RHAB
prereq: Prerequisites: RHAB 702

title: Group Counseling in Rehabilitation Settings
description: Principles and practice of group counseling applied to persons with a disability, or disadvantaged persons.
subject: RHAB
prereq: Prerequisites: RCON 601.

title: Case Management and Community Resources in Rehabilitation
description: Focuses on factors which facilitate or deter rehabilitation caseload movement. A detailed task analysis of case intake, case study, individualized written rehabilitation program planning, case services, and case closure within agency procedural regulations are emphasized.
subject: RHAB

title: Technology and Exceptional Populations
description: The application of microcomputers and other technology in services for special populations. Case management, assessment, and instructional uses of technology are included.
subject: RHAB

title: Disability and Sexuality
description: Impact of major disabling conditions on sexual functioning; sex education and counseling of disabled persons.
subject: RHAB

title: Rehabilitation and Severe Disability
description: Course examines the specialized knowledge and techniques required to rehabilitate persons with severe physical, mental-emotional, and social disabilities.
subject: RHAB
prereq: Prerequisites: RCON 700.

title: Counseling and Death Education
description: Counseling approaches with the terminally ill and surviving family members.
subject: RHAB

title: Counseling Practicum I
description: Supervised counseling experience in an approved institution or agency. Official application must be submitted at least one month before the end of the semester preceding enrollment. Supervised counseling experience in an approved institution or agency. Required 150 hours.
subject: RHAB

title: Counseling Internship
description: Counseling experience will be gained in a work setting similar to that in which a counselor will eventually be employed.
subject: RHAB
prereq: Prerequisites: RHAB 880

title: Elementary Russian
description: Grammar and practical vocabulary necessary for fundamental communication skills. Assumes no prior experience in the language.
subject: RUSS

title: Basic Proficiency in Russian
description: Practice and further development of essential listening, reading, speaking, and writing skills.
subject: RUSS
prereq: Prerequisites: RUSS 121.

title: Intermediate Russian I
description: Continued exposure to the fundamentals of Russian grammar, along with increased focus on reading and speaking skills.
subject: RUSS
prereq: Prerequisites: RUSS 122 or satisfactory score on language placement test.

title: Intermediate Russian II
description: Completion of exposure to the fundamentals of Russian grammar, with emphasis on writing, reading, and conversation.
subject: RUSS
prereq: Prerequisites: RUSS 201 or satisfactory score on language placement test.

title: Introduction to Russian Civilization
description: A multimedia introduction to Russian culture from its beginnings to the present. No knowledge of Russian required.
subject: RUSS

title: Selected Topics
description: Introductory-level study of selected topics in Russian culture. Does not apply toward the Russian major. May be repeated for credit under a different title.
subject: RUSS

title: Russian Conversation and Composition I
description: Conversation, reading, composition, comprehensive review of grammar.
subject: RUSS
prereq: Prerequisites: RUSS 202 or satisfactory score on language placement test.

title: Russian Conversation and Composition II
description: Emphasis on oral proficiency, using contemporary authentic materials from Russian newspapers, textbooks, and television newscasts.
subject: RUSS
prereq: Prerequisites: RUSS 301 or satisfactory score on language placement test.

title: Nineteenth-Century Russian Literature in Translation
description: Masterworks of Russian literature by Tolstoy, Dostoevsky, Turgenev, Pushkin, Chekov, and others.
subject: RUSS

title: Nineteenth-Century Russian Literature in Russian
description: A Russian-language course designed to supplement 319. Reading and discussion in Russian of 19th-century poetry and prose.
subject: RUSS
prereq: Prerequisites: RUSS 302.

title: Twentieth-Century Russian Literature in Translation
description: Masterworks of Russian literature by Bely, Pasternak, Bulgakov, Nabokov, Solzhenitsyn, and others.
subject: RUSS

title: Twentieth-Century Russian Literature in Russian
description: A Russian-language course designed to supplement RUSS 320 .
subject: RUSS

title: Structure of Russian
description: An introduction to the linguistic structure of Russian, with particular attention to phonology and morphology.
subject: RUSS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in RUSS 202 or by placement.

title: Russian Folklore and Fairy Tales
description: An introduction to Russian fairy tales and other genres of folklore, both in their original form within an oral tradition and as reworked in Russian art and literature.
subject: RUSS

title: Selected Topics
description: Intensive study of selected topics in Russian cultural and/or literary movements. Taught in English. May be repeated for credit under a different title.
subject: RUSS

title: Independent Study
description: Contract approved by instructor, advisor, and department chair is required for undergraduate students.
subject: RUSS

title: Advanced Russian I
description: Acquisition of subtleties of Russian grammar. Increased focus on reading, writing, and discussion.
subject: RUSS
prereq: Prerequisites: RUSS 302 or satisfactory score on language placement test.

title: Advanced Russian II
description: Exposure to prose and poetry from a wide variety of sources and periods. Focus on oral proficiency, reading, comprehension, and writing.
subject: RUSS
prereq: Prerequisites: RUSS 401 or satisfactory score on language placement test.

title: Topics in Russian Language and Culture
description: Topics in Russian language and culture. Course conducted in Russian.
subject: RUSS
prereq: Prerequisites: C or better in RUSS 202.

title: Medieval Russian Culture
description: An introduction to the culture of medieval Russia through its written records, folklore, icons, and ancient religious chant.
subject: RUSS

title: Russian Modernism: Love, Sex and Politics in Revolutionary Russia
description: An exploration of Russian modernist culture, with particular attention to the themes of social and political change. Authors under discussion include Kuzmin, Bely, and Zamyatin.
subject: RUSS

title: Homer in Russia
description: An examination of the influence of Homer’s epic poems The Iliad and The Odyssey on Russian culture, as seen in works by Russian writers including Tolstoy, Pasternak, and Brodsky.
subject: RUSS

title: Writing Russian National Identity
description: An examination of Russian writers reflecting on Russian national identity, including Solzhenitsyn, Dostoevsky, and Grossman.
subject: RUSS

title: Selected Topics in Russian
description: Reading and research on selected topics in Russian. Course content varies and will be announced in the schedule of courses by title.
subject: RUSS

title: Intensive Readings in Russian
description: Intensive reading course for non-majors. Primarily for graduate students to fulfill the foreign- language reading requirement. It will not be applied toward the degree language requirements nor will it be accepted as a substitute in the course sequence leading to the various degree requirements.
subject: RUSS

title: Intensive Readings in Russian
description: Intensive reading course for non-majors. Primarily for graduate students to fulfill the foreign-language reading requirement. It will not be applied toward the degree language requirements nor will it be accepted as a substitute in the course sequence leading to the various degree requirements.
subject: RUSS
prereq: Prerequisites: RUSS 615.

title: Supervised Instruction in Teaching Foreign Languages in College
description: Supervised direction of foreign language teaching in college. Required of all graduate assistants who are teaching. This course will not count toward the MA or PhD degree.
subject: RUSS

title: Social Advocacy and Ethical Life
description: Introduction to nature and relationship of ethics and oral forms of advocacy. Includes foundational training in ethical theory and its relevance to socio-political expression and training in the principles and performance of ethical oral communication, with emphasis on argumentation and audience engagement.
subject: SAEL

description: Extensive study of state government and various facets of public service in South Carolina for students enrolled in the South Carolina Honors College State Government Program. Additional topics may be included as specific needs and interests dictate. This is a required course for the South Carolina Honors College State Government Program.
